topics.title: Classification of Separation Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Separation Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **separation process** is a unit operation (or combination of operations) employed in chemical engineering to transform a homogeneous or heterogeneous mixture into two or more distinct products that differ in composition. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (PECHT756), separation processes are broadly classified based on the **physical principle** exploited to achieve segregation of components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A separation process is a method that converts a mixture into a stream with a higher concentration of a desired component (or vice versa) by exploiting differences in the *physical* or *chemical* properties of the constituent species. The **property exploited** is the primary classification axis.

The major physical/chemical properties that drive separation are:

1. **Size** (e.g., sieving, membrane filtration)
2. **Density** (e.g., centrifugation, sedimentation)
3. **Vapour pressure / Volatility** (e.g., distillation)
4. **Solubility** (e.g., liquid-liquid extraction, leaching)
5. **Charge / Electrical properties** (e.g., electrophoresis, electrodialysis)
6. **Magnetic susceptibility** (e.g., magnetic separation)
7. **Surface / Interfacial tension** (e.g., flotation)
8. **Affinity / Chemical potential** (e.g., adsorption, ion exchange, chromatography)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tea Strainer:**
> Imagine you have brewed tea with loose tea leaves floating in hot water. To separate the *clear tea* (the desired product) from the *leaves* (the impurity), you pour the mixture through a **strainer**.
> - The **size difference** between water molecules and tea leaves is what the strainer exploits.
> - If instead you wanted to separate *two dissolved salts* (e.g., salt and sugar) from water, a strainer would fail — because both are molecularly dissolved. You would need to exploit a different property, such as **volatility** (boiling off water) or **solubility** (crystallization).
> - **The core insight:** The choice of separation technique is dictated by *which property* differentiates your target species. This is the essence of classification.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** A student should never ask *"What is the best separation process?"* The correct question is: *"What property distinguishes my target species from the rest of the mixture?"* — that answer picks the technique.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters

Classification is not academic bookkeeping — it is the **decision-making framework** an engineer uses during process selection. Choosing the wrong classification branch leads to:
- Thermodynamically infeasible designs (e.g., using a membrane to separate a dissolved gas from a liquid when the membrane is selective for ions).
- Excessive energy consumption (e.g., distillation when extraction would suffice).
- Equipment damage (e.g., using a centrifuge rated below the required G-force).

**Standard metrics used in the KTU framework:**
- **Selectivity (α)** — dimensionless, indicates preference for one species.
- **Recovery (R)** — dimensionless fraction (0 to 1) of feed recovered.
- **Capacity** — typically in **kg/m³** or **mol/m³** of sorbent/membrane.
- **Flux (J)** — in **kg/(m²·s)** for membrane-based systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Classification Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme adopts a **two-tier classification** rooted in the *Handbook of Separation Process Technology* (Rowe / Richardson & Coulson) and the *King's separation-process taxonomy* (C.J. King, *Separation Processes*, 2nd ed., 1980 — referenced as per standard KTU reading lists).

**Tier 1 — Separating agent used:**
- **Equilibrium-stage (or rate-governed with no mass separating agent):** e.g., distillation, evaporation, crystallization, drying.
- **Rate-governed with a mass separating agent (MSA):** e.g., extraction, absorption, adsorption, ion exchange, membrane processes.

**Tier 2 — Property exploited:**

| Class | Property Exploited | Typical Processes | Driving Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical / Size-based | Size, shape, density | Filtration, sedimentation, sieving, centrifugation | Gravity, pressure, centrifugal force |
| Equilibrium-based | Volatility, solubility, melting point | Distillation, extraction, crystallization, evaporation | Phase-equilibrium difference |
| Rate / Membrane-based | Permeability, diffusivity, charge | Microfiltration, ultrafiltration, reverse osmosis, pervaporation, gas permeation | Pressure, concentration, electric potential |
| Sorption-based | Affinity, surface area, ionic charge | Adsorption, ion exchange, chromatography | Concentration gradient, Donnan potential |
| Electrical / Magnetic | Charge, magnetic susceptibility, conductivity | Electrodialysis, electrophoresis, magnetic separation | Electric / magnetic field |
| Field / External-force based | Thermal, optical, density gradient | Zone refining, supercritical extraction, foam fractionation | Temperature, pressure, light |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although classification is a conceptual topic, the following equations form the analytical backbone of *every* separation problem you'll encounter. Master these before moving to specific modules.

$$
\text{Recovery} \quad R = \frac{\text{Amount of solute in product}}{\text{Amount of solute in feed}} = \frac{C_p \, V_p}{C_f \, V_f}
$$

$$
\text{Selectivity} \quad \alpha_{ij} = \frac{y_i / y_j}{x_i / x_j}
$$

$$
\text{Stage Requirement} \quad N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{x_D}{1 - x_D}\right)\!\left(\frac{1 - x_W}{x_W}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha}
$$

$$
\text{Membrane Flux (Darcy's Law)} \quad J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_m}
$$

$$
\text{Separation Factor (Membranes)} \quad \beta_{ij} = \frac{C_{i,p} / C_{j,p}}{C_{i,f} / C_{j,f}}
$$

**Notation Reference Table:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Recovery | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $\alpha$ | Selectivity / Relative volatility | dimensionless |
| $J$ | Permeate flux | $\text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$ |
| $\Delta P$ | Transmembrane pressure | $\text{Pa}$ |
| $\mu$ | Viscosity | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ |
| $R_m$ | Membrane resistance | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| $N_{\min}$ | Minimum number of stages | dimensionless |
| $\beta$ | Separation factor | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> In exam answers, always state **the property exploited** as the *first line* of your classification argument. Examiners deduct marks if you only name the process without identifying the driving property.

### 2.3 The Equilibrium vs. Rate-Governed Distinction (High-Yield)

This is a frequently tested distinction in KTU university exams:

- **Equilibrium-stage processes** reach true thermodynamic equilibrium between phases (or pseudo-equilibrium). Examples: distillation, absorption, extraction, leaching, crystallization. Designed using the **McCabe-Thiele** or **Kremser** methods.
- **Rate-governed processes** are limited by the *kinetics* of mass/heat/charge transfer, not by equilibrium. Examples: membrane separations, chromatography, ion exchange, electrophoresis. Designed using **mass-transfer coefficients** ($k_c$, $k_L$, $k_G$) and the **resistance-in-series** model.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's heuristic:** If the question contains a *phase boundary* (vapour-liquid, liquid-liquid, solid-liquid) at equilibrium, it is an **equilibrium-stage** problem. If it contains a *membrane, sorbent bed, or electric field*, it is **rate-governed**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: General Mass Balance Framework for a Separator

A generic separator accepts a **single feed stream** and produces **two product streams** (a *raffinate* / *bottom product* and an *extract* / *top product*). For a binary mixture of components A (target) and B (carrier):

**Step 1 — Total mass balance.**
Around the separator boundary (steady state, no accumulation):

$$
F = P_1 + P_2
$$

**Step 2 — Component balance for solute A.**

$$
F \cdot x_{A,f} = P_1 \cdot x_{A,1} + P_2 \cdot x_{A,2}
$$

**Step 3 — Component balance for carrier B.**

$$
F \cdot x_{B,f} = P_1 \cdot x_{B,1} + P_2 \cdot x_{B,2}
$$

**Step 4 — Recovery of A in stream 1 (definition).**

$$
R_A = \frac{P_1 \cdot x_{A,1}}{F \cdot x_{A,f}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express purity in stream 1.**

$$
\text{Purity}_1 = \frac{P_1 \cdot x_{A,1}}{P_1 \cdot x_{A,1} + P_1 \cdot x_{B,1}} = \frac{x_{A,1}}{x_{A,1} + x_{B,1}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute mass balance to eliminate $P_2$.**

$$
P_2 = F - P_1
$$

$$
F \cdot x_{A,f} = P_1 \cdot x_{A,1} + (F - P_1) \cdot x_{A,2}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $P_1$ in terms of compositions.**

$$
P_1 = F \cdot \frac{x_{A,f} - x_{A,2}}{x_{A,1} - x_{A,2}}
$$

This is the *lever-arm rule* — geometrically, $P_1 / P_2$ lies on a line connecting $(x_{A,2}, 0)$ to $(x_{A,1}, F)$ on a Ponchon-Savarit diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> Every separation process — from distillation to reverse osmosis — satisfies these balances. The difference between processes lies entirely in **how** $P_1$, $P_2$, and the compositions are constrained by thermodynamics or kinetics.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Separation Classes

The following matrix is the most exam-relevant comparative table for KTU. Memorize the diagonal logic.

| Process Class | Property Exploited | MSA Required? | Equilibrium vs. Rate | Selectivity Source | Typical α Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distillation | Volatility (vapour pressure) | No (heat is energy MSA) | Equilibrium | Relative volatility $\alpha = K_i / K_j$ | 1.1 – 50 |
| Extraction | Solubility | Yes (solvent) | Equilibrium | Distribution coefficient $K_D$ | 2 – 1000 |
| Absorption | Solubility in liquid | Yes (solvent) | Equilibrium | Henry's law / $K_G$ | 5 – 500 |
| Adsorption | Surface affinity | Yes (sorbent) | Rate (often) | Langmuir isotherm, $\alpha = (q_A / q_B)/(c_A / c_B)$ | 2 – 10⁶ |
| Ion Exchange | Electrostatic affinity | Yes (resin) | Rate | Selectivity coefficient $K_{A/B}$ | 1.1 – 100 |
| Microfiltration | Size | No (pressure is energy) | Rate | Pore size cut-off | ∞ (binary) |
| Reverse Osmosis | Solvent diffusivity | No (pressure) | Rate | Solubility + diffusivity | 1 – 100 |
| Electrodialysis | Ionic charge | No (electricity) | Rate | Ion transport number | 2 – 50 |
| Crystallization | Melting point / solubility curve | No (cooling/heat) | Equilibrium (with kinetics) | Solubility difference | 2 – 100 |
| Centrifugation | Density | No (centrifugal force) | Rate (sedimentation) | Stokes' law | ∞ (immiscible) |
| Magnetic Separation | Magnetic susceptibility | No (field) | Rate | Susceptibility ratio | 1.01 – 100 |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Identifying the Correct Class

**Problem.** A petroleum feed contains *n*-hexane (b.p. 69 °C) and *n*-heptane (b.p. 98 °C) in roughly equal amounts. A KTU examiner asks: *"Which separation process is most appropriate, and what property is exploited?"*

**Step 1 — Identify the mixture type.**
Both components are **miscible liquids** of similar molecular weight. The mixture is **homogeneous**.

**Step 2 — Identify differentiating properties.**
- Size: nearly identical. ✗
- Charge: both neutral. ✗
- Magnetic: both diamagnetic. ✗
- Volatility: significant difference (29 °C boiling-point gap). ✓
- Solubility in a selective solvent: moderate difference. ✓

**Step 3 — Compare the two viable options.**
Distillation exploits volatility → high α, no MSA needed → **preferred**.
Extraction would require an MSA (e.g., furfural) and a secondary distillation step to recover the solvent.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
**Distillation** is the optimal choice. Property exploited: **volatility (relative volatility, $\alpha \approx 2.16$ for n-hexane/n-heptane)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the classification logic **directly** leads to equipment selection. This is why the KTU 2024 module places classification at the very start of the syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["SEPARATION PROCESSES"] --> B["EQUILIBRIUM STAGE"]
    A --> C["RATE GOVERNED"]

    B --> B1["Distillation"]
    B --> B2["Extraction"]
    B --> B3["Absorption"]
    B --> B4["Crystallization"]
    B --> B5["Evaporation"]

    C --> C1["Membrane Based"]
    C --> C2["Sorption Based"]
    C --> C3["Field Based"]
    C --> C4["Mechanical Based"]

    C1 --> C1a["Microfiltration"]
    C1 --> C1b["Ultrafiltration"]
    C1 --> C1c["Reverse Osmosis"]
    C1 --> C1d["Pervaporation"]
    C1 --> C1e["Gas Permeation"]

    C2 --> C2a["Adsorption"]
    C2 --> C2b["Ion Exchange"]
    C2 --> C2c["Chromatography"]

    C3 --> C3a["Electrodialysis"]
    C3 --> C3b["Electrophoresis"]
    C3 --> C3c["Magnetic Separation"]
    C3 --> C3d["Supercritical Extraction"]

    C4 --> C4a["Filtration"]
    C4 --> C4b["Sedimentation"]
    C4 --> C4c["Centrifugation"]
    C4 --> C4d["Flotation"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Single-Stage Separator)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Feed["FEED STREAM F"] --> Mixer["MIXING ZONE"]
    Mixer --> Separator["SEPARATION DEVICE"]
    MSA["MASS SEPARATING AGENT"] -.-> Separator
    Energy["ENERGY INPUT HEAT OR WORK"] -.-> Separator
    Separator --> Top["PRODUCT 1 ENRICHED PHASE"]
    Separator --> Bot["PRODUCT 2 DEPLETED PHASE"]
    Top --> Check1["RECOVERY CHECK R1"]
    Bot --> Check2["RECOVERY CHECK R2"]
    Check1 --> Out1["OUTLET P1"]
    Check2 --> Out2["OUTLET P2"]
```

### 4.3 Property-to-Process Mapping (Engineering Selection)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["WHAT PROPERTY DIFFERS?"] --> P1["Size?"]
    Q --> P2["Volatility?"]
    Q --> P3["Solubility?"]
    Q --> P4["Charge?"]
    Q --> P5["Magnetic?"]
    Q --> P6["Affinity?"]

    P1 --> S1["Filtration Sieving Centrifugation"]
    P2 --> S2["Distillation Evaporation"]
    P3 --> S3["Extraction Crystallization Leaching"]
    P4 --> S4["Electrodialysis Electrophoresis"]
    P5 --> S5["Magnetic Separation"]
    P6 --> S6["Adsorption Ion Exchange Chromatography"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** A common KTU Part-A question is *"Given component X has property Y, which separation is suitable?"* Use the Property-to-Process mapping above as your mental model. Draw a quick decision tree in the margin if allowed.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define a separation process. What is the fundamental basis for classifying them?**

**Model Answer:**

A **separation process** is a unit operation that converts a mixture of two or more components into two or more products that differ in composition, by exploiting differences in the physical or chemical properties of the constituent species.

The **fundamental basis for classification** is the **property that is exploited** to achieve separation, combined with whether the process is **equilibrium-stage governed** or **rate-governed**, and whether a **mass separating agent (MSA)** is required. The principal properties used for classification are: size, density, volatility (vapour pressure), solubility, charge, magnetic susceptibility, and surface affinity. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`
**Differentiate between equilibrium-stage and rate-governed separation processes with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Equilibrium-Stage | Rate-Governed |
|---|---|---|
| Limiting factor | Thermodynamic equilibrium | Mass/heat transfer kinetics |
| Design basis | Phase-equilibrium data ($K$, $\alpha$) | Mass-transfer coefficients ($k_c$, $k_L$) |
| Examples | Distillation, Absorption, Extraction | Reverse Osmosis, Adsorption, Electrodialysis |
| Phase contact | Multiple stages for high purity | Continuous (often single-stage sufficient) |
| Use of MSA | Sometimes (extraction) or not (distillation) | Always (membrane, sorbent) or energy-driven |

Equilibrium-stage processes assume that the exiting phases reach true thermodynamic equilibrium, while rate-governed processes are limited by how fast molecules migrate across an interface or through a medium. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Classify separation processes used in the chemical industry. Discuss in detail the classification based on the **property exploited**, listing at least six properties with one example process for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a suitable schematic, explain the operation of a **single-stage equilibrium separator**. Derive the equations for component recovery and selectivity for a binary mixture, clearly defining all terms. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark).**
Separation processes are classified along three orthogonal axes: (i) the **physical property exploited**, (ii) the **separating agent used** (mass separating agent vs. energy separating agent), and (iii) **equilibrium vs. rate-governed** character.

**Step 2 — Enumerate the six properties (4 marks).**

1. **Size (and shape):** Exploited in filtration, sieving, microfiltration. The driving force is pressure.
2. **Density (and mass):** Exploited in centrifugation, sedimentation, hydrocyclones. The driving force is gravity or centrifugal acceleration.
3. **Volatility (vapour pressure):** Exploited in distillation, evaporation. The driving force is heat input.
4. **Solubility in a selective solvent:** Exploited in liquid-liquid extraction, leaching, supercritical extraction. The MSA is the solvent.
5. **Charge / electrical mobility:** Exploited in electrodialysis, electrophoresis. The driving force is an electric field.
6. **Affinity for a surface / ionic group:** Exploited in adsorption, ion exchange, chromatography. The MSA is the sorbent or resin.

**Step 3 — Supplementary property (2 marks).**
- **Magnetic susceptibility:** Exploited in magnetic separation (e.g., removing ferrous impurities from ores).
- **Thermal conductivity / diffusivity:** Exploited in thermodiffusion (rare, specialized).
- **Optical absorption:** Exploited in photophoresis (research-grade).

**Step 4 — Concluding statement.**
The choice of property is dictated by the *largest exploitable contrast* between the target species and the matrix, weighted against the energy and capital cost of exploiting that contrast.

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Schematic description (2 marks).**
A single-stage equilibrium separator consists of an inlet (feed), a mixing zone, a separation zone, and two outlets. The two phases are contacted until they reach thermodynamic equilibrium, then mechanically separated. The phase enriched in the target species is the **extract** (or **top product**), and the depleted phase is the **raffinate** (or **bottom product**).

**Step 2 — Mass balances (3 marks).**
For a binary mixture of A (solute) and B (carrier) with feed $F$ and products $P_1$, $P_2$:

$$
F = P_1 + P_2
$$

$$
F \cdot z_A = P_1 \cdot x_A + P_2 \cdot y_A
$$

where $z_A$, $x_A$, $y_A$ are the mass fractions of A in the feed, product 1, and product 2 respectively.

**Step 3 — Define recovery (1 mark).**

$$
R_A = \frac{P_1 \cdot x_A}{F \cdot z_A}
$$

**Step 4 — Define selectivity (1 mark).**

$$
\alpha = \frac{x_A / x_B}{y_A / y_B}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A high $\alpha$ (>> 1) means strong preference of the separator for A. Single-stage equilibrium processes are practical only when $\alpha > 10$–$50$; otherwise multi-stage cascades are required.

**Step 5 — Design implication (additional credit).**
For binary distillation with relative volatility $\alpha = 2.16$ (e.g., n-hexane/n-heptane), the minimum number of stages can be computed by the Fenske equation:

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{x_D}{1 - x_D}\right)\!\left(\frac{1 - x_W}{x_W}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha}
$$

A high-purity separation (e.g., $x_D = 0.99$, $x_W = 0.01$) requires approximately $N_{\min} = \frac{\ln(99 \times 99)}{\ln 2.16} = \frac{9.20}{0.770} \approx 12$ stages. This demonstrates why classification (here: volatility-based) drives both equipment choice and column sizing.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of a **mass separating agent (MSA)** in separation processes. Distinguish between a mass separating agent and an energy separating agent with two examples each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **membrane separation**, **adsorption**, and **ion exchange** under the following heads: property exploited, MSA used (if any), equilibrium or rate-governed, and one industrial application. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Definition of MSA (2 marks).**
A **mass separating agent (MSA)** is a substance added to the mixture to facilitate separation. It is immiscible (or selectively interactive) with one phase, and it is regenerated in a subsequent step. Common MSAs include: solvent in extraction, sorbent in adsorption, resin in ion exchange, and membrane in permeation.

**Step 2 — Definition of energy separating agent (2 marks).**
An **energy separating agent** is energy (heat, work, electric field, magnetic field) supplied to the separator. It is *not* a material stream. Examples: heat in distillation (reboiler duty), pressure in reverse osmosis, electric field in electrodialysis.

**Step 3 — Comparison table (3 marks).**

| Feature | Mass Separating Agent | Energy Separating Agent |
|---|---|---|
| Form of input | Material stream | Energy (Q, W, electric, magnetic) |
| Must be regenerated? | Yes (cost penalty) | No (consumed as Q or W) |
| Example 1 | Solvent in LLE | Heat in distillation |
| Example 2 | Sorbent in adsorption | Pressure in reverse osmosis |
| Process examples | Extraction, absorption, ion exchange | Distillation, evaporation, R.O. |
| Adds to mass balance? | Yes | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual point:** All separation processes must obey the **second law of thermodynamics**. The minimum work of separation for an isothermal, isobaric binary mixture is:

$$
W_{\min} = -RT \sum_i n_i \ln(y_i / x_i)
$$

Any MSA or energy agent must supply *at least* this minimum; real processes are far less efficient. *(Additional credit, $W_{\min}$ is rarely required but is high-yield when asked.)*

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Membrane separation (2 marks).**
- **Property exploited:** Size, diffusivity, or solubility-diffusivity (depending on the membrane type).
- **MSA used:** No material MSA — the membrane is a *physical barrier*, not consumed. Pressure is the energy input.
- **Equilibrium or rate:** **Rate-governed** (mass transfer through the membrane).
- **Application:** Desalination of seawater by **reverse osmosis**.

**Step 2 — Adsorption (2 marks).**
- **Property exploited:** Surface affinity (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding) of the adsorbate for the solid surface.
- **MSA used:** Yes — the **adsorbent** (e.g., activated carbon, zeolite, silica gel).
- **Equilibrium or rate:** Often rate-governed, but design uses the **Langmuir** or **Freundlich** isotherm to set the equilibrium loading.
- **Application:** Removal of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from industrial off-gases using activated carbon beds.

**Step 3 — Ion exchange (2 marks).**
- **Property exploited:** Electrostatic affinity (charge) of ions for oppositely charged functional groups on a resin.
- **MSA used:** Yes — the **ion-exchange resin** (cation or anion type).
- **Equilibrium or rate:** Rate-governed, governed by ion-diffusion in the resin pores.
- **Application:** Water softening — replacing $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ in hard water with $\text{Na}^+$ on a strong-acid cation resin.

**Step 4 — Synthesizing comparison (1 mark).**
All three are *rate-governed sorption/membrane* processes; the *property exploited* and the *form of the separating agent* are the only true discriminators.

---

### 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfalls for this topic:**
>
> 1. **Omitting the property exploited.** Writing *"distillation"* without writing *"volatility"* costs 1–2 marks. Examiners *always* want the property stated explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing MSA and energy separating agent.** A student once wrote *"steam is a mass separating agent in distillation."* Steam is an *energy* carrier (it provides latent heat). The MSA in extraction is a *solvent* — that is the correct analogy.
> 3. **Stating *all* classifications as equilibrium-based.** A common error is to label reverse osmosis or adsorption as equilibrium-stage. Both are *rate-governed* — the *equilibrium loading* is used in design, but the *limiting step* is mass-transfer kinetics.
> 4. **Forgetting the recovery equation.** Every 7-mark problem involving a single-stage separator should be backed by the mass balance, the recovery definition, and (if applicable) the selectivity / $\alpha$ definition.
> 5. **Mixing up extract and raffinate.** Extract = the *added* phase's product (enriched in solute). Raffinate = the *original* (feed) phase, now depleted. Conflating them costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A separation process converts a mixture into two or more products differing in composition.
- **Three classification axes:**
  1. *Property exploited* (size, density, volatility, solubility, charge, magnetic susceptibility, affinity).
  2. *Separating agent* (mass separating agent vs. energy separating agent).
  3. *Limiting step* (equilibrium-stage vs. rate-governed).
- **Equilibrium-stage** processes — Distillation, absorption, extraction, crystallization, evaporation. Designed using McCabe-Thiele / Kremser.
- **Rate-governed** processes — Membrane separations, adsorption, ion exchange, chromatography, electrodialysis, magnetic separation. Designed using mass-transfer coefficients.
- **Six core equations to memorize:** $R$, $\alpha$, $N_{\min}$ (Fenske), $J$ (Darcy), $\beta$ (membrane separation factor), $W_{\min}$ (thermodynamic minimum work).
- **Recoveries** are dimensionless, 0 to 1. **Selectivities** are dimensionless, > 1 (typically).
- **MSA** must be regenerated — this is a major operating cost in extraction and ion-exchange plants.
- **Energy separating agents** (heat, pressure, electricity, magnetic field) are *consumed* and not regenerated.
- **Decision heuristic for exam:** Identify the *largest exploitable property contrast* between the target and matrix — that is your process.
- **Stoke's law** governs sedimentation/centrifugation; **Henry's law** governs absorption; **Langmuir/Freundlich** govern adsorption; **Nernst-Planck** governs ion exchange; **Darcy's law** governs membrane flux.
- Always write the **mass balance** first, then the **component balance**, then the **recovery / selectivity definition** — in that exact order. Examiners reward methodological clarity.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mnemonic for the Six Exploitable Properties:** *"Silly Dogs Vaporize, Some Call Magnetic Affairs"* — **S**ize, **D**ensity, **V**apor pressure, **S**olubility, **C**harge, **M**agnetic, **A**ffinity. (Seventh letter works as a bonus for surface/affinity.)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing the Choice of Separation Process and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing the Choice of Separation Process and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Separation Process Selection** is the systematic engineering methodology used to identify, evaluate, and choose the most economically and technically feasible separation technique from a set of alternatives, based on the thermodynamic, kinetic, physical, and chemical properties of the feed mixture, the desired product specifications, and process constraints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with NEP 2020 and Outcome-Based Education), this topic is foundational to **Course Outcome CO1** of PECHT756, which emphasizes "understanding the principles governing the selection of advanced separation processes for chemical, petrochemical, and bioprocess industries."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The choice of a separation process is **NOT** based on a single dominant parameter. It is a **multi-objective optimization** problem where at least 8–12 independent factors must be simultaneously evaluated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **chefs planning a multi-course dinner for 500 guests** from a single mixed basket of raw ingredients (vegetables, spices, grains, dairy, meat).

- The **raw basket** is analogous to the **feed mixture**.
- The **final plated dishes** represent the **product specifications** (purity, recovery).
- The **cooking method** you choose (boiling, frying, baking, fermenting) is your **separation technique**.
- The **tools you use** (pressure cooker, oven, blender) are your **equipment**.

**Why would you not use only ONE method for everything?**
- Pressure cookers waste energy for salads.
- Ovens cannot mix cold beverages.
- Certain spices lose flavor when boiled.

Similarly, in chemical engineering, **no single separation process fits all scenarios**. The decision-maker must weigh efficiency, cost, safety, and product quality — exactly like a chef balancing flavor, time, and budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Separation is fundamentally the act of **overcoming the natural tendency of mixing (entropy)** and restoring order. The energy and capital you must spend to "undo" this mixing governs the choice of process.

## 1.3 The Separation Factor — The Unifying Metric

The **Separation Factor (α)** is the universal yardstick for quantifying "how well" a separation process can distinguish between two components (A and B). For two key components, it is mathematically defined as:

$$\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{product 1}}}{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{product 2}}}$$

where:
- $x_A$ and $x_B$ are the mole (or mass) fractions of components A and B.
- A higher value of $\alpha_{A,B}$ indicates an **easier** separation.
- $\alpha = 1$ means **NO separation is possible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always quote the **relative volatility (α)** for distillation, **selectivity (S)** for membranes and absorption, and **distribution coefficient (K)** for liquid-liquid extraction — these are special cases of the generic **separation factor**.

## 1.4 Classification of Factors (Primary Framework)

For systematic analysis, the factors influencing separation process selection are grouped into **seven primary categories** as per King’s separation process hierarchy (a standard reference framework):

| # | Factor Category | Engineering Domain |
|---|------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Feed Characteristics** | Thermodynamics, Physical Chemistry |
| 2 | **Product Specifications** | Process Economics, Quality Control |
| 3 | **Operating Conditions** | Process Intensification |
| 4 | **Capital & Operating Costs** | Chemical Engineering Economics |
| 5 | **Safety & Environmental** | HSE, Green Engineering |
| 6 | **Physical/Chemical Properties** | Transport Phenomena |
| 7 | **Throughput & Scale** | Process Design |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Separation Process Selection

### Pillar 1 — Feed Characteristics

These are properties you **cannot control** but must adapt to.

- **Concentration of the key (target) component:** Dictates the minimum number of stages.
  - *Dilute solutions (< 5%)* → Adsorption, Ion exchange, Membrane separation.
  - *Concentrated mixtures (> 50%)* → Distillation, Crystallization.
- **Phase of the feed:** Solid, liquid, gas, or multi-phase.
- **Presence of azeotropes** (non-ideal mixtures) → Precludes simple distillation; requires extractive or azeotropic distillation.
- **Thermal stability of components:** Heat-sensitive (antibiotics, vitamins) → Avoid high-temperature distillation; use membrane or chromatographic separation.
- **Corrosive nature** → Restricts material of construction.

### Pillar 2 — Product Specifications

The "**what do I need at the outlet?**" factor.

- **Purity** (e.g., 99.9% pharmaceutical-grade vs. 95% industrial-grade).
- **Recovery** (e.g., must recover ≥ 98% of the gold from ore).
- **Product form** (liquid concentrate, dry crystals, compressed gas).
- **End-use** of the product (food, pharma, petrochemical, semiconductor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Trade-off:** Doubling purity from 99% to 99.9% often **multiplies equipment cost by 5–10×** due to additional stages and reflux requirements.

### Pillar 3 — Operating Conditions

- **Temperature** (cryogenic for air separation, ambient for water treatment).
- **Pressure** (vacuum distillation for heat-sensitive materials, high pressure for supercritical extraction).
- **Presence/absence of a separating agent** (e.g., solvent in extraction, absorbent in absorption).

### Pillar 4 — Capital and Operating Costs

Capital cost ($C_{cap}$) includes equipment, installation, and instrumentation.
Operating cost ($C_{op}$) includes energy, labor, utilities, and maintenance.

The **Total Annualized Cost ($TAC$)** governs the economic choice:

$$TAC = C_{cap} \cdot CRF + C_{op}$$

where $CRF$ = Capital Recovery Factor.

### Pillar 5 — Safety and Environmental Constraints

- Toxicity of solvents (benzene, chloroform).
- Flammability limits.
- Waste disposal regulations (e.g., zero liquid discharge mandates).
- Carbon footprint (kg CO₂ equivalent per kg product).

### Pillar 6 — Physical and Chemical Property Differentials

This is the **thermodynamic driver** of the separation. The most important property differences include:

- **Relative Volatility (α)** → Distillation.
- **Solubility Difference** → Extraction, Crystallization.
- **Adsorption Affinity** → Adsorption, Chromatography.
- **Molecular Size / Shape** → Membrane separation (Nanofiltration, Ultrafiltration, Reverse Osmosis).
- **Magnetic Susceptibility** → Magnetic separation.
- **Density Difference** → Centrifugation, Sedimentation.
- **Particle Size** → Filtration, Sieving.
- **Charge / Ionization** → Electrodialysis, Ion Exchange.
- **Reactivity** → Reactive Distillation, Reactive Extraction.

### Pillar 7 — Throughput, Scale, and Continuous vs. Batch

- **Lab/pilot scale** (< 10 kg/h) → Batch chromatography, membrane modules.
- **Industrial scale** (> 1000 kg/h) → Continuous distillation columns, large extractors.

## 2.2 The King Hierarchy of Property Differences (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **C. Judson King (1980)** proposed a logical hierarchy of property differences for selecting separation processes. This is a **favourite KTU question** in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) sections.

The hierarchy, from **most common/easy to least common/difficult**:

| Rank | Property Exploited | Typical Process |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Volatility | Distillation |
| 2 | Solubility | Extraction, Leaching, Crystallization |
| 3 | Adsorption affinity | Adsorption, Chromatography |
| 4 | Molecular size/shape | Membrane separations, Filtration |
| 5 | Charge | Electrodialysis, Ion exchange |
| 6 | Density | Centrifugation |
| 7 | Magnetic susceptibility | Magnetic separation |
| 8 | Thermal diffusion | Thermal diffusion |
| 9 | Pressure diffusion | Pressure diffusion |
| 10 | Mass diffusivity | Mass diffusion |

**King’s Selection Rule:** Always choose the process corresponding to the **highest-ranked property difference** that is practically applicable. For example, if both volatility difference and solubility difference exist, **choose distillation first** (higher rank) unless azeotrope or thermal sensitivity forces you to extraction.

## 2.3 Decision Tree for Separation Process Selection

```
                     START
                       |
       Does feed contain >1 phase?
                /             \
             YES               NO (single phase)
              |                 |
        Use mechanical       Are components
        separation first    thermally stable?
        (filtration,         /         \
        centrifugation)    YES          NO
                            |            |
                     Is there a clear    Use membrane,
                     volatility diff?    adsorption,
                        /       \        chromatography
                      YES        NO
                       |          |
                  DISTILLATION   Use extraction,
                                 crystallization,
                                 adsorption (based
                                 on solubility /
                                 affinity)
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{x_{A,1}/x_{B,1}}{x_{A,2}/x_{B,2}}$ | Separation Factor | All separations |
| 2 | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Fenske Equation (min. stages) | Distillation |
| 3 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \dfrac{x_D}{x_F} - \dfrac{\alpha (1-x_D)}{1-x_F} \right]$ | Underwood Equation (min. reflux) | Distillation |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{C_{A,\text{permeate}}}{C_{A,\text{feed}}}$ | Selectivity (Membrane) | Membrane separation |
| 5 | $J = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta t}$ | Permeate Flux (m³/m²·h) | Membrane separation |
| 6 | $K_D = \dfrac{C_{A,\text{extract}}}{C_{A,\text{raffinate}}}$ | Distribution Coefficient | LLE, Extraction |
| 7 | $TAC = C_{cap} \cdot CRF + C_{op}$ | Total Annualized Cost | Economics |
| 8 | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor | Economics |
| 9 | $\beta = \dfrac{y_i / x_i}{y_j / x_j}$ | Relative Selectivity | Adsorption, Extraction |
| 10 | $E = 1 - \dfrac{C_{A,\text{out}}}{C_{A,\text{in}}}$ | Removal / Separation Efficiency | All processes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** KTU examiners **often give numerical data** (compositions, relative volatility, and recovery targets) and ask: *"Choose a suitable separation process and justify your answer."* The expected answer structure is:
> 1. Calculate the separation factor α.
> 2. Identify the dominant property difference.
> 3. Match to King’s hierarchy.
> 4. Recommend a process.
> 5. State one limitation or alternative.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The methodology taught in this topic is applied across:

- **Petroleum Refining** — Crude oil distillation tower selection (atmospheric vs. vacuum).
- **Pharmaceuticals** — Choosing crystallization vs. chromatography for API purification.
- **Water Treatment** — RO vs. multi-effect evaporation for desalination.
- **Food Industry** — Cold-press extraction vs. solvent extraction for edible oils.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing** — Ultra-pure water (UPW) via ion exchange + RO + EDI.
- **Green Energy** — CO₂ capture using amine absorption vs. membrane separation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Process Selection

The selection of a separation process can be systematically reduced to a **multi-criteria decision matrix**. Below is the exhaustive analytical framework used in advanced process design (also referred to as the **Douglas hierarchy** in conceptual design).

### Step 1 — Characterize the Feed

**Step 1.1:** Identify the **number of components** in the feed ($N_c$).
- If $N_c = 1$: No separation required → return.
- If $N_c = 2$: A **binary** separation problem (simpler).
- If $N_c \geq 3$: A **multi-component** problem requiring sequencing decisions (e.g., direct vs. indirect distillation sequence).

**Step 1.2:** Identify the **state of aggregation** (solid, liquid, gas, multi-phase).
- Multi-phase feed → first use **mechanical separation** (filtration, decantation, centrifugation).
- Single phase → proceed to step 2.

**Step 1.3:** Determine the **concentration regime** of the target species.

Let $z_F$ = mole fraction of target component in feed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } z_F &\geq 0.5 \quad \rightarrow \text{Concentrated regime.} \\
\text{If } 0.05 &\leq z_F < 0.5 \quad \rightarrow \text{Intermediate regime.} \\
\text{If } z_F &< 0.05 \quad \rightarrow \text{Dilute regime (trace removal).}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Separation Factor

**Step 2.1:** Calculate the **thermodynamic separation factor** $\alpha_{A,B}$ using the most relevant property.

For a distillation case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{A,B} &= \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_i$ is the K-value (vapor-liquid equilibrium ratio) and $P_i^{sat}$ is the vapor pressure.

**Step 2.2:** Interpret the value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{A,B} &\geq 1.10 \quad \rightarrow \text{Easy distillation (typical).} \\
1.05 \leq \alpha_{A,B} &< 1.10 \quad \rightarrow \text{Marginal — large column, high reflux.} \\
\alpha_{A,B} &\approx 1.00 \quad \rightarrow \text{No distillation possible (azeotrope).} \\
\alpha_{A,B} &< 1.00 \quad \rightarrow \text{Choose extraction/adsorption/membrane.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the King Hierarchy

Match the dominant property difference to the highest-ranked applicable process from the King hierarchy table in Section 2.2.

### Step 4 — Screen Feasibility Constraints

For each shortlisted process, check:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Thermal stability:} \quad T_{process} &\leq T_{decomposition} \\
\text{(ii) Chemical compatibility:} \quad \text{Solvent/adsorbent must be inert.} \\
\text{(iii) Mass transfer feasibility:} \quad \frac{D \cdot a}{\delta} &\geq \text{Minimum acceptable value.}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $D$ = diffusivity, $a$ = interfacial area, $\delta$ = diffusion path length.

### Step 5 — Economic Optimization

Calculate the **Total Annualized Cost (TAC)** for each candidate process:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC &= C_{cap} \cdot CRF + C_{op} \\
CRF &= \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.1:** The process with the **minimum TAC** that satisfies all product specifications and constraints is the **recommended choice**.

**Step 5.2:** Perform a **sensitivity analysis** to verify the recommendation is robust against ±20% variation in feed composition and ±10% in energy cost.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — KTU 2024 Style

> **Problem:** A binary mixture of **ethanol (A)** and **water (B)** contains $z_F = 0.40$ mole fraction ethanol. The product specifications require $x_D = 0.89$ (distillate) and $x_B = 0.01$ (bottom). The relative volatility is $\alpha_{AB} = 2.43$. Decide whether distillation is feasible and calculate the minimum number of stages.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Verify distillation is thermodynamically possible.**

Since $\alpha_{AB} = 2.43 \gg 1$, distillation is the obvious choice per King’s hierarchy (rank 1 — volatility).

**Step 2: Calculate the minimum number of stages using the Fenske Equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\min} &= \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right) \left( \frac{1 - x_B}{x_B} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.1:** Substitute the distillate specification $x_D = 0.89$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} &= \frac{0.89}{1 - 0.89} = \frac{0.89}{0.11} = 8.091
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.2:** Substitute the bottom specification $x_B = 0.01$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1 - x_B}{x_B} &= \frac{1 - 0.01}{0.01} = \frac{0.99}{0.01} = 99.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.3:** Multiply the two ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( \frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right) \left( \frac{1 - x_B}{x_B} \right) &= (8.091)(99.00) = 800.99
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.4:** Take natural logarithm of numerator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(800.99) &= 6.685
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.5:** Take natural logarithm of the relative volatility:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln \alpha_{avg} &= \ln(2.43) = 0.888
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.6:** Compute $N_{\min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\min} &= \frac{6.685}{0.888} = 7.53 \approx 8 \text{ stages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Decision.**

Since $N_{\min} = 8$ stages is feasible (typical industrial columns have 20–80 stages), and the $\alpha = 2.43$ is well above the azeotropic limit, **distillation is the recommended process**.

**Step 4: Equipment Selection.**

For a binary separation with $N_{\min} = 8$, a **packed column with structured packing (e.g., Sulzer Mellapak)** or a **sieve-tray column with ~25–30 actual stages** (assuming Murphree efficiency $E_M \approx 0.6$ at total reflux) is recommended. Material of construction: **SS-304** (sufficient for ethanol-water at low pressure).

## 3.3 Decision Matrix Template (for KTU Board Answers)

When a KTU question asks: *"Suggest a suitable separation process for the following mixture..."*, use this template:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Solution Framework} &= \begin{cases}
\text{Step 1: Feed characterization} \\
\text{Step 2: } \alpha \text{ calculation} \\
\text{Step 3: King hierarchy match} \\
\text{Step 4: Constraint screening} \\
\text{Step 5: Economic comparison (TAC)} \\
\text{Step 6: Final recommendation + equipment}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Flow Diagram — King’s Property Difference Hierarchy

This Mermaid diagram visually represents King’s hierarchy of property differences and the corresponding separation processes. Use this diagram in your KTU exam answer scripts to fetch **extra presentation marks**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Feed Mixture Analysis] --> B1{Phase of feed?}
    B1 -- Multi phase --> M1[Mechanical Separation]
    M1 --> M2[Filtration]
    M1 --> M3[Centrifugation]
    M1 --> M4[Decantation]
    B1 -- Single phase --> C1{Thermally stable?}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1{Volatility diff large?}
    D1 -- Yes alpha greater than 1.1 --> P1[DISTILLATION]
    D1 -- No --> D2{Solubility diff exists?}
    D2 -- Yes --> P2[EXTRACTION]
    D2 -- No --> D3{Adsorption affinity diff?}
    D3 -- Yes --> P3[ADSORPTION]
    D3 -- No --> D4{Molecular size diff?}
    D4 -- Yes --> P4[MEMBRANE SEPARATION]
    D4 -- No --> D5{Charge diff?}
    D5 -- Yes --> P5[ELECTRODIALYSIS]
    C1 -- No --> P4
    P1 --> E1[Process selected]
    P2 --> E1
    P3 --> E1
    P4 --> E1
    P5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Equipment design and TAC analysis]
    F1 --> G1[Final selection]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Separation Process Selection Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Feed Properties] --> I2[Feed Properties Analysis]
    I3[Product Specs] --> I2
    I4[Constraints] --> I2
    I2 --> I5[Calculate Separation Factor alpha]
    I5 --> I6[Apply King Hierarchy]
    I6 --> I7[Generate Candidate Processes]
    I7 --> I8[Safety and Environmental Screening]
    I8 --> I9[Cost and TAC Analysis]
    I9 --> I10[Recommended Process and Equipment]
```

```mermaid

    end

```

> **Exam Tip:** Drawing the King hierarchy as a **ranked ladder** in the answer sheet (1 → 10) along with the corresponding process and ONE example is worth **3–4 extra marks** under the KTU 2024 valuation scheme.
