<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define separation factor. What does a separation factor of 1.0 indicate about the feasibility of a separation process?**

**Model Answer:**

The **separation factor** ($\alpha_{A,B}$) is a quantitative measure of the ease of separating two components A and B, defined as the ratio of the ratio of their mole fractions in the two product phases:

$$
\alpha_{A,B} = \frac{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{product 1}}}{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{product 2}}}
$$

A value of $\alpha_{A,B} = 1.0$ indicates that **the compositions of products 1 and 2 are identical with respect to A and B**. This means the two phases have achieved **thermodynamic equilibrium** with respect to A and B, and **no further separation is possible** under the given conditions.

For practical purposes, separation is considered feasible only when $\alpha_{A,B} \geq 1.05$. Special techniques (azeotropic distillation, extractive distillation, pressure-swing distillation) are required when $\alpha \to 1$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any six factors that influence the selection of a separation process. Briefly explain why feed concentration is a critical factor.**

**Model Answer:**

The six factors influencing separation process selection are:

1. **Feed composition and concentration** of the target component.
2. **Physical and chemical properties** of the components (volatility, solubility, molecular size, etc.).
3. **Product specifications** (purity, recovery, end-use form).
4. **Operating conditions** (temperature, pressure, continuous vs. batch).
5. **Capital and operating costs** (TAC, payback period).
6. **Safety, environmental, and regulatory constraints.**

**Why feed concentration is critical:**

The feed concentration determines the **minimum number of stages** required and influences the **type of contacting device** used. For example:

- **Dilute solutions** ($< 5\%$) are most economically treated by **adsorption, ion exchange, or membrane separation**, since the recovery (not the concentration) is the bottleneck.
- **Concentrated mixtures** ($> 50\%$) are most efficiently handled by **distillation or crystallization**, where the product purity dominates the design.
- **Intermediate concentrations** often require **extractive or azeotropic methods** when a simple distillation is infeasible due to close boiling points.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner’s Pitfall:** Many students **forget to mention thermal stability and corrosiveness** of the feed. A feed containing chlorides (e.g., HCl) demands SS-316 or glass-lined equipment, which **doubles the capital cost** and may shift the optimal process from distillation to membrane separation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> **KTU Rule:** *“Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each full question carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.”*

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain King’s hierarchy of property differences for the selection of separation processes. Why is distillation the most frequently chosen industrial separation process?

**Model Answer:**

King’s hierarchy of property differences ranks the physical and chemical property differences that can be exploited to achieve separation, from the most common and easily utilized to the least common. The hierarchy, in order of decreasing preference, is:

**Step 1 — Property difference identification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank 1} &: \text{Volatility (Distillation)} \\
\text{Rank 2} &: \text{Solubility (Extraction, Crystallization, Leaching)} \\
\text{Rank 3} &: \text{Adsorption Affinity (Adsorption, Chromatography)} \\
\text{Rank 4} &: \text{Molecular Size and Shape (Membrane Separation)} \\
\text{Rank 5} &: \text{Charge (Electrodialysis, Ion Exchange)} \\
\text{Rank 6} &: \text{Density (Centrifugation, Sedimentation)} \\
\text{Rank 7} &: \text{Magnetic Susceptibility (Magnetic Separation)} \\
\text{Rank 8-10} &: \text{Thermal, Pressure, and Mass Diffusion (Rare industrial use)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Selection logic:**

Always select the process corresponding to the **highest-ranked property difference** that is feasible for the given mixture. If the highest-ranked property difference is not exploitable (e.g., azeotrope in distillation), proceed to the next rank.

**Why distillation is the most frequently chosen industrial process:**

1. **Highest rank in King’s hierarchy** — most mixtures exhibit volatility differences.
2. **No mass separating agent required** — unlike extraction (needs solvent) or adsorption (needs sorbent), distillation uses only heat.
3. **Continuous operation** is straightforward and easily scaled up.
4. **Well-developed design procedures** (McCabe–Thiele, Fenske, Underwood, Gilliland) are well-established.
5. **Mature equipment** (tray columns, packed columns) is available in a wide range of materials.
6. **Energy integration** through heat integration networks is well-optimized.
7. **No secondary waste streams** are generated (unlike solvent extraction).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A binary mixture of benzene and toluene containing 60 mol% benzene is to be separated to obtain 95 mol% benzene in the distillate and 5 mol% in the bottom. The relative volatility is $\alpha_{BT} = 2.47$. Calculate the minimum number of stages required and recommend the equipment.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $z_F = 0.60$ (feed mole fraction of benzene).
- $x_D = 0.95$ (distillate purity).
- $x_B = 0.05$ (bottom purity).
- $\alpha_{BT} = 2.47$.

**Step 1: Confirm distillation feasibility.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{BT} = 2.47 \gg 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Distillation is feasible.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Fenske Equation for minimum stages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\min} &= \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right) \left( \frac{1 - x_B}{x_B} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{BT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.1:** Calculate the distillate ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} &= \frac{0.95}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.2:** Calculate the bottom ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1 - x_B}{x_B} &= \frac{1 - 0.05}{0.05} = \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.3:** Multiply the ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( \frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right) \left( \frac{1 - x_B}{x_B} \right) &= (19.00)(19.00) = 361.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.4:** Compute natural logs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(361.0) &= 5.889 \\
\ln(2.47) &= 0.904
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.5:** Calculate $N_{\min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\min} &= \frac{5.889}{0.904} = 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Equipment recommendation.**

- **Contacting type:** Sieve-tray column (cost-effective for this scale and pressure).
- **Actual number of stages:** $N_{actual} \approx 1.5 \times N_{\min} = 10$ stages (accounting for Murphree efficiency).
- **Column internals:** SS-304 trays with 0.5 m spacing.
- **Operating pressure:** Atmospheric (since benzene-toluene are thermally stable below 200 °C).
- **Reflux ratio:** Optimum reflux $\approx 1.2 \times R_{\min}$ to minimize TAC.
- **Reboiler and condenser:** Forced circulation reboiler + total condenser.

**Final Answer:** Minimum 7 theoretical stages, **sieve-tray distillation column with 10 actual stages** is recommended.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various factors that influence the choice of a separation process in the chemical industry. Classify them under feed-related, product-related, and process-related categories.

**Model Answer:**

The factors influencing the choice of a separation process can be broadly classified into three categories:

**(A) Feed-Related Factors:**

1. **Composition and concentration** of the target component.
2. **Phase of the feed** (solid, liquid, gas, multi-phase).
3. **Presence of impurities** and azeotropes.
4. **Physical properties** of components (boiling point, melting point, solubility, density).
5. **Chemical properties** (reactivity, corrosiveness, thermal stability).
6. **Feed flow rate** (kg/h) and variability.

**(B) Product-Related Factors:**

1. **Purity specification** (% purity of desired product).
2. **Recovery specification** (% of target component recovered).
3. **Product form** (liquid, solid crystals, dry powder, compressed gas).
4. **End-use** (food, pharma, semiconductor, fuel).
5. **Maximum permissible impurity levels** (e.g., heavy metals < 1 ppm).

**(C) Process-Related Factors:**

1. **Operating temperature and pressure** (e.g., vacuum for heat-sensitive materials).
2. **Capital cost** of equipment and installation.
3. **Operating cost** (energy, labor, utilities, maintenance).
4. **Safety** (toxicity, flammability, explosion limits).
5. **Environmental impact** (VOC emissions, wastewater, solid waste).
6. **Need for a separating agent** (solvent, adsorbent, membrane).
7. **Energy efficiency and integration potential**.
8. **Continuous vs. batch** operation.
9. **Ease of control and automation**.

**Selection Rule:** The optimal process must simultaneously satisfy:
- Maximum purity and recovery targets.
- Minimum total annualized cost.
- Compliance with safety and environmental regulations.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dilute aqueous solution containing 2 wt% acetic acid is to be purified to obtain 99.5 wt% acetic acid. Suggest a suitable separation scheme and justify your answer with reference to King’s hierarchy.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Feed: 2 wt% acetic acid in water.
- Product: 99.5 wt% acetic acid.
- This is a very dilute source → recovery is a major issue.

**Step 1: Apply King’s hierarchy.**

| Property Difference | Rank | Applicable? |
|---------------------|------|-------------|
| Volatility (α of acetic acid/water ≈ 1.13 at 100 °C) | 1 | Marginally applicable — many stages needed |
| Solubility | 2 | Yes — acetic acid is highly soluble in solvents like ethyl acetate |
| Adsorption | 3 | Possible for trace removal |
| Molecular size | 4 | Not for organic acid |

**Step 2: Pre-concentration step using liquid-liquid extraction.**

Since the feed is very dilute, **direct distillation is highly energy-intensive** (need to vaporize 98% water). The standard industrial route is:

**Step 2.1:** **Liquid-liquid extraction** with ethyl acetate or isopropyl ether as solvent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distribution coefficient, } K_D = \frac{C_{\text{extract}}}{C_{\text{raffinate}}} \approx 0.5 - 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

This step **concentrates acetic acid from 2 wt% to ~30–40 wt%** in the extract phase.

**Step 2.2:** **Distillation of the extract** to recover the solvent (overhead) and obtain crude acetic acid (~85–95 wt%).

**Step 2.3:** **Final purification via azeotropic distillation** with water (or extractive distillation with a higher-boiling entrainer) to break the water-acetic acid azeotrope and reach 99.5 wt%.

**Step 3: Justification based on King’s hierarchy.**

- **Rank 1 (Volatility)** alone is **inefficient** at 2 wt% feed — confirmed by Fenske equation yielding $N_{\min} > 25$ stages.
- **Rank 2 (Solubility)** is **highly effective** for pre-concentration.
- **Combined extraction + distillation** reduces **overall energy consumption by ~60%** compared to direct distillation.

**Final Recommended Scheme:**

```
Dilute AcOH (2 wt%)
        |
   [LLE Extraction]  →  Raffinate (water + trace AcOH)
        |                  ↑
   Extract (30-40 wt% AcOH)
        |
   [Solvent Recovery Distillation]
        |
   Crude AcOH (95 wt%)
        |
   [Azeotropic Distillation]
        |
   Pure AcOH (99.5 wt%)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do NOT** recommend direct distillation for dilute aqueous acetic acid. This is the most common mistake. The energy cost is prohibitive (~$200/ton product vs. $40/ton for extraction+distillation).
> 2. **Do NOT** recommend simple membrane separation for organic acid concentration. NF/RO membranes are fouled by organic acids and have low rejection.
> 3. **Always state** that the final purification requires **azeotropic or extractive distillation** because the boiling point of acetic acid (118 °C) is close to water (100 °C) and they form a near-azeotrope.
> 4. In numerical problems, **always show the natural log calculation step-by-step**. Skipping the intermediate value of $x_D/(1-x_D)$ will cost **2–3 marks** under the strict KTU 2024 valuation scheme.
> 5. **Do NOT confuse** separation factor (α) with selectivity (S) or relative volatility. They look similar but are defined differently. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for misuse of terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Use this section as your final-night revision sheet before the KTU Board Exam.**

### **Conceptual Pillars**

- **Separation Factor (α):** Universal metric; $\alpha = 1$ means no separation; $\alpha > 1$ means feasible.
- **King’s Hierarchy:** Volatility > Solubility > Adsorption > Molecular Size > Charge > Density > Magnetic.
- **Total Annualized Cost (TAC):** The economic decision metric; combines CAPEX and OPEX.

### **High-Yield Formulas to Memorize**

- Fenske equation: $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \right) \left( \dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha}$
- Underwood equation: $R_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\dfrac{x_D}{x_F} - \dfrac{\alpha(1-x_D)}{1-x_F}\right]$
- TAC: $TAC = C_{cap} \cdot CRF + C_{op}$
- CRF: $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$

### **Decision Rules**

1. **Multi-phase feed** → mechanical separation first.
2. **Heat-sensitive feed** → membrane, adsorption, or extraction.
3. **Azeotrope present** → extractive/azeotropic/pressure-swing distillation.
4. **Dilute feed (< 5%)** → adsorption, ion exchange, or extraction for pre-concentration.
5. **High recovery + high purity** → multi-stage counter-current operation.
6. **Corrosive feed** → exotic alloys, glass-lined, or membrane-based process.

### **Common Exam Traps**

- Confusing selectivity (S) with separation factor (α).
- Forgetting to apply King’s hierarchy before recommending a process.
- Recommending distillation for azeotropic mixtures.
- Skipping the constraint-screening step (T, P, corrosion) before economic analysis.

### **Last-Minute Mnemonic**

> **"V-S-A-M-C-D-M"** — **V**olatility, **S**olubility, **A**dsorption, **M**olecular size, **C**harge, **D**ensity, **M**agnetic (King’s hierarchy in order).

> **"MEPS-PACE"** — **M**ulti-phase → **E**nergy → **P**urity → **S**afety → **P**roperty → **A**gent → **C**ost → **E**nvironment (Decision factors checklist).

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — PECHT756 | Novel Separation Process | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Processes: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Membrane Processes: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Membrane and Membrane Separation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Membrane (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A membrane is a **selective barrier** that permits the passage of certain components of a mixture while rejecting others, based on differences in physical and/or chemical properties such as size, charge, solubility, diffusivity, or affinity. The separation is achieved without a phase change and is governed by relative transport rates through the membrane matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Membrane Separation Process (MSP):** A unit operation in which a feed mixture is contacted with a semi-permeable membrane, producing two streams — a **permeate** (the fraction that passes through the membrane, enriched in the preferentially transported species) and a **retentate** (the fraction retained, enriched in the rejected species).

Mathematically, a membrane can be expressed as:

$$J_i = -P_i \cdot \frac{d\mu_i}{dx}$$

Where:
- $J_i$ = steady-state flux of component $i$ (mol·m$^{-2}$·s$^{-1}$)
- $P_i$ = permeability coefficient
- $\dfrac{d\mu_i}{dx}$ = chemical potential gradient across the membrane

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Kitchen Sieve & Coffee Filter

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a membrane like a **window screen**. The screen physically blocks leaves and insects (large particles) but allows air to pass freely (small molecules). In a kitchen, a coffee filter holds back coffee grounds (solid particles) while letting the brewed liquid (solvent) drip through. **The membrane does exactly this at a molecular level** — it acts as a molecular-scale sieve that discriminates between components based on size, shape, charge, or chemical affinity.

A simpler way to imagine it:

- **Feed** = a glass of muddy water (water + dirt particles)
- **Membrane** = a very fine cloth
- **Permeate** = clean water dripping through
- **Retentate** = concentrated mud left behind

## 1.3 Why Membrane Processes are "Novel"

Conventional separation techniques (distillation, extraction, absorption) rely on **phase equilibrium** and often require **energy-intensive phase changes** (e.g., vaporization). Membrane processes are novel because they:

1. Operate **without phase change** → lower energy consumption.
2. Function at **ambient temperature** → ideal for thermolabile compounds.
3. Are **modular and compact** → easy to scale up.
4. Combine **separation with reaction** (membrane reactors).
5. Achieve **high selectivity** for components with similar volatilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The novel separation processes module emphasizes processes that either consume less energy than conventional equivalents or enable separations that were previously impossible.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Membrane Processes

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permeate Flux | $J$ | L·m$^{-2}$·h$^{-1}$ (LMH) | Volume of permeate per unit area per unit time |
| Transmembrane Pressure | $\Delta P$ | Pa or bar | Pressure difference across the membrane |
| Rejection Coefficient | $R$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of solute rejected by membrane |
| Selectivity / Separation Factor | $\alpha$ | dimensionless | Ratio of permeabilities of two species |
| Permeability | $P$ | mol·m·m$^{-2}$·s$^{-1}$·Pa$^{-1}$ | Intrinsic transport property |

The **rejection coefficient** is formally defined as:

$$R_i = 1 - \frac{C_{i,p}}{C_{i,f}}$$

Where $C_{i,p}$ and $C_{i,f}$ are the permeate and feed concentrations of species $i$, respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Membrane

A practical membrane is not a single homogeneous film — it is a structured composite. Understanding this structure is crucial for KTU problems on flux and selectivity trade-offs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymmetric Membrane Structure:** Most industrial membranes are asymmetric, consisting of:
> - A **thin dense top layer (skin)** — 0.1–1 µm thick — that controls selectivity.
> - A **thick porous support layer** — 50–200 µm thick — that provides mechanical strength.
> - Sometimes a **non-woven fabric backing** for further reinforcement.

## 2.2 Classification of Membranes

Membranes can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### A. By Structure (Morphology)
- **Porous membranes:** Have discrete pores; separation by size exclusion (microfiltration, ultrafiltration).
- **Dense (non-porous) membranes:** Separation by solution-diffusion (reverse osmosis, gas separation, pervaporation).
- **Composite membranes:** A thin selective layer coated on a porous support (most commercial RO membranes).

### B. By Material
- **Polymeric:** Cellulose acetate, polysulfone, polyamide, PTFE — cheap, easy to fabricate, but limited thermal/chemical resistance.
- **Ceramic:** Alumina, zirconia, titania — high temperature/chemical stability, expensive.
- **Metallic:** Palladium, silver — used for hydrogen separation.
- **Liquid membranes:** Emulsion or supported liquid films — for facilitated transport.
- **Mixed-matrix membranes (MMM):** Inorganic fillers in polymer matrix — combines selectivity with processability.

### C. By Geometry / Configuration
- **Flat sheet / Plate-and-Frame**
- **Spiral-wound** (most common in RO/NF)
- **Hollow fiber** (highest surface area per volume)
- **Tubular** (used for fouling-prone feeds)
- **Capillary**

## 2.3 Classification of Membrane Processes (Driven by Applied Force)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The driving force is the heart of every membrane process. Examiners frequently ask students to identify the driving force for a given membrane process.

| Membrane Process | Driving Force | Particle/Molecule Size Retained | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| Microfiltration (MF) | $\Delta P$ (0.1–2 bar) | 0.1–10 µm | Sterile filtration, cell harvesting |
| Ultrafiltration (UF) | $\Delta P$ (1–10 bar) | 1–100 nm (1 kDa – 500 kDa) | Protein concentration, dye removal |
| Nanofiltration (NF) | $\Delta P$ (10–40 bar) | > 200 Da, multivalent ions | Water softening, lactose separation |
| Reverse Osmosis (RO) | $\Delta P$ (15–80 bar) | All dissolved salts | Seawater desalination, ultrapure water |
| Pervaporation (PV) | Partial vapor pressure | Liquid mixtures by solubility | Dehydration of ethanol, VOC removal |
| Gas Separation (GS) | $\Delta P$ (partial pressure) | Gas molecules by size/solubility | O$_2$/N$_2$, CO$_2$/CH$_4$, H$_2$ recovery |
| Electrodialysis (ED) | Electrical potential | Ions via ion-exchange membranes | Salt production, demineralization |
| Dialysis | Concentration gradient | Small solutes from colloids | Artificial kidney (hemodialysis) |

## 2.4 Historical Development

> [!NOTE]
> **Membrane Process Timeline (KTU Context):**
> - **1748:** Abbé Nollet — first observation of osmosis (semipermeable membrane).
> - **1855:** Fick — diffusion laws formulated.
> - **1861–1866:** Graham — studied gas permeation through rubber membranes.
> - **1907:** Bechhold — first synthetic nitrocellulose membrane (MF).
> - **1950s:** W.J. Kolff — first practical artificial kidney (hemodialysis).
> - **1959–1960:** Loeb–Sourirajan — asymmetric cellulose acetate membrane (RO breakthrough).
> - **1970s:** Reverse osmosis plants for water desalination.
> - **1980s–1990s:** Industrial-scale gas separation, pervaporation, nanofiltration.
> - **2000s–Present:** Mixed-matrix membranes, membrane bioreactors, forward osmosis.

## 2.5 Ideal Membrane Properties

For any membrane to be commercially viable, it should exhibit:

1. **High permeability** (high flux at low driving force) → reduces membrane area required.
2. **High selectivity** → high product purity in single pass.
3. **Mechanical stability** → withstands operating pressure.
4. **Chemical resistance** → tolerance to feed pH, solvents, oxidants.
5. **Thermal stability** → operates at required temperature.
6. **Fouling resistance** → long-term operation without flux decline.
7. **Manufacturability** → reproducible and affordable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Permeability-Selectivity Trade-off (Robeson Upper Bound):** A fundamental relationship in membrane science states that highly permeable membranes tend to be less selective, and vice versa. Robeson (1991) quantified this trade-off for gas pairs. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU examinations.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions asked in KTU Module 1.**

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permeate Flux | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m \cdot t}$ | L·m$^{-2}$·h$^{-1}$ (LMH) | $Q_p$ = permeate volume, $A_m$ = membrane area |
| Transmembrane Pressure | $\Delta P = P_f - P_p$ | Pa or bar | $P_f$ feed-side, $P_p$ permeate-side |
| Net Driving Pressure (RO) | $\Delta P_{net} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi$ | Pa | $\Delta \pi$ osmotic pressure difference |
| Rejection Coefficient | $R_i = 1 - \dfrac{C_{i,p}}{C_{i,f}}$ | dimensionless | $R=1$ complete rejection, $R=0$ no rejection |
| Observed/Apparent Rejection | $R_{obs} = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_f}$ | dimensionless | For mixed solutes |
| Intrinsic Rejection | $R_{int} = \dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_m}$ | dimensionless | $C_m$ is membrane surface concentration |
| Selectivity (binary) | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{y_i/y_j}{x_i/x_j}$ | dimensionless | Permeate over feed mole ratios |
| Solution-Diffusion Flux | $J_i = \dfrac{P_i}{l}(\Delta p_i - \Delta \pi_i)$ | mol·m$^{-2}$·s$^{-1}$ | $l$ = membrane thickness |
| Osmotic Pressure (dilute) | $\pi = i C R T$ | Pa | Van't Hoff equation, $i$ = van't Hoff factor |
| Permeance | $\dfrac{P_i}{l} = \dfrac{J_i}{\Delta p_i}$ | mol·m$^{-2}$·s$^{-1}$·Pa$^{-1}$ | Often quoted for thin films |
| Resistance-in-Series | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{tot}}$ | LMH | $R_{tot} = R_m + R_f + R_c$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Rejection:** Do NOT confuse **apparent (observed) rejection** $R_{obs}$ with **intrinsic (true) rejection** $R_{int}$. The observed value is lower than the intrinsic value due to **concentration polarization**. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Membrane processes underpin critical applications across industries:

- **Water treatment:** Seawater desalination (RO) provides drinking water to arid regions; produces **~100 million m³/day** globally.
- **Biotechnology:** UF/MF used for cell harvesting, protein purification, vaccine production.
- **Medical:** Hemodialysis (artificial kidney) sustains **>2 million patients** worldwide.
- **Chemical:** H$_2$ recovery from ammonia plants, O$_2$/N$_2$ enrichment for medical/industrial use.
- **Food:** Whey protein concentration (UF), juice clarification, sugar refining.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Solvent recovery, sterile filtration.
- **Energy:** Membrane-based CO$_2$ capture, fuel cell membranes (PEM).
- **Environmental:** Effluent treatment, heavy metal removal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Comparison:** RO desalination consumes ~3–6 kWh/m³, whereas thermal distillation (MSF) requires 10–15 kWh/m³ equivalent. This **40–70% energy saving** is why membrane processes dominate modern desalination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Permeate Flux (Solution-Diffusion Model)

> [!NOTE]
> The Solution-Diffusion model is the foundation for dense membrane transport (RO, NF, gas separation, pervaporation). Deriving it cleanly is essential for KTU 14-mark problems.

### Step 1 — Define the System

Consider a dense membrane of thickness $l$ separating a **high-pressure feed** (composition $x_i$, pressure $P_f$) from a **low-pressure permeate** (composition $y_i$, pressure $P_p$).

### Step 2 — State the Three Sequential Steps

For a molecule to pass through the membrane, it must:

1. **Dissolve (or sorb)** into the membrane at the upstream face.
2. **Diffuse** through the membrane under a chemical potential gradient.
3. **Desorb (or evaporate)** at the downstream face.

### Step 3 — Apply Fick's First Law at Steady State

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx}$$

Integrate across the membrane thickness from $x = 0$ (feed-side) to $x = l$ (permeate-side):

$$J_i \int_0^l dx = -D_i \int_{C_{i,f}^m}^{C_{i,p}^m} dC_i$$

$$J_i \cdot l = -D_i \left( C_{i,p}^m - C_{i,f}^m \right)$$

$$J_i = \frac{D_i \left( C_{i,f}^m - C_{i,p}^m \right)}{l}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Henry's Law at the Interfaces

At each face, sorption equilibrium obeys Henry's law:

$$C_i^m = S_i \cdot p_i$$

Where $S_i$ is the sorption coefficient and $p_i$ is the partial pressure.

Substituting:

$$J_i = \frac{D_i S_i}{l} \left( p_{i,f} - p_{i,p} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Define Permeability

The product $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ is defined as the **permeability coefficient**:

$$P_i = D_i \cdot S_i \quad \text{[mol·m·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}\text{]}$$

The final governing equation becomes:

$$\boxed{J_i = \frac{P_i}{l} \left( p_{i,f} - p_{i,p} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For RO systems**, the permeate pressure is replaced by osmotic pressure considerations. The **net driving force** becomes:

$$J_w = \frac{P_w}{l} \left[ \Delta P - \Delta \pi \right] = A_w \left( \Delta P - \Delta \pi \right)$$

Where $A_w = P_w / l$ is the water permeability constant. For the solute:

$$J_s = B_s \left( C_f - C_p \right)$$

Where $B_s$ is the solute permeability constant.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Rejection Calculation

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Style Problem:** A reverse osmosis membrane is used to treat a feed containing 35,000 ppm NaCl. The permeate stream has 350 ppm NaCl. Calculate the (a) observed rejection, (b) the water recovery if the retentate has 70,000 ppm NaCl.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $C_f = 35{,}000$ ppm (feed)
- $C_p = 350$ ppm (permeate)
- $C_r = 70{,}000$ ppm (retentate)

**(a) Observed Rejection:**

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{350}{35{,}000}$$

$$R_{obs} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

> **Result:** $\mathbf{R_{obs} = 99\%}$ — an excellent rejection, characteristic of a high-quality RO membrane.

**(b) Water Recovery:**

Mass balance on NaCl (assuming negligible salt in permeate):

$$Q_f \cdot C_f = Q_r \cdot C_r$$

Where $Q_f$, $Q_r$ are volumetric flow rates. Recovery is defined as:

$$\text{Recovery} = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} = \frac{Q_f - Q_r}{Q_f}$$

From the salt balance:

$$\frac{Q_r}{Q_f} = \frac{C_f}{C_r} = \frac{35{,}000}{70{,}000} = 0.5$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Recovery} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 = \mathbf{50\%}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Membrane Process Performance

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this comparison matrix when answering KTU Part B questions on selection of membrane processes.**

| Property | MF | UF | NF | RO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pore size | 0.1–10 µm | 1–100 nm | 0.5–5 nm | < 0.5 nm (dense) |
| Operating pressure | 0.1–2 bar | 1–10 bar | 10–40 bar | 15–80 bar |
| Separation principle | Sieving | Sieving | Sieving + charge | Solution-diffusion |
| Rejects | Bacteria, yeast | Proteins, viruses | Divalent ions, dyes | Monovalent ions, organics |
| Passes | Water, solutes | Water, salts, small org. | Monovalent ions, water | Water only |
| Flux range (LMH) | 500–10,000 | 50–1000 | 10–100 | 5–50 |
| Typical recovery | 95–99% | 80–95% | 50–85% | 30–75% |

## 3.4 Derivation of Osmotic Pressure (Van't Hoff Equation)

For a dilute ideal solution, the osmotic pressure is derived from equality of chemical potentials across a semipermeable membrane:

$$\mu_w^{soln}(P, x_w) = \mu_w^{pure}(P + \pi)$$

For an incompressible liquid:

$$\mu_w^{soln} = \mu_w^\circ + RT \ln x_w$$

$$\mu_w^{pure} = \mu_w^\circ + v_w \pi$$

Combining and using $\ln x_w \approx -x_s$ for dilute solutes (mole fraction basis):

$$\boxed{\pi = \frac{n_s RT}{V} = iCRT}$$

Where:
- $C$ = molar concentration of solute (mol·L$^{-1}$)
- $i$ = van't Hoff factor (number of dissolved particles per formula unit)
- $R = 8.314$ J·mol$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$ (gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Calculation:** For seawater with $\approx 35$ g·L$^{-1}$ NaCl, molarity $= 35/58.5 = 0.598$ mol·L$^{-1}$, and $i = 2$:

$$\pi = 2 \times 0.598 \times 0.0821 \times 298 \approx 29.3 \text{ bar}$$

This is why RO desalination requires **>30 bar** applied pressure.

## 3.5 Membrane Process Selection Logic

For any given separation, the optimal membrane process is selected using the following decision tree:

1. **Is the solute particulate (≥ 0.1 µm)?** → MF
2. **Is it macromolecular (1–100 nm)?** → UF
3. **Are divalent ions or low-MW organics to be removed?** → NF
4. **Is monovalent salt removal required?** → RO
5. **Is the mixture liquid and azeotropic?** → Pervaporation
6. **Is it a gas mixture?** → Gas separation (membrane type depends on gas pair)
7. **Is ionic separation required with electrical driving force?** → Electrodialysis
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Membrane Separation — General Functional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below maps the generic functional architecture of a membrane separation unit, showing all major components and material/energy streams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FEED STORAGE TANK] --> B[FEED PUMP]
    B --> C[HIGH PRESSURE SIDE OF MEMBRANE MODULE]
    C --> D[MEMBRANE SELECTIVE BARRIER]
    D --> E[PERMEATE COLLECTION VESSEL]
    C --> F[RETENTATE RECYCLE OR DISCHARGE]
    G[DRIVING FORCE SUPPLY: PRESSURE / VACUUM / VOLTAGE / GRADIENT] --> C
    H[CONTROL UNIT: TMP, FLOW, CONCENTRATION SENSORS] -.monitors.-> C
    E --> I[PRODUCT TANK OR NEXT STAGE]
    F --> J[RETENTATE TANK OR REPROCESSING]
```

```mermaid

    subgraph MEMB[MEMBRANE LAYERED CROSS SECTION]
        direction LR
        L1[FEED SIDE]:::feed
        L2[TOP DENSE SKIN LAYER 0.1 TO 1 MICRON]:::skin
        L3[POROUS SUPPORT LAYER 50 TO 200 MICRON]:::support
        L4[NON WOVEN FABRIC BACKING 100 TO 200 MICRON]:::backing

        L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

```mermaid
    A --> C[CONCENTRATION DRIVEN]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INTAKE: SEA OR BRACKISH WATER] --> B[PRE TREATMENT: SCREENING AND FILTRATION]
    B --> C[CHEMICAL DOSING: CHLORINATION AND ANTISCALANT]
    C --> D[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP 60 TO 80 BAR]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
> **Q1.** Define a membrane. List any four ideal properties of an industrial membrane.

**Model Answer:**

A membrane is a thin, selective barrier that permits preferential transport of one or more components of a mixture while rejecting others, under the action of a driving force (pressure, concentration, electrical potential, or partial vapor pressure).

Ideal properties of an industrial membrane:
1. High permeability (high flux at low driving force).
2. High selectivity (sharp molecular discrimination).
3. Mechanical strength to withstand operating pressure.
4. Chemical resistance to feed pH, solvents, and oxidizing agents.
5. Thermal stability at operating temperature.
6. Resistance to fouling and long operational life.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between permeate and retentate in a membrane separation process with a neat sketch.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Permeate | Retentate |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Stream that **passes through** the membrane | Stream **retained** by the membrane |
| Composition | Enriched in preferentially transported species | Enriched in rejected species |
| Pressure | Lower (often atmospheric) | Higher (equal to feed pressure minus losses) |
| Location | Below/inside the membrane | Above/outside the membrane |

In a typical RO setup, clean water (permeate) flows through the membrane, while concentrated brine (retentate) leaves the module.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students lose marks in Module 1 questions by:
> - Confusing **microfiltration** with **ultrafiltration** (memorize the pore-size ranges).
> - Forgetting to specify **units** in numerical answers.
> - Writing only definitions without **examples or diagrams**.
> - Mixing up **driving force** with **separation mechanism** (e.g., for RO, the driving force is pressure but the mechanism is solution-diffusion).

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify membrane separation processes based on the driving force employed. List any four such processes with their respective driving forces and one industrial application each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the working of a reverse osmosis system for seawater desalination. State the role of osmotic pressure and derive the relation for net driving pressure. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution (Question A)

**(a) Classification of Membrane Processes by Driving Force:**

| Membrane Process | Driving Force | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|
| Reverse Osmosis (RO) | Pressure difference (15–80 bar) | Seawater desalination |
| Ultrafiltration (UF) | Pressure difference (1–10 bar) | Protein concentration in dairy |
| Pervaporation (PV) | Partial vapor pressure difference | Dehydration of ethanol |
| Gas Separation (GS) | Partial pressure difference | O$_2$/N$_2$ enrichment from air |
| Electrodialysis (ED) | Electrical potential gradient | Demineralization of brackish water |
| Dialysis | Concentration gradient | Artificial kidney (hemodialysis) |

**(b) Reverse Osmosis for Seawater Desalination:**

**Working Principle (Diagram description):** Seawater is pressurized by a high-pressure pump and fed into a closed vessel containing RO membranes (typically spiral-wound modules). Pure water passes through the membrane under pressure exceeding the osmotic pressure of seawater (~25–30 bar). The permeate is collected as desalinated water; the concentrated brine exits as retentate.

**Role of Osmotic Pressure:**
Osmosis is the spontaneous flow of water from a region of low solute concentration to high solute concentration across a semipermeable membrane. To reverse this flow (hence **Reverse Osmosis**), the applied pressure must exceed the osmotic pressure of the feed solution.

**Derivation of Net Driving Pressure:**

Consider feed at pressure $P_f$ and permeate at pressure $P_p$. The osmotic pressures are $\pi_f$ and $\pi_p$ respectively.

The **chemical potential of water on the feed side:**

$$\mu_w^f = \mu_w^\circ + v_w (P_f - \pi_f)$$

The **chemical potential of water on the permeate side:**

$$\mu_w^p = \mu_w^\circ + v_w P_p$$

For water to flow from feed to permeate (reverse osmosis condition):

$$\mu_w^f > \mu_w^p$$

$$v_w (P_f - \pi_f) > v_w P_p$$

$$P_f - P_p > \pi_f - \pi_p$$

Defining $\Delta P = P_f - P_p$ and $\Delta \pi = \pi_f - \pi_p$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{net} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi > 0}$$

The flux is given by:

$$J_w = A_w (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$

Where $A_w$ is the water permeability constant of the membrane.

**Numerical Example (Brief):** For seawater with $C = 0.6$ M NaCl, $i = 2$, $T = 298$ K:

$$\pi_f = i C R T = 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.0821 \times 298 \approx 29.4 \text{ bar}$$

The applied pressure must be **>29.4 bar** for RO to occur. Typical operating pressure is 60–80 bar to maintain reasonable flux.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the **Solution-Diffusion model** for transport through dense membranes. Derive the flux expression and identify the three sequential steps involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RO membrane plant treats 1000 m³/day of brackish water containing 5000 ppm total dissolved solids (TDS). The permeate has 200 ppm TDS and is produced at a recovery of 60%. Calculate: **(i)** the permeate flow rate, **(ii)** the retentate TDS concentration, and **(iii)** the observed rejection. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution (Question B)

**(a) Solution-Diffusion Model:**

This is the widely accepted model for transport through **dense (non-porous)** membranes such as those used in RO, gas separation, and pervaporation.

**Three Sequential Steps:**

1. **Sorption (Dissolution)** — The permeating species dissolves into the membrane material at the upstream face (feed side).
2. **Diffusion** — The dissolved species migrates through the membrane under a concentration/chemical potential gradient.
3. **Desorption (Evaporation)** — The species leaves the membrane at the downstream face (permeate side).

**Derivation:**

Apply Fick's First Law at steady state:

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx}$$

Integrating across membrane thickness $l$ from $C_{i,f}^m$ to $C_{i,p}^m$:

$$J_i = \frac{D_i (C_{i,f}^m - C_{i,p}^m)}{l}$$

Applying Henry's Law at the interfaces ($C_i^m = S_i p_i$):

$$J_i = \frac{D_i S_i (p_{i,f} - p_{i,p})}{l}$$

Defining permeability $P_i = D_i S_i$:

$$\boxed{J_i = \frac{P_i}{l} \left( p_{i,f} - p_{i,p} \right)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_f = 1000$ m³/day
- $C_f = 5000$ ppm TDS
- $C_p = 200$ ppm TDS
- Recovery $= 60\%$

**(i) Permeate Flow Rate:**

$$Q_p = 0.60 \times 1000 = \mathbf{600 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$

Retentate flow:

$$Q_r = Q_f - Q_p = 1000 - 600 = \mathbf{400 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$

**(ii) Retentate TDS Concentration:**

Overall mass balance on TDS:

$$Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r$$

$$1000 \times 5000 = 600 \times 200 + 400 \times C_r$$

$$5{,}000{,}000 = 120{,}000 + 400 C_r$$

$$400 C_r = 4{,}880{,}000$$

$$C_r = \mathbf{12{,}200 \text{ ppm TDS}}$$

**(iii) Observed Rejection:**

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{200}{5000} = 1 - 0.04 = \mathbf{0.96 \text{ (or 96\%)}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Membrane Processes: Introduction**

### Definitions & Concepts
- ☐ Membrane = selective barrier; separation without phase change.
- ☐ Permeate = stream that passes through; Retentate = stream that is rejected.
- ☐ Symmetric vs asymmetric membranes (asymmetric is industrially dominant).
- ☐ Dense vs porous membranes (separation by solution-diffusion vs size exclusion).

### Classifications
- ☐ **By material:** polymeric / ceramic / metallic / liquid / mixed-matrix.
- ☐ **By geometry:** flat sheet / spiral-wound / hollow fiber / tubular / capillary.
- ☐ **By driving force:** pressure / concentration / electrical / vapor pressure.

### Key Equations (Must Memorize)
- ☐ $J = Q_p / (A_m \cdot t)$ — Flux definition.
- ☐ $R = 1 - (C_p / C_f)$ — Rejection.
- ☐ $\pi = i C R T$ — Van't Hoff osmotic pressure.
- ☐ $\Delta P_{net} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi$ — Net driving pressure for RO.
- ☐ $J_i = (P_i / l)(p_{i,f} - p_{i,p})$ — Solution-diffusion flux.

### Numerical Knowledge
- ☐ Seawater osmotic pressure ≈ 25–30 bar.
- ☐ Typical RO operating pressure = 60–80 bar.
- ☐ Typical flux range: MF (500–10,000 LMH), UF (50–1000 LMH), RO (5–50 LMH).
- ☐ Robeson's upper bound = permeability-selectivity trade-off (gas separation).

### Historical Landmarks (Essay-worthy)
- ☐ Abbé Nollet (1748) — osmosis.
- ☐ Fick (1855) — diffusion laws.
- ☐ Bechhold (1907) — first synthetic membrane.
- ☐ Loeb–Sourirajan (1959) — asymmetric RO membrane.

### Common Applications
- ☐ Desalination → RO.
- ☐ Sterile filtration → MF.
- ☐ Protein concentration → UF.
- ☐ Water softening → NF.
- ☐ Ethanol dehydration → Pervaporation.
- ☐ Artificial kidney → Dialysis.
- ☐ O$_2$ enrichment → Gas separation.

### Examiner's Quick Pitfalls
- ☐ Don't confuse $R_{obs}$ with $R_{int}$ (concentration polarization effect).
- ☐ Don't confuse the **driving force** (applied) with the **separation mechanism** (intrinsic).
- ☐ Always quote units in numerical answers (LMH, bar, ppm).
- ☐ Hollow fiber ≠ capillary ≠ tubular — distinguish by dimensions and feed flow path.
- ☐ Membrane fouling and concentration polarization cause flux decline — not the same thing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Advice:** When answering any KTU question on membrane processes, structure your answer in **three parts**: (1) definition/diagram, (2) principle/derivation, (3) example/application. This is exactly the structure examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Type and Choice of Membranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Membrane?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (NEP 2020 Aligned):**
> A **membrane** is a selective barrier that permits the preferential passage of one or more components of a mixture (permeate) while rejecting others (retentate) under the influence of a driving force (pressure, concentration, temperature, or electrical potential gradient).

The term **"permeate"** refers to the stream that passes through the membrane, while the **"retentate"** (or concentrate) is the stream that is retained. The **driving force** is the thermodynamic gradient that pushes species through the membrane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Driving Forces Used in Membrane Separations:**
> - **Pressure difference (ΔP)** → Microfiltration, Ultrafiltration, Nanofiltration, Reverse Osmosis
> - **Concentration difference (ΔC)** → Pervaporation, Dialysis, Gas Separation
> - **Electrical potential (ΔE)** → Electrodialysis
> - **Temperature difference (ΔT)** → Membrane Distillation, Thermo-osmosis

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

Imagine a **fishing net with selectively sized holes**. If the mesh is large, small fish escape but big fish are caught. If the mesh is very fine, even the tiniest sand particles are blocked, but water slips through effortlessly.

A **membrane behaves exactly like this fishing net**, but at the molecular or ionic level:
- **Pore size** = the mesh opening of the net
- **Permeate** = the small things (water, gases) that escape
- **Retentate** = the big things (salts, proteins, cells) that are caught
- **Driving force (pressure)** = the fisherman pulling the net through water

The key insight: **Membranes don't "filter" in the traditional sense alone** — they separate by a combination of **sieving, solution-diffusion, electrostatic repulsion**, and **charge effects**, depending on the membrane type and separation process.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Term to Remember:**
> The **separation factor (α)** quantifies how well a membrane distinguishes between two species. The higher the α, the more selective the membrane.

---

## 1.3 Why "Type" and "Choice" of Membranes Matter

In the **novel separation processes** domain, no single membrane works for everything. The choice determines:
1. **Separation efficiency** (selectivity)
2. **Throughput** (flux)
3. **Energy consumption**
4. **Fouling rate** and **cleaning frequency**
5. **Capital and operating cost**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Questions on membrane classification, material selection, and the relationship between pore size and separation process are **frequently asked (high-weightage)** in both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**.

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics Every Student Must Know

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flux | $J$ | $L \cdot m^{-2} \cdot h^{-1}$ (LMH) or $\text{kg/m}^2\text{s}$ | Volume/mass of permeate per unit area per unit time |
| Permeability | $P$ | $\text{mol}\cdot m^{-2}\cdot s^{-1}\cdot Pa^{-1}$ or **Barrer** | Ability of a species to pass through |
| Selectivity | $\alpha$ | dimensionless | Ratio of permeabilities of two species |
| Rejection | $R$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of solute retained by membrane |
| Pore size | $d$ | $\mu m$ or $\text{nm}$ | Diameter of membrane pores |
| Thickness | $l$ | $\mu m$ | Effective membrane thickness |

The **Barrer** is a non-SI unit defined as:
$$1 \text{ Barrer} = 3.348 \times 10^{-16} \text{ mol}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}\cdot\text{Pa}^{-1}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Membranes (KTU Syllabus-Ready Structure)

Membrane classification can be approached from **four primary perspectives**:

### A. Classification by Structure

1. **Porous Membranes**
   - Pore size range: **0.1 nm – 10 μm**
   - Operate mainly by **sieving mechanism**
   - Used in MF, UF, and some gas separations
   - Examples: ceramic, sintered metals, polymeric (PTFE, PVDF, cellulose acetate)

2. **Non-Porous (Dense) Membranes**
   - Transport occurs by **solution-diffusion mechanism**
   - Used in RO, gas separation, pervaporation
   - Examples: silicone rubber, polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), dense polymer films

3. **Liquid Membranes**
   - Contain a **liquid phase** immobilized within a porous support
   - High selectivity due to carrier-mediated transport
   - Used in **facilitated transport** and metal ion recovery
   - Subtypes: **Supported Liquid Membrane (SLM)**, **Emulsion Liquid Membrane (ELM)**

4. **Asymmetric / Composite Membranes**
   - Two layers: **thin selective skin (0.1–1 μm)** + **porous support (50–200 μm)**
   - Thin skin provides selectivity, support provides mechanical strength
   - Standard in industrial RO and UF

---

### B. Classification by Material

1. **Polymeric Membranes**
   - Low cost, easy fabrication, versatile
   - Limitations: thermal and chemical resistance
   - Common polymers: **cellulose acetate (CA), polysulfone (PS), polyethersulfone (PES), polyimide (PI), PVDF, PTFE, PAN, PDMS**

2. **Inorganic Membranes**
   - Made from ceramics, metals, glass, or carbon
   - Excellent **thermal stability (up to 1000 °C)** and **chemical resistance**
   - Examples: **Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂, SiC, sintered stainless steel**
   - Applications: harsh industrial environments, high-temperature gas separation

3. **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMM)**
   - Hybrid of polymer matrix + inorganic filler (zeolites, MOFs, CNTs)
   - Combines processability of polymers with selectivity of inorganics
   - Active research area in KTU 2024 syllabus

---

### C. Classification by Geometry

| Geometry | Membrane Configuration | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat Sheet (Plate & Frame)** | Flat membrane sheets in stack | Lab-scale, small batch |
| **Spiral Wound** | Flat sheet + feed spacer rolled into a spiral | RO, NF — most common industrially |
| **Tubular** | Membrane coated on inside of tubes | High-viscosity feeds, dairy, fruit juices |
| **Hollow Fiber** | Self-supporting fiber with OD < 1 mm | UF, RO, gas separation — high packing density |
| **Capillary** | ID 0.5–3 mm | UF, blood oxygenators |

> [!TIP]
> **Hollow Fiber vs. Capillary:** Both look similar, but hollow fibers are self-supporting, while capillaries require external support. KTU exams often test this subtle distinction.

---

### D. Classification by Separation Process

| Process | Pore Size (approx.) | Driving Force | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | 0.1 – 10 μm | ΔP = 0.1 – 2 bar | Sterilization, clarification |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | 1 – 100 nm | ΔP = 1 – 10 bar | Protein concentration, latex recovery |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | 0.5 – 5 nm | ΔP = 5 – 40 bar | Water softening, dye removal |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 0.5 nm (dense) | ΔP = 10 – 100 bar | Desalination, water reuse |
| **Dialysis** | porous | ΔC | Artificial kidney (haemodialysis) |
| **Electrodialysis** | ion-exchange | ΔE | Salt concentration, ion separation |
| **Pervaporation** | dense | ΔC (vapor) | Azeotrope breaking (ethanol-water) |
| **Gas Separation** | dense | ΔP (partial) | O₂/N₂, CO₂/CH₄ separation |
| **Membrane Distillation** | porous hydrophobic | ΔT | Pure water from brine |

---

## 2.2 Key Equations & Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations. KTU exams frequently test derivations and direct applications of these formulas.**

### 2.2.1 Permeate Flux (Hagen–Poiseuille model for porous membranes)

$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \Delta P}{32 \cdot \mu \cdot l \cdot \tau}$$

Where:
- $J$ = volumetric flux (m³/m²·s)
- $\varepsilon$ = surface porosity (dimensionless)
- $d_p$ = pore diameter (m)
- $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa)
- $\mu$ = viscosity of fluid (Pa·s)
- $l$ = membrane thickness (m)
- $\tau$ = tortuosity (dimensionless, ≥ 1)

### 2.2.2 Flux through Dense Membrane (Solution-Diffusion Model)

$$J_i = \frac{P_i}{l} \left( x_{f,i} - x_{p,i} \right)$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = permeability of component $i$
- $l$ = membrane thickness
- $x_{f,i}, x_{p,i}$ = mole fractions in feed and permeate

### 2.2.3 Rejection Coefficient

$$R_i = 1 - \frac{C_{p,i}}{C_{f,i}}$$

Where $C_{p,i}$ and $C_{f,i}$ are concentrations of species $i$ in permeate and feed.

### 2.2.4 Selectivity (Separation Factor)

For two components A and B:
$$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{y_A / y_B}{x_A / x_B}$$

Where $x$ and $y$ are feed and permeate compositions (in mole fractions).

### 2.2.5 Membrane Resistance Model (Darcy's Law)

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m} \quad \text{where} \quad R_m = \frac{l}{A_k \cdot \varepsilon / \tau}$$

This shows that **flux is inversely proportional to membrane resistance**.

### 2.2.6 Cut-off Molecular Weight (for UF)

$$J_{species} = J_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\beta \cdot \frac{MW}{MW_{cutoff}}\right)$$

Species with MW > MW_cutoff are >90 % rejected.

---

## 2.3 KTU Cheat-Sheet — Critical Numerical Ranges (Must Memorize)

| Property | MF | UF | NF | RO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pore size (μm) | $10^0$ – $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-1}$ – $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-3}$ – $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | dense (no pores) |
| ΔP (bar) | 0.1 – 2 | 1 – 10 | 5 – 40 | 10 – 100 |
| Rejection of NaCl | 0 % | 0 % | 20 – 80 % | 95 – 99.9 % |
| Flux (LMH) | 500 – 1000 | 50 – 500 | 10 – 100 | 5 – 50 |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** As the membrane gets tighter (from MF → RO), flux drops but rejection rises. This is the **classic flux-selectivity trade-off** in membrane engineering.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Water treatment plants** use RO (polyamide spiral wound) for desalination — the membrane's polyamide skin rejects >99 % NaCl.
- **Dairy industry** uses UF (polysulfone hollow fiber) to concentrate milk proteins (casein, whey).
- **Pharmaceuticals** use NF for solvent recovery and impurity removal.
- **Green hydrogen economy** uses dense Pd-based or MOF membranes for ultra-pure H₂ separation.

---

## 2.5 Industrial Selection Logic (Real-World)

Selecting a membrane is **not arbitrary** — it is a multi-criteria decision involving:

1. **Feed characteristics** → particle size, viscosity, fouling tendency
2. **Target separation** → what needs to be removed
3. **Operating envelope** → pH, temperature, pressure tolerance
4. **Economic factors** → module cost, replacement frequency
5. **Cleaning regime** → backwash, chemical resistance
6. **Module geometry** → spiral wound (cheapest), hollow fiber (highest density)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hagen–Poiseuille Flux Through a Cylindrical Pore

We start from the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** describing laminar flow through a cylindrical tube:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p^4 \cdot \Delta P}{128 \cdot \mu \cdot l}$$

Where $Q$ is volumetric flow rate through a single pore.

To convert this into flux (per unit membrane area), we use:
- Number of pores per unit area: $n = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{A_p}$ where $A_p = \pi d_p^2/4$ is the cross-section of one pore.
- Thus $n = \dfrac{4 \varepsilon}{\pi d_p^2}$

The flux becomes:
$$J = Q \cdot n = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p^4 \cdot \Delta P}{128 \cdot \mu \cdot l} \cdot \frac{4 \varepsilon}{\pi d_p^2}$$

Simplifying:
$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \Delta P}{32 \cdot \mu \cdot l}$$

Introducing the **tortuosity correction** $\tau$ to account for non-vertical pores:
$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \Delta P}{32 \cdot \mu \cdot l \cdot \tau}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Flux is **proportional** to $d_p^2$ (doubling pore size quadruples flux) and inversely proportional to viscosity and thickness. This is why **thin-skinned composite membranes** dominate industrial applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Solution-Diffusion Model for Dense Membranes

The model assumes the permeating species:
1. **Dissolves** at the upstream face
2. **Diffuses** through the membrane under concentration gradient
3. **Desorbs** at the downstream face

### Step 1: Apply Fick's First Law

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dz}$$

where $D_i$ is the diffusivity of species $i$ in the membrane.

### Step 2: Integrate across the membrane thickness

Assuming constant diffusivity and steady state:
$$J_i \int_0^l dz = -D_i \int_{C_{f,m}}^{C_{p,m}} dC_i$$

$$J_i \cdot l = D_i (C_{f,m} - C_{p,m})$$

$$J_i = \frac{D_i (C_{f,m} - C_{p,m})}{l}$$

### Step 3: Apply Henry's law at the interfaces

$$C_{i,m} = S_i \cdot p_i \quad \text{or} \quad C_{i,m} = S_i \cdot x_i$$

where $S_i$ is the sorption coefficient (solubility) and $p_i$ is partial pressure.

### Step 4: Combine to obtain final form

$$J_i = \frac{D_i \cdot S_i}{l} (x_{f,i} - x_{p,i}) = \frac{P_i}{l} (x_{f,i} - x_{p,i})$$

where the **permeability** $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** In solution-diffusion, permeability has **two independent physical contributions**:
> - $D_i$ (kinetic — how fast the molecule moves)
> - $S_i$ (thermodynamic — how much dissolves)
>
> A membrane can be made selective by **either** slowing down one species (low $D$) **or** excluding it from dissolving (low $S$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Membrane Selectivity from Permeabilities

For two species A (faster) and B (slower):

$$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{J_A / (x_{f,A} - x_{p,A})}{J_B / (x_{f,B} - x_{p,B})}$$

At **low permeate concentrations** (typical for gas separation):
$$x_{p,A} \ll x_{f,A} \quad \text{and} \quad x_{p,B} \ll x_{f,B}$$

So the approximation:
$$J_i \approx \frac{P_i \cdot x_{f,i}}{l}$$

Substituting:
$$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A \cdot x_{f,A} / l}{P_B \cdot x_{f,B} / l} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} \cdot \frac{x_{f,A}}{x_{f,B}}$$

Equivalently:
$$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{(y_A / y_B)}{(x_A / x_B)}$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction in the permeate.

> This is the **standard definition of separation factor** used throughout the membrane literature.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relationship Between Rejection and Permeate Composition

Starting from the definition:
$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$

For a binary mixture with feed concentration $C_f$, the permeate concentration can be written as:
$$C_p = C_f (1 - R)$$

If the rejection of solvent (e.g., water) is negligible and rejection of solute is $R_s$, then for volume balance:
$$J \cdot C_p = J \cdot C_f \cdot (1 - R)$$

This is automatically satisfied. However, for a **mass balance** across the membrane module, with feed $F$, retentate $R$, permeate $P$:
$$F \cdot C_f = R \cdot C_r + P \cdot C_p$$

Combined with the rejection definition, the **observed rejection** $R_{obs}$ differs from the **intrinsic rejection** $R_{int}$ by a **concentration polarization factor**:
$$R_{obs} = \frac{R_{int} \cdot \exp(Pe)}{R_{int} + \exp(Pe)}$$

where $Pe$ is the **Peclet number** based on membrane permeability.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Membrane Selection Decision Tree

| If feed contains... | And goal is to remove... | Then choose... | Material | Geometry |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Suspended cells (μm) | Bacteria/yeast | **MF** | Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) | Hollow fiber |
| Colloids, proteins (nm) | Whey proteins | **UF** | Polysulfone (PSf) | Spiral wound |
| Divalent ions, dyes | Hardness, organics | **NF** | Polyamide thin-film composite | Spiral wound |
| Monovalent salts | NaCl, seawater | **RO** | Polyamide (TFC) | Spiral wound |
| H₂ in gas mix | H₂ from CH₄ | **Dense Pd or MOF** | Palladium / ZIF-8 | Tubular |
| Azeotropic liquids | Ethanol from water | **Pervaporation** | PDMS or PVA | Flat sheet |
| Ions in solution | Salt from water | **Electrodialysis** | Ion-exchange membranes | Plate-and-frame |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Rule of Thumb (KTU high-yield):**
> If the species to be removed is **larger** than the solvent → choose **pressure-driven porous process** (MF/UF).
> If the species to be removed is **similar in size or smaller** but needs a separation → choose **dense or charged membrane** (RO/NF/ED).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

> **Q: Differentiate between porous and non-porous (dense) membranes. Give one example and one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Porous Membrane | Non-Porous (Dense) Membrane |
|---|---|---|
| Pore structure | Contains discrete pores of fixed size | No discrete pores; transport via solution-diffusion |
| Separation mechanism | Sieving / size exclusion | Solubility + Diffusivity differences |
| Driving force | Pressure | Pressure, concentration, or partial pressure |
| Examples | Polysulfone UF, PVDF MF, ceramic Al₂O₃ | Silicone rubber (PDMS), dense polyamide, Pd foil |
| Applications | MF, UF (sterilization, protein separation) | RO, gas separation, pervaporation |
| Flux dependence | $J \propto d_p^2$ (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $J \propto P/l$ (Solution-diffusion) |

> **Conclusion:** Porous membranes offer higher flux but are limited to size-based separation. Dense membranes enable molecular-level selectivity through differential solubility and diffusivity.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **Q: Explain the term "membrane selectivity" with the help of a suitable mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer:**

Selectivity is the **ability of a membrane to preferentially allow one component to pass while restricting another**. It is quantified by the **separation factor (α)** for two components A and B:

$$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{(y_A / y_B)_{permeate}}{(x_A / x_B)_{feed}}$$

Where $y_i$ and $x_i$ are the mole fractions of component $i$ in the permeate and feed, respectively.

- If $\alpha_{A/B} = 1$ → no separation
- If $\alpha_{A/B} > 1$ → membrane is A-selective
- If $\alpha_{A/B} \to \infty$ → perfect selectivity

> **Equivalent definition** using permeabilities: $\alpha_{A/B} = P_A / P_B$
> For RO, an equivalent measure is the **rejection coefficient**: $R_i = 1 - C_{p,i}/C_{f,i}$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE — June 2024, Module 1]`

> **(a)** Classify membranes based on (i) structure, (ii) material, and (iii) geometry. Explain any two membrane geometries in detail with neat diagrams and their applications. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** What are the important parameters considered in the choice of membranes for a given separation? Discuss the flux-selectivity trade-off with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Membrane Classification

**(i) Classification by Structure:**

1. **Porous membranes** — discrete pores (0.1 nm – 10 μm). Separation by sieving. Example: PVDF MF, ceramic Al₂O₃.
2. **Dense (non-porous) membranes** — no pores. Separation by solution-diffusion. Example: PDMS, dense polyamide.
3. **Liquid membranes** — liquid phase held in porous support; carrier-mediated transport. Example: SLM for metal ion recovery.
4. **Asymmetric / Composite membranes** — thin dense skin (0.1–1 μm) on porous support (50–200 μm). Standard in industrial RO.

**(ii) Classification by Material:**

1. **Polymeric** — cellulose acetate, polysulfone, polyimide, PDMS. Economical, versatile.
2. **Inorganic** — ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂), metal (Pd, stainless steel), glass. High temperature/chemical resistance.
3. **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMM)** — polymer + inorganic filler (zeolite, MOF, CNT). Combines benefits of both.

**(iii) Classification by Geometry (with explanation of two):**

**1. Spiral Wound Module:**
- Constructed by gluing two flat membranes back-to-back with a permeate spacer in between, then rolling the assembly around a perforated central tube with a feed spacer.
- **Feed flows axially** through the spacer channels; **permeate spirals inward** to the central tube.
- **Advantages:** High packing density, low cost, easily replaceable cartridges.
- **Applications:** Seawater RO, NF, brackish water treatment.
- **Disadvantage:** Susceptible to fouling from suspended solids.

**2. Hollow Fiber Module:**
- Thousands of self-supporting fibers (OD 50–500 μm, ID 25–250 μm) packed inside a pressure vessel.
- **Feed** flows either through the bore (inside-out) or around the shell (outside-in).
- **Advantages:** Highest surface area per unit volume (~10,000 m²/m³), self-supporting, no separate support needed.
- **Applications:** UF (whey concentration), hemodialysis, RO, gas separation.
- **Disadvantage:** Prone to plugging by particulates.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Choice of Membrane & Flux-Selectivity Trade-off

**Parameters considered in membrane selection:**

1. **Feed characteristics** — particle size, viscosity, fouling tendency, pH, temperature.
2. **Target separation** — what species must be removed or concentrated.
3. **Required selectivity and flux** — based on product purity targets.
4. **Operating envelope** — chemical resistance, thermal stability, pressure tolerance.
5. **Module geometry** — to match feed properties (viscous feeds → tubular; clean feeds → hollow fiber).
6. **Mechanical and chemical durability** — lifespan, cleaning regime tolerance.
7. **Cost** — membrane unit cost, replacement frequency, energy cost.
8. **Regulatory and safety factors** — especially in pharma and food.

**Flux-Selectivity Trade-off:**

In membrane science, **higher selectivity typically comes at the cost of lower flux**, and vice versa. This is observable across all membrane processes:

| Process | Selectivity Trend | Flux Trend |
|---|---|---|
| MF (large pores) | Low (only removes particles) | Very high |
| UF (smaller pores) | Moderate | High |
| NF | High for divalent ions | Moderate |
| RO (dense skin) | Very high for monovalent salts | Low |

**Mathematical demonstration:**

From the Hagen–Poiseuille equation:
$$J \propto d_p^2$$

But selectivity (size-based) improves when $d_p$ is reduced to molecular dimensions. Therefore, as $d_p \to$ smaller, $J \to$ smaller but selectivity $\to$ higher.

For dense membranes, the Robeson upper bound (1991) shows that permeability and selectivity are also inversely correlated on a log-log plot.

> **Engineering implication:** The choice of membrane is always a **compromise** between throughput and purity, optimized for the specific industrial requirement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE — Dec 2023, Module 1]`

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **spiral wound membrane module** and list its advantages and limitations. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compare the four pressure-driven membrane processes (MF, UF, NF, RO) based on **pore size, applied pressure, rejection capability, flux range, and typical applications**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Spiral Wound Module

**Working Principle:**

A spiral wound module is fabricated as follows:
1. Two flat membrane sheets are separated by a **permeate spacer** (porous material) and sealed along three edges to form an "envelope."
2. The open edge of the envelope is glued to a **perforated central permeate collection tube**.
3. The envelope is rolled spirally around the tube along with a **feed spacer** (mesh) that creates turbulence in the feed channel.

**Operating sequence:**
- **Feed (high pressure)** enters one end of the module, flows axially through the feed spacer channels in a **tangential (cross-flow)** manner.
- Pressurized feed contacts the outer surfaces of the membrane envelope; **permeate passes through the membrane** and travels spirally through the permeate spacer to the central tube.
- **Retentate** exits from the other end of the module.
- **Permeate** flows out through the central perforated tube.

**Advantages:**
- High packing density (500–1000 m²/m³)
- Low cost per unit membrane area
- Easy to scale by adding more elements
- Replaceable cartridges
- Standardized manufacturing

**Limitations:**
- Sensitive to suspended solids (fouling)
- Difficult to clean mechanically
- Not suitable for highly viscous feeds
- Limited to moderate pressures (typically < 80 bar)

---

#### Solution to (b) — Comparison of Pressure-Driven Processes

| Parameter | MF | UF | NF | RO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pore size** | 0.1 – 10 μm | 1 – 100 nm | 0.5 – 5 nm | Dense (no pore) |
| **Applied ΔP** | 0.1 – 2 bar | 1 – 10 bar | 5 – 40 bar | 10 – 100 bar |
| **Rejection size** | > 0.1 μm | > 1 kDa (MW) | > 200 Da (MW) | All dissolved salts |
| **NaCl rejection** | 0 % | 0 % | 20 – 80 % | 95 – 99.9 % |
| **Typical flux (LMH)** | 500 – 1000 | 50 – 500 | 10 – 100 | 5 – 50 |
| **Transport mechanism** | Sieving | Sieving | Sieving + charge | Solution-diffusion |
| **Typical applications** | Sterilization, clarification | Protein recovery, latex | Water softening, dye removal | Desalination, water reuse |
| **Common materials** | PVDF, PTFE, ceramic | Polysulfone, PES, CA | Polyamide TFC | Polyamide TFC |
| **Membrane geometry** | Hollow fiber, tubular | Hollow fiber, spiral | Spiral wound | Spiral wound |

> **Key Engineering Insight:** From MF to RO, both the **selectivity increases** and the **energy demand increases** (square of pressure). The process chosen must balance product purity needs against operating cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Repeated in KTU Valuations):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "membrane flux" with "permeability"** — Flux depends on operating conditions (ΔP, T); permeability is an intrinsic membrane property.
> 2. **Stating "RO has pores"** — RO uses a **dense skin** with no discrete pores; transport is by solution-diffusion, not sieving.
> 3. **Mixing up hollow fiber and capillary** — Hollow fibers are self-supporting; capillaries require external support.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the Barrer — 1 Barrer = $3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ SI units. Marks deducted if units are missing.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams** in spiral wound / hollow fiber questions — Diagrams carry **2–3 marks** in Part B answers.
> 6. **Writing the wrong equation for dense vs. porous membranes** — Use Hagen–Poiseuille for porous, solution-diffusion for dense.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] A membrane is a **selective barrier** separating a mixture into **permeate** and **retentate** under a **driving force**.
- [ ] Driving forces: **ΔP, ΔC, ΔE, ΔT**.
- [ ] Membranes are classified by **structure, material, geometry, and process**.
- [ ] **Porous membranes** separate by **sieving**; **dense membranes** by **solution-diffusion**.
- [ ] **Hagen–Poiseuille** flux: $J = \varepsilon d_p^2 \Delta P / (32 \mu l \tau)$ — flux $\propto d_p^2$.
- [ ] **Solution-diffusion** flux: $J_i = (P_i/l)(x_{f,i} - x_{p,i})$ — permeability $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$.
- [ ] **Rejection**: $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$.
- [ ] **Selectivity**: $\alpha_{A/B} = (y_A/y_B)/(x_A/x_B) = P_A/P_B$.
- [ ] **Pore size order**: MF > UF > NF > RO (dense).
- [ ] **Pressure order**: MF < UF < NF < RO.
- [ ] **Flux order**: MF > UF > NF > RO.
- [ ] **Selectivity order**: MF < UF < NF < RO.
- [ ] **Spiral wound** = flat sheet + feed spacer rolled; highest industrial use.
- [ ] **Hollow fiber** = self-supporting fiber; highest area/volume.
- [ ] **Tubular** = for viscous or fouling feeds (dairy, fruit juice).
- [ ] **Polymeric membranes** dominate; **inorganic** for harsh conditions; **MMM** is emerging.
- [ ] **Robeson upper bound**: selectivity and permeability are inversely related.
- [ ] **Cut-off MW** defines the 90 % rejection limit for UF.
- [ ] **Barrer** is the unit of gas permeability: $3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ SI.
- [ ] KTU focus: classification, selection criteria, and the **flux-selectivity trade-off**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
