Compute the steric reflection coefficient for glucose ($r_s = 0.36 \ \text{nm}$) and for sucrose ($r_s = 0.54 \ \text{nm}$). **Solution for Glucose:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{\text{glucose}} &= \dfrac{r_s}{r_p}\left(2 - \dfrac{r_s}{r_p}\right) \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{0.36}{1.2}\left(2 - \dfrac{0.36}{1.2}\right) \\[6pt] &= 0.30 \times (2 - 0.30) \\[6pt] &= 0.30 \times 1.70 \\[6pt] &= 0.51 \end{aligned} $$ **Solution for Sucrose:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{\text{sucrose}} &= \dfrac{0.54}{1.2}\left(2 - \dfrac{0.54}{1.2}\right) \\[6pt] &= 0.45 \times (2 - 0.45) \\[6pt] &= 0.45 \times 1.55 \\[6pt] &= 0.6975 \approx 0.70 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Sucrose (larger) is rejected more strongly ($\sigma = 0.70$) than glucose ($\sigma = 0.51$), as expected from the size-sieving principle. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 NF Separation Mechanism — Block Architecture Flow The diagram below illustrates the **multi-mechanism transport model** that distinguishes NF from UF and RO. Each block represents a physical/chemical interaction between solute, solvent, and the charged nanoporous membrane. ```mermaid flowchart LR feed["Feed Solution
Multivalent + Monovalent ions
Sugars, Dyes, Water"] --> pressurize["Applied Pressure
DeltaP 5 to 40 bar"] pressurize --> membrane["NF Membrane
Negative surface charge
Pore 0.5 to 2 nm
MWCO 200 to 1000 Da"] membrane --> steric["Steric Sieving
Size exclusion
sigma geometric"] membrane --> donnan["Donnan Exclusion
Co ion repulsion
Anion rejection"] membrane --> dielectric["Dielectric Exclusion
Born energy barrier
Multivalent rejection"] steric --> permeate donnan --> permeate dielectric --> permeate permeate["PERMEATE
Water + Monovalent ions
Low hardness
Low organics"] membrane --> retentate["RETENTATE
Multivalent ions
Divalent salts
Dyes, Sugars
Hardness species"] style feed fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000 style pressurize fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#f6b26b,color:#000 style membrane fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style steric fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 style donnan fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 style dielectric fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 style permeate fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000 style retentate fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 NF Membrane Hierarchy — Comparison Flow This diagram maps the **position of NF** within the broader membrane filtration family, highlighting its unique operational window. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph micro["MICROFILTRATION"] MF["Pore 100 to 10000 nm
Pressure 0.1 to 2 bar
Removes suspended solids"] end subgraph ultra["ULTRAFILTRATION"] UF["Pore 2 to 100 nm
Pressure 1 to 5 bar
Removes macromolecules proteins"] end subgraph nano["NANOFILTRATION"] NF["Pore 0.5 to 2 nm
Pressure 5 to 40 bar
Removes multivalent ions and dyes"] end subgraph reverse["REVERSE OSMOSIS"] RO["Pore less than 0.5 nm
Pressure 15 to 80 bar
Removes monovalent ions"] end MF --> UF --> NF --> RO style MF fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000 style UF fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style NF fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 style RO fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 NF Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Setup The block diagram below shows a typical **cross-flow NF system** as deployed in water-softening or dye-recovery plants. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Tank] --> B[High Pressure Pump] B --> C[NF Membrane Module] C --> D[Retentate Recycle Loop] D --> A C --> E[Permeate Collection Tank] style A fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#f6b26b,color:#000 style C fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style D fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 style E fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` **Component Functions (for KTU Board Answers):** - **Feed Tank:** Holds the raw solution with multivalent ions and target solutes. - **High-Pressure Pump:** Provides the 5–40 bar transmembrane pressure required for NF. Operates at lower pressure than RO pumps. - **NF Membrane Module:** Contains spiral-wound or hollow-fiber membranes with negatively charged surfaces (typical: polyamide thin-film composite). - **Retentate Recycle Loop:** Returns the unpermeated, ion-rich stream to the feed tank, improving overall recovery and reducing waste. - **Permeate Collection Tank:** Stores the softened/purified water (low hardness, low dye content). ## 4.4 Donnan Exclusion Mechanism — Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB feedside["FEED SIDE
High anion concentration
Cation passes preferentially"] --> membraneCharged["NF MEMBRANE
Fixed negative charge on surface"] membraneCharged --> repulsed["REPELLED
Co ions like SO4 2- and Cl-
High rejection"] membraneCharged --> allowed["PASSES
Counter ions like Na+
Low rejection"] membraneCharged --> neutral["PASSES NEUTRALLY
Uncharged solutes like glucose
Depending on size"] style feedside fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1a73e8,color:#000 style membraneCharged fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#f6b26b,color:#000 style repulsed fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style allowed fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style neutral fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Define Nanofiltration. State any two distinguishing characteristics that place it between Ultrafiltration and Reverse Osmosis.** **Model Answer:** > **Nanofiltration (NF)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process with nominal pore diameters of **0.5–2 nm** and a molecular weight cut-off of **200–1000 Da**, operating at **5–40 bar** transmembrane pressure. Two distinguishing characteristics: 1. **Intermediate MWCO:** UF retains > 1000 Da, RO retains < 200 Da; NF selectively retains the **200–1000 Da** band, including low-molecular-weight organics and multivalent salts. 2. **Dual-Mode Separation:** Unlike UF (purely size-based) and RO (purely solution-diffusion), NF combines **steric sieving** with **Donnan (charge) exclusion**, allowing preferential rejection of multivalent ions over monovalent ones. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Explain Donnan exclusion with reference to NF membrane separation.** **Model Answer:** > **Donnan exclusion** is the electrostatic phenomenon by which a charged membrane repels ions carrying the same charge as its fixed surface groups, while permitting counter-ions to pass (partially). For typical NF membranes with **fixed negative surface charge** (e.g., carboxylate –COO⁻ groups): - **Co-ions** (anions like Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻) are electrostatically repelled → **high rejection**. - **Counter-ions** (cations like Na⁺, Ca²⁺) are partially retained to maintain electroneutrality → **partial rejection**. - This is why NF selectively removes **divalent and trivalent ions** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻) more effectively than monovalent ones, making it ideal for **water softening** without removing all dissolved salts. --- ## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) ### INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B --- ### ⭐ Question A `[KTU ESE - December 2023 Equivalent - 14 Marks]` **(a)** Derive the **Kedem–Katchalsky equation** for volumetric flux through an NF membrane. Discuss the physical significance of the reflection coefficient $\sigma$. **(b)** The following data were obtained for an NF membrane operated at 25 °C: | Feed concentration of MgSO₄ | Permeate concentration of MgSO₄ | Transmembrane pressure | |---|---|---| | 2000 mg/L | 80 mg/L | 15 bar | If the osmotic pressure of MgSO₄ follows $\pi = i C R T$ with van't Hoff factor $i = 2$, calculate: 1. The **observed rejection** $R_{obs}$. 2. The **osmotic pressure difference** $\Delta \pi$ across the membrane. 3. The **effective net driving pressure** if the reflection coefficient $\sigma = 0.85$. --- ### Model Answer (a) — [7 Marks] **Derivation:** From non-equilibrium thermodynamics, two coupled linear equations govern the flux of solvent ($J_v$) and solute ($J_s$): $$ J_v = L_p \Delta P - \sigma L_p \Delta \pi $$ $$ J_s = \bar{C}(1-\sigma) J_v - P_s \dfrac{dC}{dx} $$ **Combining and simplifying:** $$ \boxed{J_v = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi)} $$ **Physical significance of $\sigma$ (Staverman reflection coefficient):** - $\sigma = 1$ → membrane is perfectly selective (ideal RO); full osmotic pressure opposes flow. - $\sigma = 0$ → membrane has no selectivity (UF-like); solute passes freely; $\Delta \pi$ has no effect. - $0 < \sigma < 1$ → characteristic of NF; partial reflection of solutes. The reflection coefficient is **also linked to the size-sieving capability** via the Ferry equation: $$ \sigma = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{r_s}{r_p}\right)^2 $$ Thus, $\sigma$ serves as a **quantitative measure of membrane selectivity** and is central to the Spiegler–Kedem intrinsic rejection equation: $$ R = \dfrac{\sigma (1-F)}{1 - \sigma F}, \quad F = \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{J_v (1-\sigma)}{P_s}\right] $$ --- ### Model Answer (b) — [7 Marks] **Given:** $C_f = 2000 \ \text{mg/L}$, $C_p = 80 \ \text{mg/L}$, $\Delta P = 15 \ \text{bar}$, $i = 2$, $T = 298 \ \text{K}$, $R = 0.08314 \ \text{L·bar·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$, $\sigma = 0.85$. **Step 1: Observed Rejection $R_{obs}$** $$ R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \dfrac{80}{2000} = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 $$ **$R_{obs} = 96\%$** (excellent divalent salt rejection, characteristic of NF) **Step 2: Osmotic Pressure Difference $\Delta \pi$** Convert feed concentration to molarity: - Molar mass of MgSO₄ = 24.3 + 32 + 64 = **120.3 g/mol** - $C_f = 2000 \ \text{mg/L} = 2.0 \ \text{g/L} = \dfrac{2.0}{120.3} = 0.01663 \ \text{mol/L} = 0.01663 \ \text{M}$ - $C_p = 80 \ \text{mg/L} = 0.08 \ \text{g/L} = 6.65 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{M}$ Osmotic pressures (using $\pi = i C R T$): $$ \pi_f = 2 \times 0.01663 \times 0.08314 \times 298 = 0.824 \ \text{bar} $$ $$ \pi_p = 2 \times 6.65 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.08314 \times 298 = 0.0329 \ \text{bar} $$ $$ \Delta \pi = \pi_f - \pi_p = 0.824 - 0.0329 = 0.791 \ \text{bar} $$ **Step 3: Net Effective Driving Pressure** $$ \Delta P_{\text{eff}} = \Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi = 15 - (0.85)(0.791) = 15 - 0.672 = 14.33 \ \text{bar} $$ **Conclusion:** Out of 15 bar applied, only **14.33 bar** is the net effective driving pressure for solvent flux; 0.672 bar is "consumed" by the partial osmotic effect. --- ### ⭐ Question B `[KTU ESE - May 2024 Equivalent - 14 Marks]` *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)* **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **mechanism of nanofiltration**. Differentiate between the **solution-diffusion model** and the **pore-flow model** for NF membranes. **(b)** An NF membrane has an average pore radius $r_p = 1.5 \ \text{nm}$, surface porosity $\varepsilon = 0.05$, membrane thickness $\Delta x = 0.5 \ \mu\text{m}$, and tortuosity $\tau = 1.3$. If the viscosity of water is $\eta = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{Pa·s}$ and applied pressure is $\Delta P = 20 \ \text{bar}$, calculate: 1. The **hydraulic permeability** $L_p$. 2. The **pure water flux** $J$. --- ### Model Answer (a) — [7 Marks] **Mechanism of NF:** NF membranes separate solutes via **three simultaneous mechanisms**: 1. **Steric (size) sieving:** Solutes larger than the membrane's nanometer pores are physically excluded. Dominant for uncharged species like sugars, dyes, and antibiotics (MW 200–1000 Da). 2. **Donnan (electrostatic) exclusion:** The membrane's fixed negative charge repels co-ions (e.g., SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻) and indirectly retains counter-ions to maintain electroneutrality. Dominant for ionic species, especially multivalent ones. 3. **Dielectric exclusion:** Inside nanopores, the solvent's reduced dielectric constant increases ion-solvation energy, creating an additional barrier against ion permeation. **Difference between Solution-Diffusion and Pore-Flow Models:** | Feature | **Solution-Diffusion Model** | **Pore-Flow Model** | |---|---|---| | Membrane structure | Dense, non-porous (applies to RO) | Porous with discrete pores (applies to NF, UF) | | Solvent transport | Diffuses through membrane matrix under chemical potential gradient | Pressure-driven viscous flow through pores (Hagen–Poiseuille) | | Solute transport | Dissolves in membrane, then diffuses | Convection + diffusion within pore | | Flux equation | $J_v = \dfrac{P_w}{\Delta x}(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | $J_v = \dfrac{\varepsilon r_p^2 \Delta P}{8 \eta \tau \Delta x}$ | | Applicable to | RO (and partially NF dense skin) | NF, UF, MF | | Reflection coeff. $\sigma$ | Implicit (≈1) | Explicit (Ferry equation) | **NF best fits the Pore-Flow Model** because of its nanoporous structure, but hybrid models (Solution-Diffusion with Imperfection) are also used for the dense top layer. --- ### Model Answer (b) — [7 Marks] **Given:** $r_p = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}$, $\varepsilon = 0.05$, $\Delta x = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}$, $\tau = 1.3$, $\eta = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{Pa·s}$, $\Delta P = 20 \ \text{bar} = 20 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa}$. **Step 1: Hydraulic Permeability $L_p$** $$ L_p = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot r_p^2}{8 \eta \tau \Delta x} $$ $$ L_p = \dfrac{0.05 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-9})^2}{8 \times (8.9 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1.3 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6})} $$ Compute numerator: $$ 0.05 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-18} = 1.125 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{m}^2 $$ Compute denominator: $$ 8 \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.628 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ L_p = \dfrac{1.125 \times 10^{-19}}{4.628 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.43 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1} $$ **Step 2: Pure Water Flux $J$** $$ J = L_p \cdot \Delta P = 2.43 \times 10^{-11} \times 20 \times 10^5 = 4.86 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} $$ Convert to LMH (liters per m² per hour): $$ J = 4.86 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 175 \ \text{L} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1} $$ > **Verification:** This flux (175 LMH) is higher than typical NF operating flux (10–50 LMH), indicating that the assumed pore radius of 1.5 nm corresponds to the upper end of the NF range (closer to loose UF). At higher pressures, NF flux is typically lower due to denser top-layer resistance. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU NF questions:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Flux Calculations:** Always convert $\Delta P$ from bar to Pa (×10⁵) before computing $L_p$. Mixing bar and Pa is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers. > 2. **Confusing $R_{obs}$ vs. $R$:** $R_{obs}$ uses **bulk** feed concentration, while $R$ (intrinsic) uses **membrane surface** concentration (which is higher due to concentration polarization). KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 3. **Van't Hoff Factor $i$:** Forgetting the $i$ factor for $\pi$ of MgSO₄ or CaCl₂ (both dissociate into 2 or 3 ions) leads to wrong $\Delta \pi$. > 4. **Pore Radius vs. Pore Diameter:** When stating the NF range, students often write "0.5–2 nm pore size" ambiguously. Always specify **"pore radius" or "pore diameter"** to avoid losing 1 mark. > 5. **Identifying Mechanism:** Saying "NF rejects everything" is wrong. Always clarify: NF **partially** passes monovalent ions and **strongly** rejects multivalent ions and organics above MWCO. > 6. **Staverman Coefficient Boundary:** $\sigma = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$ limits must be stated explicitly when deriving the Kedem–Katchalsky equation. > 7. **Cross-flow vs. Dead-end:** NF is almost always operated in **cross-flow mode** to mitigate concentration polarization. State this in long-answer diagrams. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **NF pore radius:** 0.5 – 2 nm | **MWCO:** 200 – 1000 Da | **Pressure:** 5 – 40 bar. - [x] NF is **between UF and RO** — often called "loose RO" or "low-pressure RO". - [x] **Three mechanisms:** Steric sieving + Donnan exclusion + Dielectric exclusion. - [x] **Selective rejection:** Multivalent ions >> Monovalent ions (NF signature property). - [x] **Reflection coefficient $\sigma$:** $0 \le \sigma \le 1$; $\sigma = 1$ for perfect RO, $\sigma = 0$ for UF-like. - [x] **Ferry equation:** $\sigma = 1 - (1 - r_s/r_p)^2$ — KTU board favorite. - [x] **Kedem–Katchalsky:** $J_v = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi)$ — must-know flux equation. - [x] **Spiegler–Kedem rejection:** $R = \sigma(1-F)/(1-\sigma F)$ with $F = \exp[-J_v(1-\sigma)/P_s]$. - [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille (pore-flow):** $J = \varepsilon r_p^2 \Delta P / (8 \eta \tau \Delta x)$. - [x] **Donnan equilibrium:** $\ln(\phi_i^m/\phi_i^s) = -(z_i F/RT)(\psi_m - \psi_s)$ — relates ion partition to membrane potential. - [x] **Applications:** Water softening, dye recovery, lactose–mineral separation in dairy, antibiotic concentration, juice clarification. - [x] **Typical flux:** 10–50 LMH (lower than UF, higher than RO). - [x] **Membrane material:** Polyamide thin-film composite (TFC), with negatively charged carboxylate surface. - [x] **Operation mode:** Cross-flow (to reduce concentration polarization). - [x] **Concentration polarization** increases $C_m$ above $C_f$, reducing observed rejection. - [x] **Observed rejection:** $R_{obs} = 1 - C_p/C_f$ — directly measurable. - [x] **Intrinsic rejection:** $R = 1 - C_p/C_m$ — reflects true membrane selectivity.

topics.title: Ultrafiltration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ultrafiltration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Ultrafiltration (UF)** is a *barrier-separation* (membrane) process that uses a finely porous, typically asymmetric, polymeric (or ceramic) membrane to **physically exclude** macromolecules, colloids, proteins, viruses, emulsified oils, and suspended particulates from a liquid solvent (usually water) under a moderate hydrostatic pressure differential. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology, UF occupies the **intermediate pore-size window** between microfiltration (MF) and nanofiltration (NF), retaining species in the approximate size range of **1 nm to 100 nm** (or molecular weight cut-off, MWCO ≈ **1,000 – 500,000 Da**). | Membrane Process | Pore Size (nm) | MWCO (Da) | Operating ΔP (bar) | |---|---|---|---| | Microfiltration (MF) | 100 – 10,000 | > 500,000 | 0.1 – 2 | | **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | **1 – 100** | **1,000 – 500,000** | **0.5 – 10** | | Nanofiltration (NF) | 0.5 – 5 | 200 – 1,000 | 5 – 35 | | Reverse Osmosis (RO) | < 0.5 | < 200 | 10 – 80 | > [!NOTE] > **Sieve-sorting mechanism:** UF is fundamentally a *size-exclusion (sieving)* process, NOT a solution-diffusion process. The membrane acts like a molecular sieve. No phase change occurs, making UF energetically economical. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Student Intuition Imagine a **kitchen colander** straining cooked pasta from boiling water. The water (small molecules) rushes through the holes, while the pasta (larger particles) stays behind. Ultrafiltration is the **nano-scale, engineered cousin** of this colander. A better analogy for a chemical engineer: think of UF as the **"bouncer at a club door."** The bouncer (membrane) checks the size of every molecule approaching. Tiny solvent molecules (water) get a stamp and pass through; large molecules (proteins, colloids) are politely rejected and remain in the upstream side (retentate). The nightclub has only **one-way traffic** — the permeate exits, the retentate stays. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition Point:** In UF, the solvent moves toward the membrane due to a **pressure gradient**, not a concentration gradient (unlike dialysis). Pressure is the *driving force*. The retained species pile up at the membrane surface, creating a phenomenon called **concentration polarization** — a critical concept we will dissect in Section 2. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Favourites) The following bolded metrics will appear repeatedly in KTU exam questions and *must* be memorized: - **Permeate Flux (J):** Volumetric flow rate of permeate per unit membrane area, units **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** or **m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹**. - **Trans-Membrane Pressure (TMP or ΔP):** The net pressure pushing solvent through the membrane. - **Rejection Coefficient (R):** Fraction of solute retained by the membrane, ranging from 0 (no rejection) to 1 (complete rejection). - **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO):** The molecular weight at which the membrane rejects ≥ **90 %** of a standard reference solute. - **Recovery (Y or Rec):** Fraction of feed converted to permeate. - **Concentration Polarization:** Buildup of retained solute at the membrane surface, forming a *gel layer* when concentrated beyond the gel concentration Cg. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a UF Membrane UF membranes are predominantly manufactured as **asymmetric (anisotropic) membranes** consisting of two integrated layers: 1. **Thin, dense top "skin" layer (0.1 – 1 µm):** Contains the actual pore structure responsible for selectivity. This is where separation occurs. 2. **Thick, porous sub-layer (50 – 200 µm):** A sponge-like support providing mechanical strength and negligible hydraulic resistance. > [!NOTE] > **Why asymmetric?** A thin skin gives high flux (∝ 1/thickness), while the porous sublayer provides strength without adding resistance — a clever engineering trade-off pioneered by Loeb-Sourirajan (1960). **Common UF Membrane Materials (Board Favourite):** - **Polymeric:** Polysulfone (PS), Polyethersulfone (PES), Cellulose Acetate (CA), Polyacrylonitrile (PAN), Regenerated Cellulose. - **Ceramic:** Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), Titania (TiO₂) on carbon support — used for harsh pH/temperature/chemical environments. - **Composite:** Thin film composite (TFC) membranes. ## 2.2 Operational Modes of UF UF is conducted in two principal modes: | Mode | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage | |---|---|---|---| | **Dead-end (Static)** | Feed flows perpendicular to membrane; ALL flux passes through | Simple, high flux (initially), low capital | Severe fouling, batch operation | | **Cross-flow (Tangential)** | Feed flows parallel to membrane surface; permeate passes transversely | Continuous, self-cleaning, lower fouling | Lower flux, higher pumping cost | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Industrial UF is almost universally **cross-flow** because continuous operation and lower fouling outweigh the pumping cost penalty. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates **every critical equation** required for solving UF problems. Note: All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` for markdown compatibility. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$ | Permeate flux (volumetric flux) | m/s or LMH | | 2 | $\Delta P_{TM} = \dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_p$ | Trans-membrane pressure (cross-flow) | Pa or bar | | 3 | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | Observed rejection coefficient | dimensionless | | 4 | $R_{true} = \dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_m}$ | True (intrinsic) rejection at membrane surface | dimensionless | | 5 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{tot}}$ | Resistance-in-Series (Darcy form) | m/s | | 6 | $R_{tot} = R_m + R_{cp} + R_{f}$ | Membrane + cake + fouling resistance | m⁻¹ | | 7 | $J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$ | **Gel-polarization model** (limiting flux) | m/s | | 8 | $Y = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} = 1 - \dfrac{C_f}{C_{ret}}$ | Volumetric recovery / conversion | dimensionless | | 9 | $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3} \, d_p^{2} \, \Delta P}{180 \, \mu \, (1-\varepsilon)^{2} \, \Delta x}$ | Kozeny-Carman (for cake layer) | m/s | | 10 | $C_{ret} = \dfrac{C_f}{1 - Y}$ | Retentate concentration via mass balance | kg/m³ | | 11 | $k = 0.023 \, \dfrac{D^{0.67} \, v^{0.8}}{d_h^{0.2}} \left(\dfrac{\rho}{\mu}\right)^{0.47}$ | Mass transfer coefficient (Dittus-Boelter-like, turbulent) | m/s | | 12 | $\gamma = \dfrac{C_{ret}}{C_f}$ | Concentration ratio (volumetric concentration factor) | dimensionless | **Where:** - $J$ = permeate flux; $V_p$ = permeate volume; $A_m$ = membrane area; $t$ = time - $\Delta P_{TM}$ = TMP; $P_{f,in}$, $P_{f,out}$ = feed inlet/outlet pressures; $P_p$ = permeate pressure - $R_{obs}$, $R_{true}$ = observed/intrinsic rejection; $C_f$, $C_p$, $C_m$, $C_b$ = feed, permeate, membrane-surface, bulk retentate concentrations - $\mu$ = viscosity of permeate; $R_m$, $R_{cp}$, $R_f$ = membrane, concentration-polarization, fouling resistances - $k$ = mass transfer coefficient; $C_g$ = gel concentration; $D$ = solute diffusivity; $v$ = cross-flow velocity; $d_h$ = hydraulic diameter - $\varepsilon$ = cake porosity; $d_p$ = particle diameter; $\Delta x$ = cake thickness - $Y$ = recovery; $C_{ret}$ = final retentate concentration ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Equation **Equation 5 (Darcy form):** Analogous to Ohm's law ($I = V/R$). Flux is voltage, TMP is potential, and resistance opposes the flow. The flow is *viscous* (Darcy regime), not turbulent through the membrane. **Equation 7 (Gel polarization):** The keystone of UF design. As flux increases, retained solute accumulates at the membrane until its concentration reaches a gel value $C_g$ (a saturated, immobilized layer). At this point, further pressure increases do NOT increase flux — the gel layer imposes its own resistance. This gives a **limiting flux** independent of pressure. **Equation 11 (Mass transfer coefficient):** Higher cross-flow velocity → higher shear → thinner boundary layer → higher k → higher limiting flux. This is why industrial UF systems run at high cross-flow velocities (1–6 m/s). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Ultrafiltration is industrially indispensable across: - **Water & Wastewater Treatment:** Drinking water clarification, greywater reuse, removal of turbidity, pathogens, and macromolecular organics. - **Biotechnology / Pharmaceuticals:** Protein concentration and purification, vaccine manufacturing, enzyme recovery, blood plasma fractionation. - **Food & Dairy:** Whey protein concentration, milk standardization, clarification of fruit juices and wines, sugar refining. - **Paints & Coatings:** Electrocoat paint recovery in automotive industry (a classical UF success story — Dorr-Oliver, 1960s onward). - **Oil-Water Emulsification:** Breaking emulsified cutting oils and bilge water. - **Semiconductor Industry:** Ultra-pure water (UPW) preparation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The derivations below are **board-exam style**, expanded step-by-step so that every algebraic transition is fully justified. No skipped steps, no "the rest follows" — every line is mathematically traceable. ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resistance-in-Series Model from Darcy's Law ### Starting Point: Darcy's Law for Flow Through a Porous Medium For a viscous, incompressible fluid (permeate) flowing through a porous membrane, the volumetric flux is proportional to the applied pressure drop and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity and the total hydraulic resistance. $$ J = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, R_{tot}} $$ ### Step 1: Decompose the Total Resistance In a UF system, three distinct resistances act in **series** along the permeate pathway: 1. $R_m$ → intrinsic resistance of the clean membrane (pore geometry, skin thickness). 2. $R_{cp}$ → resistance of the *concentration-polarization boundary layer* (viscous gel/solute-rich layer near the membrane surface). 3. $R_f$ → resistance of *irreversible fouling* (solute adsorption within pores, pore plugging, biofilm formation). Because these layers are stacked in series, by the analogy of electrical resistors, the total resistance is the **arithmetic sum**: $$ R_{tot} = R_m + R_{cp} + R_{f} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute Back into Darcy's Equation $$ J = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, \left(R_m + R_{cp} + R_{f}\right)} $$ ### Step 3: Isolate the Contribution of Each Resistance For a **clean membrane** (no polarization, no fouling), $R_{cp} = R_f = 0$: $$ J_{clean} = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, R_m} $$ Solving for $R_m$: $$ R_m = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, J_{clean}} $$ ### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation - $R_m$ is a **constant** for a given membrane (depends only on pore structure). - $R_{cp}$ grows with flux and time during operation (reversible — disappears when operation stops). - $R_f$ grows **monotonically and irreversibly** — this is why membrane cleaning (CIP, "clean-in-place") is essential. > **Board Tip:** If a question provides $J$ at two time-points, you can solve for $R_f$ growth by subtracting the two flux equations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Gel-Polarization Model (Limiting Flux) This is the **most critical derivation** for KTU UF questions. It links flux, mass transfer, and gel concentration. ### Step 1: Set Up a Steady-State Solute Mass Balance on a Differential Slice Consider a thin slice of the concentration-polarization boundary layer near the membrane surface, of thickness $dx$. Solute is brought to the membrane by **convective flux** $J \cdot C$ (where $C$ is local concentration) and is removed by **back-diffusion** $D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ (Fick's law). At **steady state** (no accumulation): $$ J \cdot C(x) - D \cdot \frac{dC(x)}{dx} = J \cdot C_p $$ ### Step 2: Rearrange into Separable ODE Form Move all $C$ terms to one side: $$ J \, \bigl(C - C_p\bigr) = D \, \frac{dC}{dx} $$ Separating variables: $$ \frac{dC}{C - C_p} = \frac{J}{D} \, dx $$ ### Step 3: Integrate Across the Boundary Layer Integrate from the **bulk** ($x = 0$, $C = C_b$) to the **membrane surface** ($x = \delta$, $C = C_m$): $$ \int_{C_b}^{C_m} \frac{dC}{C - C_p} = \frac{J}{D} \int_{0}^{\delta} dx $$ Left-hand side integral (logarithmic): $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right) = \frac{J \, \delta}{D} $$ ### Step 4: Introduce the Mass Transfer Coefficient By definition, the mass transfer coefficient is $k = D / \delta$. Substituting: $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right) = \frac{J}{k} $$ ### Step 5: Apply the Gel-Layer Assumption When flux is high, the solute concentration at the membrane surface reaches a maximum gel value $C_m \to C_g$. Also, $C_g \gg C_p$ (gel layer is essentially impermeable to the solute), so $(C_m - C_p) \to C_g$ and $(C_b - C_p) \to C_b$ (assuming $C_p \ll C_b$, common for macromolecular UF). ### Step 6: Solve for the Limiting Flux $J_{lim}$ $$ J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right) $$ This is the **gel-polarization equation** — the cornerstone of UF design. > **Board Tip:** This shows that **increasing TMP does NOT increase flux** once the gel layer is formed. Only increasing $k$ (via cross-flow velocity) or decreasing $C_b$ (via diafiltration) raises $J_{lim}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: True Rejection from Observed Rejection Many students mistakenly use $R_{obs}$ directly. KTU examiners love testing the distinction. ### Step 1: Definition of Observed Rejection $$ R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} $$ This compares permeate concentration to the **bulk feed**, ignoring concentration polarization. ### Step 2: True (Intrinsic) Rejection at the Membrane Surface By definition: $$ R_{true} = \frac{C_m - C_p}{C_m} $$ ### Step 3: Link via the Gel-Polarization Equation Recall: $$ \frac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\!\left(\frac{J}{k}\right) $$ If $C_p \ll C_b$ (typical for UF of macromolecules): $$ \frac{C_m}{C_b} \approx \exp\!\left(\frac{J}{k}\right) $$ Hence: $$ C_m \approx C_b \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{J}{k}\right) $$ ### Step 4: Compute True Rejection in Terms of Measurable Quantities $$ R_{true} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_m} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_b \cdot \exp(J/k)} = 1 - R_{obs} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{J}{k}\right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** Because $C_m > C_b$ in UF (concentration polarization), we always have $\boxed{R_{true} > R_{obs}}$. This means **the membrane actually rejects more than what bulk measurements suggest**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Recovery vs. Retentate Concentration (Mass Balance) A common KTU numerical: given feed concentration and desired retentate concentration, find recovery. ### Step 1: Component Mass Balance (Solute) Assume negligible solute in permeate (high rejection, $C_p \approx 0$). Then **all solute entering** stays in the **retentate**: $$ C_f \cdot V_f = C_{ret} \cdot V_{ret} $$ where $V_{ret}$ is the final retentate volume. ### Step 2: Overall Volumetric Balance $$ V_f = V_p + V_{ret} $$ ### Step 3: Express Recovery $Y$ By definition: $$ Y = \frac{V_p}{V_f} $$ Hence $V_{ret} = V_f(1 - Y)$. ### Step 4: Combine to Obtain Retentate Concentration $$ C_f \cdot V_f = C_{ret} \cdot V_f (1 - Y) $$ Dividing both sides by $V_f$: $$ C_f = C_{ret} \cdot (1 - Y) $$ ### Step 5: Solve for $Y$ (or for $C_{ret}$) $$ Y = 1 - \frac{C_f}{C_{ret}} $$ $$ C_{ret} = \frac{C_f}{1 - Y} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A UF unit processes $V_f = 1000$ L of feed containing $C_f = 2$ g/L protein. Recovery is $Y = 0.80$. Find $V_p$, $V_{ret}$, and $C_{ret}$. **Solution:** - $V_p = Y \cdot V_f = 0.80 \times 1000 = 800$ L - $V_{ret} = V_f - V_p = 200$ L - $C_{ret} = \dfrac{2}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{2}{0.20} = 10$ g/L **Verification (mass balance):** $2 \times 1000 = 10 \times 200 = 2000$ g ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams below adhere strictly to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguarding rules: purely alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown formatting inside quoted labels, and no reserved keywords as standalone IDs. ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: UF Cross-Flow Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FEED TANK
CF VF] --> B[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP
PIN POUT] B --> C[CROSS FLOW MEMBRANE MODULE
SKIN LAYER PORES 1 TO 100 NM] C --> D[RETENTATE RECYCLE LOOP
CRET HIGH] D --> B C --> E[PERMEATE COLLECTION
CP LOW] E --> F[PERMEATE STORAGE TANK
VP] G[CONCENTRATION POLARIZATION LAYER
CM INCREASING TO CG] -.-> C H[FOULING DEPOSITION
RF IRREVERSIBLE] -.-> C ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **Feed Tank** holds the input solution. - A **High-Pressure Pump** raises the feed to the operating TMP (typically 1–10 bar). - The **Cross-Flow Membrane Module** performs the sieving. Two streams exit: - **Retentate** (rejected solute + remaining solvent) → recycled or drawn off. - **Permeate** (purified solvent + small molecules) → collected. - The dashed arrows highlight **resistance phenomena**: concentration polarization and fouling, both of which diminish flux. ## 4.2 Block Architecture: Gel-Polarization Boundary Layer ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph BL["CONCENTRATION POLARIZATION BOUNDARY LAYER"] direction TB X1[BULK RETENTATE CB LOW] --> X2[DIFFUSIVE LAYER GRADIENT DDC GREATER THAN 0] X2 --> X3[MEMBRANE SURFACE CM MAX] X3 --> X4[GEL LAYER CG SATURATION LIMIT] X4 --> X5[MEMBRANE SKIN PORES ACTUAL SEPARATION] end X5 --> X6[PERMEATE SIDE CP TYPICALLY ZERO] X1 -. CONVECTIVE FLUX J .-> X4 X4 -. BACK DIFFUSION D DC BY DX .-> X1 ``` **Interpretation:** - Concentration rises from $C_b$ (bulk) through a diffusive gradient to a maximum $C_m$ at the membrane surface. - When $C_m$ reaches $C_g$, a **gel layer** forms. Beyond this, increasing TMP does not increase flux. - The convective flux $J$ drives solute toward the membrane; back-diffusion $D(dC/dx)$ opposes it. Steady-state equilibrium yields $J = k \cdot \ln(C_g/C_b)$. ## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting Operating Conditions in UF ```mermaid flowchart TD S1{WHAT IS THE
DESIRED OUTPUT?} -->|High purity permeate| S2[OPERATE BELOW LIMITING FLUX] S1 -->|High concentration retentate| S3[OPERATE NEAR LIMITING FLUX] S2 --> S4[LOW TMP
LOW CROSS FLOW VELOCITY] S3 --> S5[HIGH TMP
HIGH CROSS FLOW VELOCITY] S4 --> S6[RECOMMENDATION DEAD END MODE BATCH] S5 --> S7[RECOMMENDATION CROSS FLOW MODE CONTINUOUS] S6 --> S8[MONITOR RF OVER TIME] S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9[PERIODIC CIP CLEANING] ``` **Interpretation:** - For **permeate quality**, mild conditions avoid fouling. - For **retentate concentration** (e.g., protein concentration), push toward limiting flux. - Cross-flow + periodic CIP ("clean-in-place") is the industrial standard. ## 4.4 Membrane Module Configuration Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD M[UF MEMBRANE MODULE TYPES] --> M1[FLAT SHEET
LAB AND PILOT SCALE] M --> M2[SPIRAL WOUND
HIGH PACKING DENSITY] M --> M3[HOLLOW FIBER
HIGHEST SURFACE AREA] M --> M4[TUBULAR
HIGH FOULING STREAMS] M1 --> M1A[LOWEST SURFACE AREA
EASY TO CLEAN] M2 --> M2A[HIGH PACKING
PRONE TO FOULING] M3 --> M3A[INTERNAL OR EXTERNAL SKINNED
BACKWASHABLE] M4 --> M4A[HIGHEST CROSS FLOW VELOCITY
EXPENSIVE] ``` **Reading guide:** This hierarchy helps students recall **module selection** — KTU often asks for module comparison. Hollow fiber dominates biotech; tubular dominates high-fouling industrial streams. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define Ultrafiltration. List any two industrial applications of UF.** **Model Answer:** **Ultrafiltration (UF)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process employing asymmetric membranes with pore sizes in the range **1 – 100 nm** (MWCO: 1,000 – 500,000 Da) to selectively retain macromolecules, colloids, proteins, and suspended solids while allowing solvent and low-molecular-weight solutes to pass through. The driving force is **trans-membrane pressure** (typically **0.5 – 10 bar**), and separation occurs via a **size-exclusion (sieving) mechanism**. **Two Industrial Applications:** 1. **Dairy industry:** Concentration and fractionation of whey proteins from cheese-making effluent. 2. **Pharmaceutical industry:** Concentration and purification of proteins, enzymes, and vaccines from fermentation broths. *(Other acceptable answers: Drinking water clarification, electrocoat paint recovery in automotive industry, fruit juice clarification.)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: What is concentration polarization? How does it affect the performance of a UF membrane?** **Model Answer:** **Concentration Polarization** is the accumulation of retained solute particles at the upstream surface of the membrane, forming a layer of higher concentration than that of the bulk feed solution. It occurs because solvent (permeate) flows toward the membrane convectively, dragging solute with it; solute cannot pass through and is partially re-entrained into the bulk by back-diffusion. **Effects on UF Performance:** 1. **Reduced flux:** The polarized layer (or gel layer, if $C_m \to C_g$) adds hydraulic resistance, lowering permeate flux for a given TMP. 2. **Lower observed rejection:** Since permeate solute concentration $C_p$ is compared to bulk $C_b$ rather than to $C_m$, the observed rejection $R_{obs}$ underestimates the true membrane capability. 3. **Increased fouling propensity:** The polarized layer can precipitate, crystallize, or adsorb onto the membrane, accelerating irreversible fouling. 4. **Limiting flux behavior:** Beyond a critical TMP, flux plateaus at $J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln(C_g / C_b)$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question B(A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **gel-polarization model** for ultrafiltration, clearly stating the assumptions involved. Using this model, explain why flux becomes independent of trans-membrane pressure at high TMP values. **(b)** [7 Marks] A UF unit is used to concentrate a protein solution. Feed: $C_f = 5$ g/L, $V_f = 500$ L. The membrane has a gel concentration $C_g = 200$ g/L. The mass transfer coefficient is $k = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. Determine the **limiting flux** and the **recovery** needed to reach a retentate concentration of $C_{ret} = 50$ g/L. --- ### Model Solution to B(A)(a) **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state operation. 2. One-dimensional transport perpendicular to the membrane. 3. Constant physical properties ($D$, $\rho$, $\mu$) across the boundary layer. 4. No chemical reaction within the boundary layer. 5. Solute rejection is high enough that $C_p \ll C_b$ and $C_p \ll C_m$. 6. The boundary layer is well-mixed on the bulk side. **Derivation:** Consider a steady-state solute mass balance over a differential element $dx$ of the polarization layer: $$ J \cdot C - D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx} = J \cdot C_p $$ Rearrange: $$ J(C - C_p) \, dx = D \, dC $$ Separate variables and integrate from bulk ($x = 0$, $C = C_b$) to membrane surface ($x = \delta$, $C = C_m$): $$ J \int_{0}^{\delta} dx = D \int_{C_b}^{C_m} \frac{dC}{C - C_p} $$ $$ J \cdot \delta = D \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right) $$ Introduce the mass transfer coefficient $k = D / \delta$: $$ J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right) $$ Apply the **gel-layer assumption**: at high flux, $C_m \to C_g$, and since $C_g \gg C_p$: $$ J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right) $$ **Why flux becomes TMP-independent at high TMP:** Once the gel layer forms ($C_m = C_g$), the polarization layer itself is the bottleneck. Any further increase in TMP compresses the gel layer marginally (or not at all), and the additional driving force is dissipated by the viscous resistance of the gel. The flux plateaus at $J_{lim}$, which depends only on **hydrodynamics** ($k$) and **bulk concentration** ($C_b$), not on TMP. --- ### Model Solution to B(A)(b) **Step 1: Compute recovery from retentate concentration** $$ Y = 1 - \frac{C_f}{C_{ret}} = 1 - \frac{5}{50} = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90 \;\;\text{(or 90 %)} $$ **Step 2: Compute permeate volume** $$ V_p = Y \cdot V_f = 0.90 \times 500 = 450 \text{ L} $$ **Step 3: Compute limiting flux** We need the **bulk retentate concentration at the operating point**. For an instantaneous snapshot, we approximate $C_b \approx C_{ret} = 50$ g/L: $$ J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right) = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{200}{50}\right) $$ $$ J_{lim} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times \ln(4) = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.3863 $$ $$ \boxed{J_{lim} = 2.77 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = 99.8 \text{ LMH}} $$ **Verification of mass balance:** - Solute in feed: $5 \times 500 = 2500$ g - Solute in retentate: $50 \times (500 - 450) = 50 \times 50 = 2500$ g ✓ --- ### Question B(B) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the **resistance-in-series model** for UF membrane fouling. Derive the flux equation and discuss how fouling resistance $R_f$ evolves during long-term operation. **(b)** [7 Marks] A UF membrane has the following characteristics: clean water flux $J_0 = 150$ LMH at TMP = 2 bar, viscosity $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. During operation with a protein feed, the steady-state flux drops to $J = 50$ LMH at the same TMP due to concentration polarization and fouling. Determine the **total resistance** and the **ratio** $(R_{cp} + R_f) / R_m$. --- ### Model Solution to B(B)(a) **Diagram (described in text since the schematic was given in Section 4):** Three layers in series perpendicular to the membrane: 1. **Concentration Polarization Layer** ($R_{cp}$, reversible, on the upstream side). 2. **Fouling Layer** ($R_f$, irreversible, partly within pores and on surface). 3. **Membrane Skin** ($R_m$, fixed, intrinsic). **Derivation:** The permeate is a viscous fluid passing through three resistances in series. By the Hagen-Poiseuille/Darcy analogy: $$ J = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, R_{tot}} = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, (R_m + R_{cp} + R_f)} $$ For a **clean membrane** ($R_{cp} = R_f = 0$): $$ J_0 = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, R_m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_m = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, J_0} $$ **Evolution of $R_f$:** - $R_f$ **increases monotonically** during operation due to: - Pore plugging by solutes. - Adsorption of macromolecules onto pore walls. - Biofilm formation (for biological feeds). - $R_f$ is **irreversible** — only chemical cleaning (CIP) or backwashing can partially restore flux. - $R_{cp}$ is **reversible** — disappears when operation stops or cross-flow velocity is increased. **Engineering implications:** - Long-term flux decline is governed by $R_f$. - Membrane life is limited by accumulation of $R_f$ beyond an economic threshold. - Periodic CIP is essential. --- ### Model Solution to B(B)(b) **Step 1: Compute intrinsic membrane resistance $R_m$** First, convert TMP and flux to SI units. - TMP: $\Delta P_{TM} = 2 \text{ bar} = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$ - Clean flux: $J_0 = 150 \text{ LMH} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 4.167 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s $$ R_m = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, J_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.167 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{4.167 \times 10^{-8}} $$ $$ \boxed{R_m = 4.80 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}} $$ **Step 2: Compute total resistance $R_{tot}$ at operating flux** - Operating flux: $J = 50 \text{ LMH} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 1.389 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s $$ R_{tot} = \frac{\Delta P_{TM}}{\mu \, J} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.389 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{1.389 \times 10^{-8}} $$ $$ \boxed{R_{tot} = 1.44 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-1}} $$ **Step 3: Compute $R_{cp} + R_f$** $$ R_{cp} + R_f = R_{tot} - R_m = 1.44 \times 10^{13} - 4.80 \times 10^{12} = 9.60 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1} $$ **Step 4: Compute the ratio** $$ \frac{R_{cp} + R_f}{R_m} = \frac{9.60 \times 10^{12}}{4.80 \times 10^{12}} = 2.0 $$ **Interpretation:** During operation, the **polarization-plus-fouling resistance is 2× the clean membrane resistance**, indicating substantial performance degradation. A CIP cycle is warranted. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Unit conversion blunder:** Forgetting to convert LMH → m/s is the **#1 reason students lose marks**. Always show the unit conversion explicitly. > 2. **Confusing $R_{obs}$ with $R_{true}$:** If asked for "rejection," clarify whether it is observed or intrinsic — examiners explicitly award or deduct based on this distinction. > 3. **Forgetting the gel assumption:** When deriving $J_{lim}$, students often stop at the unsimplified logarithmic form and forget to substitute $C_m = C_g$. This costs ~2 marks. > 4. **Missing assumptions:** A derivation without listed assumptions is incomplete — KTU 2024 rubric deducts 1–2 marks for unstated assumptions. > 5. **Mass-balance closure:** Always verify $C_f V_f = C_{ret} V_{ret} + C_p V_p$ at the end of any numerical. A non-closure indicates an arithmetic error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] **UF** is a pressure-driven, size-exclusion membrane process with pore sizes **1–100 nm**, MWCO **1,000–500,000 Da**, operating at TMP **0.5–10 bar**. - [x] **Asymmetric membranes** (thin skin + porous support) dominate UF design. - [x] Two operational modes: **dead-end** (simple, high fouling) and **cross-flow** (continuous, lower fouling — industrial standard). - [x] **Permeate flux** $J$ is measured in **LMH** or **m/s**. - [x] **Trans-membrane pressure** for cross-flow: $\Delta P_{TM} = (P_{in} + P_{out})/2 - P_p$. - [x] **Resistance-in-series:** $R_{tot} = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$. - [x] **Darcy form:** $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_{tot})$. - [x] **Gel-polarization equation:** $J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln(C_g / C_b)$. - [x] At high TMP, **flux plateaus** — pressure increases cannot overcome gel-layer resistance. - [x] **Rejection:** $R_{obs} = 1 - C_p/C_f$; **True:** $R_{true} = (C_m - C_p)/C_m$. - [x] **Concentration polarization** causes $C_m > C_b$, reducing observed rejection. - [x] **Recovery:** $Y = V_p / V_f = 1 - C_f / C_{ret}$. - [x] **Mass transfer coefficient** $k$ scales with $D^{0.67}$ and $v^{0.8}$ — increasing cross-flow velocity boosts $k$ and $J_{lim}$. - [x] **Common UF membranes:** Polysulfone (PS), Polyethersulfone (PES), Cellulose Acetate (CA), Ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂). - [x] **Module types:** Flat sheet (lab), Spiral wound (high packing), Hollow fiber (max area), Tubular (high-fouling streams). - [x] **Applications:** Water treatment, dairy whey, protein purification, electrocoat paint, fruit juice clarification, oil-water separation, biotech. - [x] **Fouling control:** Cross-flow, backwashing, periodic CIP, pretreatment, low TMP operation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Memory Rule:** For any KTU UF problem, always identify — (1) the **mode** (dead-end vs cross-flow), (2) the **driving force** (TMP), (3) the **dominant resistance** ($R_m$, $R_{cp}$, or $R_f$), and (4) the **rejection type** (observed vs true). Then apply the appropriate formula from the cheat sheet. This 4-point diagnostic is what examiners expect in every answer.

topics.title: Reverse Osmosis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reverse Osmosis

# Reverse Osmosis (RO) — A Complete KTU 2024 Study Module

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which a feed solution (typically an aqueous saline stream) is forced through a *semipermeable membrane* by applying a hydraulic pressure **in excess of the osmotic pressure difference** across the membrane. The membrane preferentially permits the passage of the **solvent (water)** while rejecting dissolved ionic and molecular solutes, thereby producing a purified **permeate** stream and a concentrated **retentate (brine)** stream.

In formal KTU terminology, RO belongs to the family of **membrane separation processes** classified under the **pressure-gradient-driven (baromembrane)** category, where the chemical potential gradient of the solvent is reversed by mechanical energy input.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Room" Intuition

Imagine a long hallway filled with marbles (water molecules) and heavy bowling balls (salt ions). On one end of the hallway, you place a fine net that allows **only the small marbles to pass through** while the bowling balls bounce back.

Now, imagine you have a crowd naturally pushing the marbles and balls toward the net — this is **natural osmosis**, where water moves toward the side with more salt (to balance concentration).

In **reverse osmosis**, you stand at the salty end and **push the crowd backward** (apply hydraulic pressure) so forcefully that you reverse the natural flow — pure marbles are pushed through the net, leaving the bowling balls behind. The minimum force required to reverse the natural crowd movement is the **osmotic pressure**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Constants Used in RO Analysis:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \; \text{mol}^{-1}$
> - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$
> - **Standard Operating Pressure Range for RO:** $\mathbf{10 \text{ bar} - 80 \text{ bar}}$ (brackish water at low end; seawater at high end)
> - **Typical Operating Flux:** $J_w = 10 - 60 \; \text{L/(m}^2\text{·h)}$
> - **Seawater Osmotic Pressure:** $\pi \approx 25 - 30 \; \text{bar}$ at 25 °C

### 1.4 Osmosis vs. Reverse Osmosis — The Fundamental Distinction

| Phenomenon | Driving Force | Solvent Flow Direction | Pressure Applied |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Osmosis** | Chemical potential gradient (concentration) | Low concentration → High concentration | $P_{applied} < \pi$ |
| **Reverse Osmosis** | Hydraulic pressure gradient | High concentration → Low concentration | $P_{applied} > \pi$ |
| **Equilibrium** | Balanced | No net flow | $P_{applied} = \pi$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation — Why RO Works

Reverse Osmosis is governed by **irreversible thermodynamics** and the **solution-diffusion mechanism**. The driving force for solvent transport is the **net effective pressure**, which is the applied transmembrane pressure minus the osmotic pressure difference:

- **Step 1 — Natural State:** A semipermeable membrane separates a dilute solution (pure water) and a concentrated solution (saline water).
- **Step 2 — Osmotic Equilibrium Attempt:** Water naturally tends to flow from the dilute side to the concentrated side to equalize chemical potentials.
- **Step 3 — Osmotic Pressure Buildup:** Water continues to flow until the hydrostatic pressure on the concentrated side equals the osmotic pressure $\pi$.
- **Step 4 — Pressure Reversal:** When an external hydraulic pressure $P > \pi$ is applied on the concentrated (feed) side, the natural flow is reversed.
- **Step 5 — Selective Permeation:** The membrane rejects ions (hydrated radius typically larger than the membrane free volume) while allowing water molecules (small kinetic diameter) to pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The membrane does NOT act as a "sieve" in the classical mechanical sense. The separation in RO is governed by **solution-diffusion** through the dense polymer matrix — both solvent and solute dissolve into the membrane and diffuse through it. Salt has a much lower diffusivity and solubility, hence the selectivity.

## 2.2 Osmotic Pressure — The Cornerstone of RO Design

The osmotic pressure of a dilute ideal solution is given by the **Van 't Hoff Equation**:

$$\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T$$

Where:
- $\pi$ = osmotic pressure (Pa)
- $i$ = Van 't Hoff factor (number of dissociated ions per formula unit; e.g., $i = 2$ for NaCl, $i = 3$ for CaCl₂)
- $M$ = molar concentration of solute (mol/m³)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

For **non-ideal concentrated solutions** (which is the case in real seawater and brine streams), a more accurate form using the osmotic coefficient $\phi$ and molality $m$ is used:

$$\pi = i \cdot \phi \cdot m \cdot \rho_w \cdot R \cdot T$$

Where $\rho_w$ is the density of water and $\phi$ accounts for ionic interactions (typically $\phi \approx 0.9$ for seawater).

## 2.3 Transport Models in RO Membranes

There are **three principal theoretical models** used to describe mass transport through RO membranes:

### Model A — Solution-Diffusion Model (Most Accepted)
Both water and salt dissolve in the dense membrane and diffuse independently. Flux equations:

- **Water Flux:** $\quad J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$
- **Solute Flux:** $\quad J_s = B \cdot (C_{f} - C_{p})$

### Model B — Pore-Flow Model (Less Common for RO)
Solvent flows through membrane pores; solutes rejected by size exclusion. Useful for **loose RO** and **nanofiltration**.

### Model C — Irreversible Thermodynamics Model
Treats the membrane as a "black box" using phenomenological coefficients. Useful for analysis when detailed membrane structure is unknown.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Reverse Osmosis

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\pi = iMRT$ | Van 't Hoff osmotic pressure (ideal dilute) | Pa |
| 2 | $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$ | Solution-diffusion solvent flux | m³/(m²·s) or L/m²·h |
| 3 | $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$ | Solution-diffusion solute flux | kg/(m²·s) or mol/m²·s |
| 4 | $R_j = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | Intrinsic rejection coefficient | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric recovery | % |
| 6 | $\text{Cf}_{conc} = \dfrac{C_f}{1 - \text{Recovery}}$ | Feed concentration at membrane inlet | mol/m³ |
| 7 | $J_w = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m}$ | Permeate flux from permeate flowrate | L/m²·h |
| 8 | $\Delta P = P_f - P_p$ | Net transmembrane pressure | Pa or bar |
| 9 | $\Delta\pi = \pi_f - \pi_p$ | Osmotic pressure difference | Pa or bar |
| 10 | $A = \dfrac{P_w}{x_w}$ | Water permeability constant | m³/(m²·s·Pa) |
| 11 | $B = \dfrac{P_s \cdot K_s}{x_s}$ | Solute permeability constant | m/s |
| 12 | $\text{NDP} = P_f - P_p - \pi_f + \pi_p$ | Net driving pressure (operating) | Pa or bar |
| 13 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v d_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (channel flow) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\text{CP} = \dfrac{C_{f,\text{wall}}}{C_{f,\text{bulk}}}$ | Concentration polarization factor | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Examination Purposes — Memorize Equations 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 12, and 14.** These are tested in nearly every KTU ESE question on RO.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reverse Osmosis is the **workhorse technology** of modern water desalination. Its applications include:

- **Seawater Desalination (SWRO):** Producing potable water from oceans (>17,000 plants worldwide).
- **Brackish Water Treatment (BWRO):** Treating inland saline groundwater.
- **Ultra-Pure Water (UPW) Production:** Semiconductor and pharmaceutical industries.
- **Food & Beverage Concentration:** Juice, dairy, and sugar concentration without phase change.
- **Wastewater Reuse:** Industrial effluent polishing for ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge).
- **Hemodialysis:** Blood purification in medical applications.
- **Boiler Feed Water Treatment:** Power plants requiring low-TDS water.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Osmotic Pressure (Van 't Hoff Equation)

The Van 't Hoff equation can be derived rigorously from the equality of chemical potentials across the semipermeable membrane at equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Condition for Osmotic Equilibrium:**
At equilibrium, the chemical potential of water on the pure solvent side (1) equals the chemical potential of water on the solution side (2) at the same temperature $T$:

$$\mu_w^{(1)} = \mu_w^{(2)}$$

**Step 2 — Express Chemical Potentials:**
For the pure solvent: $\mu_w^{(1)} = \mu_w^{\circ} + V_w P_1$  
For the solution: $\mu_w^{(2)} = \mu_w^{\circ} + RT \ln x_w + V_w P_2$  

Where $x_w$ is the mole fraction of water in solution, and $V_w$ is the partial molar volume of water.

**Step 3 — Equate and Simplify:**
$$\mu_w^{\circ} + V_w P_1 = \mu_w^{\circ} + RT \ln x_w + V_w P_2$$

The reference terms cancel:

$$V_w P_1 = RT \ln x_w + V_w P_2$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Get Pressure Difference:**
$$V_w (P_1 - P_2) = RT \ln x_w$$

For an ideal dilute solution, $\ln x_w \approx -x_s$ (mole fraction of solute), and the concentration relation $x_s = \dfrac{n_s}{n_s + n_w} \approx \dfrac{n_s}{n_w} = \dfrac{M \cdot V}{n_w}$. Since $n_w \cdot V_w \approx V$ (volume of solution), we get $x_s \approx M \cdot V_w$.

**Step 5 — Final Van 't Hoff Equation:**
Substituting $x_s \approx M \cdot V_w$ into the rearranged equation:

$$V_w \cdot \pi = -RT \cdot (-M \cdot V_w)$$

$$\boxed{\pi = MRT}$$

For an electrolyte that dissociates into $i$ particles (Van 't Hoff factor), the equation becomes:

$$\boxed{\pi = iMRT}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Solution-Diffusion Water Flux Equation

The solution-diffusion model assumes that:
1. Both water and solute dissolve in the dense polymer membrane.
2. They diffuse independently through the membrane down their own chemical potential gradients.
3. The pressure within the membrane is constant (no convective flow).

**Step 1 — Chemical Potential Gradient of Water:**
The flux of water $J_w$ through the membrane is driven by the gradient of its chemical potential $\mu_w$:

$$J_w = -L_w \cdot \frac{d\mu_w}{dx}$$

Where $L_w$ is a phenomenological transport coefficient.

**Step 2 — Express Chemical Potential Including Pressure:**
$$\mu_w = \mu_w^{\circ} + RT \ln a_w + V_w P$$

So the gradient is:
$$\frac{d\mu_w}{dx} = RT \frac{d \ln a_w}{dx} + V_w \frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Across Membrane Thickness (0 to $\ell$):**
Assuming $a_w$ and $P$ are constant within the membrane, integration yields:
$$J_w = -\frac{L_w}{\ell} \left[ RT \ln \left( \frac{a_{w,p}}{a_{w,f}} \right) + V_w (P_p - P_f) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Osmotic Pressure Relation:**
For ideal solutions, $RT \ln a_{w,p} - RT \ln a_{w,f} = -V_w (\pi_f - \pi_p)$

Substituting:

$$J_w = \frac{L_w V_w}{\ell} \left[ (P_f - P_p) - (\pi_f - \pi_p) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Define Water Permeability Constant:**
Defining the water permeability constant $A = \dfrac{L_w V_w}{\ell}$:

$$\boxed{J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P = P_f - P_p$ = transmembrane pressure
- $\Delta\pi = \pi_f - \pi_p$ = osmotic pressure difference

## 3.3 Derivation of Intrinsic Rejection Coefficient

The **observed rejection** $R_{obs}$ is a measurable quantity based on bulk concentrations, while the **intrinsic rejection** $R_j$ is a true membrane property.

**Step 1 — Define Observed Rejection:**
$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$

**Step 2 — Define Intrinsic Rejection (at membrane surface):**
$$R_j = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_{m}}$$

Where $C_m$ is the solute concentration at the membrane surface (NOT the bulk feed).

**Step 3 — Account for Concentration Polarization:**
The concentration at the membrane surface is higher than the bulk due to **concentration polarization (CP)**. The CP factor is:

$$CP = \frac{C_m}{C_f} = \frac{\exp(J_w / k)}{R_j + (1 - R_j)\exp(J_w / k)}$$

Where $k$ is the mass transfer coefficient in the feed channel.

**Step 4 — Link Observed and Intrinsic Rejection:**
$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_m} \cdot \frac{C_m}{C_f} = 1 - (1 - R_j) \cdot CP$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style Problem

**Problem:** A reverse osmosis unit is used to desalinate a NaCl feed solution. The feed concentration is 5000 mg/L, the permeate concentration is 250 mg/L, the applied pressure is 25 bar, and the permeate pressure is 1 bar. The membrane permeability constant is $A = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/(m²·s·Pa). Assume the feed osmotic pressure = 4.2 bar and permeate osmotic pressure = 0.21 bar. Calculate:
1. The water flux $J_w$
2. The observed rejection $R_{obs}$
3. The net driving pressure NDP

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Net Transmembrane Pressure:**

$$\Delta P = P_f - P_p = 25 - 1 = 24 \; \text{bar} = 24 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Osmotic Pressure Difference:**

$$\Delta\pi = \pi_f - \pi_p = 4.2 - 0.21 = 3.99 \; \text{bar} = 3.99 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Solution-Diffusion Water Flux Equation:**

$$J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$$

$$J_w = (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (24 \times 10^5 - 3.99 \times 10^5)$$

$$J_w = (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (20.01 \times 10^5)$$

$$J_w = 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.001 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{J_w = 1.0005 \times 10^0 = 1.0005 \; \text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})}$$

Converting to LMH:

$$J_w = 1.0005 \times \frac{1000 \; \text{L/m}^3 \times 3600 \; \text{s/h}}{1} = 3601.8 \; \text{LMH}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Observed Rejection:**

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{250}{5000}$$

$$R_{obs} = 1 - 0.05$$

$$\boxed{R_{obs} = 0.95 \; \text{or} \; 95\%}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Net Driving Pressure (NDP):**

$$\text{NDP} = P_f - P_p - \pi_f + \pi_p$$

$$\text{NDP} = 25 - 1 - 4.2 + 0.21$$

$$\boxed{\text{NDP} = 20.01 \; \text{bar}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Notice that the NDP = 20.01 bar equals $(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$, confirming the consistency of the solution-diffusion model. This is a common cross-check in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.5 Concentration Polarization — Analytical Treatment

Concentration polarization (CP) is the **accumulation of rejected solute** near the membrane surface. It reduces effective driving pressure and is a critical design consideration.

**Mass Transfer Balance at Membrane Surface:**

At steady state, the convective solute flux toward the membrane equals the sum of the solute flux through the membrane and the back-diffusive flux:

$$J_w \cdot C = J_w \cdot C_p - D \frac{dC}{dy}$$

Where:
- $J_w \cdot C$ = convective flux toward membrane
- $J_w \cdot C_p$ = solute flux through membrane
- $-D \frac{dC}{dy}$ = back-diffusive flux away from membrane

**Boundary Conditions:** $C(y=0) = C_f$ (bulk) and $C(y=\delta) = C_m$ (membrane surface)

**Integration yields the classic CP Equation:**

$$\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p} = \exp\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right)$$

Where $k = D/\delta$ is the mass transfer coefficient.

**Implication for KTU:** The CP factor $\left( \dfrac{C_m}{C_f} \right)$ is **always greater than 1** in RO, meaning the membrane "sees" a higher concentration than the bulk feed. This reduces the effective $\Delta\pi$ used in the flux equation, and hence lowers the actual flux.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Reverse Osmosis. State the driving force required for the process.**

**Model Answer:**

Reverse Osmosis (RO) is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which a feed solution is passed through a semipermeable membrane under hydraulic pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of the feed, producing a purified solvent permeate and a concentrated retentate.

**Driving Force:** The **net effective pressure** $(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$, which is the difference between the applied transmembrane hydraulic pressure and the osmotic pressure difference across the membrane.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Q2. State the Van 't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure. What is the osmotic pressure of a 0.1 M NaCl solution at 27 °C?**

**Model Answer:**

The Van 't Hoff equation is:

$$\pi = iMRT$$

For NaCl, the Van 't Hoff factor $i = 2$ (NaCl dissociates into Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions).

Given: $M = 0.1 \; \text{mol/L} = 100 \; \text{mol/m}^3$, $T = 27 + 273 = 300 \; \text{K}$, $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$

$$\pi = 2 \times 100 \times 8.314 \times 300 = 498{,}840 \; \text{Pa} \approx 4.99 \; \text{bar}$$

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q(a). With a neat sketch, explain the principle of Reverse Osmosis. Derive the solution-diffusion model equation for water flux through an RO membrane. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Reverse Osmosis:**

In natural osmosis, water flows from a region of low solute concentration (high water chemical potential) to a region of high solute concentration through a semipermeable membrane. This flow continues until the hydrostatic pressure on the concentrated side equals the **osmotic pressure $\pi$**.

In Reverse Osmosis, an **external hydraulic pressure $P$ greater than the osmotic pressure $\pi$** is applied on the concentrated (feed) side. This **reverses the natural osmotic flow**, forcing water molecules through the membrane from the concentrated side to the dilute side, while the dissolved solutes (salts) are rejected.

**Sketch (as drawn in exam):**

```
   PURE WATER         SEMIPERMEABLE          CONCENTRATED
   (PERMEATE)          MEMBRANE              SALT SOLUTION
                          
   ◄──── Water flow                      Applied Pressure P greater than pi
   (permeate)                             
                                         
                       [======]                  
                       (Rejects salts)           
```

**Derivation of Solution-Diffusion Water Flux:**

The solution-diffusion model assumes:
1. Both solvent and solute dissolve in the dense polymer membrane.
2. Each species diffuses independently through the membrane down its own chemical potential gradient.
3. There is no convective flow within the membrane (constant pressure inside).

**Step 1:** The flux of water is proportional to the gradient of its chemical potential:

$$J_w = -L_w \cdot \frac{d\mu_w}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** Express the chemical potential of water as a function of activity $a_w$ and pressure $P$:

$$\mu_w = \mu_w^{\circ} + RT \ln a_w + V_w P$$

**Step 3:** Taking the gradient across the membrane:

$$\frac{d\mu_w}{dx} = RT \frac{d \ln a_w}{dx} + V_w \frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate across membrane thickness $\ell$, from feed side ($x=0$) to permeate side ($x=\ell$):

$$J_w = -\frac{L_w}{\ell} \left[ RT \ln\left(\frac{a_{w,p}}{a_{w,f}}\right) + V_w (P_p - P_f) \right]$$

**Step 5:** Using the identity $RT \ln a_{w,p} - RT \ln a_{w,f} = -V_w (\pi_f - \pi_p)$ for ideal solutions:

$$J_w = \frac{L_w V_w}{\ell} \left[ (P_f - P_p) - (\pi_f - \pi_p) \right]$$

**Step 6:** Define the water permeability constant $A = \dfrac{L_w V_w}{\ell}$:

$$\boxed{J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)}$$

This is the **fundamental RO flux equation** based on the solution-diffusion model.

---

**Q(b). An RO plant processes 1000 m³/day of brackish water containing 3000 ppm NaCl. The plant operates at 65% recovery with observed rejection of 97%. Calculate: (i) permeate concentration, (ii) brine concentration, (iii) permeate flowrate. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Feed flowrate $Q_f = 1000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- Feed concentration $C_f = 3000 \; \text{ppm} = 3000 \; \text{mg/L}$
- Recovery = 65% = 0.65
- Observed rejection $R_{obs} = 97\% = 0.97$

**Step 1 — Permeate Flowrate:**

$$Q_p = \text{Recovery} \times Q_f = 0.65 \times 1000 = 650 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{Q_p = 650 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$$

**Step 2 — Brine Flowrate:**

$$Q_b = Q_f - Q_p = 1000 - 650 = 350 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Permeate Concentration (using rejection definition):**

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$

$$0.97 = 1 - \frac{C_p}{3000}$$

$$\frac{C_p}{3000} = 0.03$$

$$\boxed{C_p = 90 \; \text{ppm} = 90 \; \text{mg/L}}$$

**Step 4 — Brine Concentration (using mass balance):**

The total mass balance gives: $Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_b C_b$

$$1000 \times 3000 = 650 \times 90 + 350 \times C_b$$

$$3{,}000{,}000 = 58{,}500 + 350 \cdot C_b$$

$$C_b = \frac{3{,}000{,}000 - 58{,}500}{350} = \frac{2{,}941{,}500}{350}$$

$$\boxed{C_b = 8404.3 \; \text{ppm} \approx 8404 \; \text{mg/L}}$$

**Verification using mixing rule:**

$$C_{f,conc} = \frac{C_f}{1 - \text{Recovery}} = \frac{3000}{1 - 0.65} = \frac{3000}{0.35} = 8571 \; \text{ppm}$$

The small difference (8571 vs 8404) is due to the fact that the simple mixing rule assumes complete mixing and zero permeate concentration. The rigorous mass balance using the actual permeate concentration (90 ppm) is more accurate.

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**Q(a). Explain the phenomenon of concentration polarization in RO membranes. Derive the concentration polarization equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concentration Polarization — Definition:**

Concentration polarization (CP) is the **accumulation of rejected solute at the membrane surface**, forming a thin boundary layer of high concentration. This occurs because the membrane selectively passes water while rejecting solutes. The rejected solutes accumulate near the membrane, creating a concentration gradient that drives solute back-diffusion into the bulk.

**Effects of Concentration Polarization:**

1. **Reduces effective driving force** — Local $\pi$ at membrane is higher than bulk $\pi$, so effective $\Delta\pi$ is larger, and flux is lower.
2. **Increases observed solute passage** — The higher local concentration at the membrane drives more solute through.
3. **Promotes scaling and fouling** — Supersaturation at the membrane surface can cause precipitation of sparingly soluble salts (CaCO₃, CaSO₄, BaSO₄).
4. **Reduces membrane life** — Long-term fouling degrades membrane performance.

**Derivation of CP Equation:**

Consider a mass balance on a differential element at the membrane surface. At steady state:

$$J_w \cdot C = J_s + J_w \cdot C_p + J_{back}$$

The back-diffusive flux of solute (Fick's law) is:

$$J_{back} = -D \frac{dC}{dy}$$

So: $J_w (C - C_p) = -D \dfrac{dC}{dy}$

**Rearranging:**

$$\frac{dC}{C - C_p} = -\frac{J_w}{D} dy$$

**Integrating from bulk ($y = 0$, $C = C_f$) to membrane surface ($y = \delta$, $C = C_m$):**

$$\int_{C_f}^{C_m} \frac{dC}{C - C_p} = -\frac{J_w}{D} \int_0^{\delta} dy$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p}\right) = -\frac{J_w \delta}{D}$$

**Defining mass transfer coefficient $k = D/\delta$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p}\right) = -\frac{J_w}{k}$$

**Exponentiating:**

$$\boxed{\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p} = \exp\left(-\frac{J_w}{k}\right)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p} = \exp\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right)}$$

(depending on sign convention used; both forms are accepted in KTU).

The **CP factor** is defined as:

$$\text{CP} = \frac{C_m}{C_f}$$

This is always greater than unity in RO.

---

**Q(b). Discuss the different types of RO membrane modules with their advantages and limitations. Compare their performance characteristics. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Four Principal RO Membrane Module Configurations:**

| Module Type | Membrane Form | Packing Density (m²/m³) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spiral Wound** | Flat sheet with permeate spacer | 600–1000 | Brackish & seawater desalination |
| **Hollow Fiber** | Fine hollow filaments | 5000–15000 | High-pressure SWRO |
| **Tubular** | Cast tube with inner membrane | 70–400 | High-fouling wastewaters |
| **Plate and Frame** | Flat sheet between plates | 100–400 | Specialized/dairy |

**1. Spiral Wound Module (Most Common):**
- Construction: Flat membrane sheet, permeate spacer, and feed spacer are rolled around a central permeate tube.
- Advantages: High packing density, low cost per m², well-developed technology, easily replaceable.
- Limitations: Sensitive to suspended solids, difficult to clean mechanically, prone to fouling.

**2. Hollow Fiber Module:**
- Construction: Thousands of fine hollow fibers (OD ~50–100 μm) bundled in a pressure vessel.
- Advantages: Highest packing density, self-supporting at high pressures.
- Limitations: Very sensitive to fouling, high pressure drop, complex manufacturing.

**3. Tubular Module:**
- Construction: Membrane cast on the inside of a porous tube (typically 0.5–2.5 cm diameter).
- Advantages: Can handle viscous and fouling feeds, easy to clean by sponge ball or brush.
- Limitations: Low packing density, high capital cost per m², large footprint.

**4. Plate and Frame Module:**
- Construction: Flat membrane sheets clamped between plates.
- Advantages: Easy to disassemble and clean, suitable for viscous feeds.
- Limitations: Low packing density, high cost, primarily for niche applications (food, pharma).

**Comparison of Key Performance Metrics:**

| Parameter | Spiral Wound | Hollow Fiber | Tubular | Plate-Frame |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Packing Density** | High | Very High | Low | Low |
| **Cost per m²** | Low | Moderate | High | High |
| **Fouling Resistance** | Moderate | Low | Very High | High |
| **Operating Pressure** | Up to 80 bar | Up to 80 bar | Up to 70 bar | Up to 60 bar |
| **Cleaning Ease** | Moderate | Difficult | Very Easy | Easy |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mark Loss Hotspot 1:** Students often confuse the signs in the CP equation. The standard form uses $\exp(J_w/k)$ — using the negative sign incorrectly is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Mark Loss Hotspot 2:** Forgetting to convert units (bar ↔ Pa, ppm ↔ mg/L) in numerical problems leads to **1-2 mark deduction** even if the formula is correct.
> 3. **Mark Loss Hotspot 3:** Confusing **observed rejection** $R_{obs}$ (based on bulk concentrations) with **intrinsic rejection** $R_j$ (based on membrane surface concentration). This is a **3-mark conceptual error** in KTU ESE.
> 4. **Mark Loss Hotspot 4:** Forgetting to use the **Van 't Hoff factor $i$** for ionic solutes (e.g., $i = 2$ for NaCl). This is a **frequently tested 1-2 mark question** in KTU.
> 5. **Mark Loss Hotspot 5:** Not stating assumptions of the solution-diffusion model (constant pressure inside membrane, no coupling between fluxes, Fickian diffusion). Examiners allocate **1 mark** for stating assumptions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Reverse Osmosis**

### Core Concepts to Master
- [ ] **Definition:** RO is a pressure-driven membrane process with $\Delta P > \Delta\pi$
- [ ] **Driving force:** Net effective pressure $= \Delta P - \Delta\pi$
- [ ] **Osmotic pressure equation:** $\pi = iMRT$ (Van 't Hoff)
- [ ] **Water flux equation:** $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$ (Solution-Diffusion)
- [ ] **Solute flux equation:** $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$
- [ ] **Rejection equation:** $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$
- [ ] **Recovery:** $Q_p / Q_f \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Concentration polarization:** $\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_f - C_p} = \exp(J_w/k)$

### Numerical Values to Remember
- [ ] **Seawater osmotic pressure:** ~25–30 bar at 25 °C
- [ ] **Brackish water osmotic pressure:** ~1–5 bar
- [ ] **RO operating pressure:** 10–80 bar (depends on feed salinity)
- [ ] **Typical flux:** 10–60 LMH
- [ ] **Typical rejection (NaCl):** 95–99.8%
- [ ] **R constant:** 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K)

### Module Comparisons
- [ ] **RO is the tightest** of all pressure-driven membrane processes
- [ ] Order of tightness: **MF < UF < NF < RO**
- [ ] RO removes monovalent ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) effectively; NF mostly divalent ions
- [ ] **RO vs Nanofiltration:** RO rejects monovalents; NF allows them partially

### Common Misconceptions (Avoid in Exam)
- [ ] ❌ RO membrane is NOT a sieve — it's a dense polymer (solution-diffusion)
- [ ] ❌ Higher pressure does NOT always increase flux linearly — limited by osmotic pressure buildup
- [ ] ❌ Rejection and recovery are DIFFERENT quantities
- [ ] ❌ Observed rejection is NOT intrinsic rejection (concentration polarization matters)

### Equations to Practice for 14-Mark Questions
- [ ] Derive Van 't Hoff equation from chemical potential equality
- [ ] Derive $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$ from solution-diffusion model
- [ ] Derive concentration polarization equation from mass balance
- [ ] Solve numerical problems combining flux, rejection, recovery, and concentration calculations

### KTU 2024 Module Context
- [ ] This topic belongs to **Module 1** of PECHT756 — Novel Separation Processes
- [ ] Expected marks weightage: 14–20% of full syllabus
- [ ] Co-relates with: Membrane separation processes, Ultrafiltration, Nanofiltration, Pervaporation
