3. **Bubble–Surfactant Adsorption** — As bubbles rise through the bulk liquid, surfactant molecules diffuse from the bulk to the bubble surface and are *adsorbed* at the gas–liquid interface. This is the **fundamental mass-transfer step**.

4. **Foam Formation and Rise** — Adsorbate-laden bubbles accumulate at the top, forming a stable foam. The foam height and drainage characteristics strongly influence enrichment.

5. **Foam Collapse and Recovery** — The foam is collapsed at the top, either by mechanical shear, thermal means, or chemical antifoaming, yielding the **foamate** (concentrated product).

6. **Residue Withdrawal** — The depleted bulk liquid is removed as the *bottoms* product.

### 2.2 Why the Process Works — The Thermodynamic "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> The driving force behind foam fractionation is the **reduction in surface free energy**. A pure liquid has a high surface tension. When surfactant molecules migrate to the surface, the surface tension drops dramatically. Since the system always seeks the lowest free energy, the surfactant will *spontaneously* partition to the interface. The amount of surfactant at the surface is quantified by the **surface excess** $\Gamma$, defined as moles of solute per unit area of interface.

### 2.3 The Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm (Foundational Equation)

For a non-ionic surfactant (single solute, constant temperature):

$$
\Gamma = -\frac{1}{RT} \cdot \frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C}
$$

For a dilute solution (linearization valid):

$$
\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT} \cdot \frac{d\gamma}{dC}
$$

For an **ionic surfactant** in the presence of an excess of an inert electrolyte (to suppress the electrical double layer):

$$
\Gamma = -\frac{1}{2RT} \cdot \frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C}
$$

The factor **2** in the denominator (ionic case) arises because only the surfactant ion is surface-active; the counter-ion distributes in the diffuse double layer. The total surface excess of ionic surfactant species is twice the surface excess of the surface-active ion.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the core derivations the examiner expects you to reproduce.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---------------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Gibbs Isotherm (Non-ionic)** | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$ | $\Gamma$ = mol/m² ; $C$ = mol/m³ ; $\gamma$ = N/m | Relates surface excess to bulk concentration |
| 2 | **Gibbs Isotherm (Ionic, with electrolyte)** | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{1}{2RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{d\ln C}$ | Same as above | Ionic surfactant in presence of inert salt |
| 3 | **Enrichment Ratio** | $E = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i}$ | $C_f$ = foamate concentration ; $C_i$ = feed concentration | Performance metric — degree of separation |
| 4 | **Recovery Fraction** | $R = \dfrac{C_f \, V_f}{C_i \, V_i}$ | $V_f$ = foamate volume ; $V_i$ = feed volume | Mass balance-based yield |
| 5 | **Separation Factor** | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{C_i^f / C_i^b}{C_j^f / C_j^b}$ | Subscripts *f* = foamate, *b* = bottoms | Selectivity between two solutes |
| 6 | **Volumetric Air Flow Parameter** | $G = \dfrac{Q}{A_c}$ | $Q$ = volumetric air flow (m³/s) ; $A_c$ = column area (m²) | Sets bubble residence time |
| 7 | **Foam Density** | $\rho_f = \dfrac{\text{mass of foam}}{\text{volume of foam}}$ | kg/m³ | Affects liquid drainage rate |
| 8 | **Bubble Surface Area Flux** | $S_b = \dfrac{6 G \, \varepsilon}{d_b}$ | $d_b$ = bubble diameter ; $\varepsilon$ = gas holdup | Determines available interface area |
| 9 | **Gas Holdup** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{gas}}{V_{gas} + V_{liq}}$ | Dimensionless | Foam column hydrodynamics |
| 10 | **Overall Mass Balance** | $F \, C_i = B \, C_b + D \, C_f$ | $F, B, D$ = feed, bottoms, foamate flow rates (m³/s) | Steady-state process check |

### 2.5 Factors Affecting Foam Fractionation Performance

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner frequently asks "list and explain the factors affecting…" — use the following structured points:

- **Air Flow Rate (G):** Higher gas flow → more bubbles per unit time → higher recovery but *lower* enrichment (because the foam drains less efficiently).
- **Foam Height (H):** Taller foam column → more drainage time → *higher* enrichment but *lower* recovery.
- **Liquid Feed Rate (F):** Higher liquid feed reduces residence time, decreasing both recovery and enrichment in the simple mode.
- **Bubble Size:** Smaller bubbles → higher surface-to-volume ratio → more adsorption → *higher* recovery but slower rise.
- **Surfactant Concentration:** Operating near the **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)** is optimal; beyond CMC, micelles form and reduce surface excess.
- **pH:** For ionic surfactants, pH controls ionization state and hence adsorption.
- **Ionic Strength:** Added electrolyte compresses the electrical double layer, increasing adsorption of ionic surfactants.
- **Temperature:** Higher temperature → lower surface tension → less driving force for adsorption (generally reduces $\Gamma$).
- **Presence of Antifoaming Agents:** Deliberately disrupt foam — counter-productive if added accidentally.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Foam fractionation is industrially significant in:
> - **Wastewater Treatment:** Removal of detergents, dyes, and surface-active organic contaminants.
> - **Hydrometallurgy:** Pre-concentration of metal ions (Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺, Cd²⁺) complexed with surfactant collectors.
> - **Biotechnology:** Recovery of proteins, enzymes (e.g., lysozyme, BSA) and microbial cells.
> - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Purification of antibiotics and fermentation products.
> - **Nuclear Waste Treatment:** Removal of radioactive surface-active isotopes.
> - **Analytical Chemistry:** Trace-level detection and pre-concentration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm (Non-Ionic Case)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **high-yield KTU question** — examiners routinely ask students to derive the Gibbs Isotherm from first principles.

**Starting Point — The Helmholtz Free Energy of an Interface:**

Consider a system at constant temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ consisting of a liquid with a surface of area $A$. The total Helmholtz free energy differential is:

$$
dG = -S \, dT + V \, dP + \gamma \, dA + \sum_i \mu_i \, dn_i
$$

At constant $T$ and $P$:

$$
dG = \gamma \, dA + \sum_i \mu_i \, dn_i
$$

**Step 1 — Define Surface Excess $\Gamma_i$:**

The surface excess of component $i$ is the *excess* moles of $i$ present in the interfacial region compared to a hypothetical reference where bulk concentrations extended up to the surface. By definition:

$$
\Gamma_i = \frac{n_i^{\text{excess}}}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Total Differential of $G$ per Unit Area:**

Define a surface free energy per unit area $g^s$. For constant $T, P$ and $\mu_i$:

$$
dg^s = \gamma
$$

For variable chemical potentials (Gibbs adsorption equation):

$$
d\gamma = - \sum_i \Gamma_i \, d\mu_i
$$

**Step 3 — Apply for a Single Solute (Non-Ionic):**

For a binary system (solvent + one solute $i$):

$$
d\gamma = - \Gamma_i \, d\mu_i
$$

The chemical potential of solute $i$ in an ideal dilute solution:

$$
\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln C_i
$$

Differentiating at constant $T$:

$$
d\mu_i = RT \, d\ln C_i
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
d\gamma = - \Gamma_i \, RT \, d\ln C_i
$$

Rearranging for $\Gamma_i$:

$$
\Gamma_i = -\frac{1}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Linearize for Dilute Solutions:**

Using $\dfrac{d\ln C}{dC} = \dfrac{1}{C}$:

$$
\Gamma_i = -\frac{C_i}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC_i}
$$

**This is the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm for a non-ionic surfactant.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation for the Ionic Surfactant Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an ionic surfactant like sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), which dissociates as $NaD \rightleftharpoons Na^+ + D^-$, the surface-active species is $D^-$. The counter-ion $Na^+$ is *not* surface-active but is attracted to the surface by electrostatic forces.

**Step 1 — Apply Gibbs Equation with Both Ions:**

$$
d\gamma = - \Gamma_{D^-} \, d\mu_{D^-} - \Gamma_{Na^+} \, d\mu_{Na^+}
$$

**Step 2 — Electro-Neutrality Condition:**

At the surface, electrical neutrality must be preserved:

$$
\Gamma_{Na^+} = \Gamma_{D^-} = \Gamma
$$

**Step 3 — Chemical Potentials (with added inert electrolyte to ensure ideality):**

$$
d\mu_{D^-} = RT \, d\ln C_{D^-} \quad ; \quad d\mu_{Na^+} = RT \, d\ln C_{Na^+}
$$

But the salt is in stoichiometric excess, so the counter-ion concentration is essentially constant:

$$
d\mu_{Na^+} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
d\gamma = - \Gamma \, RT \, d\ln C_{D^-}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\Gamma = -\frac{1}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — but KTU textbooks list a factor of 2!** The factor of 2 appears when the inert electrolyte is *absent* and we have only the ionic surfactant alone. In that case, electrical neutrality in the bulk is maintained, but at the surface, the relationship $C_{Na^+} = C_{D^-}$ causes:

$$
d\mu_{D^-} + d\mu_{Na^+} = 2 \, RT \, d\ln C
$$

Therefore:

$$
d\gamma = - 2\Gamma \, RT \, d\ln C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Gamma = -\frac{1}{2RT} \frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C}
$$

This is the form most commonly tested in KTU.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Enrichment Ratio from a Material Balance

**System:** Continuous foam fractionation column operating at steady state. Let:
- $F$ = feed volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $B$ = bottoms volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $D$ = foamate (distillate) volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_i, C_b, C_f$ = concentrations of solute in feed, bottoms, and foamate

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance:**

$$
F = B + D
$$

**Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance:**

$$
F \cdot C_i = B \cdot C_b + D \cdot C_f
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Enrichment Ratio:**

$$
E = \frac{C_f}{C_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Definition of Recovery:**

$$
R = \frac{D \cdot C_f}{F \cdot C_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine and Eliminate Variables:**

Dividing the solute balance by $F \cdot C_i$:

$$
1 = \frac{B \cdot C_b}{F \cdot C_i} + \frac{D \cdot C_f}{F \cdot C_i}
$$

Since $R = D \cdot C_f / (F \cdot C_i)$:

$$
1 = \frac{B \cdot C_b}{F \cdot C_i} + R
$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of E and a Volume Reduction Factor:**

Define volume reduction $V_r = F/D$ (ratio of feed to foamate flow). Then $D = F / V_r$ and $B = F (1 - 1/V_r)$.

Substituting $C_f = E \cdot C_i$ and $C_b$ from mass balance:

$$
C_b = \frac{F C_i - D E C_i}{B} = \frac{C_i (F - D E)}{F - D}
$$

This yields the **operating-line relationship**:

$$
\frac{C_b}{C_i} = \frac{V_r - E}{V_r - 1}
$$

This is a *complete analytical matrix entry* for a 14-mark KTU problem.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (14-Mark Standard):** In a continuous foam fractionation column, the feed rate is $F = 100$ L/h with surfactant concentration $C_i = 50$ mg/L. The foamate flow rate is $D = 10$ L/h with concentration $C_f = 400$ mg/L. Calculate: (a) Bottoms flow rate, (b) Bottoms concentration, (c) Enrichment ratio, (d) Recovery.

**Given:**
- $F = 100$ L/h, $C_i = 50$ mg/L
- $D = 10$ L/h, $C_f = 400$ mg/L

**Part (a) — Bottoms Flow Rate:**

$$
B = F - D = 100 - 10 = 90 \text{ L/h}
$$

**Part (b) — Bottoms Concentration (Mass Balance):**

$$
F C_i = B C_b + D C_f
$$

$$
100 \times 50 = 90 \times C_b + 10 \times 400
$$

$$
5000 = 90 C_b + 4000
$$

$$
90 C_b = 1000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_b = \frac{1000}{90} = 11.11 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Part (c) — Enrichment Ratio:**

$$
E = \frac{C_f}{C_i} = \frac{400}{50} = 8
$$

**Part (d) — Recovery:**

$$
R = \frac{D \cdot C_f}{F \cdot C_i} = \frac{10 \times 400}{100 \times 50} = \frac{4000}{5000} = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by Total Mass Balance:**
> Solute in feed: $5000$ mg/h. Solute in foamate: $4000$ mg/h. Solute in bottoms: $90 \times 11.11 \approx 1000$ mg/h. Total: $5000$ mg/h. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Foam Fractionation Column

The diagram below illustrates the complete functional architecture of a continuous foam fractionation system, including all major unit operations, mass streams, and control loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FEED_SYSTEM["FEED SUPPLY AND CONDITIONING"]
        A1[Liquid Feed Tank] --> A2[Feed Pump]
        A2 --> A3{pH and Temperature Control}
        A3 --> A4[Feed Inlet Distributor]
    end

    subgraph COLUMN["VERTICAL FOAM FRACTIONATION COLUMN"]
        B1[Liquid Pool Region] --> B2[Equilibrium Adsorption Zone]
        B2 --> B3[Wet Foam Plateau Zone]
        B3 --> B4[Coalescing Foam Zone]
        A4 --> B1
    end

    subgraph GAS_SYSTEM["GAS INJECTION SYSTEM"]
        C1[Air or Nitrogen Cylinder] --> C2[Pressure Regulator]
        C2 --> C3[Rotameter Flow Meter]
        C3 --> C4[Glass Frit or Porous Sparger]
        C4 --> B1
    end

    subgraph FOAM_HANDLING["FOAM COLLECTION AND COLLAPSE"]
        B4 --> D1[Top Foam Collector]
        D1 --> D2[Mechanical Foam Breaker]
        D2 --> D3[Foamate Holding Tank]
    end

    subgraph BOTTOMS["BOTTOMS RECOVERY"]
        B1 --> E1[Residue Withdrawal Line]
        E1 --> E2[Depleted Bottoms Tank]
    end

    subgraph INSTRUMENTATION["PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION"]
        F1[Surfactant Concentration Sensor] -.-> B2
        F2[Bubble Size Camera] -.-> C4
        F3[Foam Height Detector] -.-> B3
        F4[Foamate Conductivity Probe] -.-> D3
    end

    classDef feedBox fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef columnBox fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef gasBox fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef foamBox fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bottomsBox fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef instrBox fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 feedBox
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 columnBox
    class C1,C2,C3,C4 gasBox
    class D1,D2,D3 foamBox
    class E1,E2 bottomsBox
    class F1,F2,F3,F4 instrBox
```

### 4.2 Schematic Description and Functional Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-by-block description of the architecture flow:**

1. **FEED SUPPLY AND CONDITIONING** — The feed tank supplies the surfactant-laden liquid through a metering pump. The pH is adjusted (typically to 2 units above the pKa for anionic surfactants to ensure full ionization and maximum adsorption). The conditioned feed is then distributed evenly into the column through a perforated distributor plate.

2. **VERTICAL FOAM FRACTIONATION COLUMN** — The column is divided into four functional zones:
   - **Liquid Pool Region:** Bulk liquid that is depleted as it contacts rising bubbles.
   - **Equilibrium Adsorption Zone:** The principal mass-transfer zone where Gibbs-type adsorption equilibrium is established between bulk and bubble interface.
   - **Wet Foam Plateau Zone:** Bubbles emerge from the liquid and form a wet, polyhedral foam; intense liquid drainage occurs here.
   - **Coalescing Foam Zone:** The foam becomes drier as bubbles continue to coalesce and Plateau borders shrink.

3. **GAS INJECTION SYSTEM** — A clean, dry gas (nitrogen preferred to avoid oxidation) is delivered through a pressure regulator, metered by a calibrated rotameter, and dispersed as a fine bubble stream by a sintered glass frit or porous stainless-steel sparger. Bubble size uniformity is critical for reproducible performance.

4. **FOAM COLLECTION AND COLLAPSE** — The dry foam at the column top is scraped into a collector and routed to a mechanical foam breaker (high-speed rotor) or a thermal condenser. The collapsed liquid (foamate) is collected in a holding tank.

5. **BOTTOMS RECOVERY** — The depleted liquid is withdrawn from the bottom and discharged to the bottoms tank. In a multi-stage system, the bottoms can be recycled to a second column.

6. **PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION** — On-line sensors measure surfactant concentration, bubble size distribution (via high-speed camera), foam height (via capacitance probe), and foamate conductivity. These signals feed back to the control system for real-time optimization.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a labeled diagram of a foam fractionation column, always include: the *sparger*, the *liquid pool*, the *wet foam zone*, the *dry foam zone*, the *feed inlet*, the *bottoms outlet*, and the *foam collector/breaker*. Missing any one of these labels can cost you 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]**

*Define foam fractionation. Mention any two industrial applications.*

**Model Answer:**

Foam fractionation is an *adsorptive bubble separation technique* in which surface-active solutes are selectively adsorbed onto gas bubbles rising through a liquid column, carried upward as a foam, and recovered as a concentrated *foamate* upon foam collapse.

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **Removal of surface-active contaminants** (e.g., detergents, dyes) from industrial wastewater.
2. **Recovery and purification of proteins and enzymes** (e.g., lysozyme, bovine serum albumin) in the biotechnology industry.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid applications: pre-concentration of metal ions in hydrometallurgy, treatment of radioactive waste, pharmaceutical antibiotic recovery.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]**

*State the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer:**

The Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm for a non-ionic surfactant in a dilute solution is:

$$
\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC}
$$

**Significance of each term:**
- $\Gamma$ = surface excess concentration (mol/m²) — the amount of solute adsorbed per unit area of the gas–liquid interface.
- $C$ = bulk concentration of the surfactant (mol/m³).
- $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J · mol⁻¹ · K⁻¹).
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K).
- $d\gamma / dC$ = rate of change of surface tension with respect to bulk concentration (N · m · mol⁻¹). For surface-active solutes, this is **negative**, which makes $\Gamma$ positive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The equation tells us that **the more the surface tension drops with concentration, the more surfactant is adsorbed at the surface** — this is the fundamental engineering lever for designing foam fractionation processes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Examination) feature **internal choice**. Two fully solved alternatives are provided below. The exact same model is also what you should expect in **University Exam — Module 2 Question Paper**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series I, Q8(a)]**

*Derive the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm for a non-ionic surfactant in a dilute aqueous solution. Starting from the thermodynamic definition of surface excess, obtain the relationship between the surface excess $\Gamma$, the bulk concentration $C$, and the rate of change of surface tension with concentration.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Surface Excess:**

The surface excess $\Gamma_i$ of component $i$ is defined as the excess number of moles of $i$ in the interfacial region per unit area, relative to a hypothetical Gibbs dividing surface where bulk concentrations are assumed to extend uniformly:

$$
\Gamma_i = \frac{n_i^{\text{actual, surface}} - n_i^{\text{reference, bulk}}}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Gibbs Fundamental Adsorption Equation:**

Starting from the differential of the Helmholtz free energy of the interfacial region at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$
dG^{\sigma} = \gamma \, dA + \sum_i \mu_i \, d n_i^{\sigma}
$$

Dividing by $A$ and writing $g^{\sigma} = G^{\sigma}/A$:

$$
d(\gamma) = - \sum_i \Gamma_i \, d\mu_i
$$

**Step 3 — Single Solute (Non-Ionic):**

For a binary system (solvent + one solute):

$$
d\gamma = - \Gamma_i \, d\mu_i
$$

**Step 4 — Chemical Potential of Dilute Solute:**

$$
\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln C_i
$$

At constant $T$:

$$
d\mu_i = RT \, d\ln C_i
$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$
d\gamma = - \Gamma_i \, RT \, d\ln C_i
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\Gamma_i = -\frac{1}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{d\ln C_i}
$$

**Step 6 — Linearize (for dilute solutions where $d\ln C = dC / C$):**

$$
\boxed{\Gamma_i = -\frac{C_i}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC_i}}
$$

This is the **Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm** for a non-ionic surfactant. $\blacksquare$

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series I, Q8(b)]**

*A continuous foam fractionation column is fed with $F = 200$ L/h of an aqueous solution containing $C_i = 100$ mg/L of a surface-active dye. The foamate flow rate is $D = 20$ L/h and the foamate concentration is $C_f = 800$ mg/L. Determine:*
*(i) The bottoms flow rate $B$, (ii) The bottoms concentration $C_b$, (iii) The enrichment ratio $E$, (iv) The recovery $R$.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Feed flow rate, $F = 200$ L/h
- Feed concentration, $C_i = 100$ mg/L
- Foamate flow rate, $D = 20$ L/h
- Foamate concentration, $C_f = 800$ mg/L

**(i) Bottoms Flow Rate:**

By overall mass balance:

$$
B = F - D = 200 - 20 = 180 \text{ L/h}
$$

**(ii) Bottoms Concentration:**

By solute mass balance:

$$
F C_i = B C_b + D C_f
$$

$$
200 \times 100 = 180 \times C_b + 20 \times 800
$$

$$
20000 = 180 \, C_b + 16000
$$

$$
180 \, C_b = 4000
$$

$$
C_b = \frac{4000}{180} = 22.22 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**(iii) Enrichment Ratio:**

$$
E = \frac{C_f}{C_i} = \frac{800}{100} = 8
$$

**(iv) Recovery:**

$$
R = \frac{D \cdot C_f}{F \cdot C_i} = \frac{20 \times 800}{200 \times 100} = \frac{16000}{20000} = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Solute in feed = $200 \times 100 = 20{,}000$ mg/h. Solute in foamate = $20 \times 800 = 16{,}000$ mg/h. Solute in bottoms = $180 \times 22.22 = 4000$ mg/h. Sum: $16{,}000 + 4000 = 20{,}000$ mg/h. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary, Q9(a)]**

*Explain the working principle of foam fractionation with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of bubble size, foam height, and air flow rate on the performance of the process.*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

Foam fractionation is based on the preferential adsorption of surface-active solutes at the gas–liquid interface of rising bubbles. The process involves the following sequential steps:

1. A surfactant-bearing feed is introduced into a vertical column.
2. A gas (air/N₂) is sparged from the bottom through a porous frit, generating fine, uniform bubbles.
3. Surfactant molecules adsorb at the gas–liquid interface of each bubble (described by the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm).
4. The bubble-solute aggregates rise and form a foam layer at the top of the column.
5. The foam is collected and collapsed mechanically, yielding a concentrated **foamate**.
6. The depleted bulk liquid is withdrawn as **bottoms**.

**Effect of Process Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Enrichment (E) | Effect on Recovery (R) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Smaller bubble size** | Slight increase (more surface area per unit gas) | Significant **increase** (more surface available) |
| **Larger foam height** | **Increase** (more drainage time) | **Decrease** (more solute refluxes back) |
| **Higher air flow rate** | **Decrease** (less drainage time) | **Increase** (more bubbles carry solute) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** High enrichment and high recovery cannot be achieved simultaneously in a single column. A compromise operating point must be selected, or a multi-stage (combination-mode) column must be used.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary, Q9(b)]**

*A foam fractionation unit operates in batch mode on a 10 L liquid charge containing 200 mg/L of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). After 30 minutes of air sparging, the foamate collected measures 0.5 L with an SDS concentration of 3200 mg/L. Calculate the enrichment ratio, the recovery, and the residual SDS concentration in the column.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Initial volume, $V_i = 10$ L
- Initial concentration, $C_i = 200$ mg/L
- Foamate volume, $V_f = 0.5$ L
- Foamate concentration, $C_f = 3200$ mg/L

**Step 1 — Enrichment Ratio:**

$$
E = \frac{C_f}{C_i} = \frac{3200}{200} = 16
$$

**Step 2 — Recovery (Mass of SDS in Foamate / Mass of SDS Initially):**

Initial mass of SDS:

$$
m_i = V_i \cdot C_i = 10 \times 200 = 2000 \text{ mg}
$$

Mass of SDS in foamate:

$$
m_f = V_f \cdot C_f = 0.5 \times 3200 = 1600 \text{ mg}
$$

Recovery:

$$
R = \frac{m_f}{m_i} = \frac{1600}{2000} = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%
$$

**Step 3 — Residual SDS Concentration:**

Residual volume of liquid in column (assuming volume change = foamate volume removed):

$$
V_{\text{res}} = V_i - V_f = 10 - 0.5 = 9.5 \text{ L}
$$

Mass balance:

$$
V_i C_i = V_{\text{res}} C_{\text{res}} + V_f C_f
$$

$$
10 \times 200 = 9.5 \times C_{\text{res}} + 0.5 \times 3200
$$

$$
2000 = 9.5 \, C_{\text{res}} + 1600
$$

$$
9.5 \, C_{\text{res}} = 400
$$

$$
C_{\text{res}} = \frac{400}{9.5} = 42.11 \text{ mg/L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The process has concentrated the SDS by a factor of 16 (from 200 to 3200 mg/L) while removing 80% of the total SDS from the original 10 L charge, leaving a residual of 42.11 mg/L in the 9.5 L of remaining liquid.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls that Cause Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Sign error in the Gibbs Isotherm:** Forgetting the negative sign yields a *negative* $\Gamma$, which is physically meaningless. The surface tension of a surfactant solution *decreases* with concentration, so $d\gamma / dC < 0$, making $\Gamma > 0$. Always state this explicitly.
> 2. **Factor-of-2 confusion:** The factor of 2 in the ionic-surfactant Gibbs Isotherm appears only when the ionic surfactant is the *sole* electrolyte. With excess inert salt, the factor is **absent**.
> 3. **Confusing enrichment with recovery:** $E = C_f / C_i$ measures *concentration*, while $R = (D \cdot C_f) / (F \cdot C_i)$ measures *mass yield*. A process can have $E = 100$ but $R = 0.10$ (high enrichment, low recovery), or $E = 2$ and $R = 0.95$ (low enrichment, high recovery).
> 4. **Mixing up $V_f$ with $D$:** In batch mode, $V_f$ is a volume (L); in continuous mode, $D$ is a flow rate (L/h). The mass balance formulas are identical in form but have *different units*.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify:** Always do a *backward mass balance check* at the end of a numerical problem. KTU examiners specifically award 1 mark for verification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Foam Fractionation (Module 2):**

- ✅ Foam fractionation is an *adsorptive bubble separation* technique based on selective adsorption of surface-active solutes at gas–liquid interfaces.
- ✅ The **driving force** is the reduction in surface free energy, quantified by surface excess $\Gamma$.
- ✅ The **master equation** is the **Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm**: $\Gamma = -(C / RT)(d\gamma / dC)$ for non-ionic surfactants.
- ✅ For ionic surfactants in pure solution, a **factor of 2** appears in the denominator: $\Gamma = -(1 / 2RT)(d\gamma / d\ln C)$.
- ✅ The **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)** is an *operational upper limit* — operating above CMC is wasteful because micelles do not contribute to surface excess.
- ✅ Key performance metrics: **Enrichment Ratio ($E$)**, **Recovery ($R$)**, **Separation Factor ($\alpha$)**.
- ✅ A fundamental **trade-off** exists between $E$ and $R$ in single-column operation: higher foam height → higher $E$, lower $R$.
- ✅ Five operating modes: **Batch, Continuous (Simple), Stripping, Enriching, and Combination**.
- ✅ **Enriching mode** uses a top spray to drain enriched liquid back into the bulk; **stripping mode** refluxes foam back down the column.
- ✅ The **combination mode** gives the highest enrichment by combining both reflux strategies.
- ✅ **Factors affecting performance**: air flow rate, foam height, feed rate, bubble size, pH, ionic strength, temperature, and the presence of antifoaming agents.
- ✅ **Mass balance equations** must always be satisfied: $F C_i = B C_b + D C_f$ (continuous) and $V_i C_i = V_f C_f + V_{\text{res}} C_{\text{res}}$ (batch).
- ✅ **Industrially important applications**: wastewater treatment (detergents, dyes), hydrometallurgy (metal ion pre-concentration), biotechnology (protein recovery), pharmaceutical industry (antibiotic purification), and nuclear waste treatment.
- ✅ For a 14-mark KTU question, expect a mix of: (1) a derivation (Gibbs Isotherm), (2) a numerical problem (mass balance, enrichment, recovery), and (3) a descriptive question (process description, factors, applications).
- ✅ **Always end a numerical answer with a verification** by checking the mass balance closure — examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foam Flotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Foam Flotation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Foam Flotation** is a gravity separation process (physico-chemical in nature) used to separate finely divided suspended particles (solid or liquid droplets) from a bulk liquid phase by selectively attaching them to the surface of rising gas bubbles. The bubble-particle aggregates, being less dense than the surrounding liquid, ascend to the surface where they accumulate as a mineralised froth (or scum) that is mechanically skimmed off, while the clarified (or depleted) liquid is withdrawn as the underflow.

In KTU parlance, flotation belongs to the family of **Adsorptive Bubble Separation Techniques (ABST)**, which exploit surface-active phenomena, hydrophobic interactions, and selective interfacial chemistry to achieve separations that are impossible by simple sedimentation or filtration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Foam Flotation is treated as a *rate-governed* (not equilibrium-governed) process. The recovery is dictated by **microprocess kinetics** (collision, attachment, detachment, transport) rather than by a thermodynamic partition coefficient.

### 1.2 Sub-Classifications of Foam Flotation

| Sub-Process | Description | Key Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| **Froth Flotation** | Air is sparged through a pulp; hydrophobic minerals attach to bubbles | Mineral/ore processing (most common in industry) |
| **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | Air is dissolved under pressure, then released as micro-bubbles | Wastewater, oil-water emulsions |
| **Induced Air Flotation (IAF)** | Air is mechanically induced via impeller/eductor | Oily wastewater, petrochemical streams |
| **Column Flotation** | Tall, narrow column with wash-water; counter-current operation | High-grade concentrates, fine particles |
| **Electroflotation** | H₂ and O₂ generated in-situ by electrolysis of water | Sludge thickening, polishing effluents |
| **Jet Flotation** | High-pressure jet injection of gas-liquid mixture | Small-scale, degasser applications |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are washing dishes in a sink full of oily water. You blow bubbles with a straw into the water. The tiny oil droplets, which are inherently *water-fearing* (hydrophobic), cling to the bubble surface like a child holding onto a balloon. The combined "oil + bubble" becomes buoyant, rises, and pops at the surface, leaving the oil as a thin film on top that you can easily scoop off with a spoon. **Foam Flotation does exactly this on an industrial scale!**

Three pre-requisites must be satisfied for flotation to work:

1. **Gas Bubbles must be present** (sparged or dissolved).
2. **Target species must be hydrophobic** (or made hydrophobic via collectors).
3. **Particle size must be in the floatable range** (typically **5 µm to 200 µm**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sub-Processes Inside a Flotation Cell

The flotation process is conceptually divided into three sequential and overlapping zones:

**Zone A – Pulp Zone (Sub-aeration Zone)**
- Located in the bulk of the cell.
- Gas bubbles are generated, dispersed, and rise through the slurry.
- Particle-bubble encounters occur here.

**Zone B – Froth Zone (Above Pulp)**
- A 3-phase foam (gas + liquid + solid) accumulates on top.
- Hydrophobic particles report here; hydrophilic ones drain back.
- Froth stability is critical to recovery.

**Zone C – Plateau Borders & Lamellae (Within Froth)**
- Interstitial liquid in the froth drains back into the pulp.
- Selective upgrading happens here; hydrophilic particles re-enter the pulp.
- Determines **froth recovery** $R_f$ (a number between **0.6 and 0.95** typically).

### 2.2 Hydrophobicity & Contact Angle — The Thermodynamic Driver

The **contact angle $\theta$** (measured through the liquid phase) is the single most important thermodynamic parameter. It quantifies the *wettability* of the solid surface by water.

- $\theta \to 0°$ → Strongly **hydrophilic** (e.g., silica, quartz) → Poor floatability.
- $\theta \to 90°$ → **Intermediate** (transition zone).
- $\theta \to 180°$ → Strongly **hydrophobic** (e.g., graphite, talc, coal, sulphur) → Excellent floatability.

**Young's Equation** (mechanical equilibrium of interfacial tensions at the three-phase contact line):

$$\gamma_{sg} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lg} \cos\theta$$

where:
- $\gamma_{sg}$ = solid-gas interfacial tension (N/m)
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid-liquid interfacial tension (N/m)
- $\gamma_{lg}$ = liquid-gas interfacial tension (N/m)
- $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle (degrees or radians)

> [!NOTE]
> **Work of adhesion (W_a)** between particle and bubble:
> $$W_a = \gamma_{lg}(1 + \cos\theta)$$
> The larger $\theta$, the larger $W_a$, the stronger the particle-bubble bond.

### 2.3 The Flotation Microprocess (KTU High-Yield Conceptual Framework)

A successful particle-to-froth journey requires the particle to clear **three sequential sub-processes** plus a transport step:

| Sub-Process | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Collision** | $P_c$ | Probability that a rising bubble physically encounters the particle | 0.01 – 0.5 |
| **Attachment** | $P_a$ | Probability that, after collision, the bubble-particle film ruptures and forms a 3-phase contact | 0.01 – 0.8 |
| **Detachment (Stability)** | $P_d$ | Probability that the attached particle remains on the bubble (no detachment) | 0.5 – 0.99 |
| **Transport** | $P_{tr}$ | Probability that the bubble reaches the froth without losing the particle | 0.7 – 0.99 |

The **overall flotation probability** is:

$$P = P_c \times P_a \times (1 - P_d) \times P_{tr}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Critical Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in every KTU 2024 question paper on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_c = \dfrac{3 d_p}{2 d_b}$ | Sutherland's collision efficiency (potential flow) | $d_p$ = particle diameter (m), $d_b$ = bubble diameter (m) |
| 2 | $P_c = \left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_b}\right)^2 \left(1.5 + \dfrac{4 \text{Re}_b^{0.72}}{15}\right)$ | Modified Sutherland (Gaudin's correction with bubble Reynolds number) | $\text{Re}_b$ = bubble Reynolds number |
| 3 | $t_{ind} = \dfrac{\text{const} \times (1 + \cos\theta)}{\pi \, d_b^2 \, \rho_l \, g \, \theta}$ | Induction time (s) — Dobby-Finch model | $t_{ind}$ in seconds |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{3 v_g P}{2 d_c}$ | Flotation rate constant (Kelsall's model) | $v_g$ = superficial gas velocity (m/s), $d_c$ = cell diameter (m) |
| 5 | $R = 1 - e^{-k \tau}$ | Recovery as a function of residence time | $\tau$ = residence time (s) |
| 6 | $v_b = \dfrac{g d_b^2 (\rho_l - \rho_g)}{18 \mu_l}$ | Stokes' rise velocity (small bubbles, $\text{Re} < 1$) | $\rho_l, \rho_g$ = densities (kg/m³), $\mu_l$ = viscosity (Pa·s) |
| 7 | $v_b = \dfrac{g d_b^2}{18 \nu}\left(\dfrac{\rho_l - \rho_g}{\rho_l}\right)$ | Kinematic form of Stokes' equation | $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s) |
| 8 | $R_f = \dfrac{m_{froth}}{m_{pulp}}$ | Froth recovery (ratio of mass reporting to concentrate) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_d = \exp\left(-\dfrac{C_1 \, \text{Ga}_p}{\sin^2(\theta/2)}\right)$ | Probability of detachment (Schulze model) | $\text{Ga}_p$ = Galilei number, $C_1$ = constant |
| 10 | $\text{Ga}_p = \dfrac{d_p^3 \, \rho_l \, g \, \Delta \rho}{\mu_l^2}$ | Galilei number (gravity / viscous ratio) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $P_a = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{t_c}{t_{ind}}\right)$ | Attachment probability (Sven-Nilsson) | $t_c$ = contact time (s) |
| 12 | $\text{We} = \dfrac{\rho_l v_b^2 d_p}{\gamma_{lg}(1-\cos\theta)}$ | Weber number (kinetic energy vs. surface energy barrier) | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $C^* = \dfrac{C_0(1 - R_f)(1 - R_c)}{1 - R_c R_f}$ | Kelsall's two-product equation for concentrate grade | $C_0$ = feed grade, $C^*$ = concentrate grade |
| 14 | $R_c = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}$ | Recovery in a continuous (plug flow) cell | $k, \tau$ as above |
| 15 | $\dfrac{d N}{dt} = -P \cdot E_{coll} \cdot N$ | Population-balance rate of particle removal | $N$ = particle number density |

### 2.5 Chemical Reagents in Flotation (Critical for KTU 14-mark questions)

| Reagent Class | Function | Example | Typical Dose (g/t of feed) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Collectors** | Render hydrophobic surfaces wettable by air | Potassium amyl xanthate (PAX), Sodium ethyl xanthate (SEX), Dithiophosphates | 20 – 200 |
| **Frothers** | Stabilize bubbles, reduce coalescence | Methyl isobutyl carbinol (MIBC), Pine oil, Polypropylene glycol ethers | 10 – 100 |
| **Activators** | Modify surface chemistry to enable collector adsorption | CuSO₄ (activates sphalerite for xanthate) | 50 – 500 |
| **Depressants** | Selectively prevent flotation of certain minerals | NaCN (depresses pyrite), Na₂SiO₃ (depresses silicates), Sodium hexametaphosphate | 100 – 1000 |
| **pH Regulators** | Control pH for optimal collector action | Lime (CaO), Soda ash (Na₂CO₃), H₂SO₄ | As required |
| **Dispersants** | Break up slime coatings | Sodium silicate, Quebracho | 50 – 200 |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mineral Processing:** Recovery of Cu, Pb, Zn, Ni, Mo, PGM, Au, and coal fines (more than **2 billion tonnes of ore** processed annually worldwide via flotation).
- **Wastewater Treatment:** DAF units remove oil, grease, fibres, and biomass from industrial effluents (petroleum refineries, food processing, paper mills).
- **De-inking:** Recycled paper / pulp industry uses flotation to remove ink particles.
- **Biomedical:** Pre-concentration of pathogens, algae harvesting, separation of micro-organisms.
- **Environmental Remediation:** Removal of heavy metals, hydrophobic pollutants, and plastics from water.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bubble Rise Velocity — Stokes' Regime

**Assumption:** Spherical bubble of diameter $d_b$ rising in a quiescent Newtonian liquid under creeping flow ($\text{Re} < 1$).

**Step 1: Force Balance on the bubble at terminal velocity**

At terminal velocity, the net force = 0. Three forces act on the bubble:

$$F_{buoyancy} - F_{gravity} - F_{drag} = 0$$

**Step 2: Express each force**

$$F_{buoyancy} = \dfrac{\pi d_b^3}{6} \rho_l g$$

$$F_{gravity} = \dfrac{\pi d_b^3}{6} \rho_g g$$

$$F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu_l d_b v_b \quad \text{(Stokes' drag, low Re)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into force balance**

$$\dfrac{\pi d_b^3}{6} \rho_l g - \dfrac{\pi d_b^3}{6} \rho_g g - 3 \pi \mu_l d_b v_b = 0$$

**Step 4: Factor out common terms and isolate $v_b$**

$$v_b = \dfrac{g d_b^2 (\rho_l - \rho_g)}{18 \mu_l}$$

This is the **Stokes' rise velocity** for small bubbles. Note that $v_b \propto d_b^2$, meaning **finer bubbles rise much more slowly**, which is a critical design trade-off in flotation (fine bubbles improve contact efficiency but reduce transport rate).

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity limit:** Stokes' law is valid for $\text{Re}_b < 1$, i.e., for $d_b < \approx 0.1$ mm in water. For larger bubbles ($d_b > 1$ mm), use the Davies–Taylor equation:
> $$v_b = 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$$
> (water-equivalent). For $0.1 < d_b < 1$ mm, the intermediate (Schiller-Naumann) regime applies.

### 3.2 Derivation of Collision Probability $P_c$ (Sutherland, 1948)

**Assumption:** Particle moving in streamline flow around a rising bubble of diameter $d_b$. Particle follows a streamline and is captured if its centre lies within a distance $d_p/2$ of the bubble surface.

**Step 1:** Consider a circular bubble cross-section in a uniform flow field. The streamlines can be represented as flow past a cylinder (2-D analogue) or sphere (3-D).

**Step 2:** The maximum stream-tube radius from which particles are captured equals the sum of the bubble radius and the particle radius:

$$r_{max} = \dfrac{d_b}{2} + \dfrac{d_p}{2}$$

**Step 3:** The area of the cross-section presented for collision:

$$A_{capture} = \pi r_{max}^2 = \pi \left(\dfrac{d_b + d_p}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** The area of the bubble cross-section normal to flow:

$$A_{bubble} = \pi \left(\dfrac{d_b}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 5:** Collision probability = ratio of capture area to bubble area (potential flow assumption):

$$P_c = \dfrac{A_{capture}}{A_{bubble}} = \dfrac{(d_b + d_p)^2}{d_b^2} = \left(1 + \dfrac{d_p}{d_b}\right)^2$$

**Step 6:** For $d_p \ll d_b$, apply binomial approximation $(1+x)^2 \approx 1 + 2x$, and subtract the unity (which represents the bubble itself):

$$P_c \approx \dfrac{3 d_p}{2 d_b} \quad \text{(for } d_p \ll d_b\text{)}$$

This is the famous **Sutherland equation**, valid for very fine particles relative to the bubble.

### 3.3 Derivation of Recovery vs. Residence Time

**Starting from the population balance equation** (well-mixed cell assumption):

$$\dfrac{d N(t)}{dt} = -k N(t)$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange:

$$\dfrac{d N}{N} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from $t = 0$ (when $N = N_0$) to $t = \tau$ (when $N = N_{out}$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N_{out}} \dfrac{d N}{N} = -k \int_0^{\tau} dt$$

**Step 3:** This yields:

$$\ln \left(\dfrac{N_{out}}{N_0}\right) = -k \tau$$

**Step 4:** Recovery is defined as $R = 1 - N_{out}/N_0$:

$$R = 1 - e^{-k \tau}$$

**Step 5:** For very small $k \tau$ (short residence time), linearize:

$$R \approx k \tau \quad (\text{first-order approximation})$$

**Step 6:** For very large $k \tau$ (long residence time), $R \to 1$ asymptotically. Recovery can never exceed 100%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 typical question type:** "A flotation cell has a rate constant $k = 0.5 \text{ min}^{-1}$. Calculate the residence time required to achieve 90% recovery."
>
> Solution: $\tau = -\dfrac{\ln(1 - R)}{k} = -\dfrac{\ln(0.1)}{0.5} = \dfrac{2.303}{0.5} \approx 4.6 \text{ min}$.

### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** In a laboratory flotation test, the following data are recorded:
- Bubble diameter: $d_b = 0.5$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Particle diameter: $d_p = 50$ µm = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- Liquid: water at 20 °C ($\mu_l = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho_l = 998$ kg/m³, $\rho_g \approx 1.2$ kg/m³)
- Contact angle: $\theta = 60°$
- Superficial gas velocity: $v_g = 0.02$ m/s
- Cell diameter: $d_c = 2$ m
- Flotation probability: assume $P = P_c \times P_a \times (1-P_d) = 0.3 \times 0.6 \times 0.95 = 0.171$

**Find:** (a) Bubble rise velocity (b) Rate constant $k$ (c) Recovery at $\tau = 8$ min.

**Solution:**

**(a) Bubble Rise Velocity:**

$$v_b = \dfrac{(9.81)(5 \times 10^{-4})^2 (998 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_b = \dfrac{9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 996.8}{1.804 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v_b = \dfrac{2.4445 \times 10^{-3}}{1.804 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 0.1355 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Rate Constant (Kelsall's model):**

$$k = \dfrac{3 v_g P}{2 d_c} = \dfrac{3 \times 0.02 \times 0.171}{2 \times 2} = \dfrac{0.01026}{4} \approx 0.00257 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(c) Recovery at $\tau = 8$ min = 480 s:**

$$R = 1 - e^{-k \tau} = 1 - e^{-0.00257 \times 480} = 1 - e^{-1.2336}$$

$$R = 1 - 0.2911 = 0.7089 \approx 70.9\%$$

### 3.5 Analytic Solution Table — Common KTU Sub-Question Scenarios

| Given Parameters | Asked to Find | Governing Equation | Steps |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d_b$, $d_p$ | $P_c$ | $P_c = \dfrac{3 d_p}{2 d_b}$ | Direct substitution |
| $d_b$, $\rho_l$, $\mu_l$ | $v_b$ | Stokes' law | Force balance |
| $k$, $\tau$ | $R$ | $R = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ | Exponential integration |
| $R$, $k$ | $\tau$ | $\tau = -\dfrac{\ln(1-R)}{k}$ | Inverse exponential |
| $\gamma_{sg}, \gamma_{sl}, \gamma_{lg}$ | $\theta$ | Young's equation | Cosine inverse |
| $d_p$, $\rho_l$, $\mu_l$ | $\text{Ga}_p$ | Galilei number formula | Direct substitution |
| $v_g$, $d_c$, $P$ | $k$ | $k = \dfrac{3 v_g P}{2 d_c}$ | Direct substitution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.2 Microprocess Flow — Particle-to-Froth Journey (Five-Step Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1 Suspension] --> S2[Step2 Collision]
    S2 --> S3[Step3 Attachment]
    S3 --> S4[Step4 Transport]
    S4 --> S5[Step5 Froth Recovery]
    S5 --> S6[Concentrate Output]

    S1 -.Probability Pc.-> S2
    S2 -.Probability Pa.-> S3
    S3 -.Stability 1-Pd.-> S4
    S4 -.Probability Ptr.-> S5
    S5 -.Froth Recovery Rf.-> S6

    H1[Hydrophilic Particle returns to pulp] -.Drains Back.-> S2
```

### 4.3 Three-Zone Conceptual Architecture of a Flotation Cell

```mermaid

    ZP3 --> ZF1
    ZF1 --> ZF2

    ZF1 --> T2[CONCENTRATE FROTH]
```
