Find the total thermal energy $Q_{reg}$ required (neglecting gas sensible heat for clarity). **Step 1 — Sensible Heat of the Solid** The energy to raise the solid temperature is: $$Q_{solid} = M_{bed} \, c_{p,s} \, (T_{reg} - T_{ads})$$ **Step 2 — Latent Heat of Desorption (Release of Bond Energy)** When the adsorbed phase is liberated, the bond energy $\Delta H_{ads}$ previously released during adsorption must now be re-supplied: $$Q_{des} = m_{ads} \, \Delta H_{ads}$$ **Step 3 — Sensible Heat of the Desorbed Vapour (if vapour exits hot)** The desorbed gas leaves the bed at some average temperature $T_{avg}$: $$Q_{vap} = m_{ads} \, c_{p,g} \, (T_{reg} - T_{feed})$$ **Step 4 — Sensible Heat of the Carrier / Purge Gas** A purge of mass $m_{purge}$ must itself be heated from $T_{feed}$ to $T_{reg}$: $$Q_{purge} = m_{purge} \, c_{p,g} \, (T_{reg} - T_{feed})$$ **Step 5 — Total Regeneration Energy** $$Q_{reg} = M_{bed} c_{p,s} \Delta T + m_{ads} \Delta H_{ads} + m_{ads} c_{p,g} \Delta T + m_{purge} c_{p,g} \Delta T$$ $$\boxed{Q_{reg} = (M_{bed} c_{p,s} + (m_{ads} + m_{purge}) c_{p,g}) (T_{reg} - T_{ads}) + m_{ads} \Delta H_{ads}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Reality Check:** The **dominant** term in most industrial TSA units is the **bed sensible heat** ($M_{bed} c_{p,s} \Delta T$), often 60–80 % of the total. This is why large beds use **TSA with thermal storage** or **indirect heating** to recover bed heat. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Driving Force (LDF) Model for Desorption **Statement:** Derive the time-dependent desorption loading $q(t)$ from a single spherical adsorbent particle initially at $q_0$ and exposed to a purge gas giving an equilibrium surface loading $q^*$. **Step 1 — Physical Transport Assumption** Mass transfer across the particle boundary layer follows Fick's law. For a sphere, the average concentration $\bar{q}(t)$ evolves according to the **Glueckauf approximation**: $$\frac{\partial \bar{q}}{\partial t} = \frac{15 D_e}{r_p^2} (q^* - \bar{q})$$ where $D_e$ is the effective intraparticle diffusivity (m²/s) and $r_p$ is the particle radius (m). **Step 2 — Define the LDF Coefficient** $$k_L = \frac{15 D_e}{r_p^2} \quad [\text{s}^{-1}]$$ So the equation becomes: $$\frac{dq}{dt} = k_L (q^* - q)$$ **Step 3 — Solve the First-Order ODE** This is a linear first-order ODE. Rearrange: $$\frac{dq}{q - q^*} = -k_L \, dt$$ **Step 4 — Integrate from $q(0) = q_0$ to $q(t)$** $$\int_{q_0}^{q(t)} \frac{dq}{q - q^*} = -k_L \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{q(t) - q^*}{q_0 - q^*}\right) = -k_L t$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $q(t)$** $$\boxed{\,q(t) = q^* + (q_0 - q^*) \, e^{-k_L t}\,}$$ **Step 6 — Specialise to *Pure* Thermal Desorption** If the purge gas carries away the desorbed species efficiently, $q^* \to 0$ (infinite sink). The expression simplifies to: $$q(t) = q_0 \, e^{-k_L t}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **95 % Desorption Time ($t_{95}$):** Set $q(t_{95}) = 0.05 q_0$ and solve: > $$t_{95} = \frac{3.0}{k_L} = \frac{r_p^2}{5 D_e}$$ > This is the **design residence time** for regeneration in a TSA cycle. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Heat-Front Velocity in a Fixed Bed **Statement:** A hot purge gas at $T_g$ enters a bed initially at $T_s^0$. Determine the velocity of the temperature front. **Step 1 — Local Energy Balance on a Differential Slice $dz$** $$\rho_{bed} c_{p,bed} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_{bed} \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial z^2} - \rho_g c_{p,g} u_0 \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}$$ **Step 2 — Neglect Conduction (High Peclet — typical for gas flow)** $$\rho_{bed} c_{p,bed} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = - \rho_g c_{p,g} u_0 \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to a Moving Coordinate $\zeta = z - v_{th} t$** A constant-shape thermal wave implies $\partial T / \partial t = -v_{th} \partial T / \partial z$, so: $$\rho_{bed} c_{p,bed} (-v_{th}) \frac{dT}{d\zeta} = - \rho_g c_{p,g} u_0 \frac{dT}{d\zeta}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the Thermal Front Velocity** $$\boxed{\,v_{th} = \frac{\rho_g c_{p,g}}{\rho_{bed} c_{p,bed}} \, u_0\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical values:** $\rho_g \approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{bed} \approx 700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, ratio $\sim 0.0017$. For $u_0 = 0.1 \text{ m/s}$, the thermal front moves at $\sim 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.6 \text{ m/h}$ — a 2 m bed therefore takes **3+ hours** to heat through. This is why TSA cycles are slow. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables | Parameter ↑ | Working Capacity $\Delta q$ | Regeneration Energy $Q_{reg}$ | Cycle Time | Product Purity | |---|---|---|---|---| | $T_{reg}$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ (more heating) | ↑ | | $P_{ads}$ ↑ | ↑ (favours loading) | – | – | ↑ | | $P_{reg}$ ↓ | ↑ (favours stripping) | ↓ (less heat needed if vacuum-aided) | ↓ | ↑ | | $u_{0,purge}$ ↑ | ↑ initially | ↑ (more gas to heat) | ↓ | Mixed | | Particle $r_p$ ↓ | ↑ (faster LDF) | – | ↓ | ↑ | | $\Delta H_{ads}$ ↑ | ↑ (more capacity) | ↑↑ | ↑ | – | | $\varepsilon$ ↑ | – (less solid) | ↓ (less bed mass) | ↓ | ↓ (lower capacity) > [!TIP] > **The fundamental TSA trade-off:** Higher $T_{reg}$ improves capacity but consumes more energy. The **optimum** is the lowest $T_{reg}$ that still gives a *specified* residual loading — typically $\le 1$ % of saturation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Schematic of a Complete TSA Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Gas Inlet
Tads, Pads, y_in] --> B[Three-Way Valve V1] B --> C[Adsorption Step
Cold Bed
MTZ Advances] C --> D[Clean Product Gas Out
V2 Open to Product] D --> E{Bed Saturated?
Breakthrough t_b} E -- Yes --> F[Isolate Feed
Close V1 to Feed] E -- No --> C F --> G[Switch to Hot Purge
Treg Inlet Open] G --> H[Heat-Up Stage
Bed T: Tads to Treg] H --> I[Hot Purge Stage
Deep Desorption] I --> J[Switch to Cold Purge
Cool-Down Stage] J --> K[Bed T back to Tads
Ready for Next Cycle] K --> B I --> L[Desorbed Vapour to
Condenser / Recovery] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram of Heat-and-Mass Transfer Interactions in a Regenerating Bed ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P1[Purge Gas Stream] A1[Hot Inlet Gas
Treg, y=0] end subgraph P2[Adsorbent Particle - Sphere] B1[External Film
kf, hc] B2[Intraparticle Diffusion
De, ke] B3[Adsorbed Phase
q x, t] end subgraph P3[Outlet Stream] C1[Hot Outlet Gas
Treg, y y out] C2[Recovered Vapour
to Condenser] end A1 -- convective heat and mass --> B1 B1 -- conduction and diffusion --> B2 B2 -- surface reaction --> B3 B3 -- desorption flux --> B2 B2 -- back diffusion --> B1 B1 -- bulk transport --> C1 C1 --> C2 ``` ### 4.3 TSA Cycle State Diagram (Time-Temperature-Loading) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Stage 1 Adsorption
T equals Tads
q rises to q_sat] S2[Stage 2 Heat-Up
T rises Tads to Treg
q falls slowly] S3[Stage 3 Hot Purge
T equals Treg
q falls to q_residual] S4[Stage 4 Cool-Down
T falls Treg to Tads
q approximately constant] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 -. recycle after 1 cycle .- S1 ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — TSA vs PSA Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Feed Gas with Adsorbable Component] --> Q1{Strong adsorption?
large Delta H} Q1 -- Yes --> TSA[Use Thermal Swing Adsorption
Energy intensive but high capacity] Q1 -- No --> Q2{High purity required at
large flowrate?} Q2 -- Yes --> PSA[Use Pressure Swing Adsorption
Fast, mechanical work] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Bulk separation
low cost target} Q3 -- Yes --> Mixed[Consider Membrane or
Hybrid TSA-PSA] Q3 -- No --> Eval[Detailed Economic
Analysis Required] ``` ### 4.5 Annotations and Engineering Notes - **V1, V2** denote automated change-over valves (typically pneumatic with PLC control). - **MTZ** — Mass Transfer Zone — the region of active adsorption front inside the bed. - **$q_{sat}$** — Saturation loading at feed conditions. - **$q_{residual}$** — Residual loading after regeneration (target: < 1 % of $q_{sat}$). - **Cycle Synchronisation:** In a two-bed TSA system, one bed is in adsorption while the other is being regenerated — yielding **continuous product flow**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q.A.1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** *Define Thermal Swing Adsorption. State **two** advantages and **one** limitation of TSA over Pressure Swing Adsorption.* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA) is a cyclic adsorption-regeneration process in which a spent adsorbent bed is regenerated by raising its temperature (typically 50–300 °C above feed temperature) so that the equilibrium loading of the adsorbed species falls, liberating the solute into a purge gas stream. **Advantages of TSA over PSA:** 1. Achieves **much deeper regeneration** (residual loading $\le 1$ % of saturation), because thermal driving forces are far stronger than pressure driving forces. 2. Suitable for **strongly adsorbed species** (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, VOCs) that are difficult to strip by pressure reduction alone. **Limitation of TSA:** - Long cycle times (hours) and high energy consumption (regeneration heat ~ 0.5–5 MJ per kg of adsorbent), making TSA unsuitable for very fast cycles or energy-sensitive processes. *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for each advantage, 1 mark for the limitation. Total: 3 marks.)* --- **Q.A.2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]** *Explain the role of the **purge gas** in thermal desorption. What properties should an ideal purge gas possess?* **Model Answer:** **Role of Purge Gas:** The purge gas serves **two coupled functions** during thermal desorption: 1. **Heat carrier** — it transports thermal energy from the heater to the bed, raising the adsorbent temperature to $T_{reg}$. 2. **Mass carrier** — it sweeps the desorbed vapour out of the bed, maintaining a low partial pressure of the adsorbate at the solid surface, thereby *continuously shifting the equilibrium* and driving desorption to completion. **Ideal Properties of a Purge Gas:** - **Low heat capacity ratio** — so it does not require excessive energy to heat (some prefer inert $\text{N}_2$, some use steam for its very high $c_p$). - **Inert / non-adsorbing** — should not compete with the target adsorbate for sites. - **Low cost and easy to separate** from the desorbed product (so it can be recycled). - **Non-flammable, non-toxic, dry** (to avoid co-adsorption of water). > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often say *"the purge gas desorbs the molecules"* — this is **physically wrong**. The **heat** desorbs the molecules; the purge gas only **removes** them. Examiners deduct ½ mark for this confusion. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **four stages of a Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA) cycle** for gas purification. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A TSA unit is used to remove **acetone vapour** from an air stream using activated carbon. The following data are given: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Bed mass, $M_{bed}$ | 500 kg | | Bed avg. heat capacity, $c_{p,s}$ | 0.92 kJ/(kg·K) | | Mass adsorbed per cycle, $m_{ads}$ | 8.0 kg acetone | | Latent heat of acetone, $\lambda$ | 540 kJ/kg | | Adsorption temperature, $T_{ads}$ | 308 K | | Regeneration temperature, $T_{reg}$ | 473 K | | Purge gas (N₂) mass per cycle | 30 kg | | $c_{p,g}$ for N₂ | 1.04 kJ/(kg·K) | **Calculate the total regeneration energy $Q_{reg}$ per cycle.** **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** The four stages are: **Stage 1 — Adsorption:** Feed gas containing the adsorbable impurity (acetone vapour in air) is passed through a clean, cool bed at $T_{ads}$. The mass-transfer zone forms at the inlet and advances through the bed. Purified gas exits the outlet. Operation continues until breakthrough is detected at the outlet. **Stage 2 — Heat-Up:** Feed is cut off. A hot inert purge gas (e.g., N₂ at 473 K) is introduced, typically in the **counter-current** direction. The bed temperature rises from $T_{ads}$ to $T_{reg}$. The adsorbed species are desorbed and swept toward the feed end by the purge. **Stage 3 — Hot Purge (Stripping):** With the bed at $T_{reg}$, the hot purge continues until the exit concentration of acetone falls to a specified low level (e.g., $< 100 \text{ ppm}$). This stage ensures deep regeneration to a low residual loading $q_{res}$. **Stage 4 — Cool-Down:** The hot purge is replaced by a cold purge (or the bed is left to cool by natural convection), bringing the bed back to $T_{ads}$, ready for the next cycle. Cooling is the slowest step in some designs. *A neat labelled sketch showing the four stages is essential (3 of 7 marks).* **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Using the **Derivation 1** formula from Section 3.1: $$Q_{reg} = M_{bed} c_{p,s} \Delta T + m_{ads} \Delta H_{ads} + m_{ads} c_{p,g} \Delta T + m_{purge} c_{p,g} \Delta T$$ **Given:** - $\Delta T = T_{reg} - T_{ads} = 473 - 308 = 165$ K - $\Delta H_{ads}$ for acetone on activated carbon $\approx 1.2 \times \lambda$ (binding is stronger than vaporisation), use $\Delta H_{ads} \approx 50$ kJ/mol = $50 \times (1/0.058) = 862$ kJ/kg - $m_{ads} = 8.0$ kg, $m_{purge} = 30$ kg, $M_{bed} = 500$ kg **Step 1 — Solid sensible heat:** $$Q_{solid} = 500 \times 0.92 \times 165 = 75{,}900 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 2 — Desorption enthalpy:** $$Q_{des} = 8.0 \times 862 = 6{,}896 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 3 — Vapour sensible heat:** $$Q_{vap} = 8.0 \times 1.04 \times 165 = 1{,}373 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 4 — Purge gas sensible heat:** $$Q_{purge} = 30 \times 1.04 \times 165 = 5{,}148 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 5 — Total:** $$\boxed{Q_{reg} = 75{,}900 + 6{,}896 + 1{,}373 + 5{,}148 = 89{,}317 \text{ kJ} \approx 89.3 \text{ MJ per cycle}}$$ **Interpretation:** The bed sensible heat ($75.9$ MJ) dominates, accounting for $\sim 85$ % of the total. This is consistent with industrial TSA experience and motivates heat-recovery strategies. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Exam]** **(a)** Derive the **Linear Driving Force (LDF)** model for desorption from a single spherical adsorbent particle. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An activated carbon granule of radius $r_p = 1.5$ mm has effective intraparticle diffusivity $D_e = 4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ for $\text{CO}_2$. The granule is initially loaded with $q_0 = 6.0$ mol/kg at $T_{ads} = 298$ K. It is plunged into a high-purity N₂ purge at $T_{reg} = 423$ K, where the equilibrium loading is $q^* = 0.4$ mol/kg. **Calculate:** 1. The LDF mass-transfer coefficient $k_L$. 2. The desorbed loading after $t = 30$ minutes. 3. The time required for **95 % desorption**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** The derivation follows Section 3.2 above. The full solution requires: 1. Fick's second law for a sphere (1 mark). 2. Glueckauf approximation to replace radial profile by a lumped driving force (1 mark). 3. Identification of $k_L = 15 D_e / r_p^2$ (1 mark). 4. Final ODE: $dq/dt = k_L (q^* - q)$ (1 mark). 5. Integration from $q_0$ to $q(t)$ (1 mark). 6. Final solution: $q(t) = q^* + (q_0 - q^*) e^{-k_L t}$ (1 mark). 7. **Assumptions** (1 mark): isothermal particle, constant $D_e$, spherical geometry, linear driving force valid (i.e., $Bi_{mass} \gg 1$), and external film resistance neglected relative to intraparticle diffusion. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — LDF coefficient (1 mark):** $$k_L = \frac{15 D_e}{r_p^2} = \frac{15 \times (4 \times 10^{-9})}{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$ $$k_L = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-8}}{2.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.02667 \text{ s}^{-1} = 2.667 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Desorbed loading after 30 min = 1800 s (3 marks):** $$q(1800) = q^* + (q_0 - q^*) \, e^{-k_L t}$$ $$q(1800) = 0.4 + (6.0 - 0.4) \, e^{-0.02667 \times 1800}$$ $$q(1800) = 0.4 + 5.6 \times e^{-48.0}$$ Since $e^{-48} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-21}$ (effectively zero): $$\boxed{q(1800) \approx 0.4 \text{ mol/kg} \approx q^*}$$ **Step 3 — Time for 95 % desorption (3 marks):** In the limit $q^* \to 0$ relative to $q_0$, the desorption fraction is: $$\frac{q_0 - q(t)}{q_0 - q^*} = 1 - e^{-k_L t} = 0.95$$ $$e^{-k_L t} = 0.05 \implies k_L t = \ln(20) = 2.996$$ $$t_{95} = \frac{2.996}{0.02667} = 112.3 \text{ s} \approx 1.87 \text{ minutes}$$ $$\boxed{t_{95} \approx 1.9 \text{ min}}$$ **Conclusion:** A 30-minute purge is wildly excessive for this particle — only ~2 min is needed. The bed-scale design (which is much slower due to heat-transfer and residence-time effects) is the rate-limiting step, not the particle itself. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing desorption with adsorption direction** in the LDF equation (sign errors in $q - q^*$). > 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (mm vs m, min vs s) — a single unit slip costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Omitting the assumptions** in derivations — the *Assumptions* carry 1 mark in every KTU derivation question. > 4. **Mixing TSA with PSA** terminology — examiners do **not** award marks for pressure-swing arguments in a thermal-swing question. > 5. **Neglecting bed sensible heat** — a common error is to compute only $\Delta H_{ads}$ and report $Q_{reg}$, missing 80 % of the true energy demand. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Thermal Desorption of Gases** - **TSA = Temperature Swing Adsorption** — regeneration by heating. - **Four stages:** Adsorption → Heat-Up → Hot Purge → Cool-Down. - **Driving force** is the **exothermic heat of adsorption** $\Delta H_{ads}$; raising $T$ shifts equilibrium *off* the solid. - **Working capacity** $\Delta q = q^*_{ads} - q^*_{reg}$ — the design metric. - **Heating methods:** Direct hot purge, indirect (coils), electric, steam, microwave. - **Counter-current heating** is industry-preferred — purges contaminants back out the feed end. - **LDF model:** $dq/dt = k_L (q^* - q)$, with $k_L = 15 D_e / r_p^2$. - **LDF solution:** $q(t) = q^* + (q_0 - q^*) e^{-k_L t}$. - **95 % desorption time:** $t_{95} \approx 3.0 / k_L = r_p^2 / (5 D_e)$ (infinite-sink limit). - **Regeneration energy** is dominated by **bed sensible heat** (60–85 %), not by $\Delta H_{ads}$. - **Thermal front velocity** in a packed bed: $v_{th} = (\rho_g c_{p,g} / \rho_{bed} c_{p,bed}) u_0$ — typically mm/s, hence TSA is slow (hours). - **Industrial applications:** VOC abatement, biogas upgrading, compressed-air drying, carbon capture, nuclear waste treatment. - **TSA vs PSA:** TSA → strong adsorbates, deep regeneration, slow; PSA → weak adsorbates, fast cycles, mechanical work. - **Ergun equation** governs bed pressure drop and limits purge gas velocity. - **Sign convention trap:** In LDF, $(q^* - q)$ is the driving force — *not* $(q - q^*)$. > **End of Module 3 — Topic: Adsorption-Desorption Operations: Thermal Desorption of Gases**

topics.title: Activated Carbon Solute Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Activated Carbon Solute Recovery # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Activated Carbon Solute Recovery** is a separation process that utilizes the exceptionally high internal surface area of activated carbon (typically **500–1500 m²/g**) to preferentially adsorb target solute molecules from a fluid stream (liquid or gas), followed by a controlled desorption (regeneration) step that releases the concentrated solute as a recoverable product. The process is fundamentally a **cyclic adsorption–desorption operation** governed by physical (van der Waals) or chemical interactions between the solute and the carbonaceous adsorbent surface. It is classified under the broader category of **Solid–Fluid Separation Processes** in the KTU 2024 Novel Separation Processes syllabus. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECHT756):** > Activated carbon processes are considered "novel" in the sense that they exploit **nanoporous adsorbents** (microporous: < 2 nm, mesoporous: 2–50 nm) and modern regeneration schemes (microwave, ultrasonic, supercritical CO₂) beyond classical fixed-bed operation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **magnetic filing cabinet drawer** filled with thousands of tiny, sticky sponges (the activated carbon granules). When you pour a dilute, smelly, or colored solution through it: - **Adsorption Phase (Loading):** The "sponges" grab and hold the unwanted molecules (solute), letting the clean solvent pass through. - **Desorption Phase (Regeneration):** You then either heat the sponges, blast them with steam, or rinse them with a strong solvent — releasing the captured molecules in a **concentrated, recoverable form**. > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **reusable water filter jug (e.g., Brita-style)**. The carbon block traps chlorine, odors, and organic contaminants. When exhausted, it cannot simply be "squeezed out" in real life — but in industrial activated carbon systems, the regeneration step *is* the "squeezing," recovering the concentrated impurities for disposal or reuse. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value | Units | |-----------|---------------|-------| | BET Surface Area | **500 – 1500** | m²/g | | Apparent Density | **0.4 – 0.6** | g/cm³ | | Particle Porosity | **0.4 – 0.6** | dimensionless | | Mean Pore Diameter | **0.5 – 5.0** | nm | | Iodine Number | **800 – 1200** | mg/g | | Methylene Blue Number | **100 – 300** | mg/g | > [!NOTE] > **Iodine Number** is the most cited industrial quality metric — it represents the milligrams of iodine adsorbed per gram of carbon and is directly correlated with micropore volume. ## 1.4 Classification of Activated Carbon - **Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC):** Particle size < **0.18 mm**, used in batch contactors. - **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC):** Particle size **0.6 – 2.4 mm**, used in fixed/moving beds. - **Extruded/Pelletized AC:** Used in gas-phase applications and solvent recovery. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Sequence (The Recovery Cycle) The activated carbon solute recovery process operates in **four logical stages**: 1. **Loading (Adsorption):** Solute-laden feed enters the carbon bed. Solute molecules diffuse through the boundary layer, then through the macro-/mesopores, finally adsorbing onto the micropore walls. 2. **Exhaustion Detection:** Breakthrough curve monitoring identifies when outlet concentration reaches a pre-set threshold (typically **C/C₀ = 0.05 – 0.10**). 3. **Regeneration (Desorption):** The spent carbon is treated to release the adsorbed solute via: - **Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA):** Heating to **100 – 850 °C** in inert/steam atmosphere. - **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA):** Reducing partial pressure. - **Solvent Elution:** Washing with hot solvent or acid/alkali. 4. **Recovery & Reuse:** The desorbed solute is condensed/separated; the regenerated carbon returns to service. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why micropores dominate:** Micropores (< 2 nm) provide overlapping potential fields from opposing pore walls, vastly enhancing the adsorption potential — this is why **surface area alone is insufficient**; pore-size distribution is equally critical. - **Why regeneration is necessary:** Adsorption is **thermodynamically favored but economically cyclic** — disposal of spent carbon is expensive (₹₹₹/kg) and environmentally regulated; regeneration makes the process economically and ecologically sustainable. - **Why TSA is preferred for liquid feeds:** Liquid solutes often have low volatility, requiring **elevated temperature** to overcome adsorption enthalpy rather than pressure reduction. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Application / Boundary Condition | Units | |---|----------|----------------------------------|-------| | 1 | $q = \dfrac{x}{m}$ | Solid-phase solute concentration ($x$ = mass solute, $m$ = mass carbon) | g/g | | 2 | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)V}{m}$ | Equilibrium loading from batch test | mg/g | | 3 | $\dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{b\,C_e}{1 + b\,C_e}\,q_m$ | **Langmuir Isotherm** (monolayer, homogeneous) | g/g | | 4 | $\dfrac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | **Freundlich Isotherm** (heterogeneous, empirical) | (mg/g)(L/mg)¹/ⁿ | | 5 | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{b\,q_m} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$ | **Linearized Langmuir** (plot for $q_m$, $b$) | — | | 6 | $\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C_e$ | **Linearized Freundlich** (plot for $K_F$, $n$) | — | | 7 | $t_b = \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_b \, Z}{u \, C_0}$ | Breakthrough time (ideal, no MTZ) | s | | 8 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_s \, Z}{u \, C_0}$ | **MTZ length** equivalent time | s | | 9 | $\varepsilon \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \rho_b \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = D_{ax}\dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$ | **Fixed-bed axial dispersion model** | — | | 10 | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_s \left( q^* - q \right)$ | **LDF (Linear Driving Force)** kinetic model | s⁻¹ | | 11 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (q_{exp} - q_{calc})^2}{\sum (q_{exp} - \bar{q}_{exp})^2}$ | **Isotherm best-fit coefficient** | dimensionless | | 12 | $\dot{Q}_{regen} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda_s$ | **Steam consumption** in TSA regeneration | kW | > [!NOTE] > **Symbols:** $C_0$ = inlet concentration, $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration, $q_m$ = max monolayer capacity, $b$ = Langmuir affinity constant, $K_F$ = Freundlich constant, $n$ = heterogeneity factor, $Z$ = bed height, $u$ = superficial velocity, $\rho_b$ = bulk density, $D_{ax}$ = axial dispersion coefficient, $k_s$ = LDF mass transfer coefficient, $q^*$ = equilibrium solid loading. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Drinking Water Treatment:** Removal of trihalomethanes, pesticides, endocrine disruptors. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Recovery of antibiotics, vitamins, and APIs from fermentation broth. - **Gold Mining (CIC/CIL):** Activated carbon adsorbs gold–cyanide complex — this is a **$20+ billion/year global industry**. - **Solvent Recovery (Paint/Printing):** Recovery of toluene, xylene, acetone from exhaust air. - **Air Purification:** Gas masks, HVAC systems, biogas upgrading (CO₂/CH₄ separation). - **Sugar Decolorization:** Replacing bone char in refinery operations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Isotherm ### Assumptions 1. Monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface. 2. All adsorption sites are energetically equivalent. 3. No interactions between adsorbed molecules. 4. Each site holds only one molecule. ### Derivation Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered. Adsorption rate (forward): $r_a = k_a \, C \, (1 - \theta)$ Desorption rate (reverse): $r_d = k_d \, \theta$ At equilibrium: $$ r_a = r_d \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k_a \, C \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta $$ Solving for $\theta$: $$ k_a \, C - k_a \, C \, \theta = k_d \, \theta $$ $$ k_a \, C = \theta \, (k_d + k_a \, C) $$ $$ \theta = \dfrac{k_a \, C}{k_d + k_a \, C} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d)\, C}{1 + (k_a/k_d)\, C} $$ Defining $b = k_a / k_d$ and noting that total loading $q = q_m \, \theta$: $$ q = \dfrac{b \, q_m \, C}{1 + b \, C} $$ $$ \boxed{\dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{b\, q_m\, C_e}{1 + b\, C_e}} $$ **Linearized form** (for graphical determination of $q_m$ and $b$): $$ \dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{b\, q_m} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m} $$ A plot of $C_e/q_e$ vs. $C_e$ yields slope = $1/q_m$ and intercept = $1/(b\,q_m)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Linear Driving Force (LDF) Approximation The rigorous solid-phase diffusion model is a partial differential equation requiring numerical solution. The LDF approximation assumes the average driving force is the difference between surface and equilibrium loadings. Starting from Fick's law for spherical particle of radius $r_p$: $$ \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} = D_s \left( \dfrac{\partial^2 q}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{2}{r}\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial r} \right) $$ Averaging over the particle volume and using Glueckauf's approximation: $$ \overline{\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t}} = \dfrac{15\, D_s}{r_p^2} \left( q^* - \bar{q} \right) $$ Defining the LDF coefficient $k_s = 15\, D_s / r_p^2$: $$ \boxed{\dfrac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = k_s \left( q^* - \bar{q} \right)} $$ This is the most widely used **kinetic expression in KTU board examinations** for fixed-bed adsorption modeling. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Breakthrough Time (Ideal Plug Flow, No MTZ) Consider a bed of height $Z$, cross-section $A$, bulk density $\rho_b$, superficial velocity $u$, inlet concentration $C_0$. **Mass balance — solute entering the bed at saturation loading $q_b$:** $$ \text{Time to saturate} = \dfrac{\text{carbon mass} \times \text{loading}}{\text{flow} \times \text{concentration}} $$ $$ t_b = \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot A \cdot Z \cdot q_b}{u \cdot A \cdot C_0} $$ $$ \boxed{t_b = \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot q_b \cdot Z}{u \cdot C_0}} $$ > **Substitution Check:** Dimensions = $\dfrac{(\text{g/L})(\text{g/g})(\text{cm})}{(\text{cm/s})(\text{g/L})} = \text{s}$ ✓ ### Effect of Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) In reality, the saturation front is not sharp. Defining $\theta$ as the fractional capacity used in MTZ ($\theta = 0.5$ for symmetric MTZ): $$ t_{b,\text{real}} = t_{b,\text{ideal}} - \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_s \, Z_MTZ}{u \, C_0} \cdot \theta $$ Where $Z_{MTZ}$ is the length of the mass transfer zone. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Langmuir Isotherm Parameter Determination **Given:** Batch equilibrium data at 25 °C for phenol adsorption on GAC: | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) | |---|---| | 10 | 28.5 | | 30 | 65.0 | | 60 | 95.0 | | 100 | 120.0 | | 150 | 138.0 | **Find:** $q_m$ and $b$ using linearized Langmuir. ### Step 1: Compute $C_e / q_e$ | $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e/q_e$ | |---|---|---| | 10 | 28.5 | 0.3509 | | 30 | 65.0 | 0.4615 | | 60 | 95.0 | 0.6316 | | 100 | 120.0 | 0.8333 | | 150 | 138.0 | 1.0870 | ### Step 2: Linear regression of $C_e/q_e$ vs. $C_e$ Using least squares: - $\bar{C_e} = 70$, $\bar{(C_e/q_e)} = 0.6729$ - Slope $m = \dfrac{\sum(C_e - \bar{C_e})(C_e/q_e - \bar{C_e/q_e})}{\sum(C_e - \bar{C_e})^2} = 0.00544$ - Intercept $c = 0.6729 - (0.00544)(70) = 0.2921$ ### Step 3: Extract parameters $$ q_m = \dfrac{1}{m} = \dfrac{1}{0.00544} \approx 183.8 \;\text{mg/g} $$ $$ b = \dfrac{m}{c} = \dfrac{0.00544}{0.2921} \approx 0.0186 \;\text{L/mg} $$ ### Step 4: Verification (R²) $$ R^2 \approx 0.998 $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{q = \dfrac{0.0186 \times 183.8 \times C_e}{1 + 0.0186 \times C_e} = \dfrac{3.42 \, C_e}{1 + 0.0186\, C_e} \;\text{mg/g}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show units at every intermediate step. Marks are specifically allocated for the substitution step (Step 3 above) — examiners commonly deduct marks if you write $q_m$ without the unit mg/g. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Breakthrough Time in a GAC Column **Given:** - Bed height: $Z = 1.5$ m - Bulk density: $\rho_b = 480$ kg/m³ - Superficial velocity: $u = 0.005$ m/s - Inlet concentration: $C_0 = 50$ mg/L - Langmuir $q_m = 184$ mg/g, $b = 0.0186$ L/mg **Find:** Breakthrough time at $C_b = 5$ mg/L ($C_b/C_0 = 0.10$). ### Step 1: Equilibrium loading at $C_b$ $$ q_b = \dfrac{3.42 \times 5}{1 + 0.0186 \times 5} = \dfrac{17.10}{1.093} \approx 15.65 \;\text{mg/g} $$ ### Step 2: Apply breakthrough equation $$ t_b = \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot q_b \cdot Z}{u \cdot C_0} $$ Converting units to be consistent: - $\rho_b = 480$ kg/m³ = $480 \times 10^3$ g/m³ - $q_b = 15.65$ mg/g = $15.65 \times 10^{-3}$ g/g - $C_0 = 50$ mg/L = $50 \times 10^{-3}$ g/L $$ t_b = \dfrac{(480 \times 10^3)(15.65 \times 10^{-3})(1.5)}{(0.005)(50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000)} $$ $$ t_b = \dfrac{(480)(15.65 \times 10^{-3})(1.5)}{(0.005)(50)} $$ $$ t_b = \dfrac{11.268}{0.250} \approx 45.1 \;\text{minutes} $$ ### Result $$ \boxed{t_b \approx 45 \;\text{min}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** The most common error in KTU examinations is mixing mg/L with g/m³ and mg/g with kg/kg. Always use **one consistent unit system** (SI preferred). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Block Flow Diagram — Complete Solute Recovery Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Solution
C0, Q] --> B[Pre-filter
SS removal] B --> C[Adsorption Column
GAC Bed Z, dp] C --> D{Outlet Monitor
C vs C0} D -->|C less than Cbreak| E[Treated Effluent
Discharge] D -->|C greater than Cbreak| F[Regeneration Mode] F --> G[Steam Heater
100 to 850 C] G --> H[Desorbed Solute
Concentrated] H --> I[Condenser
Recovery] I --> J[Product Storage
Recovered Solute] I --> K[Cooling Section] K --> L[Reactivated Carbon
Return to service] L --> C C --> M[Carbon Inventory
Spent carbon purge] M --> N[Thermal Reactivation
Kiln 800 C] N --> L ``` ## 4.2 Breakthrough Curve Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P0[TIME 0] P0A[Fresh carbon, no solute, 100 percent capacity] end subgraph P1[TIME t1] P1A[Top saturated, MTZ formed, effluent clean] end subgraph P2[TIME t2] P2A[MTZ migrating down, still clean effluent] end subgraph P3[TIME t3 BREAKTHROUGH] P3A[MTZ reaches bottom, C reaches 0.05 C0] end subgraph P4[TIME t4 EXHAUSTION] P4A[Whole bed saturated, C equals C0] end P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 ``` ## 4.3 Regeneration Method Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Spent Activated Carbon] --> B{Solute Type?} B -->|Volatile organic| C[Steam Stripping
TSA at 100 to 200 C] B -->|Non-volatile organic| D[Thermal Reactivation
TSA at 700 to 850 C] B -->|Ionic Inorganic| E[Acid Alkali Wash
Solvent Elution] B -->|Gas phase VOC| F[Pressure Swing
PSA Vacuum] C --> G[Condensate Recovery] D --> H[Off-gas Treatment] E --> I[Liquid Liquid Extraction] F --> J[Vacuum Pump Capture] ``` ## 4.4 Equipment Architecture — Industrial Carbon Adsorber ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph X1[Adsorber Vessel] A1[Feed Inlet
Distributor] --> A2[Top Head
Manhole] A2 --> A3[Carbon Bed 1
GAC Layer] A3 --> A4[Support Grid
Screens] A4 --> A5[Carbon Bed 2
Polishing] A5 --> A6[Bottom Plenum] A6 --> A7[Effluent Outlet] end subgraph X2[Regeneration Loop] B1[Steam Inlet] --> B2[Heater Coil] B2 --> A2 A6 --> B3[Condensate Trap] B3 --> B4[Heat Exchanger] B4 --> B5[Decanter] B5 --> B6[Recovered Solute Tank] end A7 --> Y[Treated Effluent] B6 --> Z[Product] ``` ## 4.5 Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) Conceptual Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Z0[Zone 0: Saturated
q equals qs] --> Z1[Zone 1: MTZ
Mass transfer active] Z1 --> Z2[Zone 2: Fresh
q equals 0] Z2 --> Z3[Effluent
C equals 0] Z1 -.->|C rises from 0 to C0| Z3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The length of the MTZ is typically **3 to 6 particle diameters** for well-designed columns. The MTZ travels down the bed at a velocity lower than the fluid velocity due to the convective delay caused by adsorption. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three characteristic properties of activated carbon that make it suitable as an adsorbent for solute recovery.** **Model Answer:** 1. **High internal surface area (500–1500 m²/g):** Provides enormous sites for solute–surface interaction, primarily within micropores (< 2 nm) where overlapping van der Waals potentials enhance adsorption. 2. **High adsorption capacity and selectivity:** Activated carbon exhibits preferential uptake for organic molecules and certain inorganic species due to hydrophobic surface chemistry and tunable pore-size distribution. 3. **Chemical and thermal stability:** It is inert to most acids, bases, and solvents, and can withstand regeneration temperatures up to 850 °C without structural degradation, enabling multiple adsorption–desorption cycles. 4. *Optional bonus point:* Easy regeneration via TSA/PSA/solvent elution makes the process economically viable. > **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per property (with brief explanation). 3 properties = 3 marks. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms. State one limitation of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Langmuir | Freundlich | |---|---|---| | **Surface assumption** | Homogeneous, uniform sites | Heterogeneous, non-uniform sites | | **Coverage** | Monolayer only | Multilayer (empirical) | | **Mathematical form** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{b\,q_m\,C_e}{1 + b\,C_e}$ | $\dfrac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | | **Saturation** | Predicts finite $q_m$ | Does not predict saturation | | **Limitation** | Fails for heterogeneous surfaces or multilayer adsorption | Empirical constants have no physical meaning; fails at very high $C_e$ | > **Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for comparison table (any 4 distinct points) + 0.5 mark each for limitations = 3 marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks) **(a) Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm equation starting from kinetic considerations. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The adsorbent surface has a fixed number of identical, energetically equivalent sites. 2. Each site can hold only one adsorbate molecule (monolayer coverage). 3. There is no interaction between adsorbed molecules. 4. Adsorption is reversible and reaches dynamic equilibrium. **Derivation (5 marks):** Let $\theta$ be the fraction of surface sites occupied. - Forward (adsorption) rate: $r_a = k_a \, P \, (1 - \theta)$ (or $k_a C (1-\theta)$ for liquids) - Reverse (desorption) rate: $r_d = k_d \, \theta$ At dynamic equilibrium, $r_a = r_d$: $$ k_a C (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta $$ Rearranging: $$ k_a C = \theta (k_d + k_a C) $$ $$ \theta = \dfrac{k_a C}{k_d + k_a C} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) C}{1 + (k_a/k_d) C} $$ Defining $b = k_a/k_d$ (Langmuir constant, L/mg) and $q = q_m \theta$: $$ \boxed{q = \dfrac{b \, q_m \, C}{1 + b \, C}} $$ **Linearized form (for 1 bonus mark):** $$ \dfrac{C}{q} = \dfrac{1}{b \, q_m} + \dfrac{C}{q_m} $$ Plotting $C/q$ vs. $C$ gives slope $1/q_m$ and intercept $1/(b q_m)$. --- **(b) A wastewater stream containing 100 mg/L of phenol is to be treated using a fixed bed of granular activated carbon. The Langmuir parameters are $q_m = 200$ mg/g and $b = 0.025$ L/mg. Calculate (i) the equilibrium solid loading at the operating concentration, and (ii) the volume of carbon required to treat 10,000 L of wastewater per cycle if the bed operates at 80% of saturation loading. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $C_0 = 100$ mg/L, $q_m = 200$ mg/g, $b = 0.025$ L/mg - $V_{waste} = 10{,}000$ L, saturation fraction = 0.80 **Part (i) — Equilibrium loading at $C_0 = 100$ mg/L (3 marks):** $$ q^* = \dfrac{b \, q_m \, C_0}{1 + b \, C_0} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 200 \times 100}{1 + 0.025 \times 100} $$ $$ q^* = \dfrac{500}{1 + 2.5} = \dfrac{500}{3.5} \approx 142.86 \;\text{mg/g} $$ **Part (ii) — Carbon required (4 marks):** Operating loading = $0.80 \times 142.86 = 114.29$ mg/g Total phenol to be removed = $C_0 \times V = 100 \times 10{,}000 = 10^6$ mg = 1000 g Mass of carbon required: $$ m_{carbon} = \dfrac{\text{Total phenol}}{\text{Operating loading}} = \dfrac{1000 \;\text{g}}{0.11429 \;\text{g/g}} = 8750 \;\text{g} $$ $$ \boxed{m_{carbon} \approx 8.75 \;\text{kg}} $$ Volume of carbon (assuming $\rho_b = 480$ kg/m³): $$ V_{carbon} = \dfrac{8.75}{480} = 0.01823 \;\text{m}^3 = 18.23 \;\text{L} $$ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a thermal swing adsorption (TSA) process for activated carbon regeneration. List two advantages and two disadvantages. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The TSA cycle consists of four stages: **Adsorption → Heating/Desorption → Cooling → Re-pressurization.** ``` [Spent Carbon] → [Steam Heater, 100–200 °C] → [Desorbed Solute + Steam] → [Condenser] → [Recovered Solute] ↓ [Cooling by Purge Gas] → [Cool Carbon] → [Next Adsorption Cycle] ``` **Working (3 marks):** 1. **Adsorption Step:** The feed gas/liquid containing the solute is passed through the carbon bed at near-ambient temperature until breakthrough is detected. 2. **Heating/Desorption Step:** A hot purge gas (typically steam or nitrogen at 100–850 °C) is passed through the bed. The elevated temperature increases the vapor pressure of the adsorbed solute, shifting the adsorption equilibrium toward desorption. 3. **Cooling Step:** Cold purge gas cools the bed to near-adsorption temperature. 4. **Re-pressurization:** The bed is brought back to operating pressure, ready for the next adsorption cycle. **Advantages (0.5 each = 1 mark):** - Produces high-purity recovered solute (very low residual loading). - Excellent for strongly adsorbed solutes (high heat of adsorption). **Disadvantages (0.5 each = 1 mark):** - High energy consumption due to heating/cooling duty. - Long cycle time (hours), not suitable for rapid cycling. --- **(b) A GAC column has the following specifications: bed height $Z = 2$ m, superficial velocity $u = 0.008$ m/s, $\rho_b = 500$ kg/m³, $C_0 = 80$ mg/L, $q_b = 90$ mg/g. Calculate the ideal breakthrough time. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $Z = 2$ m, $u = 0.008$ m/s, $\rho_b = 500$ kg/m³, $C_0 = 80$ mg/L = $0.080$ g/L, $q_b = 90$ mg/g = $0.090$ g/g **Formula (1 mark):** $$ t_b = \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot q_b \cdot Z}{u \cdot C_0} $$ **Substitution (4 marks):** Unit consistency check: - $\rho_b$ in kg/m³, $q_b$ in kg/kg, $Z$ in m, $u$ in m/s, $C_0$ in kg/m³ Convert $C_0$: $80$ mg/L $= 0.080$ kg/m³ $$ t_b = \dfrac{(500)(0.090)(2)}{(0.008)(0.080)} $$ $$ t_b = \dfrac{90}{0.00064} = 140{,}625 \;\text{seconds} $$ **Final answer with unit conversion (2 marks):** $$ t_b = \dfrac{140{,}625}{3600} \approx 39.06 \;\text{hours} $$ $$ \boxed{t_b \approx 39.1 \;\text{hours} \approx 1.63 \;\text{days}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks):** Mixing $C_0$ in mg/L with $\rho_b$ in kg/m³. Always convert to **one consistent system** (preferably SI: kg/m³ for $C_0$). > 2. **Forgetting to convert seconds to hours (–1 mark):** Final answer must be expressed in convenient time units (hours/days). > 3. **Using $q_m$ instead of $q_b$ (–2 marks):** Breakthrough loading $q_b$ corresponds to the operating (exit) concentration, not the maximum monolayer capacity $q_m$. Recompute $q_b$ from the isotherm at $C = 0.05 C_0$ to $0.10 C_0$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Activated Carbon Solute Recovery** - [ ] Activated carbon surface area: **500–1500 m²/g**; pore size dominated by **micropores (< 2 nm)**. - [ ] **Langmuir** assumes monolayer + homogeneous sites; **Freundlich** is empirical + heterogeneous. - [ ] Langmuir equation: $q = \dfrac{b\,q_m\,C}{1+b\,C}$; linearization: $\dfrac{C}{q} = \dfrac{1}{bq_m} + \dfrac{C}{q_m}$. - [ ] Freundlich equation: $q = K_F \cdot C^{1/n}$; linearization: $\log q = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C$. - [ ] **LDF kinetic model:** $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_s(q^* - q)$ is the KTU-preferred approximation. - [ ] Breakthrough time: $t_b = \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_b \, Z}{u \, C_0}$. - [ ] MTZ length typically **3–6 particle diameters**; $C/C_0 = 0.05$–$0.10$ defines breakthrough. - [ ] **TSA** uses **100–850 °C**; **PSA** uses pressure reduction; **solvent elution** for ionic species. - [ ] Key industrial applications: gold cyanidation (CIC/CIL), water treatment, solvent recovery, sugar decolorization. - [ ] **Always show units and conversions** — KTU valuation explicitly tests dimensional consistency. - [ ] Steam consumption in TSA: $\dot{Q}_{regen} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda_s$. - [ ] For fixed-bed modeling, the **axial dispersion equation** is the rigorous starting point; LDF is the simplified solid-phase model. - [ ] Iodine Number (800–1200 mg/g) is the primary quality metric for activated carbon grade selection.

topics.title: Processing Liquid Using Thermal Regeneration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processing Liquid Using Thermal Regeneration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Processing Liquid Using Thermal Regeneration

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Regeneration** is a physical regeneration technique in which the saturated adsorbent or loaded ion-exchange resin is regenerated by elevating the system temperature, exploiting the **thermodynamic sensitivity of adsorption/ion-exchange equilibria to temperature**. In contrast to chemical regeneration (which uses an acid, base, or salt eluent), thermal regeneration relies solely on **heat as the driving force** to reverse the uptake of solute from the solid phase back into the liquid phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT756 / Module 3):**
> "Thermal regeneration is a separation-restoration operation in which an exhausted solid sorbent (adsorbent or ion-exchange resin) is restored to its active form by a controlled temperature cycle, with no net addition of chemical regenerant. The desorbed species are recovered as a concentrated, hot eluate."

For the case of **Processing Liquids**, thermal regeneration is exploited in three principal configurations studied in Module 3:

1. **Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA) – Liquid Phase**
2. **Parametric Pumping (a novel cyclic thermal-regeneration process)**
3. **Hot Regeneration of Ion-Exchange Resins**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sponge that Loves the Cold"**
> Imagine a kitchen sponge that absorbs soup (solutes) **much more strongly when it is cold** and **releases the soup back when warmed in the microwave**. Now, if you keep dipping this sponge alternately into *cold soup* and *hot clean water*—always pushing water in the **same direction as the cold flow**—the soup will get concentrated at one end of the sponge and clean water will collect at the other. This is essentially how thermal regeneration of a packed bed works. The adsorbent "breathes"—it holds solute at low temperature and exhales it at high temperature. By synchronizing the **flow direction** with the **temperature cycle**, solutes migrate in one preferred direction, producing a separation that is impossible in a single equilibrium stage.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard regeneration temperature window:** $T_{regen} \in [\mathbf{60^{\circ}C}, \mathbf{120^{\circ}C}]$ for most polymeric resins.
- **Working temperature swing ($\Delta T$):** typically $\mathbf{40 - 80^{\circ}C}$.
- **Adsorption enthalpy ($\Delta H_{ads}$):** of the order $\mathbf{30 - 60 \, kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$ (physical adsorption / weak chemisorption).
- **Heat of ion exchange:** $\Delta H_{IX} \approx \mathbf{5 - 15 \, kJ \cdot eq^{-1}}$.
- **Process intensification metric:** **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** in a single thermal cycle.

### 1.4 Role in the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Framework

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Topic Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| CO3 – Apply novel separation principles | Apply / Analyze | Derive TSA/parametric pumping equations |
| CO4 – Design thermal-regeneration cycles | Create | Calculate cycle time & bed length |
| CO5 – Compare novel vs conventional methods | Evaluate | Compare thermal vs chemical regeneration |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The thermal regeneration of a packed bed used for liquid processing follows a **four-stage thermodynamic cycle**:

1. **Loading (Cold Adsorption Stage)**
   - The cold feed liquid ($T_{cold} \approx 25 - 30^{\circ}C$) is passed through the bed.
   - Solute is taken up by the sorbent; exit liquid becomes depleted in solute.
   - Equilibrium loading: $q_{cold} = f(C_{feed}, T_{cold})$.

2. **Heating-Up (Transient Regeneration)**
   - A hot regenerant stream (often the same solvent, but heated) is introduced.
   - Because the equilibrium loading falls exponentially with temperature, the desorption front advances through the bed.

3. **Hot Strip (Desorption Stage)**
   - At $T_{hot}$, the sorbent holds far less solute. The hot liquid leaving the bed carries the desorbed solute as a **concentrated eluate**.

4. **Cooling & Re-Equilibration**
   - The bed is cooled back to $T_{cold}$ either by an in-situ coolant stream or by natural convection, ready for the next cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the "Why" matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently award full marks only when the student explains that *thermal regeneration works because* $\left(\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial T}\right)_C < 0$ for exothermic adsorption. A purely descriptive answer is marked down.

## 2.2 Mathematical Backbone of the Process

### 2.2.1 Temperature-Dependent Equilibrium

The simplest, KTU-acceptable equilibrium model is the **linear isotherm with temperature-dependent slope**:

$$q = K(T) \cdot C$$

where $K(T)$ is the equilibrium partition coefficient. It obeys a **van't Hoff-type Arrhenius relation**:

$$K(T) = K_{\infty} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R \, T}\right)$$

A more realistic form, used for ion-exchange resins, is the **Langmuir isotherm with temperature-dependent parameters**:

$$q = \dfrac{q_{max}(T) \cdot b(T) \cdot C}{1 + b(T) \cdot C}, \quad b(T) = b_{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R\,T}\right)$$

### 2.2.2 Governing Differential Balance

A 1-D axial-dispersion model for the liquid-phase, fixed-bed adsorber during regeneration:

$$\varepsilon \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + (1-\varepsilon)\,\rho_p\,\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} \;=\; D_L\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial z^{2}} - v\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z}$$

where the symbols are summarized in the cheat-sheet below.

### 2.2.3 The Separation Factor for Parametric Pumping

For a **direct-mode parametric pumping** cycle, the dimensionless separation factor across the column is:

$$\beta = \dfrac{C_{top,avg}}{C_{bot,avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{K(T_{cold})}{K(T_{hot})} \cdot f(N_{cyc},\, Pe,\, St)$$

with the Péclet number $Pe = v L / D_L$ and the Stanton number $St = k_L L / v$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Definition / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Liquid-phase solute concentration | $kg \cdot m^{-3}$ | Feed / eluate concentration |
| $q$ | Solid-phase loading | $kg \cdot kg^{-1}$ | Mass of solute per mass of dry sorbent |
| $K(T)$ | Partition coefficient | dimensionless | Slope of linear isotherm |
| $\Delta H_{ads}$ | Heat of adsorption | $kJ \cdot mol^{-1}$ | Negative for exothermic uptake |
| $T_{cold}, T_{hot}$ | Cycle temperature limits | $K$ or ${}^{\circ}C$ | 298 K / 373 K (typical) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Bed void fraction | — | 0.35 – 0.45 |
| $\rho_p$ | Particle density | $kg \cdot m^{-3}$ | 700 – 1500 |
| $D_L$ | Axial dispersion | $m^{2} \cdot s^{-1}$ | $10^{-5}$ – $10^{-3}$ |
| $v$ | Interstitial velocity | $m \cdot s^{-1}$ | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-2}$ |
| $L$ | Bed length | $m$ | 0.5 – 5 |
| $\beta$ | Separation factor | dimensionless | 1 (no sep.) – 10³ |
| $Pe$ | Péclet number | dimensionless | $vL/D_L$ |
| $St$ | Stanton number | dimensionless | $k_L L / v$ |
| $N_{cyc}$ | Number of cycles | — | Typically 5 – 50 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the vertical bar $\vert$ inside table cells.** The cheat-sheet above uses comma separators to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Thermal Regeneration

| Field | Application | Why thermal regeneration is preferred |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Desalination** | Na⁺-softening, demineralization | Avoids brine disposal problem |
| **Sugar Industry** | Decolorization of cane juice | No chemical contamination of product |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Antibiotic recovery, protein purification | Mild temperatures, no chemical residue |
| **Biotechnology** | Enzyme immobilization columns | Preserves biomolecule activity |
| **Petrochemical** | Trace organics removal from glycols | In-situ regeneration possible |
| **Nuclear Waste** | Cs⁺/Sr²⁺ selective resins | Avoids secondary liquid waste |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The DOWEX™ MARATHON™ resin system used in nuclear reprocessing is regenerated thermally at 80–90 °C, producing a 20× concentrated Cs⁺ eluate that can be directly vitrified — eliminating the secondary liquid waste stream that chemical regeneration would create.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Temperature Dependence of the Equilibrium Loading

**Starting Point:** The Gibbs-Helmholtz relation for adsorption equilibrium.

$$
\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -R T \ln K(T)
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $T$ at constant $C$ and using the identity $\partial(\Delta G^{\circ}/T)/\partial T \big|_C = -\Delta H^{\circ}/T^{2}$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \ln K(T)}{\partial T} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R\,T^{2}}
$$

Integrating between $T_{ref}$ and $T$:

$$
\int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \dfrac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T}\, dT = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R\,T^{2}}\, dT
$$

For constant $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$ (a valid KTU assumption over a $\sim 100\,K$ range):

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K(T)}{K(T_{ref})}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
K(T) = K(T_{ref}) \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The negative sign in $\Delta G = -RT \ln K$ converts a *favourable* (negative) $\Delta G$ into a *positive* $K$. Since adsorption is exothermic, $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} < 0$, and therefore $K(T)$ is a *monotonically decreasing* function of $T$ — the cornerstone of all thermal regeneration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Separation Factor in Direct Parametric Pumping

**Setup:** A packed column of length $L$ undergoes a periodic cycle of period $\tau$:

- For $0 \le t \le \tau/2$ : cold feed, upward flow, solute captured.
- For $\tau/2 \le t \le \tau$ : hot regenerant, downward flow, solute released.

Neglect axial dispersion ($D_L \to 0$) for the limiting case (the "equilibrium parametric pump" of Pigford, Baker & Blum, 1969).

**Material balance over one half-cycle** for a control volume of length $dz$:

$$
\varepsilon\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + (1-\varepsilon)\,\rho_p\,\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} + v\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0
$$

For local equilibrium, $q = K(T) C$. Substituting and using the chain rule with $T = T(t)$:

$$
\left[\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\,\rho_p\,K(T)\right]\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + v\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0
$$

This is a **first-order quasi-linear PDE** solved by the method of characteristics. The characteristic speed is:

$$
u_{char} = \dfrac{v}{\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\,\rho_p\,K(T)}
$$

**Solute displacement per half-cycle** for a concentration front initially at $z_0$:

$$
\Delta z = \int_{0}^{\tau/2} u_{char}(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\tau/2} \dfrac{v}{\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\,\rho_p\,K[T(t)]}\, dt
$$

For a **square-wave temperature profile** ($T = T_{cold}$ for $0 \le t \le \tau/2$, $T = T_{hot}$ for $\tau/2 \le t \le \tau$), the upward and downward displacements differ because $K$ differs. The **net upward displacement per full cycle** is:

$$
\Delta z_{net} = \dfrac{v \tau}{2} \left[\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K_{hot}} - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K_{cold}}\right]
$$

Since $K_{cold} > K_{hot}$ (sorption stronger when cold), the second term is smaller, so $\Delta z_{net} > 0$ — the solute migrates **upward**, regardless of feed direction. This is the **physical origin of the separation**.

For a column operated for $N$ cycles, the **separation factor** is:

$$
\beta = \left(\dfrac{K_{cold}}{K_{hot}}\right)^{N \cdot \eta}, \quad \eta = \dfrac{\Delta z_{net}/L}{\tau \cdot v/L}
$$

where $\eta$ is a cycle-efficiency factor ($0 < \eta < 1$) accounting for dispersion, finite mass-transfer resistance, and non-square temperature waves.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** When asked to *"derive the separation factor for parametric pumping"*, the marking scheme almost always awards the 7 marks for reaching the expression $\beta = (K_{cold}/K_{hot})^{N\eta}$. The remaining 7 marks go to the cycle description and the limiting-case discussion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Regenerant Volume for Hot-Ion-Exchange

For a bed of mass $W$ (kg dry resin) loaded to $q_{sat}$ (kg solute/kg resin), the **minimum hot-water volume** $V_{min}$ needed to desorb the solute is found by an overall mass balance:

$$
W \cdot q_{sat} = \int_{0}^{V_{min}} C_{out}(V)\, dV
$$

If the desorption isotherm at $T_{hot}$ is approximated as linear ($q_{hot} = K_{hot} C$), and assuming instantaneous equilibrium (the *Klinkenberg* limit), the regeneration efficiency approaches a step-function:

$$
V_{min} \approx \dfrac{W \cdot q_{sat}}{C_{eluate,\,max}} \;=\; W\,K_{hot}\,\left(\dfrac{K_{cold}}{K_{hot}} - 1\right)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The numerator captures the *total solute* to be desorbed; the denominator is the *maximum eluate concentration*, which the hot equilibrium permits. The ratio of $K$ values — the very same Arrhenius factor that powered the derivation in §3.1 — now appears as the **regeneration amplification factor** for hot ion-exchange systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermal-Regeneration Cycle

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the interactions between the **cold adsorption loop**, the **thermal regeneration loop**, and the **product/eluate streams** for a generic liquid-phase thermal swing process. Use this as the *flow of material and energy* whenever a textual description of the cycle is needed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Liquid Cold Tcold] -->|Pump P1| B[Top of Packed Bed]
    B --> C[Adsorption Front Moving Down]
    C --> D[Spent Bed Treated Effluent Bottom]
    D --> E[Treated Liquid to Storage]

    F[Hot Water Boiler Thot] -->|Pump P2| G[Bottom of Packed Bed]
    G --> H[Desorption Front Moving Up]
    H --> I[Concentrated Eluate Top]
    I --> J[Eluate Cooler and Product Tank]

    K[Heat Exchanger Recovers Sensible Heat] -.->|Reuse| F
    J -.->|Recycle after cooling| A

    C -.->|Solute captured qcold| L[Loaded Sorbent]
    L -.->|Heat input Qregen| H
    H -.->|Solute released qhot| J

    style A fill:#cfe5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style F fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1a4d1a,color:#000
    style J fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d00,color:#000
```

**How to read this block diagram:**

- The **blue path** (top-left) is the *cold adsorption* half-cycle: cold feed enters at the top, solute is captured, treated water exits at the bottom.
- The **orange path** (bottom) is the *hot regeneration* half-cycle: hot water enters at the bottom, desorbed solute exits at the top as a concentrated eluate.
- The **dashed yellow line** represents the *sensible-heat recovery loop* — an essential energy-saving feature in any industrial thermal-regeneration system, and a frequent 3-mark question in KTU.
- The **node `L → H`** block (centre) is the *sorbent itself*, which acts as the working "thermal mass" of the process.

## 4.2 Parametric Pumping — Synchronous Cycle Visualization

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as Cold Feed
    participant B as Packed Bed
    participant H as Hot Regenerant
    participant E as Eluate / Product

    Note over B: t = 0  Cold upflow begins
    F->>B: Upflow at Tcold (sorption on)
    B-->>E: Clean raffinate exits at bottom

    Note over B: t = τ/2  Half-cycle
    Note over B: Flow REVERSES to downflow
    H->>B: Downflow at Thot (desorption on)
    B-->>E: Concentrated eluate exits at top

    Note over B: t = τ  One full cycle complete
    Note over B: Net effect: solute migrates UPWARD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for students:** The horizontal arrows in the sequence diagram represent the **time axis**, not material flow. Each `->>` corresponds to one half-cycle. The key insight — the *flow reversal synchronised with the temperature reversal* — is what makes parametric pumping a *novel* process, as the term is used in the KTU course title.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PECHT756]`
**Define thermal regeneration of an ion-exchange resin. Why is it preferred over chemical regeneration for selected applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thermal regeneration is the process of restoring the sorption capacity of an *exhausted* ion-exchange resin or adsorbent by **raising the temperature** of a contacting liquid stream, exploiting the decrease in equilibrium loading with temperature ($\partial q / \partial T < 0$). It is preferred over chemical regeneration in **nuclear, pharmaceutical, and food industries** because (i) it produces **no secondary chemical waste stream**, (ii) it preserves the activity of **heat-stable biomolecules** on the resin, and (iii) the regenerant is just hot water, which is easy to handle and re-use. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for comparison.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECHT756]`
**State the van 't Hoff equation for temperature dependence of the partition coefficient and explain its significance in thermal regeneration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
K(T) = K(T_{ref}) \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]
$$

Since $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} < 0$ (adsorption is exothermic), $K(T)$ decreases as $T$ rises. The magnitude of the *ratio* $K(T_{cold})/K(T_{hot})$ is the **regeneration amplification factor** — the larger this ratio, the higher the eluate concentration obtained per cycle. The exponential dependence means a small $\Delta T$ swing can produce a substantial separation in many cycles. (3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for physical interpretation, 1 for amplification-factor concept.)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT756]`

**(a)** Explain the principle and operation of **direct-mode parametric pumping** for liquid-phase separation. Include a neat schematic of the cycle and identify the physical origin of the separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the 1-D transient material balance, **derive the separation factor** $\beta$ for an equilibrium parametric pump, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principle and Operation (7 marks):**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Parametric pumping is a cyclic, non-steady-state separation process in which a *thermodynamic parameter* (here, temperature) is varied synchronously with the direction of fluid flow through a fixed bed of sorbent. Because adsorption is exothermic, the sorbent holds more solute when cold and less when hot. By *reversing the flow at the same instant the temperature changes*, a net migration of solute in one direction is induced — typically *upward*, opposite to the cold feed.

2. **Operating Cycle (3 marks):**
   - *Half-cycle 1 (0 to τ/2):* Cold feed (≈ 25 °C) flows upward. The cold equilibrium loading $q_{cold} = K_{cold}\,C$ is high, so the bed removes solute and the *raffinate* leaves at the bottom clean.
   - *Half-cycle 2 (τ/2 to τ):* Flow is reversed *and* the inlet is switched to hot water (≈ 80 °C) entering at the bottom. The hot loading $q_{hot} = K_{hot}\,C$ is low, so solute is desorbed and exits at the top as a *concentrated eluate*.
   - The two streams are collected in *separate reservoirs*. After $N$ full cycles, the top reservoir contains the **enriched product** and the bottom contains the **depleted raffinate**.

3. **Physical Origin of Separation (2 marks):** Net upward displacement of the solute per cycle, $\Delta z_{net} = (v\tau/2)[1/(\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K_{hot}) - 1/(\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K_{cold})]$, is non-zero because the cold and hot characteristic speeds differ. Solute therefore *pumps* upward against the average flow.

**(b) Derivation of the Separation Factor (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): State the 1-D transient balance (no axial dispersion, local equilibrium):

$$
\left[\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K(T)\right]\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + v\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0
$$

Step 2 (2 marks): Use the method of characteristics. The characteristic speed is

$$
u_{char}(T) = \dfrac{v}{\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K(T)}
$$

Step 3 (2 marks): Compute the net displacement per cycle for square-wave temperature forcing. The half-cycle displacements are

$$
\Delta z_{up} = \dfrac{v(\tau/2)}{\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K_{cold}}, \qquad
\Delta z_{down} = \dfrac{v(\tau/2)}{\varepsilon + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p K_{hot}}
$$

Since $K_{cold} > K_{hot}$, the up-displacement is *shorter* than the down-displacement, giving a net *upward* migration.

Step 4 (2 marks): Combine over $N$ cycles and express the ratio of the concentrations at the two ends of the bed:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{C_{top}}{C_{bot}} = \left(\dfrac{K_{cold}}{K_{hot}}\right)^{N\eta}
$$

where $\eta \in (0,1)$ is the cycle-efficiency factor accounting for dispersion, finite mass-transfer resistance, and non-ideal thermal forcing. This is the **Pigford–Baker–Blum equation**, the cornerstone result of parametric-pumping theory.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT756]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Compare **thermal regeneration** and **chemical regeneration** of ion-exchange resins under the heads: (i) regenerant used, (ii) effluent quality, (iii) energy demand, (iv) equipment corrosion, (v) typical application areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A weak-acid cation-exchange resin in H⁺ form is loaded with Na⁺ from a feed containing $C_0 = 2.0$ kg·m⁻³ Na⁺. The equilibrium isotherm at $T_{cold} = 298\,K$ follows $q = 1.2\,C$ and at $T_{hot} = 348\,K$ follows $q = 0.30\,C$ (units of kg Na⁺ per kg dry resin and kg·m⁻³ respectively). The bed contains $W = 100$ kg dry resin.
  **Compute:**
    (i) the mass of Na⁺ captured per cycle, **(3 marks)**
    (ii) the **regeneration amplification factor** $K_{cold}/K_{hot}$, **(2 marks)**
    (iii) the **maximum achievable eluate concentration** at the hot temperature, assuming all captured Na⁺ is released. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Thermal Regeneration | Chemical Regeneration |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Regenerant | Hot water / hot air | Acid (HCl, H₂SO₄) or base (NaOH) |
| (ii) Effluent quality | Pure water + solute (clean) | Salt + spent acid/base (polluting) |
| (iii) Energy demand | High (latent + sensible heat) | Low (chemical potential) |
| (iv) Equipment corrosion | Low (water only) | High (corrosive chemicals) |
| (v) Applications | Pharma, food, nuclear, biotech | Water-softening, demin., large-scale WTP |
| (vi) Capital cost | Higher (boiler, HX) | Lower (chemicals tank) |
| (vii) Operating cost | Lower (energy is cheap; water free) | Higher (chemicals) |

(7 marks: 1 mark per meaningful row, max 7.)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Mass captured per cycle (3 marks):**
Using the cold isotherm $q_{cold} = 1.2\,C_0$:

$$
q_{sat} = 1.2 \times 2.0 = 2.4 \;\text{kg Na⁺ per kg dry resin}
$$

$$
m_{Na} = W \cdot q_{sat} = 100 \times 2.4 = \mathbf{240 \; kg \; Na^{+}} \text{ per cycle.}
$$

**(ii) Regeneration amplification factor (2 marks):**

$$
\dfrac{K_{cold}}{K_{hot}} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.30} = \mathbf{4.0}
$$

**(iii) Maximum eluate concentration (2 marks):**
Mass balance on regeneration: $W \cdot q_{sat} = C_{eluate,\,max} \cdot V_{min}$. Using the derivation in §3.3 with $V_{min}$ taken as the bed interstitial volume gives:

$$
C_{eluate,\,max} = \dfrac{W \cdot q_{sat}}{W \cdot K_{hot}} = \dfrac{q_{sat}}{K_{hot}} = \dfrac{2.4}{0.30} = \mathbf{8.0 \; kg \cdot m^{-3}}
$$

Thus the bed produces an eluate that is **4× more concentrated than the feed** — the practical manifestation of the regeneration amplification factor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Critical for Marks)**
> 1. **Never** state the van 't Hoff equation without explicitly mentioning that $\Delta H_{ads}$ is *negative* for exothermic sorption. Marks are routinely cut for omitting the sign.
> 2. In the parametric-pumping derivation, the **boundary condition at the flow-reversal instant** is the most-skipped step. Examiners specifically look for the condition "$C$ is continuous at $t = \tau/2$", worth at least **1 mark**.
> 3. In numerical problems, students often confuse $K$ (dimensionless partition coefficient, $q/C$) with the Langmuir constant $b$ (with units of m³·kg⁻¹). Read the units given in the problem statement carefully.
> 4. **Do not** confuse the *regeneration amplification factor* ($K_{cold}/K_{hot}$) with the *separation factor* $\beta$. The former is a property of the equilibrium alone; the latter depends also on the number of cycles and the efficiency $\eta$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Processing Liquid Using Thermal Regeneration**

- **Core idea:** Thermal regeneration exploits $\left(\partial q / \partial T\right)_C < 0$ for exothermic sorption to release solute from a solid phase by **heating only**, with no chemical regenerant.
- **Key equation (van 't Hoff for $K$):** $K(T) = K_{ref} \cdot \exp\!\left[(\Delta H_{ads}/R)(1/T_{ref} - 1/T)\right]$; with $\Delta H_{ads} < 0$ this gives $K$ a *decreasing* function of $T$.
- **Three configurations in Module 3:** TSA (liquid phase), Parametric Pumping, Hot Ion-Exchange Regeneration.
- **Parametric pumping essentials:** synchronised reversal of **flow direction** and **temperature**; net solute migration in one preferred direction (typically upward) per cycle; separation factor $\beta = (K_{cold}/K_{hot})^{N\eta}$.
- **Direct vs Recuperative mode:** Direct uses external heat exchangers; Recuperative uses the heat stored in the bed itself — more energy-efficient but harder to control.
- **Regeneration amplification factor:** $K_{cold}/K_{hot}$ — equal to the *eluate-concentration ratio* in the ideal equilibrium limit.
- **Hot-ion-exchange operating window:** typically $T_{hot} \in [60, 120]^{\circ}C$, $\Delta T = 40 - 80^{\circ}C$.
- **Governing PDE:** $\varepsilon \partial C/\partial t + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p \partial q/\partial t = D_L \partial^2 C/\partial z^2 - v \partial C/\partial z$.
- **Energy recovery:** Always include a sensible-heat exchanger on the hot eluate stream — this single feature typically halves the operating cost of industrial TSA.
- **Industrial sweet spots:** Nuclear (Cs⁺ recovery), pharma (no chemical residue), food (no contamination), biotech (gentle on enzymes).
- **Comparison mantra for 7-mark questions:** *Regenerant → Effluent → Energy → Corrosion → Application* — five points, easy to remember.
- **Common pitfall:** Saying "thermal regeneration uses less energy than chemical regeneration" — this is **false in most cases**; the *correct* statement is "thermal regeneration has lower *operating cost* because water is cheaper than chemicals, even though it uses more *energy*."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Swing and Vacuum Swing Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pressure Swing and Vacuum Swing Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** is a cyclic, non-cryogenic gas separation technology in which a gaseous mixture is selectively adsorbed onto a solid microporous/mesoporous adsorbent (e.g., zeolites, activated carbon, silica gel, or activated alumina) at an elevated **adsorption pressure ($P_H$)**, and the trapped species are subsequently desorbed by lowering the bed pressure to a lower **desorption pressure ($P_L$)** — thereby regenerating the bed for the next cycle. The driving force for separation is the **difference in equilibrium loading ($\Delta q$)** of the components on the adsorbent between the two pressure levels.

**Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA)** is a sub-class of PSA in which the *adsorption* step occurs at or slightly above **atmospheric pressure (~1.013 bar)**, while the *desorption (regeneration)* step is performed at **sub-atmospheric pressure (< 0.3 bar absolute)** by means of a vacuum pump. VSA is therefore preferred when compression of the feed is uneconomical or when strongly adsorbed species (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ on zeolite 13X) require very low pressures for regeneration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Highlight:** Both PSA and VSA fall under the umbrella of **adsorptive gas separation** and exploit the **pressure dependence of the adsorption isotherm** (Henry's Law at low loading: $q^* = K \cdot P$). They are classified as *swing processes* because the intensive variable cycled is **pressure (P)**, analogous to temperature swing (TSA) where **T** is cycled.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Picture

Imagine a **sponge that only soaks up red ink from a blue–red ink mixture**. In a **sealed, pressurised chamber** (high $P$), the sponge eagerly drinks up the red ink. When you suddenly **release the lid** (pressure drops), the sponge "sweats out" the red ink and becomes white again. By repeating this pressurise–release cycle many times, you can continuously produce a clean blue stream and recover the red ink. **PSA is the engineered, industrial-scale version of this pressurise-release cycle.**

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sponge | Microporous adsorbent (zeolite) |
| Red-Blue ink mixture | Feed gas (e.g., air, reformer gas) |
| Pressurised chamber | Adsorption column at $P_H$ |
| Lid release | Depressurisation / blowdown |
| Sponge sweat-out | Desorption (regeneration) |
| Repeated cycle | Cyclic steady state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "swing"?** The term *swing* denotes the **periodic swing (oscillation)** of an intensive thermodynamic variable — here **pressure** — across an adsorption equilibrium curve, between a high-$P$ operating point (loading) and a low-$P$ operating point (unloading). This is the conceptual backbone of the **McCabe–Thiele analog of PSA** that KTU examiners love to test.

## 1.3 Salient Features & Engineering Constants

- **Operating cycle time:** typically **1 – 10 minutes** (PSA), **3 – 30 minutes** (VSA). This is the *time per full cycle*, $t_{cycle}$.
- **Pressure ratio ($\beta = P_H / P_L$):** typically **3 – 10** for PSA, **4 – 30** for VSA. The **higher the $\beta$**, the larger the working capacity $\Delta q = q_H^* - q_L^*$.
- **Standard adsorbents:** Zeolite 5A (Ca-exchanged), Zeolite 13X (NaX), Activated Carbon, Silica Gel, Activated Alumina.
- **Standard units:** $P$ in **bar** or **kPa**, $q$ in **mol/kg** or **kg/kg**, $T$ in **K**, $F$ in **mol/s** or **Nm³/h**.
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The PSA/VSA process achieves separation in a **packed column** of adsorbent by exploiting the fact that **strongly adsorbed (heavy) species** accumulate in the bed during the *adsorption* half-cycle and are removed during the *desorption* half-cycle. The logic unfolds in **four canonical stages** (the *Skarstrom cycle*, 1958 — the foundational cycle that all modern PSA variants build upon):

1. **Adsorption (Pressurisation + Feed at $P_H$):** The feed gas enters one end of the bed at high pressure. The *strongly adsorbed* component (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ on zeolite 13X) is captured by the solid; the *weakly adsorbed* component (e.g., $\text{H}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{O}_2$) passes through and is collected as the **light product (raffinate)**.
2. **Depressurisation (Blowdown $P_H \to P_L$):** The feed inlet is closed, and the bed is vented counter-currently. The pressure drop causes partial desorption. The released gas is **recovered as the heavy product** (or recycled as purge).
3. **Purge at $P_L$:** A fraction of the light product (or feed at low P) is passed **counter-current** through the bed, sweeping out the residual heavy component and further regenerating the adsorbent.
4. **Repressurisation $P_L \to P_H$:** The bed is pressurised back to $P_H$ using either feed (co-current) or part of the light product (counter-current), readying it for the next adsorption step.

> [!NOTE]
> A **two-bed PSA/VSA system** allows one bed to be in adsorption while the other is being regenerated — ensuring **continuous product delivery**, which is the standard industrial configuration.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Stage

- **Why pressurise?** Because the **adsorption isotherm** ($q^* = f(P,T)$) is concave to the pressure axis. Raising $P$ *increases* the equilibrium loading $q^*$ (Langmuir: $q^* = q_m \, bP / (1 + bP)$). The high-$P$ point sits far up the isotherm, capturing more solute.
- **Why depressurise?** Because the isotherm predicts lower $q^*$ at low $P$. The drop in $P$ creates a **driving force for desorption** — a positive $\Delta \mu$ (chemical potential) for the sorbed species to leave the surface.
- **Why purge?** Because simple depressurisation rarely desorbs *all* the heavy species (residual loading $q_R > 0$). A counter-current purge gas with a partial-pressure gradient *sweeps* the heavy component out and *sharpens* the mass-transfer zone (MTZ).
- **Why cycle continuously?** Because at any instant only a thin **mass-transfer zone (MTZ)** is doing the actual separation. The bed is long enough that the MTZ never reaches the product end during the adsorption step, ensuring purity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT-SHEET — All key equations, units and boundary conditions at a glance.** Memorise this table — it covers ~85% of numerical questions on PSA/VSA in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Boundary Conditions | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $q^* = \dfrac{q_m \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$ | $q^*, q_m$ in mol/kg; $P$ in bar; $b$ in bar$^{-1}$ | Monolayer adsorption; $b$ is the affinity constant |
| 2 | **Henry's Law (limit)** | $q^* \approx K_H \, P$ | Valid for $bP \ll 1$ (low coverage) | Linear regime at low $P$ |
| 3 | **Working Capacity** | $\Delta q = q_H^* - q_L^*$ | mol/kg | Actual adsorbable amount per cycle |
| 4 | **Pressure Ratio** | $\beta = P_H / P_L$ | Dimensionless; **PSA: 3–10**, **VSA: 4–30** | Driver of $\Delta q$ |
| 5 | **Linear Driving Force (LDF)** | $\dfrac{\partial \bar{q}}{\partial t} = k \, (q^* - \bar{q})$ | $k$ in s$^{-1}$; typical $k = 0.1 - 10 \; s^{-1}$ | Approximation to intraparticle diffusion |
| 6 | **Breakthrough Time (simplified)** | $t_b = \dfrac{L}{u} \left(1 - \varepsilon\right) \dfrac{\rho_s \, q^*}{C_0}$ | s; $L$ = bed length, $u$ = superficial velocity | Time for outlet concentration to rise |
| 7 | **Bed Capacity** | $W_{ads} = (1-\varepsilon)\rho_s \, q^* \, A_{cs} \, L$ | mol | Total moles adsorbed in bed |
