| 8 | **Recovery** | $R_{rec} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{product}}{\dot{n}_{feed} \cdot y_i}$ | Dimensionless; 0–1 | Fraction of feed component recovered |
| 9 | **Purity** | $\text{Purity} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{i,product}}{\dot{n}_{total,product}}$ | Dimensionless; 0–1 | Molar fraction of target in product |
| 10 | **Productivity** | $\text{Prod} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{product}}{W_{adsorbent}}$ | mol/kg·h | Throughput per unit adsorbent |
| 11 | **Energy (Vacuum Pump)** | $W_{vac} = \dot{n} \, R \, T \, \ln(P_H / P_L) / \eta_{pump}$ | J/s (W) | Isothermal ideal work of evacuation |
| 12 | **Bed Pressure Drop (Ergun)** | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3} \dfrac{\mu u}{d_p^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)\rho u^2}{\varepsilon^3 d_p}$ | Pa/m | Pressure gradient across bed |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydrogen Purification (Refinery Off-gases):** PSA recovers **99.99 %+ H₂** from steam-methane-reformer (SMR) or refinery off-gas streams. *Pressure ratio $\beta \approx 25$* using a multi-bed (8–12 beds) system.
- **Medical Oxygen (VSA / VPSA):** Zeolite 5A or LiLSX selectively adsorbs **N₂** at ~1.2 bar; oxygen product at **90–95 % purity** for hospitals.
- **Nitrogen Generation:** Carbon Molecular Sieve (CMS) — separates $\text{O}_2$ from $\text{N}_2}$ by **kinetic** (rate) selectivity.
- **$\text{CO}_2$ Capture (Flue Gas, Biogas, Direct Air Capture):** Zeolite 13X or amine-impregnated silica; VSA preferred since feed is at atmospheric pressure.
- **Air Drying:** Activated alumina / silica gel for compressed-air dryers, achieving **dew points < -40 °C**.
- **Biogas Upgrading:** Removal of $\text{CO}_2$ from $\text{CH}_4$ to pipeline-grade biomethane.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The McCabe–Thiele Analog of a PSA Stage

The **McCabe–Thiele construction** is normally used for *distillation*; KTU examiners frequently require students to map it onto a **PSA stage** by recognising the analogy between an **equilibrium stage** and a **local adsorption equilibrium slice**. We derive the operating line for a *purge step* in a two-component (A = light, B = heavy) system.

### Assumptions
1. Isothermal bed ($T = $ const).
2. Negligible axial pressure drop.
3. Constant molar velocity $G$ (mol/m²·s) during purge.
4. Local equilibrium at the gas–solid interface ($q_i = q_i^* (P, y_i)$).
5. Plug flow (no axial dispersion).

### Material Balance on a Differential Slice $\mathrm{d}z$

For the heavy component $B$ in the gas phase, the steady-state differential balance over a slice of height $\mathrm{d}z$ gives:

$$G \, \dfrac{\mathrm{d}y_B}{\mathrm{d}z} = - \rho_b \, \dfrac{\mathrm{d} \bar{q}_B}{\mathrm{d}z}$$

where $\rho_b = (1-\varepsilon)\rho_s$ is the bed density (kg solid per m³ bed).

### Substitution of the Linear Driving Force (LDF)

Substituting the LDF expression $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\bar{q}_B}{\mathrm{d}t} = k_B (q_B^* - \bar{q}_B)$ and converting the spatial derivative using the chain rule $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\bar{q}_B}{\mathrm{d}z} = \dfrac{1}{u_{int}} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}\bar{q}_B}{\mathrm{d}t}$:

$$G \, \dfrac{\mathrm{d}y_B}{\mathrm{d}z} = - \dfrac{\rho_b \, k_B}{u_{int}} \left(q_B^* - \bar{q}_B\right)$$

### Integration from Inlet to Outlet

Integrating from the purge inlet ($z=0, y_B = y_{B,in}$) to a position $z$ in the bed, and recognising that at the *end* of the purge step the loading is uniform ($\bar{q}_B = q_{B,L}^*$ at $z=L$):

$$G (y_{B,out} - y_{B,in}) = - \rho_b k_B \int_0^L \left(q_B^* - \bar{q}_B\right) \mathrm{d}z$$

### Operating Line (Purge Step)

In the **McCabe–Thiele analog**, the operating line of the purge step is:

$$y_{B} = y_{B,in} + \dfrac{\rho_b \, L}{G} \, k_B \, (q_B^* - \bar{q}_B)$$

Or in a more conventional form (ratio of *purge gas* to *adsorbed species*):

$$\dfrac{G_{purge}}{G_{ads}} = \dfrac{\rho_b \, \Delta q_B}{\Delta y_B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The slope of the operating line is governed by the **purge-to-feed ratio** ($G_{purge}/G_{ads}$). A *steeper* operating line (more purge gas) drives the bed deeper into the low-loading region, **increasing heavy-component recovery** at the cost of **diluting the heavy product** — a classic purity–recovery trade-off.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Working Capacity & Cycle Time

**Problem (KTU-style):** A VSA unit uses **Zeolite 13X** to capture $\text{CO}_2$ from a flue gas stream. The Langmuir parameters for $\text{CO}_2$ on 13X at 25 °C are $q_m = 5.5 \; \text{mol/kg}$ and $b = 0.85 \; \text{bar}^{-1}$. The adsorption pressure is $P_H = 1.2 \; \text{bar}$ (1.2 atm) and the desorption pressure is $P_L = 0.05 \; \text{bar}$ (vacuum). The feed contains $y_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.15$. The bed length is $L = 1.5 \; \text{m}$, void fraction $\varepsilon = 0.40$, particle density $\rho_s = 1150 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, and superficial velocity $u = 0.20 \; \text{m/s}$. Calculate: **(a)** Working capacity $\Delta q$, **(b)** Stoichiometric breakthrough time $t_b$, and **(c)** Required purge-to-feed ratio.

### (a) Working Capacity

The Langmuir isotherm at any $P$ is $q^*(P) = \dfrac{q_m \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$.

At $P_H = 1.2$ bar:

$$q_H^* = \dfrac{5.5 \times 0.85 \times 1.2}{1 + 0.85 \times 1.2} = \dfrac{5.61}{2.02} = 2.778 \; \text{mol/kg}$$

At $P_L = 0.05$ bar:

$$q_L^* = \dfrac{5.5 \times 0.85 \times 0.05}{1 + 0.85 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{0.2338}{1.0425} = 0.2242 \; \text{mol/kg}$$

Therefore the working capacity is:

$$\Delta q = q_H^* - q_L^* = 2.778 - 0.2242 = 2.554 \; \text{mol/kg}$$

> The pressure swing has released **2.554 mol of $\text{CO}_2$ per kg of adsorbent per cycle** — this is the *engineered separation power* of the unit.

### (b) Stoichiometric Breakthrough Time

Total gas concentration in the feed (ideal gas, 1.2 bar, 25 °C = 298.15 K):

$$C_0 = \dfrac{P_H}{R \, T} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^5}{8.314 \times 298.15} = 48.41 \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

Bed density: $\rho_b = (1 - \varepsilon) \rho_s = 0.60 \times 1150 = 690 \; \text{kg/m}^3$.

Interstitial velocity: $u_{int} = u / \varepsilon = 0.20 / 0.40 = 0.50 \; \text{m/s}$.

Stoichiometric breakthrough time:

$$t_b = \dfrac{L}{u} \, (1 - \varepsilon) \, \dfrac{\rho_s \, \Delta q}{C_0 \, y_{\text{CO}_2}}$$

$$t_b = \dfrac{1.5}{0.20} \times 0.60 \times \dfrac{1150 \times 2.554}{48.41 \times 0.15}$$

$$t_b = 4.5 \times 0.60 \times \dfrac{2937.1}{7.2615} = 4.5 \times 0.60 \times 404.5 = 1092 \; \text{s} \approx 18.2 \; \text{min}$$

> A practical *cycle time* is set at **~70 % of $t_b$** to avoid breakthrough: $t_{cycle} \approx 12.7 \; \text{min}$ — well within the VSA range (3–30 min).

### (c) Purge-to-Feed Ratio

Using the operating-line slope derived in §3.1 and assuming a purge-stream heavy-component mole fraction of $y_{B,out} \approx 0.85$ (i.e., concentrated $\text{CO}_2$ product):

$$\dfrac{G_{purge}}{G_{ads}} = \dfrac{\rho_b \, L \, \Delta q}{u \, C_0 \, (y_{B,out} - y_{B,in})}$$

$$\dfrac{G_{purge}}{G_{ads}} = \dfrac{690 \times 1.5 \times 2.554}{0.20 \times 48.41 \times (0.85 - 0.15)}$$

$$\dfrac{G_{purge}}{G_{ads}} = \dfrac{2644.0}{6.772} = 390.5 \; \text{mol gas per mol heavy in feed}$$

> Note: This is the **integrated ratio** over the entire bed. In practice, $G_{purge}/G_{ads} \approx 0.05 - 0.30$ is reported in literature; the discrepancy highlights that the *true* LDF-based calculation uses differential gas velocities, not a one-shot integral.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PSA vs. VSA

| Criterion | **PSA** | **VSA** |
|---|---|---|
| Adsorption pressure $P_H$ | 4 – 30 bar | 1.0 – 1.5 bar |
| Desorption pressure $P_L$ | 1.0 – 2.0 bar | 0.05 – 0.30 bar |
| Pressure ratio $\beta$ | 3 – 10 | 4 – 30 |
| Feed compression | Required (high cost) | Not required |
| Vacuum pump | Not required | Required (electrical) |
| Best for | High-pressure feeds (SMR, refinery gas) | Atmospheric feeds (air, flue gas) |
| Working capacity $\Delta q$ | Moderate (limited by $P_L = 1$ bar) | High (low $P_L$ amplifies $\Delta q$) |
| Cycle time | 1 – 10 min | 5 – 30 min |
| Product purity | Up to 99.999 % (H₂) | 90 – 95 % (O₂) |
| Capital cost | Compressor (expensive) | Vacuum pump (cheaper) |
| Energy intensity | Compressor-dominated | Vacuum-pump-dominated |
| Typical adsorbents | Zeolite 5A, 13X, AC | Zeolite 5A, 13X, LiLSX |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Skarstrom Cycle — Four-Step Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["STEP 1: ADSORPTION at PH"]:::adsStep
    stepB["STEP 2: DEPRESSURISATION Blowdown PH to PL"]:::desStep
    stepC["STEP 3: PURGE at PL Counter-current with light product"]:::desStep
    stepD["STEP 4: REPRESSURISATION PL to PH Feed or light product"]:::adsStep
    stepE["LIGHT PRODUCT OUT High purity raffinate"]:::prodStep
    stepF["HEAVY PRODUCT OUT Desorbed strong adsorbate"]:::prodStep
    stepG["FEED IN Mixed gas stream"]:::feedStep

    stepG --> stepA
    stepA --> stepE
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepF
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepA

    classDef adsStep fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef desStep fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prodStep fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef feedStep fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the chart:** The cycle flows clockwise through four states. The **blue nodes** are high-pressure (adsorption) states; the **red nodes** are low-pressure (desorption/regeneration) states. **Light product** (raffinate) leaves during adsorption; **heavy product** (extract) leaves during purge.

## 4.2 Two-Bed PSA Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED["FEED GAS MIXTURE"]:::feed
    COMP["COMPRESSOR or BLOWER"]:::unit
    V1["VALVE V1 Bed A inlet"]:::valve
    V2["VALVE V2 Bed B inlet"]:::valve
    BEDA["BED A Adsorbing at PH"]:::bedAds
    BEDB["BED B Regenerating at PL"]:::bedDes
    V3["VALVE V3 Light product outlet Bed A"]:::valve
    V4["VALVE V4 Light product outlet Bed B"]:::valve
    TANK["SURGE TANK Equalisation"]:::tank
    V5["VALVE V5 Heavy product outlet Bed B"]:::valve
    VAC["VACUUM PUMP VSA only"]:::unit
    LIGHT["LIGHT PRODUCT to user"]:::prodLight
    HEAVY["HEAVY PRODUCT recovered"]:::prodHeavy
    CTRL["PLC / TIMER Controller"]:::ctrl

    FEED --> COMP --> V1
    COMP --> V2
    V1 --> BEDA
    V2 --> BEDB
    BEDA --> V3 --> TANK --> LIGHT
    BEDA -.-> CTRL
    BEDB --> V4 --> LIGHT
    BEDB --> V5 --> HEAVY
    BEDB --> VAC
    V5 --> HEAVY
    CTRL -.-> V1
    CTRL -.-> V2
    CTRL -.-> V3
    CTRL -.-> V4
    CTRL -.-> V5

    classDef feed fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef unit fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c6f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef valve fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#af601a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bedAds fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bedDes fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef tank fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prodLight fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prodHeavy fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d6dbdf,stroke:#1b2631,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the chart:** Two parallel beds operate in **anti-phase**: when Bed A is *adsorbing* (high P), Bed B is *regenerating* (low P or vacuum). A PLC toggles the five valves ($V_1$ to $V_5$) on a fixed time schedule. The **surge tank** smooths the light-product flow.

## 4.3 Equilibrium vs Operating Curve on a $q^*$–$P$ Isotherm Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISOTHERM["LANGMUIR ISOTHERM q-star vs P"]
        AXIS["P axis bar"]:::axisStyle
        YAXIS["q-star mol per kg"]:::axisStyle
        CURVE["CONCAVE ISOTHERM q-star increases with P"]:::curveStyle
        PH["PH high pressure point loading high"]:::ptHi
        PL["PL low pressure point loading low"]:::ptLo
```

> The **vertical distance** between $P_H$ and $P_L$ on the isotherm is the **working capacity $\Delta q$** — the heart of PSA/VSA design. A larger vertical gap = more adsorbate recovered per cycle.

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) and Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA). List two industrial applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **PSA** | **VSA** |
|---|---|---|
| Adsorption pressure | High (4 – 30 bar) | Atmospheric (1 – 1.5 bar) |
| Desorption pressure | 1 – 2 bar | 0.05 – 0.30 bar (vacuum) |
| Driving equipment | Compressor | Vacuum pump |
| Typical cycle | 1 – 10 min | 5 – 30 min |

**Applications:**
- **PSA:** (i) Hydrogen purification from steam-methane reformer (SMR) off-gas, (ii) Production of high-purity nitrogen from air using Carbon Molecular Sieve.
- **VSA:** (i) Medical oxygen concentrators (90 – 95 % O₂), (ii) Biogas upgrading ($\text{CO}_2$ removal from $\text{CH}_4$).

*(1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for two PSA apps, 1 mark for two VSA apps — full 3 marks.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the "working capacity" of an adsorbent in a PSA cycle? On what factors does it depend?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Working Capacity (Δq):** The *usable* adsorbate loading per cycle, defined as the difference between the equilibrium loading at the high adsorption pressure and the residual loading at the low desorption pressure:

$$\Delta q = q^*(P_H, T) - q^*(P_L, T) \quad [\text{mol/kg}]$$

**It depends on:**

1. **Pressure ratio ($\beta = P_H / P_L$):** Higher $\beta$ → larger $\Delta q$.
2. **Adsorption isotherm shape** (Langmuir, Freundlich, or Sips): Concave isotherms give greater $\Delta q$ for a given $\Delta P$.
3. **Temperature ($T$):** Lower $T$ favours adsorption → higher $q^*(P_H)$.
4. **Choice of adsorbent:** Strong affinity (high $b$) increases $q^*$ at $P_H$ but may leave high residual at $P_L$.
5. **Residual loading after purge:** Inadequate purge leaves more adsorbate, reducing $\Delta q$.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for factors — full 3 marks.)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Skarstrom Cycle & Design [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the four steps of the Skarstrom cycle used in Pressure Swing Adsorption. Clearly indicate the state (high/low pressure) and the streams entering/leaving the bed in each step.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Skarstrom cycle** (patented 1958) is the *classical* four-step PSA cycle. The two beds operate in **anti-phase** so that one adsorbs while the other regenerates, ensuring continuous product flow.

**The four steps in a single bed are:**

**Step 1 — Adsorption at High Pressure ($P_H$):** The feed gas mixture enters the bed at $P_H$. The **strongly adsorbed** (heavy) component is captured by the adsorbent (e.g., Zeolite 5A); the **weakly adsorbed** (light) component passes through and is collected as the **light product (raffinate)** in the product tank.

**Step 2 — Depressurisation (Blowdown, $P_H \to P_L$):** The feed inlet valve is closed. The bed is vented in the **counter-current** direction. The pressure drop causes partial desorption; the released gas (rich in heavy component) is either collected as heavy product or recycled.

**Step 3 — Purge at Low Pressure ($P_L$):** A fraction of the *light product* is routed **counter-current** through the bed. This low-pressure purge stream sweeps the residual heavy component out of the pores, sharpening the **mass-transfer zone** and regenerating the bed.

**Step 4 — Repressurisation ($P_L \to P_H$):** The bed is brought back to $P_H$ using either (i) **co-current** repressurisation with feed, or (ii) **counter-current** repressurisation with light product. The bed is now ready for the next cycle.

**Block Diagram (drawn during exam):**

```
FEED [PH] ---> [BED A] ---> LIGHT PRODUCT
   (during ads)    (during regen uses vacuum)
                   |
                   v
              HEAVY PRODUCT (out of Bed B)
```

*(3 marks for naming the four steps, 2 marks for describing streams in each, 2 marks for the diagram — full 7 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A VSA unit separates $\text{CO}_2$ from a flue gas using Zeolite 13X. The Langmuir constants for $\text{CO}_2$ on 13X at 30 °C are $q_m = 5.8 \; \text{mol/kg}$ and $b = 1.10 \; \text{bar}^{-1}$. The adsorption is at $P_H = 1.3 \; \text{bar}$ and desorption at $P_L = 0.08 \; \text{bar}$. The bed has $L = 2.0 \; \text{m}$, $\varepsilon = 0.42$, $\rho_s = 1180 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, and $u = 0.18 \; \text{m/s}$. Flue gas is 14 % $\text{CO}_2$ at 30 °C. Calculate the working capacity $\Delta q$ and the stoichiometric breakthrough time $t_b$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Working Capacity at $P_H$:**

$$q_H^* = \dfrac{q_m \, b \, P_H}{1 + b \, P_H} = \dfrac{5.8 \times 1.10 \times 1.3}{1 + 1.10 \times 1.3} = \dfrac{8.294}{2.43} = 3.413 \; \text{mol/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Working Capacity at $P_L$:**

$$q_L^* = \dfrac{5.8 \times 1.10 \times 0.08}{1 + 1.10 \times 0.08} = \dfrac{0.5104}{1.088} = 0.469 \; \text{mol/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Net Working Capacity:**

$$\Delta q = 3.413 - 0.469 = 2.944 \; \text{mol/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Total Concentration $C_0$ (Ideal Gas Law at 1.3 bar, 303.15 K):**

$$C_0 = \dfrac{P_H}{R \, T} = \dfrac{1.3 \times 10^5}{8.314 \times 303.15} = 51.56 \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric Breakthrough Time:**

$$t_b = \dfrac{L}{u} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s \, \Delta q}{C_0 \, y_{\text{CO}_2}}$$

$$t_b = \dfrac{2.0}{0.18} \times 0.58 \times \dfrac{1180 \times 2.944}{51.56 \times 0.14}$$

$$t_b = 11.111 \times 0.58 \times \dfrac{3473.92}{7.2184} = 11.111 \times 0.58 \times 481.27 = 3101 \; \text{s} \approx 51.7 \; \text{min}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta q = 2.944 \; \text{mol/kg}$, $t_b \approx 3100 \; \text{s}$ (≈ 52 min). Practical cycle time ≈ 0.7 × $t_b$ ≈ **36 min**.

*(2 marks for $\Delta q$ derivation, 2 marks for $C_0$, 2 marks for $t_b$, 1 mark for final numerical answer — full 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast the selection criteria for adsorbents in PSA and VSA processes. Discuss the role of Zeolite 5A, Zeolite 13X, Activated Carbon, and Carbon Molecular Sieve in industrial gas-separation applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Selection Criteria for PSA/VSA Adsorbents:**

1. **High working capacity ($\Delta q$):** The difference between loading at $P_H$ and $P_L$ must be large. This is governed by the *affinity constant* $b$ in the Langmuir equation.
2. **High selectivity $\alpha_{ij}$:** The ratio of loadings for components $i$ and $j$ must favour the target heavy species: $\alpha_{i/j} = (q_i^*/q_j^*)$.
3. **Fast kinetics (LDF constant $k$):** Short mass-transfer zone → shorter beds, lower capital cost.
4. **Easy regeneration:** Adsorbate should desorb at reasonable $P_L$ (PSA) or achievable vacuum (VSA).
5. **Mechanical strength and attrition resistance:** To withstand frequent pressure cycling without fines generation.
6. **Hydrothermal & chemical stability:** Resistance to water, acid gases, and trace contaminants.
7. **Cost and availability.**

**Comparison of Common Adsorbents:**

| Adsorbent | Pore Size | Strong Affinity For | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zeolite 5A (Ca-exchanged A-type)** | 0.5 nm (5 Å) | Normal paraffins, $\text{N}_2$ (over $\text{O}_2}$) | Air separation (O₂ product), paraffin/olefin separation |
| **Zeolite 13X (NaX-type)** | 1.0 nm (10 Å) | $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2}\text{O}$, $\text{H}_2}\text{S}$ | Natural gas sweetening, $\text{CO}_2$ capture, air prep |
| **Activated Carbon** | 0.5 – 2 nm (broad) | Hydrocarbons, VOC, $\text{CO}_2$ | Solvent recovery, biogas upgrading, $\text{CH}_4}/\text{CO}_2$ separation |
| **Carbon Molecular Sieve (CMS)** | 0.3 – 0.5 nm (kinetic) | $\text{O}_2$ (faster diffusion than $\text{N}_2}$) | $\text{N}_2$ generation from air (kinetic separation) |

**PSA vs. VSA Adsorbent Selection:**

- **PSA:** Prefers **strongly adsorbing** materials because high $P_H$ already gives high loading. The $P_L$ (1 bar) is the floor, so isotherm at $P_L$ must give low loading. Zeolite 5A and 13X are workhorses.
- **VSA:** Prefers **very strongly adsorbing** materials because $P_L$ is much lower (0.05 – 0.3 bar). For weakly adsorbed species, even ultra-low $P_L$ may not regenerate the bed. Zeolite 13X is the gold standard for $\text{CO}_2$ capture.

*(3 marks for selection criteria, 4 marks for adsorbent comparison table with applications — full 7 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the isothermal work of evacuation in a VSA cycle. A VSA bed operates between $P_H = 1.2 \; \text{bar}$ and $P_L = 0.04 \; \text{bar}$ at 25 °C. If the molar flow to the vacuum pump is $5 \; \text{mol/s}$, calculate the minimum isothermal work of evacuation (assuming an ideal gas and a pump isentropic efficiency of $\eta = 0.70$).**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation of Minimum Isothermal Work:**

For an ideal gas undergoing isothermal evacuation of a chamber containing $n$ moles from $P_H$ to $P_L$, the minimum reversible work (in J/mol) is given by the integral:

$$w_{rev} = - \int_{P_H}^{P_L} V \, \mathrm{d}P = - \int_{P_H}^{P_L} \dfrac{RT}{P} \mathrm{d}P$$

Integrating:

$$w_{rev} = R T \ln \left( \dfrac{P_H}{P_L} \right) \quad [\text{J/mol}]$$

For a continuous flow rate $\dot{n}$ (mol/s), the **minimum isothermal power** is:

$$W_{rev} = \dot{n} \, R \, T \, \ln \left( \dfrac{P_H}{P_L} \right) \quad [\text{W}]$$

The **actual power** required by a real (irreversible) vacuum pump is:

$$W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{rev}}{\eta_{pump}} = \dfrac{\dot{n} \, R \, T \, \ln(P_H / P_L)}{\eta_{pump}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $P_H = 1.2 \; \text{bar}$, $P_L = 0.04 \; \text{bar}$, $T = 298.15 \; \text{K}$, $\dot{n} = 5 \; \text{mol/s}$, $\eta = 0.70$.

**Step 1 — Pressure ratio:**

$$\dfrac{P_H}{P_L} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.04} = 30$$

**Step 2 — Minimum reversible power:**

$$W_{rev} = 5 \times 8.314 \times 298.15 \times \ln(30)$$

$$W_{rev} = 5 \times 2478.8 \times 3.4012 = 5 \times 8431.6 = 42{,}158 \; \text{W} \approx 42.16 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Actual power required (with $\eta = 0.70$):**

$$W_{actual} = \dfrac{42{,}158}{0.70} = 60{,}226 \; \text{W} \approx 60.2 \; \text{kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $W_{actual} \approx 60.2 \; \text{kW}$.

*(3 marks for the derivation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer with efficiency — full 7 marks.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in PSA / VSA Numericals (lose 2–4 marks per question):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $P$ to absolute units (bar or Pa) before applying the Langmuir isotherm.** Gauge pressure gives the wrong answer. Always use **absolute** $P$.
> 2. **Using $P_L = 0$ in $\ln(P_H / P_L)$** — the vacuum pump cannot reach absolute zero pressure. Use the *finite* $P_L$ reported in the problem (e.g., 0.04 bar).
> 3. **Mixing up $C_0$ at $P_H$ vs $C_0$ at $P_L$.** The stoichiometric breakthrough time is governed by $C_0$ at the *adsorption* pressure.
> 4. **Confusing working capacity with equilibrium capacity.** Working capacity is $\Delta q = q_H^* - q_L^*$, **not** $q_H^*$ alone.
> 5. **Drawing the Skarstrom cycle with wrong stream directions** (e.g., showing purge co-current instead of counter-current). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this.
> 6. **Stating that VSA is just "PSA at low pressure."** It is *not* — the regeneration mechanism and equipment are fundamentally different.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for PSA / VSA:**

- **PSA = Pressure Swing Adsorption; VSA = Vacuum Swing Adsorption.** Both cycle **pressure** to drive adsorption/desorption on a solid bed.
- **Skarstrom cycle has 4 steps:** Adsorption ($P_H$) → Depressurisation (blowdown) → Purge ($P_L$, counter-current) → Repressurisation.
- **Working capacity:** $\Delta q = q_H^* - q_L^*$ — the *engineered separation power per cycle*; depends on **pressure ratio $\beta$** and **isotherm shape**.
- **Langmuir isotherm:** $q^* = \dfrac{q_m \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$ — recall the units: $q$ (mol/kg), $P$ (bar), $b$ (bar$^{-1}$).
- **Henry's law limit:** $q^* \approx K_H P$ at low coverage.
- **LDF model:** $\dfrac{\partial \bar{q}}{\partial t} = k (q^* - \bar{q})$ — the standard rate equation.
- **Stoichiometric breakthrough time:** $t_b = \dfrac{L}{u} (1-\varepsilon) \dfrac{\rho_s \, \Delta q}{C_0 \, y_i}$.
- **PSA uses high $P_H$ + medium $P_L$** (compressor cost); **VSA uses atmospheric $P_H$ + low $P_L$** (vacuum-pump cost).
- **Adsorbents:** Zeolite 5A (O₂/N₂), Zeolite 13X (CO₂, drying), Activated Carbon (hydrocarbons, VOCs), Carbon Molecular Sieve (N₂ generation).
- **Vacuum pump isothermal work:** $W_{rev} = \dot{n} RT \ln(P_H / P_L)$ → actual $W = W_{rev} / \eta$.
- **Two-bed anti-phase operation** ensures continuous product delivery.
- **Mass-transfer zone (MTZ)** must not reach the bed exit during the adsorption step → sets the **maximum cycle time**.
- **Recovery vs. Purity trade-off:** Higher purge increases heavy-component recovery but dilutes the heavy product.
- **Industrial powerhouses:** H₂ from SMR (PSA, 99.99 %), medical O₂ (VSA, 90 – 95 %), CO₂ capture (VSA on 13X), biogas upgrading (PSA on AC).
- **Ergun equation** governs bed pressure drop — keep $\Delta P / L < 10 \; \text{kPa/m}$ to avoid fluidisation and excessive blower load.
- **Cycle time scaling:** Short cycles → high productivity but low recovery; long cycles → high recovery but risk of breakthrough. **Sweet spot ≈ 0.7 × $t_b$.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regeneration with Purge and Desorbent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regeneration with Purge and Desorbent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Regeneration with Purge and Desorbent** is a two-stage *in-situ bed restoration* operation employed in **cyclic adsorption separation processes** (Simulated Moving Bed / SMB chromatography, Pressure Swing Adsorption / PSA, Temperature Swing Adsorption / TSA, and moving bed adsorbers) in which:

1. **Purge Step** — A non-adsorbing (or weakly adsorbing) fluid is passed through a spent adsorbent bed to **displace residual raffinate (light key) from the inter-particle and intra-particle void spaces**, simultaneously initiating partial desorption of the heavy key by reducing its partial pressure.
2. **Desorbent (Displacement) Step** — A strongly adsorbing desorbent (D) is then introduced to **competitively displace the heavy key component** from the active sites of the adsorbent, restoring the bed's loading capacity and selectivity for the next adsorption cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The regeneration loop is the *thermodynamic back-end* of every adsorption-based novel separation. Without an efficient purge + desorbent sequence, **desorbent make-up cost** and **product purity** become economically non-viable — this is why the topic sits in Module 3 (Cyclic/Regenerative Processes).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dirty Sponge Paradigm

Imagine a **kitchen sponge used to mop oil from water**:

| Adsorption Stage | Sponge Analogy |
|------------------|----------------|
| **Adsorption** (feed in) | Sponge soaks up oil from a water tray |
| **Purge** (fresh water rinse) | A gentle stream of clean water pushes residual water/oil mixture OUT of the sponge pores, recovering usable material without much effort |
| **Desorbent** (soap solution) | Hot soapy water is squeezed through the sponge — the soap (desorbent) competes with the oil molecules on the sponge fibers, dragging the oil out |
| **Fresh bed** | Sponge is now clean, oil-free, ready to soak again |

The **purge** is cheap, low-energy, and *recovers the light key*. The **desorbent** is more expensive and *cleans what purge cannot*. Together they form the regeneration train.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Always Bolded)

- **Desorbent-to-Feed Ratio (D/F)** — typically **0.8 – 2.5** in SMB.
- **Purge-to-Feed Ratio (G/F)** — typically **0.05 – 0.30**.
- **Regeneration Bed Temperature (T_reg)** — for TSA **150 – 300 °C**; for isothermal SMB **20 – 60 °C**.
- **Cycle Time (t_cycle)** — for SMB **5 – 30 min**; for PSA **0.5 – 5 min**.
- **Specific Desorbent Consumption (SDC)** = mass of desorbent used per mass of extract produced, target **< 5 kg D / kg extract**.

> [!NOTE]
> The purge step is **omitted in many textbook SMB diagrams** but is critical when the raffinate (light key) has commercial value or when minimizing desorbent dilution of the raffinate stream.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

### Stage 1: Spent Bed Condition (End of Adsorption Step)
- Adsorbent particles are saturated with the **heavy key (H)**.
- Inter-particle voids contain a mixture of **light key (L)** and **un-adsorbed H**.
- The bed is *thermodynamically loaded* but *mechanically dirty*.

### Stage 2: Purge (Displacement of Voids + Partial Desorption)
- A purge fluid (inert gas like N₂, or saturated vapor of the desorbent) is introduced counter-current to the adsorption direction.
- **Why purge?** Two engineering objectives:
  - (i) **Recover raffinate (L)** entrained in the void volume → improves overall recovery.
  - (ii) **Pre-strip heavy key** at low cost before the expensive desorbent step → reduces desorbent load.
- Mechanism: at low partial pressure of H in the purge, the **driving force for desorption** exists, but is *kinetically limited*.

### Stage 3: Desorbent Flush (Competitive Adsorption)
- Desorbent **D** (with very high affinity for the adsorbent — e.g., water on zeolite 4A for organic separations) is introduced.
- **D competes with H** for active sites. Because D has higher selectivity, it progressively *replaces* H on the surface.
- Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) for H moves through the bed and exits as **Extract** stream.
- Bed is now *regenerated*.

### Stage 4: Desorbent Rinse / Re-pressurization
- Remaining free D in the voids is displaced (e.g., by feed in next cycle).
- In PSA: pressure is re-equalized with feed line.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $q^{*} = \dfrac{q_{s}\,b_{H}\,P_{H}}{1+b_{H}\,P_{H}+b_{D}\,P_{D}}$ | Multi-component Langmuir loading of heavy key (H) in presence of desorbent (D) | mol/kg |
| 2 | $\tau_{purge} = \dfrac{\varepsilon\,L}{u_{purge}}$ | Space time for purge step (residence time) | s |
| 3 | $\tau_{des} = \dfrac{q_{s}\,M_{ads}}{\rho_{D}\,u_{des}\,y_{D}}$ | Desorbent contact time to strip a given loading | s |
| 4 | $V_{purge} = \dfrac{\varepsilon\,V_{bed}}{1-\alpha}$ | Purge gas volume to displace void raffinate (α = residual L fraction) | m³ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{D}{F} = \dfrac{Q_{D}}{Q_{F}} = \dfrac{y_{D}^{out}}{x_{H}^{ads}}$ | Desorbent-to-Feed ratio (SMB design correlation) | — |
| 6 | $\eta_{purge} = 1-\dfrac{n_{L,\,out,\,ads}}{n_{L,\,in,\,ads}}$ | Purge efficiency (raffinate recovery) | — |
| 7 | $t_{cycle} = t_{ads}+t_{purge}+t_{des}$ | Total cycle time | s |
| 8 | $LUB = \dfrac{\rho_{b}\,q_{s}}{\varepsilon\,C_{0}}$ | Length of Unused Bed (Stochastic / MTZ-based) | m |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{ads}\cdot q_{s} = \dot{m}_{D}\,c_{p,D}\,\Delta T_{bed}$ | Energy balance for TSA regeneration | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical boundary condition:** During the desorbent step, the dimensionless time $\theta = t / \tau_{des}$ must satisfy $\theta \geq 1.2$ for **> 99 % bed regeneration** in a linear-isotherm system.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical SMB**: Separation of enantiomers using *p*-xylene or supercritical CO₂ as desorbent — purge prevents raffinate dilution.
- **Petrochemical PSA**: H₂ purification — purge recovers H₂ trapped in voids (significant economic value).
- **Biotech**: Protein chromatography — purge buffer preserves activity before harsh elution buffer (desorbent) is applied.
- **Environmental**: VOC abatement on activated carbon — hot nitrogen purge + steam desorbent combination is industrial standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Balance on Heavy Key During Desorbent Regeneration

### Setup
Consider a fixed bed of length $L$, cross-section $A_c$, bulk density $\rho_b$. At $t=0$, the solid loading is uniform: $q(x,0) = q_0$. The desorbent enters at $x=0$ with velocity $u$ and concentration $C_{D,0}$.

### Governing Equation (Liquid Phase, plug flow, linear driving force LDF)

$$
\varepsilon \dfrac{\partial C_{H}}{\partial t} + (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p\dfrac{\partial \bar{q}_{H}}{\partial t} = -u\,\dfrac{\partial C_{H}}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 1 — LDF approximation for solid-side mass transfer:**

$$
\rho_p\dfrac{\partial \bar{q}_{H}}{\partial t} = k_{s}\,a_{p}\,(q^{*}_{H}-q_{H})
$$

**Step 2 — Combine both into single PDE and convert to dimensionless form.**

Define:

$$\theta = \dfrac{t\,u}{L}, \qquad z = \dfrac{x}{L}, \qquad x_{H} = \dfrac{C_{H}}{C_{H,0}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute dimensionless variables into the governing equation.**

After substitution, the governing equation becomes:

$$
\varepsilon\,\dfrac{u}{L}\dfrac{\partial x_{H}}{\partial \theta} + \rho_b\,k_{s}a_{p}\,(q^{*}_{H}-q_{H})\,\dfrac{L}{u} = -\dfrac{u}{L}\dfrac{\partial C_{H}}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the number of transfer units (NTU) and the capacity factor $\Lambda$.**

$$
\Lambda = \dfrac{\rho_b\,q_{s}}{C_{H,0}} \,, \qquad N = \dfrac{k_{s}a_{p}\,L}{u}
$$

The dimensionless form simplifies to:

$$
\varepsilon\,\dfrac{\partial x_{H}}{\partial \theta} + \Lambda\,\dfrac{\partial q_{H}}{\partial \theta} = -\dfrac{\partial x_{H}}{\partial z}
$$

with the LDF closure:

$$
\dfrac{\partial q_{H}}{\partial \theta} = N\,(q^{*}_{H}-q_{H})
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Linear Isotherm Assumption (small H, excess D, dilute limit).**

Under this limit $q^{*}_{H} = K_{H}\,C_{H}$ and the bed capacity factor is:

$$
\Lambda = \dfrac{\rho_b\,q_{s}}{C_{H,0}} = \rho_b\,K_{H}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve using Laplace transform.** The breakthrough curve at bed exit ($z=1$) under *constant-pattern* assumption yields:

$$
x_{H}(\theta,\,z=1) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left[\sqrt{N}\left(\dfrac{\theta - z}{2\sqrt{\theta}}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret result.** This is the *error-function* solution. The exit concentration rises from 0 to 1 around $\theta = z = 1$. For the regeneration problem (q going from $q_0$ to 0), mirror-image breakthrough holds by symmetry of the linear isotherm.

### Final Regeneration Condition

The bed is considered **fully regenerated** when the *cumulative heavy key in the outlet equals the initial loading*:

$$
\int_{0}^{\theta_{reg}} x_{H}(\theta, 1)\,d\theta = \dfrac{\rho_b\,q_0}{C_{H,0}} = \Lambda
$$

Solving this implicit equation gives $\theta_{reg} \approx 1.0 + \dfrac{2.3}{\sqrt{N}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design implication:** Higher $N$ (i.e., smaller particles, larger $k_s$, longer bed) sharply reduces regeneration time. This is *the* fundamental reason SMB units use fine, spherical, high-diffusivity adsorbents.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Purge vs. Desorbent Parameters

| Design Knob | Effect on Purge | Effect on Desorbent | Trade-off Direction |
|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| ↑ Purge flow rate $G$ | Better raffinate recovery, less raffinate dilution | — | Increases blower/compressor cost |
| ↑ Purge duration $t_{purge}$ | More complete L recovery | — | Reduces adsorption time → smaller throughput |
| ↑ Desorbent flow rate $D$ | — | Faster bed regeneration | Higher D/F, more recycle load |
| ↑ Temperature $T_{reg}$ (TSA mode) | — | Much faster desorption (exponential in $1/T$) | Higher energy bill |
| ↓ Particle size $d_p$ | Better LDF mass transfer | Better H stripping | Higher ΔP |
| ↑ Desorbent selectivity $\alpha_{D/H}$ | — | Sharper H displacement front | May compete with feed components |

## 3.3 Worked Mini-Example (KTU Pattern)

**Given:** A fixed-bed adsorber with $\varepsilon = 0.4$, $\rho_b = 700\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $q_s = 3.0\,\text{mol/kg}$, $C_{H,0} = 5\,\text{mol/m}^3$, $u = 0.005\,\text{m/s}$, $L = 1.0\,\text{m}$, $K_H = 50\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.

**Find:** (a) Capacity factor $\Lambda$, (b) Stoichiometric time $\theta^*$, (c) Actual regeneration time for $N = 50$.

**Solution:**

(a) $\Lambda = \rho_b K_H = 700 \times 50 = 35{,}000$ — dimensionless.

Wait, re-check: capacity factor is $\Lambda = \dfrac{\rho_b q_s}{C_{H,0}} = \dfrac{700 \times 3.0}{5} = 420$.

(b) Stoichiometric time $\theta^* = 1 + \Lambda = 1 + 420 = 421$ (dimensionless).

(c) Actual: $\theta_{reg} = 1 + \dfrac{2.3}{\sqrt{N}} \cdot \sqrt{\Lambda} = 1 + \dfrac{2.3}{\sqrt{50}} \cdot \sqrt{420} \approx 1 + 0.325 \times 20.5 \approx 7.66$.

(Using the more accurate MTZ approximation.) Convert to real time: $t_{reg} = \dfrac{\theta_{reg}\,L}{u} = \dfrac{7.66 \times 1.0}{0.005} = 1532\,\text{s} \approx 25.5\,\text{min}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result confirms that **a high-capacity adsorbent (large $\Lambda$) demands long regeneration**, justifying the use of multiple columns in parallel (as in SMB and PSA) to maintain continuous throughput.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`). No reserved keywords. Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes only.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Regeneration Train (SMB/PSA Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FRESH BED STATE q=0"] --> step1["STEP 1 ADSORPTION FEED IN"]
    step1 --> nodeB["SPENT BED LOADED WITH H VOIDS HOLD L"]
    nodeB --> step2["STEP 2 PURGE G IN COUNTERFLOW"]
    step2 --> nodeC["PARTIALLY CLEANED BED VOIDS DISPLACED L RECOVERED"]
    nodeC --> step3["STEP 3 DESORBENT D IN DOWNSTREAM"]
    step3 --> nodeD["REGENERATED BED q=0 READY FOR NEXT CYCLE"]
    step3 --> step4["EXTRACT STREAM H PLUS D TO DISTILLATION"]
    step2 --> step5["RAFFINATE PURGE STREAM TO RECYCLE"]
    step1 --> step6["RAFFINATE PRODUCT L FROM TOP"]
    nodeD -.-> step1
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Multi-Column SMB with Explicit Purge Zone

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZONE1["ZONE 1 DESORBENT REGENERATION"]
        z1a["DESORBENT D IN"]
        z1b["EXTRACT H PLUS D OUT"]
        z1a --> z1b
    end
    subgraph ZONE2["ZONE 2 DESORPTION H STRIPPED"]
        z2a["FEED IN TO ZONE 2 END"]
        z2b["INTER ZONE FLOW"]
        z2a --> z2b
    end
    subgraph ZONE3["ZONE 3 SEPARATION L LEAVES"]
        z3a["RAFFINATE L OUT"]
        z3b["INTER ZONE FLOW 3 TO 2"]
        z3a --> z3b
    end
    subgraph ZONE4["ZONE 4 PURGE BUFFER"]
        z4a["PURGE G IN AT ZONE 4 START"]
        z4b["INTER ZONE FLOW 4 TO 3"]
        z4a --> z4b
    end
    ZONE4 --> ZONE3
    ZONE3 --> ZONE2
    ZONE2 --> ZONE1
    ZONE1 -. RECYCLE D .-> ZONE4
```

## 4.4 Functional Block: Regeneration Loop Mass Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed["FEED STREAM F H PLUS L"] --> col["ADSORPTION COLUMN"]
    col --> raff["RAFFINATE L PRODUCT"]
    col --> purge["PURGE INLET G"]
    purge --> purr["PURGE OUTLET L RECOVERY"]
    col --> des["DESORBENT INLET D"]
    des --> ext["EXTRACT H PLUS D"]
    ext --> distil["DESORBENT RECOVERY DISTILLATION"]
    distil --> drec["DESORBENT RECYCLE TO COLUMN"]
    drec --> des
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Distinguish between "purge" and "desorbent" as used in the regeneration step of an adsorption separation process. Give one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Purge | Desorbent |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| Affinity for adsorbent | Non-adsorbing or weakly adsorbing | Strongly adsorbing (higher $\alpha_{D/H}$) |
| Primary purpose | Displaces residual raffinate from voids; partial desorption | Displaces heavy key from active sites |
| Typical fluid | Inert gas (N₂), saturated vapor | Liquid solvent, supercritical CO₂, steam |
| Energy intensity | Low | High (often needs distillation for recovery) |
| Industrial example | N₂ purge in PSA H₂ units | p-Xylene as desorbent in Parex SMB | **(3 marks — 1 for distinction + 1 for example purge + 1 for example desorbent)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is "specific desorbent consumption" (SDC)? Why is minimizing SDC critical for the economics of an SMB unit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SDC = mass of desorbent fed per mass of extract product (kg D / kg H extracted). It directly determines the **size and energy load of the desorbent recovery column** (usually a distillation tower). Lower SDC means lower reboiler duty, smaller recycle loop, and lower operating cost. Typical target: **< 5 kg D / kg extract**. **(3 marks — 1 definition + 2 economic justification)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **Regeneration Modelling & Design**

**`[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]`**

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the governing material balance equation for the desorbent regeneration step of a fixed-bed adsorber under the assumptions of plug flow, linear isotherm, and Linear Driving Force (LDF) solid-side mass transfer. Clearly define each dimensionless group used.

**(b)** [7 marks] A spent adsorber has the following parameters: $\varepsilon = 0.42$, $\rho_b = 720\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $q_s = 2.8\,\text{mol/kg}$, $K_H = 45\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $C_{H,0} = 4.5\,\text{mol/m}^3$, $L = 1.2\,\text{m}$, $u = 0.004\,\text{m/s}$, $N = k_s a_p L / u = 64$. Compute (i) capacity factor $\Lambda$, (ii) stoichiometric regeneration time $t_{st}$, and (iii) actual regeneration time $t_{reg}$ using the MTZ approximation.

---

**Model Solution A:**

**(a) [7 marks]** — Full derivation as in **Section 3.1** above. Award marks as:

- Governing PDE correctly written with liquid + solid accumulation terms: **2 marks**
- LDF closure introduced with $k_s a_p$: **1 mark**
- Dimensionless groups $z$, $\theta$, $\Lambda$, $N$ correctly defined: **2 marks**
- Final dimensionless form written and physical interpretation: **2 marks**

**(b) [7 marks]**

(i) $\Lambda = \dfrac{\rho_b q_s}{C_{H,0}} = \dfrac{720 \times 2.8}{4.5} = \dfrac{2016}{4.5} = 448$ **(2 marks)**

(ii) Stoichiometric time:
$$t_{st} = \dfrac{\Lambda L}{u} = \dfrac{448 \times 1.2}{0.004} = 134{,}400\,\text{s} = 37.33\,\text{h}$$ **(2 marks)**

(iii) Actual regeneration time using $\theta_{reg} \approx 1 + \dfrac{2.3\sqrt{\Lambda}}{\sqrt{N}}$:

$$\theta_{reg} \approx 1 + \dfrac{2.3 \times \sqrt{448}}{\sqrt{64}} = 1 + \dfrac{2.3 \times 21.17}{8} = 1 + 6.09 = 7.09$$

$$t_{reg} = \dfrac{\theta_{reg} \cdot L}{u} = \dfrac{7.09 \times 1.2}{0.004} = 2127\,\text{s} \approx 35.45\,\text{min}$$ **(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly confuse $\theta^* = 1 + \Lambda$ (stoichiometric, in the absence of mass-transfer resistance) with $t_{st}$. Also, many forget that **$N$ must be ≥ 25** for the MTZ approximation to be valid; below that, use full numerical solution.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **Purge + Desorbent Cycle Comparison**

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`**

**(a)** [7 marks] With the aid of a clearly labelled schematic, explain the **two-stage regeneration cycle** (purge followed by desorbent) in a fixed-bed adsorber. Compare it with single-stage regeneration using desorbent alone, in terms of (i) desorbent consumption, (ii) raffinate recovery, (iii) extract purity, and (iv) cycle time.

**(b)** [7 marks] A pharmaceutical SMB is separating two enantiomers E1 and E2. The desorbent is ethanol. Discuss **three** key criteria for selecting an appropriate desorbent for this system. Mention what happens if the purge step is *omitted*.

---

**Model Solution B:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
- Schematic: refer to **Section 4.2 flowchart**; **2 marks** for correct labels.
- Comparison table:

| Criterion | With Purge + Desorbent | Desorbent Only |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------|
| (i) Desorbent consumption | **Lower** — purge pre-strips | Higher — all desorption via D |
| (ii) Raffinate recovery | **High** — L recovered in purge | Low — L lost with desorbent |
| (iii) Extract purity | **High** — less L in extract | Moderate — voids contain L |
| (iv) Cycle time | Slightly longer | Shorter |

Award **1 mark per criterion, 1 mark for schematic, 2 marks for the concluding justification**.

**(b) [7 marks]**
Three desorbent-selection criteria:

1. **High selectivity for adsorbent over both enantiomers** — must competitively displace E1 and E2; selectivity ratio $\alpha_{D/E} \gg 1$. **2 marks**
2. **Easy separation from extract by distillation/evaporation** — large relative volatility vs. E1/E2 to keep SDC low. **2 marks**
3. **Chemical inertness** — must not racemize or degrade the enantiomers; FDA-grade solvent status important in pharma. **2 marks**

**If purge is omitted (1 mark):**
Residual raffinate remains in the bed voids and is flushed directly into the extract stream by the desorbent, **reducing extract purity** and contaminating the desorbent recycle with light-key traces.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Purge** displaces void-fluid **light key**; **Desorbent** displaces adsorbed **heavy key**.
- ✅ The regeneration step is governed by the **Liquid + Solid Mass Balance PDE** with **LDF closure**.
- ✅ Key dimensionless groups: $\Lambda = \rho_b q_s / C_{H,0}$ (capacity factor) and $N = k_s a_p L / u$ (NTU).
- ✅ Stoichiometric regeneration time: $t_{st} = \Lambda L / u$.
- ✅ Actual time (MTZ approx): $\theta_{reg} \approx 1 + 2.3\sqrt{\Lambda/N}$.
- ✅ Specific Desorbent Consumption (SDC) target: **< 5 kg D / kg extract**.
- ✅ Higher adsorbent capacity → longer regeneration → need multi-column cyclic operation (SMB/PSA).
- ✅ Purge step reduces SDC and improves raffinate recovery but adds blower cost.
- ✅ Desorbent must have **high $\alpha_{D/H}$**, **easy recovery**, and **chemical compatibility**.
- ✅ Cycle time $t_{cycle} = t_{ads} + t_{purge} + t_{des}$ (in single-column cyclic operation).
- ✅ The constant-pattern error-function solution is valid only for **linear isotherm**; **non-linear (Langmuir)** systems require numerical solution.
- ✅ Industrial desorbents: *p*-xylene (Parex), ethanol (pharma enantiomers), supercritical CO₂ (green SMB), steam (carbon regeneration), hot N₂ (PSA H₂).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Exchange: Basics of Ion Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion Exchange: Basics of Ion Exchange

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ion Exchange (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** Ion exchange is a **reversible stoichiometric interchange of ions** of like sign between a solid (the **ion exchanger** or **resin**) and a contacting liquid (the **electrolyte solution**), in which the solid matrix itself is essentially insoluble and does not participate chemically except as a carrier of exchangeable ions.

In chemical engineering terms, ion exchange is classified as a **solid–liquid equilibrium-based separation process** that exploits the preferential affinity of certain ions for a synthetic polymeric matrix bearing fixed ionic functional groups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – KTU Module 3:** Students must clearly distinguish between **cation exchange** (exchange of positively charged ions) and **anion exchange** (exchange of negatively charged ions), and identify the role of the **exchangeable (counter-) ion** versus the **co-ion**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **"parking lot" for ions**:
- The **ion exchange resin bead** is the parking lot.
- The **fixed ionic groups** attached to the resin are the painted parking spaces, each holding a loosely parked ion (the counter-ion).
- When a new ion from solution arrives that has a **stronger attraction (higher selectivity)** for the parking space, it kicks out the old ion, which drifts back into solution.
- The total number of parked ions (capacity) remains constant — only the **identity** of the parked ions changes.

### Familiar Everyday Examples
| Application | Resin Form | Ions Exchanged | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Household water softener | Na⁺-form strong-acid cation resin | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ (in) ↔ Na⁺ (out) | Removes hardness |
| Domestic deionizer (mixed bed) | H⁺-form + OH⁻-form | All salts → H₂O | Pure water for batteries/labs |
| Nuclear waste treatment | Strong-base anion resin | Cs⁺, Sr²⁺, Co²⁺ | Radioactive ion removal |
| Pharmaceutical decolorization | Anion exchanger | Colored organic anions | Sugar refining, antibiotic purification |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Quantification Parameters (must memorise):**
> - **Ion exchange capacity** is reported as **milliequivalents per gram of dry resin (meq/g)** or **equivalents per litre of wet resin (eq/L)**.
> - **1 equivalent (eq) = 1 mole of charge**, i.e., 1 eq of Ca²⁺ = 0.5 mol Ca²⁺.
> - **Particle size** of commercial resin: **0.3 – 1.2 mm** (20–50 mesh).
> - **Operating temperature limit** of styrene–DVB resins: up to **~150 °C**.
> - The process is governed by **electroneutrality** at all times inside the resin phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Ion Exchange Resins

A modern ion exchange resin is a **crosslinked polymer bead** (typically a copolymer of **styrene** and **divinylbenzene (DVB)**) onto which **fixed ionic functional groups** are chemically bonded.

### 2.1.1 The Two Essential Components of a Resin

1. **Polymer Matrix (R):** A three-dimensional hydrophobic network of crosslinked polystyrene that gives the bead its mechanical strength, insolubility, and porosity.
2. **Fixed Ionic Group:** A covalently bonded group that cannot leave the matrix. It carries a permanent charge and is accompanied by a **mobile, exchangeable counter-ion** of opposite sign.

### 2.1.2 Classification of Resins by Fixed Group

> [!NOTE]
> **Four canonical KTU-classified resin types:**

| Resin Type | Fixed Group | Counter-ion | Typical pH Range | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strong-Acid Cation (SAC)** | –SO₃⁻ | H⁺ or Na⁺ | 0 – 14 | "Sulfonate stays strong" |
| **Weak-Acid Cation (WAC)** | –COO⁻ | H⁺ | 4 – 14 | "Carboxyl is choosy" |
| **Strong-Base Anion (SBA)** | –N⁺(CH₃)₃ | OH⁻ or Cl⁻ | 0 – 14 | "Quaternary ammonium" |
| **Weak-Base Anion (WBA)** | –N(CH₃)₂ | Free base | 0 – 7 | "Tertiary amine picks up H⁺" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The crosslinking ratio (% DVB)** controls swelling, selectivity, and kinetics:
> - **Low DVB (2–4 %)** → highly swollen, soft, fast kinetics, low selectivity differences.
> - **High DVB (8–12 %)** → rigid, slow kinetics, high selectivity, low swelling.

## 2.2 The General Ion Exchange Reaction

For two competing counter-ions **A** and **B** (same sign), the equilibrium is:

$$
A^{z_A+} + \overline{B^{z_B+}} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \overline{A^{z_A+}} + B^{z_B+}
$$

where the **overline** denotes the ion residing inside the resin phase.

### 2.2.1 Why "How" and "Why"?

- **Why reversible?** The driving force is purely electrostatic and steric; no covalent bond is broken.
- **Why stoichiometric?** Electroneutrality requires that the **total equivalents of positive charge entering the resin equals equivalents leaving it**.
- **How fast?** Limited by **film diffusion** (external liquid film around bead) or **particle diffusion** (inside the bead). Whichever is slower controls the rate.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The equations below are the core toolkit for every KTU numerical on this module. Master them.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent fraction in resin | $x_A = \dfrac{q_A}{q_T}$ | dimensionless | $q_T$ = total capacity |
| 2 | Equivalent fraction in solution | $y_A = \dfrac{c_A}{c_T}$ | dimensionless | $c_T$ = total solution conc. |
| 3 | **Selectivity Coefficient** (counter-ions A,B) | $K_{A/B} = \dfrac{x_A \, c_B}{x_B \, c_A}$ | dimensionless | Non-ideal equilibrium constant |
| 4 | **Separation Factor** (binary) | $\alpha_{A/B} = \dfrac{x_A / x_B}{c_A / c_B} = K_{A/B} \cdot \dfrac{q_T}{c_T}$ | dimensionless | Measures ease of separation |
| 5 | Mass balance on ion A | $q_T (x_{A,0} - x_A) = c_T (y_A - y_{A,i})$ | eq/L | Operating line equation |
| 6 | Operating capacity | $q_{op} = (\text{meq fed to bed}) - (\text{meq leaked})/(\text{regen level})$ | meq/mL | Practical design metric |
| 7 | Regenerant efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{stoichiometric regen}}{\text{actual regen used}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Always < 100 % |
| 8 | Bed voidage (typical) | $\epsilon \approx 0.35 - 0.45$ | dimensionless | For backwashed resin |

## 2.4 Selectivity Series — The "Preference Order"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The selectivity series tells the engineer which ion will preferentially load the resin from a multicomponent mixture. The order is a **direct consequence** of ionic charge, hydrated radius, polarisability, and resin crosslink density.

### Strong-Acid Cation Resin (H⁺/Na⁺ form)
$$
\text{Ba}^{2+} > \text{Pb}^{2+} > \text{Sr}^{2+} > \text{Ca}^{2+} > \text{Ni}^{2+} > \text{Cu}^{2+} > \text{Co}^{2+} > \text{Zn}^{2+} > \text{Mg}^{2+} > \text{Ag}^+ > \text{Cs}^+ > \text{Rb}^+ > \text{K}^+ > \text{NH}_4^+ > \text{Na}^+ > \text{H}^+ > \text{Li}^+
$$

### Strong-Base Anion Resin (OH⁻ form)
$$
\text{SO}_4^{2-} > \text{I}^- > \text{HSO}_4^- > \text{NO}_3^- > \text{Br}^- > \text{Cl}^- > \text{HCO}_3^- > \text{F}^- > \text{OH}^-
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** Higher valence wins; within the same valence, the ion with the **smaller hydrated radius** wins (because it has higher charge density).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Ion Exchange

- **Water Treatment:** Hardness removal (Na-softening), demineralisation (H-OH cycle), nitrate removal.
- **Hydrometallurgy:** Recovery of uranium, gold, rare earths from leach liquors.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Biotechnology:** Antibiotic purification, protein chromatography (DEAE-cellulose), amino acid separation.
- **Catalysis:** Sulfonated polystyrene resins (e.g., Amberlyst-15) as **solid acid catalysts** in esterification.
- **Food Industry:** Decolorisation of sugar syrups, deacidification of fruit juices.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Removal of radioactive Cs⁺ and Sr²⁺ from coolant water.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Selectivity Coefficient from Mass Action

We will derive the fundamental equation for ion exchange equilibrium in a binary system, starting from a reversible stoichiometric reaction and the law of mass action.

### 3.1.1 Setup — The Generic Binary Exchange

Consider two counter-ions **A** and **B** with valences $z_A$ and $z_B$ exchanging between the resin ($\overline{\phantom{x}}$) and the solution:

$$
z_B \, \overline{A^{z_A+}} + z_A \, B^{z_B+} \;\rightleftharpoons\; z_B \, A^{z_A+} + z_A \, \overline{B^{z_B+}}
$$

> The coefficients $z_B$ and $z_A$ in front of each species enforce **electroneutrality** — $z_B$ equivalents of A on the resin are released into solution to make room for $z_A$ equivalents of B.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the equilibrium constant expression using **activities** $a$. For ideal dilute solution and assuming ideal resin phase behaviour, activities are approximated by concentrations and equivalent fractions.

**Step 2:** The convention in ion exchange is to express the resin-phase composition as an **equivalent fraction** (not mole fraction) because stoichiometry is in equivalents.

$$
x_A = \frac{q_A}{q_T}, \qquad x_B = \frac{q_B}{q_T}, \qquad y_A = \frac{c_A}{c_T}, \qquad y_B = \frac{c_B}{c_T}
$$

**Step 3:** The "true" thermodynamic equilibrium constant is:

$$
K_{A \to B} = \frac{(a_A)^{z_B} \, (\overline{a_B})^{z_A}}{(\overline{a_A})^{z_B} \, (a_B)^{z_A}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the equivalent-fraction convention. The resulting working equation (called the **corrected selectivity coefficient** or **molal selectivity coefficient** in the KTU syllabus) is:

$$
K_{A/B} = \frac{x_A^{\,z_B} \, \cdot \, y_B^{\,z_A}}{x_B^{\,z_A} \, \cdot \, y_A^{\,z_B}} \cdot \left(\frac{q_T}{c_T}\right)^{(z_A - z_B)} \quad \cdot \quad \frac{\gamma_A^{\,z_B} \, \overline{\gamma}_B^{\,z_A}}{\gamma_B^{\,z_A} \, \overline{\gamma}_A^{\,z_B}}
$$

> For the **special case** of **equal-valence exchange** ($z_A = z_B = z$), the activity-coefficient ratio simplifies to unity in dilute solution, and the $q_T/c_T$ term vanishes, leaving the elegant KTU form:

$$
K_{A/B} = \frac{x_A \, \cdot \, y_B}{x_B \, \cdot \, y_A}
$$

**Step 5 (Mass balance):** Across a differential contactor, the equivalents of A leaving solution enter the resin:

$$
q_T \, dx_A = c_T \, dy_A
$$

Integrating from feed ($y_{A,i}, x_{A,0}$) to operating point gives the **operating line**:

$$
q_T \, (x_A - x_{A,0}) = c_T \, (y_A - y_{A,i})
$$

**Step 6 (Combination):** Eliminate $y_B = 1 - y_A$ using the selectivity expression. For equal-valence exchange, the **equilibrium isotherm** relating resin loading to solution concentration is:

$$
\frac{x_A}{1 - x_A} = K_{A/B} \cdot \frac{y_A}{1 - y_A}
$$

This is the **Langmuir-type** ion exchange isotherm.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Typical Problem (Dec 2022 style):** A strong-acid cation resin in H⁺-form is contacted with a solution containing **0.05 N Na⁺** and **0.05 N K⁺**. The total resin capacity is $q_T = 2.0$ eq/L. The selectivity coefficient $K_{\text{Na}^+/\text{H}^+} = 1.5$ and $K_{\text{K}^+/\text{H}^+} = 2.5$. Determine the equilibrium loading of Na⁺ and K⁺ on the resin.

**Solution Path:**

The reference ion is H⁺. For the binary Na⁺/H⁺ subsystem:

$$
K_{\text{Na/H}} = \frac{x_{\text{Na}} \, c_{\text{H}}}{x_{\text{H}} \, c_{\text{Na}}} = 1.5
$$

With $c_{\text{H}} \to 0$ (since the resin will not release H⁺ to a buffered Na⁺ solution unless displaced), and the practical assumption that $x_{\text{Na}} + x_{\text{K}} + x_{\text{H}} = 1$, a stepwise calculation across both exchange equilibria yields the equilibrium distribution. The order of preference from the selectivity series (K⁺ preferred over Na⁺) means K⁺ will dominate the loading on the resin, with Na⁺ taking the residual capacity.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Separation Factor

Starting from the definition of separation factor for a binary system:

$$
\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{(x_A / x_B)}{(y_A / y_B)}
$$

Substituting the selectivity coefficient relation $x_A / x_B = K_{A/B} \cdot (y_A / y_B)$ for equal-valence exchange, we obtain:

$$
\alpha_{A/B} = K_{A/B}
$$

For **unequal-valence** exchange (e.g., Ca²⁺/Na⁺), the separation factor acquires a concentration dependence:

$$
\alpha_{A/B} = K_{A/B} \cdot \left( \frac{q_T}{c_T} \right)^{(z_A - z_B)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because of the $q_T/c_T$ factor, **divalent ions are more strongly held at low total solution concentration**. This is why hard water (low total dissolved solids) is softened efficiently even with limited resin — a key design lever.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Equilibrium Behaviour Summary

| Regime | Condition | Behaviour | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $K_{A/B} \gg 1$ | A strongly preferred | Resin saturates with A first in column | A recovered at column outlet last |
| $K_{A/B} = 1$ | No preference | Resin behaves as ideal mixer | No separation possible |
| $K_{A/B} \ll 1$ | B strongly preferred | Resin holds B; A slips through | A breakthrough early |
| $\alpha \to \infty$ | Total preference | Rectangular isotherm | Sharpest possible separation |
| $\alpha \approx 1$ | Difficult separation | Linear isotherm | Long bed, many stages needed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Ion Exchange Process Architecture

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water or Process Solution] --> B[Pre-treatment Filtration]
    B --> C[Service Run - Loading Phase]
    C --> D{Resin Saturated?}
    D -- No --> E[Treated Effluent to User]
    D -- Yes --> F[Stop Service Run]
    F --> G[Backwash - Reclassify Bed]
    G --> H[Regenerant Injection - H/Na/Cl-OH]
    H --> I[Regeneration Complete]
    I --> J[Rinse and Sweeten-On]
    J --> K[Ready for Next Service Run]
    K --> C
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

## 4.2 Schematic — Functional Architecture of an Ion Exchange Bead

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Styrene-DVB Matrix - Hydrophobic Network] --> B[Fixed Ionic Group - eg SO3 minus]
    B --> C[Exchangeable Counter-ion - eg Na plus]
    D[Solution Phase - Bulk Liquid] --> E[Film Layer - External Boundary]
    E --> F[Bead Surface - Pore Mouth]
    F --> G[Internal Pore - Particle Diffusion Zone]
    C -. exchange reaction .-> F
    F <--> G
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The Service-Regeneration Cycle

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 - Loading: Counter-ions replaced] --> P2[Phase 2 - Backwash: Loosens bed, removes fines]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 - Regeneration: Resin restored to original form]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 - Rinse: Removes excess regenerant]
    P4 --> P1
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

## 4.4 Schematic — Cation vs. Anion Exchange (Functional View)

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Resin Bead
        A1[Fixed Anionic Group - SO3 minus] --- A2[Mobile Cation - H or Na plus]
    end
    subgraph Solution
        B1[Target Cations - Ca2 plus, Mg2 plus]
    end
    A2 <-->|Ion Exchange| B1

    subgraph Anion Resin Bead
        C1[Fixed Cationic Group - N plus CH3 3] --- C2[Mobile Anion - OH minus]
    end
    subgraph Anion Solution
        D1[Target Anions - Cl minus, SO4 2 minus]
    end
    C2 <-->|Ion Exchange| D1
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

## 4.5 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Right Resin

<!-- DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Target Ion Identified]) --> Q1{Cation or Anion?}
    Q1 -- Cation --> Q2{Need full pH 0-14 range?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> SAC[Use Strong-Acid Cation Resin - SAC]
    Q2 -- No --> WAC[Use Weak-Acid Cation Resin - WAC]
    Q1 -- Anion --> Q3{Need full pH 0-14 range?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> SBA[Use Strong-Base Anion Resin - SBA]
    Q3 -- No --> WBA[Use Weak-Base Anion Resin - WBA]
    SAC --> R[Regenerate with HCl or H2SO4 or NaCl]
    WAC --> R2[Regenerate with HCl or H2SO4]
    SBA --> R3[Regenerate with NaOH or NaCl]
    WBA --> R4[Regenerate with NaOH or NH4OH]
```
<!-- DIAGRAM_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **ion exchange capacity** and state its two commonly used units.

**Model Answer:**
Ion exchange capacity is the **total number of exchangeable counter-ions per unit mass or volume of resin**. It quantifies the maximum equivalents of ions a resin can hold.

- **meq per gram of dry resin** (gravimetric basis) — typically 4–5 meq/g for SAC resins.
- **equivalents per litre of wet, fully swollen resin** (volumetric basis) — typically 1.5–2.5 eq/L for commercial SAC.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Distinguish between a **strong-acid cation resin** and a **weak-acid cation resin** with respect to (a) functional group, (b) effective pH range, and (c) ease of regeneration.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Strong-Acid Cation (SAC) | Weak-Acid Cation (WAC) |
|---|---|---|
| Functional group | Sulfonic acid (–SO₃H) | Carboxylic acid (–COOH) |
| Effective pH | 0 – 14 (functional everywhere) | 4 – 14 (needs pH > 4 to ionise) |
| Regeneration | Difficult — needs stoichiometric strong acid | Easy — near-stoichiometric acid works; uses 110–120 % theoretical |
| Affinity for H⁺ | Lowest of all cations (H⁺ displaced easily) | Highest — H⁺ strongly preferred |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **selectivity coefficient** for a binary ion exchange reaction between counter-ions A⁺ and B⁺ of equal valence starting from the law of mass action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-litre bed of strong-acid cation resin ($q_T = 1.8$ eq/L) in the H⁺-form is used to soften water containing 200 mg/L of Ca²⁺ and 50 mg/L of Mg²⁺. The total hardness is to be reduced to < 20 mg/L as CaCO₃. If the selectivity coefficient of Ca²⁺ relative to H⁺ is $K_{\text{Ca/H}} = 5.5$ and that of Mg²⁺ is $K_{\text{Mg/H}} = 3.2$, estimate the **volume of water treated** before breakthrough. Assume complete utilisation of bed capacity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a)** Begins with the stoichiometric reaction: $z_B \overline{A^{z_A}} + z_A B^{z_B} \rightleftharpoons z_B A^{z_A} + z_A \overline{B^{z_B}}$.

Apply mass action using equivalent fractions:
- $x_A = q_A / q_T$, $x_B = q_B / q_T$, $y_A = c_A / c_T$, $y_B = c_B / c_T$.
- For equal valence, activity coefficients → 1 in dilute limit.
- Final form: $K_{A/B} = (x_A \, y_B) / (x_B \, y_A)$.

Substitute equilibrium relation into the differential mass balance $q_T \, dx_A = c_T \, dy_A$, integrate, and obtain the isotherm $x_A / (1 - x_A) = K_{A/B} \cdot y_A / (1 - y_A)$.

**(b)** Step 1 — Convert hardness to equivalents:
- Ca²⁺: 200 mg/L ÷ 20 mg/meq = 10 meq/L; Mg²⁺: 50 mg/L ÷ 12.15 mg/meq ≈ 4.12 meq/L.
- Total hardness in feed: ≈ 14.12 meq/L of cations to be removed.

Step 2 — Total bed capacity: 5 L × 1.8 eq/L = 9.0 eq = 9000 meq.

Step 3 — Volume treated until breakthrough: $V = 9000 \text{ meq} \div 14.12 \text{ meq/L} \approx 637$ L.

Step 4 — Account for the fact that Ca²⁺ is preferred over Mg²⁺ (from selectivity series), so Ca²⁺ exhausts the bed first. The breakthrough curve will show Ca²⁺ leaking at a slower rate, but Mg²⁺ breakthrough will be sharper. Practically, the operating capacity is taken as ~75–85 % of total → treat ~480–540 L.

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | KTU Supplementary Exam – Aug 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a fixed-bed ion exchange column**. Describe the sequence of operations: service, backwash, regeneration, and rinse. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strong-base anion exchange resin in OH⁻-form is used to remove Cl⁻ from a 0.01 N NaCl solution. The resin has a total capacity of 1.2 eq/L. If $K_{\text{Cl/OH}} = 22$ and the desired outlet Cl⁻ concentration is 0.0001 N, calculate the **breakthrough equivalent fraction** $y_{\text{Cl}}$ in the resin and comment on the **practical difficulty** of complete Cl⁻ removal in a single pass. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a)** Diagram of packed column with:
- Inlet distributor at top, outlet collector at bottom.
- Resin bed (depth typically 0.6 – 2.5 m).
- Service valves (operate during loading).
- Backwash line (bottom-up flow to expand bed 50 – 100 %).
- Regenerant distribution system (top-down or co-current).
- Rinse step uses deionised water.

Sequence in time: **Service → Backwash → Regeneration → Slow rinse → Fast rinse → Service**.

**(b)** Using the selectivity relation:
$$
K_{\text{Cl/OH}} = \frac{x_{\text{Cl}} \cdot c_{\text{OH}}}{x_{\text{OH}} \cdot c_{\text{Cl}}} = 22
$$

Given $c_{\text{Cl}} = 0.0001$ N, $c_{\text{OH}} \approx 0.01 - 0.0001 \approx 0.0099$ N (electroneutrality), and $x_{\text{Cl}} + x_{\text{OH}} = 1$:

$$
22 = \frac{x_{\text{Cl}} \cdot 0.0099}{(1 - x_{\text{Cl}}) \cdot 0.0001}
$$

Solving:
$$
22 \times 0.0001 \times (1 - x_{\text{Cl}}) = 0.0099 \, x_{\text{Cl}}
$$
$$
0.0022 - 0.0022 \, x_{\text{Cl}} = 0.0099 \, x_{\text{Cl}}
$$
$$
0.0022 = 0.0121 \, x_{\text{Cl}} \Rightarrow x_{\text{Cl}} \approx 0.182
$$

So only **18.2 %** of the resin capacity is loaded with Cl⁻ at breakthrough — confirming that the strong preference for Cl⁻ over OH⁻ means the resin holds Cl⁻ tightly but is difficult to regenerate. **Practical implication:** A two-bed (anion + mixed-bed) configuration is used for ultra-low conductivity water.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Equivalents vs. moles:** Students frequently use *moles* instead of *equivalents* in selectivity calculations. Always normalise by valence: 1 eq of Ca²⁺ = 0.5 mol Ca²⁺.
> 2. **Forgetting electroneutrality:** Never write a resin-phase reaction without showing that the total equivalents in = equivalents out.
> 3. **Mixing up co-ion and counter-ion:** Co-ions are repelled by the resin fixed charge (Donnan exclusion); they do **not** exchange. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 4. **Selectivity coefficient sign convention:** Be consistent. KTU prefers the form $K_{A/B}$ with A as the *preferred* ion in the numerator.
> 5. **Missing the regeneration step in column diagrams:** Drawing only the service step loses at least 2 marks in 7-mark questions on column design.
> 6. **Units of capacity:** meq/g (dry) and eq/L (wet) are **not interchangeable** without a bulk density and moisture content correction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2-Hour Exam**

- ✅ Ion exchange is a **reversible, stoichiometric, electroneutral** interchange of counter-ions between an insoluble solid and a solution.
- ✅ **Four resin types:** SAC, WAC, SBA, WBA — know the fixed group of each.
- ✅ The **fixed ionic group** cannot leave the resin; only the **counter-ion** is mobile.
- ✅ **Selectivity coefficient** $K_{A/B} = (x_A \, c_B) / (x_B \, c_A)$ for equal-valence exchange.
- ✅ **Separation factor** $\alpha_{A/B} = K_{A/B} \cdot (q_T/c_T)^{(z_A - z_B)}$ for general case.
- ✅ Higher **valence**, smaller **hydrated radius**, and higher **polarisability** ⇒ higher selectivity.
- ✅ **Strong-acid** cation resins work at any pH; **weak-acid** resins need pH > 4.
- ✅ **Strong-base** anion resins work at any pH; **weak-base** resins are effective only in acidic medium.
- ✅ **Donnan exclusion** prevents co-ions from entering the resin — only counter-ions exchange.
- ✅ Crosslinking (% DVB) controls **selectivity, kinetics, and swelling**.
- ✅ **Capacity** is reported as meq/g (dry) or eq/L (wet) — both are equivalent to moles of charge.
- ✅ **Operating cycle:** Service → Backwash → Regeneration → Rinse.
- ✅ For **unequal-valence exchange**, selectivity depends on total concentration $c_T$.
- ✅ **Mixed-bed deionisers** combine SAC and SBA resins to produce ultrapure water (resistivity > 18 MΩ·cm).
- ✅ **Regeneration efficiency** is always less than 100 %; over-design the regenerant dose.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Exchange Resins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion Exchange Resins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ion Exchange Resin (IER)** — An *insoluble, high-molecular-weight, cross-linked polymer matrix* carrying fixed, labile (mobile) ionic functional groups that are capable of exchanging their counter-ions with ions of the same charge sign present in a surrounding aqueous (or non-aqueous) electrolyte solution, without any appreciable physical change in the bulk of the solid.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT756) terminology, an **ion exchange resin** is classified as a **sorption-type separation medium** that operates via a *stoichiometric, reversible, and isothermal* chemical exchange between the solid (resin) phase and the liquid (electrolyte) phase. The functional groups anchored to the polymer backbone dictate the chemistry of the exchange.

### 1.1.1 Parent Polymer Backbone

The most widely used matrix is the **copolymer of styrene (vinylbenzene) and divinylbenzene (DVB)**. The DVB content (cross-linking density) controls:

- **Swelling** behaviour in water
- **Mechanical strength** of the bead
- **Diffusion resistance** of exchanging ions
- **Selectivity** for ions of different sizes

> [!NOTE]
> Cross-linking percentage is expressed as **% DVB**. Typical commercial resins contain **8 %–12 % DVB** for general-purpose water treatment, while high-strength resins use **up to 20 % DVB**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Mailbox"

Imagine every resin bead is a **secure mailbox** locked to a wall. Inside each mailbox is a "guest" ion (the counter-ion, e.g., H⁺ or Na⁺) sitting on a chair. When a solution containing "wanting" ions (e.g., Ca²⁺ or Mg²⁺ from hard water) flows past:

1. The new ion **knocks on the door** (diffuses through the gel matrix).
2. If the new ion has a **higher "priority tag"** (higher charge or selectivity), it **buckles the guest ion out** of the chair.
3. The displaced guest is **washed away** with the effluent.
4. Eventually, **all chairs are occupied by wanting ions** — the resin is **exhausted** and must be **regenerated** (a fresh batch of guest ions is sent in to reclaim the chairs).

This analogy captures the three essential behaviours: **stoichiometry** (one ion out for one ion in), **reversibility** (you can regenerate), and **competition** (selectivity ordering).

## 1.3 Classification of Ion Exchange Resins

> [!NOTE]
> IERs are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (a) the **type of exchangeable ion** (cation vs anion) and (b) the **acid/base strength** of the functional group (strong vs weak).

### 1.3.1 By Type of Exchange

| Class | Exchangeable Ion | Functional Group Example | Resin in H⁺ / OH⁻ Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cation Exchange Resin (CER)** | H⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺ | –SO₃⁻, –COO⁻ | H⁺-form, Na⁺-form |
| **Anion Exchange Resin (AER)** | OH⁻, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻ | –N⁺(CH₃)₃, –NH₂ | OH⁻-form, Cl⁻-form |

### 1.3.2 By Functional Group Strength

**Cation Resins:**
- **Strongly Acidic Cation (SAC)** — sulphonic acid (–SO₃H). Works across the **full pH range (0–14)**. Example: *Amberlite IR-120, Dowex 50*.
- **Weakly Acidic Cation (WAC)** — carboxylic acid (–COOH). Effective only in **pH ≥ 7**. Example: *Amberlite IRC-50*.

**Anion Resins:**
- **Strongly Basic Anion (SBA)** — quaternary ammonium (–N⁺R₃). Dissociates in **pH 0–14**. Example: *Amberlite IRA-400*.
- **Weakly Basic Anion (WBA)** — tertiary or secondary amine (–NHR, –NR₂). Effective in **pH ≤ 7**. Example: *Amberlite IRA-93*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The exam expects students to know that **SAC + SBA** combination is called a **Two-Bed Demineraliser**, while **WAC + WBA** is used for partial dealkalisation. A **Mixed Bed (MB)** unit mixes SAC and SBA in the *same vessel* to produce ultrapure water (resistivity ≈ 18 MΩ·cm).

## 1.4 Physical Form of Resins

| Form | Structure | Surface Area | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gel-type (microreticular)** | Homogeneous, no true pores; ion diffuses through swollen gel | Low (only the swollen gel) | Water softening, deionisation |
| **Macroporous (macroreticular)** | Discrete permanent pores in the matrix | High (50–800 m²/g) | Non-aqueous media, organic catalysis |
| **Isoporous** | Uniform pore size (modern resins) | Tunable | Biopharmaceutical separations |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard commercial resin **bead size** is **0.3 – 1.2 mm** (≈ 16–50 mesh). The bead is a translucent spherical particle through which exchange occurs by **Fickian diffusion**.

## 1.5 Key Operating Metrics (Standard Definitions)

- **Total Capacity (Q_T):** Total milli-equivalents of exchangeable ions per gram of dry resin (or per mL of wet resin). Units: **meq/g** or **meq/mL**.
- **Operating Capacity (Q_op):** Useful capacity under actual operating flow; **always < Q_T** (typically 60–80 % of Q_T).
- **Breakthrough:** The point in the effluent history when the *leakage* of the unwanted ion exceeds a pre-set threshold (e.g., 1 % of inlet concentration).
- **Resin Density:** Bulk density ≈ **0.7–0.9 g/mL** (wet); true density ≈ **1.1–1.4 g/mL**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ion Exchange Reaction (Stoichiometry)

A generalised exchange between a cation resin (in A⁺ form) and a counter-cation B⁺ from solution is written as:

$$n\, \overline{\text{R-A}^+} + \text{B}^{n+} \rightleftharpoons n\, \text{A}^+ + \overline{\text{R}_n\text{-B}^{n+}}$$

Where:
- The **overbar** denotes the species residing in the *resin phase*.
- **R** is the fixed anionic group (e.g., –SO₃⁻).
- The reaction is **reversible** and **electroneutral** (charges balance on both sides).

For a typical divalent/monovalent exchange (e.g., softening, Ca²⁺ replacing Na⁺):

$$2\,\overline{\text{R-Na}^+} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \overline{\text{R}_2\text{-Ca}^{2+}} + 2\,\text{Na}^+$$

## 2.2 Selectivity & The Selectivity Coefficient (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Selectivity Coefficient (K_B^A)** is the single most important thermodynamic descriptor of an ion exchange resin. KTU examiners routinely test this.

For the generic reaction:

$$\overline{\text{A}^+} + \text{B}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{A}^+ + \overline{\text{B}^+}$$

The **mass-action selectivity coefficient** is defined as:

$$K_B^A \;=\; \frac{[\overline{\text{B}^+}]\,[\text{A}^+]}{[\overline{\text{A}^+}]\,[\text{B}^+]}$$

**For a divalent-for-monovalent exchange (A²⁺ replacing B⁺):**

$$K_B^{A} \;=\; \frac{[\overline{\text{A}^{2+}}]\,[\text{B}^+]^{2}}{[\overline{\text{B}^+}]^{2}\,[\text{A}^{2+}]}$$

### 2.2.1 Interpretation Rules

- **K > 1** ⇒ Resin *prefers* A⁺ (incoming ion) over B⁺ (resident ion).
- **K < 1** ⇒ Resin *prefers* B⁺ over A⁺.
- **K = 1** ⇒ No preference; behaves ideally (rare in practice).

### 2.2.2 Typical Selectivity Hierarchy (Styrene-DVB, SAC)

For **strongly acidic cation resins** in dilute solution:

$$\text{Al}^{3+} \;>\; \text{Ba}^{2+} \;>\; \text{Pb}^{2+} \;>\; \text{Ca}^{2+} \;>\; \text{Ni}^{2+} \;>\; \text{Cu}^{2+} \;>\; \text{Zn}^{2+} \;>\; \text{Mg}^{2+} \;>\; \text{K}^+ \;>\; \text{NH}_4^+ \;>\; \text{Na}^+ \;>\; \text{H}^+ \;>\; \text{Li}^+$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The selectivity order above applies to **dilute solutions**. In concentrated solutions, the order **inverts** for many ions (because activity coefficients of multivalent ions drop sharply in concentrated brine — a phenomenon called the **"concentration-valency effect"**). This is exploited during **regeneration** with concentrated NaCl: although Na⁺ is *low* on the selectivity list, the high concentration of Na⁺ still displaces Ca²⁺ from the resin.
