## 2.3 Distribution Coefficient (D) vs Selectivity Coefficient (K)

For a more thermodynamically rigorous treatment, especially in chromatography:

$$D_A \;=\; \frac{[\overline{\text{A}}]}{[\text{A}]}$$

For the binary case with equivalent fractions:

$$\alpha_B^A \;=\; \frac{D_A}{D_B} \;=\; \frac{[\overline{\text{A}}]\,[\text{B}]}{[\text{A}]\,[\overline{\text{B}}]}$$

The dimensionless **separation factor** $\alpha_B^A$ is essentially the selectivity coefficient expressed in equivalent fractions.

## 2.4 Capacity Relationships

### 2.4.1 Total Capacity (Q_T)

$$Q_T \;=\; \frac{\text{equivalents of fixed ionic groups}}{\text{mass of dry resin (or volume of wet bed)}}$$

For a typical **SAC resin**: Q_T ≈ **4.5 – 5.0 meq/g dry** or **1.8 – 2.2 meq/mL wet bed**.

### 2.4.2 Operating Capacity (Q_op)

$$Q_{op} \;=\; Q_T \times \eta$$

Where **η (utilisation)** depends on:
- Regeneration level (g regenerant per L of resin)
- Service flow rate
- Inlet ion concentration
- Temperature

For softening: Q_op ≈ 60 – 80 % of Q_T.

## 2.5 Breakthrough Curve Analysis

The breakthrough curve is a plot of **effluent concentration (C) vs time (t)** or **vs throughput volume (V)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *symmetric S-shaped* breakthrough indicates **constant-pattern behaviour** (typical of strongly favoured exchange). An *asymmetric curve* with a long tail indicates **favourable equilibrium** but **slow kinetics**.

### 2.5.1 Useful Capacity from Breakthrough

$$Q_{useful} \;=\; \frac{C_0 \cdot V_{break} - \displaystyle\int_0^{V_{break}} C\, dV}{m_{resin}}$$

Where:
- C₀ = inlet concentration
- V_break = volume at breakthrough
- The integral is the **area under the breakthrough curve** representing leakage.

### 2.5.2 Length of Unused Bed (LUB)

$$LUB \;=\; L \left(1 - \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}}\right)$$

Where L = total bed height. **LUB is the engineering buffer** that the designer leaves so that breakthrough does not occur prematurely due to kinetics or dispersion.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table verbatim. Every KTU exam in PECHT756 has at least one question requiring one of these equations.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Typical Units | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Selectivity Coefficient (uni-univalent) | $K_B^A = \frac{[\overline{B^+}][A^+]}{[\overline{A^+}][B^+]}$ | dimensionless | Thermodynamic preference |
| 2 | Selectivity Coefficient (di-univalent) | $K_B^{A} = \frac{[\overline{A^{2+}}][B^+]^{2}}{[\overline{B^+}]^{2}[A^{2+}]}$ | dimensionless | Water softening |
| 3 | Separation Factor | $\alpha_B^A = D_A / D_B$ | dimensionless | Chromatographic separations |
| 4 | Total Capacity | $Q_T = \text{equiv. fixed groups} / \text{mass dry resin}$ | meq/g or meq/mL | Resin specification |
| 5 | Operating Capacity | $Q_{op} = Q_T \cdot \eta$ | meq/g or meq/mL | Process design |
| 6 | Regeneration Efficiency | $\eta_R = \frac{\text{regenerant used theoretically}}{\text{regenerant actually used}}$ | % | Cost optimisation |
| 7 | LUB | $LUB = L(1 - Q_{useful}/Q_{total})$ | m | Column design |
| 8 | Service Cycle Time | $t_{cycle} = \frac{Q_{op} \cdot V_{bed}}{C_0 \cdot Q_{flow}}$ | h or s | Operational scheduling |
| 9 | Regenerant Dose (theoretical) | $D_{th} = Q_{op} \cdot V_{bed} \cdot M_{reg} / z$ | g or kg | Stoichiometric minimum |
| 10 | Equilibrium Constant (Donnan) | $K_D = [\overline{X^+}][X^-] / [X^+][\overline{X^-}]$ | — | Membrane selectivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols to be exact:** $C_0$ = inlet concentration, $V_{bed}$ = bed volume, $Q_{flow}$ = volumetric flow, $M_{reg}$ = molar mass of regenerant, $z$ = charge number of regenerant ion, $\eta_R$ = regeneration efficiency. Always state units in KTU answer sheets.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Ion Exchange Resins

- **Water Treatment:** Softening (Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ removal), demineralisation, dealkalisation, nitrate removal.
- **Hydrometallurgy:** Recovery of uranium, gold, platinum-group metals, rare earths.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Purification of antibiotics, amino acids, vitamins; chiral separations.
- **Food Industry:** Decaffeination of coffee, sugar refining, juice deacidification.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Separation of Cs-137 and Sr-90 from radioactive waste.
- **Catalysis:** Sulfonated resins (e.g., Amberlyst-15) as solid acid catalysts in esterification and alkylation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Selectivity Coefficient from First Principles of Mass Action

We begin with the stoichiometric equilibrium between a cation resin (in A-form) and a competing ion B⁺ in solution.

### Step 1 — Write the elementary exchange reaction

$$\overline{\text{R-A}^+} \;+\; \text{B}^+ \;\rightleftharpoons\; \overline{\text{R-B}^+} \;+\; \text{A}^+$$

The bar denotes the resin phase; absence of bar denotes the solution phase.

### Step 2 — Apply the Law of Mass Action

For a reaction at equilibrium, the ratio of product activities to reactant activities (each raised to its stoichiometric coefficient) equals the thermodynamic equilibrium constant.

**Forward rate:**

$$r_f \;=\; k_f\,[\overline{\text{R-A}^+}]\,[\text{B}^+]$$

**Reverse rate:**

$$r_r \;=\; k_r\,[\overline{\text{R-B}^+}]\,[\text{A}^+]$$

### Step 3 — Set forward rate = reverse rate at equilibrium

$$k_f\,[\overline{\text{R-A}^+}]\,[\text{B}^+] \;=\; k_r\,[\overline{\text{R-B}^+}]\,[\text{A}^+]$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to define the equilibrium (selectivity) constant

$$\frac{k_f}{k_r} \;=\; \frac{[\overline{\text{R-B}^+}]\,[\text{A}^+]}{[\overline{\text{R-A}^+}]\,[\text{B}^+}$$

Define the left side as the selectivity coefficient:

$$\boxed{\,K_B^A \;=\; \frac{[\overline{\text{R-B}^+}]\,[\text{A}^+]}{[\overline{\text{R-A}^+}]\,[\text{B}^+]}\,}$$

This is identical to the definition in §2.2. The derivation is complete; the selectivity coefficient is the *kinetically derived* equilibrium constant for the inter-phase ion exchange reaction.

## 3.2 Derivation of Operating Capacity from Mass Balance

Consider a single service cycle of a softening column.

### Step 1 — Define the inputs to the column

- Inlet hardness: $C_0$ (meq/L)
- Volumetric flow: $Q$ (L/h)
- Service time: $t_s$ (h)
- Bed volume: $V_B$ (L)
- Operating capacity: $Q_{op}$ (meq/L of bed)

### Step 2 — Total hardness ions removed (left side of mass balance)

The resin captures all Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ fed during the service run.

$$\text{Total meq captured} \;=\; C_0 \cdot Q \cdot t_s$$

### Step 3 — Total meq capacity available in the bed

$$\text{Total meq capacity} \;=\; Q_{op} \cdot V_B$$

### Step 4 — Equate (assuming the bed is exhausted at end of service)

$$C_0 \cdot Q \cdot t_s \;=\; Q_{op} \cdot V_B$$

### Step 5 — Solve for service cycle time

$$\boxed{\,t_s \;=\; \frac{Q_{op} \cdot V_B}{C_0 \cdot Q}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental design equation** of any ion exchange service cycle. KTU examiners will expect you to derive this in any 7-mark design sub-question.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Hard-Water Softening

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *typical KTU 2024 Scheme-style* calculation. Practice it until you can reproduce it in 8 minutes.

**Problem.** A water-softening column contains **100 L** of a strong-acid cation resin in the Na⁺ form. The operating capacity is **1.6 meq/mL of wet bed**. The water to be treated has a total hardness of **200 mg/L as CaCO₃**, and the flow rate is **2000 L/h**. The regeneration is with NaCl at 100 % stoichiometric efficiency. Calculate:

(a) The service cycle time.
(b) The minimum mass of NaCl required per regeneration.

### Step 1 — Convert hardness to meq/L

Molar mass of CaCO₃ = 100 g/mol, equivalent weight = 50 g/eq.

$$C_0 \;=\; \frac{200 \text{ mg/L}}{50 \text{ mg/meq}} \;=\; 4 \text{ meq/L}$$

**Conversion logic:** meq/L = (mg/L as CaCO₃) / 50.

### Step 2 — Convert operating capacity to meq/L of bed

$$Q_{op} \;=\; 1.6 \text{ meq/mL} \times 1000 \text{ mL/L} \;=\; 1600 \text{ meq/L}$$

**Conversion logic:** 1 L = 1000 mL, so capacity scales linearly.

### Step 3 — Compute service time using the derived formula

$$t_s \;=\; \frac{Q_{op} \cdot V_B}{C_0 \cdot Q} \;=\; \frac{1600 \times 100}{4 \times 2000}$$

$$t_s \;=\; \frac{160\,000}{8\,000} \;=\; 20 \text{ hours}$$

**Conversion logic:** numerator has units of meq, denominator has units of meq/h, giving time in h.

### Step 4 — Compute total meq to be removed per cycle

$$\text{meq per cycle} \;=\; C_0 \cdot Q \cdot t_s \;=\; 4 \times 2000 \times 20 \;=\; 160\,000 \text{ meq} \;=\; 160 \text{ eq}$$

**Conversion logic:** substituting the values from above and noting $1 \text{ eq} = 1000 \text{ meq}$.

### Step 5 — Convert equivalents of hardness to equivalents of Na⁺ needed

The exchange reaction:

$$2\,\overline{\text{R-Na}^+} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \overline{\text{R}_2\text{-Ca}^{2+}} + 2\,\text{Na}^+$$

For every equivalent of Ca²⁺ removed, **1 equivalent of Na⁺ is consumed**. At 100 % stoichiometric efficiency:

$$\text{eq of NaCl} \;=\; 160 \text{ eq}$$

**Conversion logic:** regenerant stoichiometry is 1:1 in equivalents for the above reaction.

### Step 6 — Convert equivalents of NaCl to mass

Molar mass NaCl = 58.5 g/mol, equivalent weight = 58.5 g/eq (Na⁺ is monovalent).

$$m_{NaCl} \;=\; 160 \times 58.5 \;=\; 9360 \text{ g} \;=\; 9.36 \text{ kg}$$

### Final Answers

$$\boxed{\,t_s = 20 \text{ h}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,m_{NaCl} = 9.36 \text{ kg/cycle}\,}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four Resin Classes

| Property | SAC | WAC | SBA | WBA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Functional Group | –SO₃H | –COOH | –N⁺(CH₃)₃ | –NR₂ / –NHR |
| Active pH Range | 0 – 14 | ≥ 7 | 0 – 14 | ≤ 7 |
| Total Capacity (meq/mL) | 1.8 – 2.2 | 3.0 – 4.0 | 1.0 – 1.4 | 1.2 – 1.6 |
| Regenerant | HCl / H₂SO₄ | HCl | NaOH | NaOH |
| Regenerant Efficiency | 30 – 50 % | 80 – 95 % | 30 – 50 % | 80 – 95 % |
| Salt-Splitting Ability | Yes | No | Yes | No |
| Typical Use | Full demineralisation | Partial dealkalisation | Silica removal | Organic acid removal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Weak resins have **higher total capacity** and **higher regeneration efficiency** than strong resins because the functional groups are *fully neutralised* at operating pH. The trade-off is the limited pH window of operation.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation of Breakthrough Curve Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners in PECHT756 frequently ask students to **sketch and label** the breakthrough curve, identify the **breakpoint, exhaustion point, and stoichiometric point**, and use these to calculate LUB.

### Step 1 — Define the three characteristic points

- **Breakpoint (b):** Effluent concentration first reaches the *threshold* (e.g., C/C₀ = 0.05).
- **Stoichiometric point (s):** The point where the resin is in equilibrium with the inlet (mass-transfer zone is at the column exit).
- **Exhaustion point (e):** Effluent concentration = inlet concentration (C/C₀ = 1); bed is fully spent.

### Step 2 — Relate to capacity

The **stoichiometric capacity** Q_st is the area of the rectangle up to the stoichiometric point:

$$Q_{st} \;=\; \frac{C_0 \cdot V_s}{V_B}$$

Where V_s is the throughput at the stoichiometric point.

### Step 3 — The breakthrough capacity

$$Q_{b} \;=\; \frac{C_0 \cdot V_b}{V_B}$$

### Step 4 — Length of Unused Bed (LUB) in terms of curve geometry

$$LUB \;=\; L \cdot \frac{(V_s - V_b)}{V_s} \;=\; L \left(1 - \frac{V_b}{V_s}\right)$$

This is mathematically equivalent to $L \left(1 - Q_{useful}/Q_{total}\right)$ when Q_useful = Q_b and Q_total = Q_st.

### Step 5 — Engineering implication

LUB is the *wasted* portion of the bed. A **shorter mass-transfer zone** ⇒ **smaller LUB** ⇒ **higher utilisation**. Faster kinetics (smaller bead, higher temperature) reduce LUB.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Single Ion-Exchange Column

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional interactions* inside a single ion-exchange column during the **service (loading) cycle**. Every interaction is annotated to help a first-time reader visualise the process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Water Inlet] --> A2[Distribution Header]
    A2 --> A3[Resin Bed - Top Layer - Exhausted Zone]
    A3 --> A4[Resin Bed - Middle Layer - Mass Transfer Zone MTZ]
    A4 --> A5[Resin Bed - Bottom Layer - Fresh Resin Zone]
    A5 --> A6[Underdrain Collector]
    A6 --> A7[Treated Water Outlet]
    A8[Waste / Backwash Outlet] -.-> A3
    A9[Regenerant Inlet] -.-> A6
    A10[Regenerant Outlet] -.-> A3

    A11[Ion A in Solution] -->|enters bed| A4
    A4 -->|exchanges with counter ion| A12[Ion B desorbed into solution]
    A12 -->|leaves with effluent| A7

    A13[Functional group -SO3-Na+] -.->|hosts| A11
    A14[Functional group -SO3-A+] -.->|forms after exchange| A4
```

**Reading guide:**
- Solid arrows = direction of liquid flow (downward in service mode).
- Dashed arrows = direction of regenerant flow (upward, counter-current).
- A3 = **Exhausted zone** (resin fully loaded with the unwanted ion; no further exchange).
- A4 = **Mass-Transfer Zone (MTZ)** — the active exchange band; height H_MTZ is the key design variable.
- A5 = **Fresh resin zone** — still fully regenerated; provides reserve capacity.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Bed Demineraliser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Raw Water] --> B2[Strongly Acidic Cation Column SAC]
    B2 -->|cations replaced by H+| B3[Degasifier - CO2 stripper]
    B3 --> B4[Strongly Basic Anion Column SBA]
    B4 -->|anions replaced by OH-| B5[Demineralised Water]
    B6[Acid Regenerant HCl to SAC] -.-> B2
    B7[Caustic Regenerant NaOH to SBA] -.-> B4
```

**Reading guide:**
- SAC removes *all* cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺, etc.) and replaces them with H⁺.
- The H⁺ combines with bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) to form H₂CO₃ → CO₂↑, which the degasifier strips out.
- SBA removes *all* anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻, SiO₂) and replaces them with OH⁻.
- The H⁺ and OH⁻ combine to form pure H₂O.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mixed-Bed Polisher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Pre-demineralised water] --> C2[Mixed Bed Column]
    C2 --> C3[Ultrapure Water Outlet]
    C4[Backwash - separates SAC and SBA] -.-> C2
    C5[Regenerant HCl] -.-> C6[Regenerate SAC layer]
    C5 -.-> C2
    C7[Regenerant NaOH] -.-> C8[Regenerate SBA layer]
    C7 -.-> C2
    C9[Air Mix - recombines resins] -.-> C2
```

**Reading guide:**
- Mixed bed mixes SAC and SBA in the *same vessel*, producing water with resistivity **> 18 MΩ·cm**.
- For regeneration, water backwash **separates** the two resins by density (SAC is denser, settles at bottom).
- Each layer is regenerated separately, then air-mixed back into a homogeneous bed.

## 4.4 Resin Bead — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Polystyrene - Divinylbenzene Matrix] --> D2[Cross-linked Polymer Network]
    D2 --> D3[Fixed Ionic Group e.g. SO3-]
    D3 --> D4[Mobile Counter Ion e.g. Na+ or H+]
    D4 -.-> D5[Solution Phase Ion e.g. Ca2+]
    D5 -->|diffuses through gel| D2
    D5 -->|exchanges with| D4
    D4 -.->|is released to| D2
```

**Reading guide:**
- D1 is the inert polymer backbone.
- D3 is the *fixed* ionic group (cannot leave the resin).
- D4 is the *mobile* counter-ion (can leave the resin).
- D5 is the *incoming* ion from the solution.
- The exchange D4 ⇄ D5 occurs only when D5 has diffused through the swollen gel D2.

## 4.5 Selectivity vs Equivalent Fraction — Typical Isotherm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Pure A in resin, none in solution] --> E2[Selectivity K greater than 1]
    E2 --> E3[Curve lies above diagonal - A favoured]
    E3 --> E4[Pure B in resin, none in solution]
    E4 --> E5[Curve passes through 0,0 and 1,1]
    E5 --> E6[Ideal case K equals 1 - straight line]
    E6 --> E7[Curve lies below diagonal - B favoured]
```

**Reading guide:**
- Plot equivalent fraction of A in *resin* (y) vs equivalent fraction of A in *solution* (x).
- Diagonal = K = 1 (no preference).
- Above diagonal = resin *prefers* A (K > 1).
- Below diagonal = resin *prefers* B (K < 1).
- This is a **KTU-favourite** concept: relate the isotherm shape to the breakthrough curve.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define an ion exchange resin. With a neat sketch, distinguish between cation and anion exchange resins.**

**Model Answer:**

> An ion exchange resin is an insoluble, cross-linked polymer matrix (typically styrene-divinylbenzene) containing fixed, labile ionic functional groups capable of exchanging their counter-ions with ions of like sign from a surrounding electrolyte solution, without any physical change in the solid matrix.

**Cation Exchange Resin (CER):** Contains fixed *anionic* groups (e.g., –SO₃⁻, –COO⁻) on the matrix; exchanges *cations* (H⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺) from solution.

**Anion Exchange Resin (AER):** Contains fixed *cationic* groups (e.g., –N⁺R₃, –NH₂⁺) on the matrix; exchanges *anions* (OH⁻, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻) from solution.

A neat sketch should show:
- Polymer backbone (PS-DVB) drawn as cross-linked chains.
- Fixed group on backbone.
- Mobile counter-ion (leaving) and incoming ion (entering).

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for CER description, 1 for AER description]**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the term "selectivity coefficient" of an ion exchange resin. State the typical selectivity order for a strongly acidic cation resin.**

**Model Answer:**

> The selectivity coefficient (K_B^A) of an ion exchange resin is the equilibrium constant of the ion exchange reaction, expressed in terms of activities (or concentrations) of the exchanging ions in the resin and solution phases. It quantifies the *preference* of the resin for one ion over another.

For the reaction $\overline{\text{R-A}^+} + \text{B}^+ \rightleftharpoons \overline{\text{R-B}^+} + \text{A}^+$, the selectivity coefficient is:

$$K_B^A \;=\; \frac{[\overline{\text{B}^+}]\,[\text{A}^+]}{[\overline{\text{A}^+}]\,[\text{B}^+]}$$

**Typical selectivity order for SAC resin (dilute solution):**

$$\text{Al}^{3+} > \text{Ba}^{2+} > \text{Pb}^{2+} > \text{Ca}^{2+} > \text{Mg}^{2+} > \text{K}^+ > \text{Na}^+ > \text{H}^+ > \text{Li}^+$$

If K > 1, the resin prefers ion A; if K < 1, it prefers ion B.

**[3 Marks: 2 for definition + equation, 1 for the selectivity order]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 3 carries **one full 14-mark question (with internal choice)**. Below are **two alternative 14-mark questions** for practice.

---

### Question B-A [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Pattern] (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **mixed-bed ion exchange unit** for producing ultrapure water. Discuss the regeneration procedure in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strong-acid cation resin in the Na⁺ form is used to soften a water supply with a total hardness of **250 mg/L as CaCO₃**. The column contains **150 L** of resin with an operating capacity of **45 g CaCO₃ per L of resin** (as CaCO₃-equivalent). If the flow rate is **3000 L/h**, calculate:
   1. The service cycle time.
   2. The minimum mass of NaCl required per regeneration, assuming 100 % regeneration efficiency.
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction of Mixed-Bed Unit:**

A mixed-bed deioniser is a single pressure vessel packed with an intimate mixture of a **strongly acidic cation (SAC) resin** in the H⁺ form and a **strongly basic anion (SBA) resin** in the OH⁻ form. The two resins are selected with a density difference of about **20–30 %** so that they can be hydraulically separated during regeneration (SAC is denser, settles at the bottom).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Inlet: Pre-treated water] --> F2[Top Distribution]
    F2 --> F3[Mixed Resin Bed - SAC plus SBA]
    F3 --> F4[Bottom Collector]
    F4 --> F5[Outlet: Ultrapure Water]
    F6[Backwash Water] -.->|separates layers| F3
    F7[HCl from top distributor] -.->|regenerates SBA| F3
    F8[NaOH from bottom distributor] -.->|regenerates SAC| F3
    F9[Compressed Air] -.->|re-mixes resins| F3
    F10[Rinse Water] --> F3
```

**Working Principle:**

When water flows through the mixed bed:
1. The H⁺ from SAC exchanges with all cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺, K⁺).
2. The OH⁻ from SBA exchanges with all anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻, HCO₃⁻, SiO₂).
3. The H⁺ and OH⁻ produced by these exchanges **immediately combine** to form H₂O, shifting both equilibria forward.
4. This co-operative effect produces water of extremely high purity (resistivity > 18 MΩ·cm).

**Regeneration Procedure (4 Steps):**

1. **Backwash:** Water is passed upwards to fluidise and separate the bed. The denser SAC settles at the bottom; the lighter SBA rises to the top.
2. **Regenerant Introduction:**
   - HCl (4–6 %) is passed *downward* from the top distributor → regenerates SBA.
   - NaOH (4–6 %) is passed *upward* from the bottom distributor → regenerates SAC.
3. **Rinse:** Both layers are rinsed with deionised water to remove excess regenerant.
4. **Air Mix:** Compressed air is blown from the bottom to re-mix the two resins into a homogeneous bed. Final rinse restores service.

**[7 marks: 1 for construction, 2 for diagram, 2 for working, 2 for regeneration]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**

- Total hardness, C₀ = 250 mg/L as CaCO₃
- Bed volume, V_B = 150 L
- Operating capacity, Q_op = 45 g CaCO₃ per L of resin
- Flow rate, Q = 3000 L/h
- Regeneration efficiency = 100 %

**Step 1 — Convert hardness to meq/L**

Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50 g/eq.

$$C_0 \;=\; \frac{250 \text{ mg/L}}{50 \text{ mg/meq}} \;=\; 5 \text{ meq/L}$$

**Step 2 — Convert operating capacity to meq/L of bed**

$$Q_{op} \;=\; \frac{45 \text{ g CaCO}_3 / \text{L}}{50 \text{ mg/meq}} \;=\; \frac{45\,000 \text{ mg/L}}{50 \text{ mg/meq}} \;=\; 900 \text{ meq/L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the service time equation**

$$t_s \;=\; \frac{Q_{op} \cdot V_B}{C_0 \cdot Q} \;=\; \frac{900 \times 150}{5 \times 3000} \;=\; \frac{135\,000}{15\,000} \;=\; 9 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Total equivalents of hardness to be removed per cycle**

$$E_{cycle} \;=\; C_0 \cdot Q \cdot t_s \;=\; 5 \times 3000 \times 9 \;=\; 135\,000 \text{ meq} \;=\; 135 \text{ eq}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalents of NaCl needed (1:1 stoichiometry)**

$$E_{NaCl} \;=\; 135 \text{ eq} \quad \text{(at 100 \% efficiency)}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass**

Molar mass of NaCl = 58.5 g/eq.

$$m_{NaCl} \;=\; 135 \times 58.5 \;=\; 7897.5 \text{ g} \;\approx\; 7.90 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{\,t_s = 9 \text{ hours}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,m_{NaCl} \approx 7.90 \text{ kg per cycle}\,}$$

**[7 marks: 1 for data tabulation, 2 for conversions, 2 for service time, 2 for regenerant mass]**

---

### Question B-B [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Pattern] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of ion exchange resins** based on (i) the nature of the exchangeable ion and (ii) the strength of the functional group. Give at least one example of each class and write the relevant exchange reaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the term **"breakthrough curve"** in ion exchange. With a neat sketch, label the breakthrough point, stoichiometric point, and exhaustion point. Derive the relationship between the **length of unused bed (LUB)** and the breakthrough capacity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification by Nature of Exchangeable Ion:**

1. **Cation Exchange Resins (CER):** Carry fixed *anionic* groups (–SO₃⁻, –COO⁻); exchange *cations*. Example: *Amberlite IR-120*.
   - Reaction: $\overline{\text{R-SO}_3^-\text{H}^+} + \text{Na}^+ \rightleftharpoons \overline{\text{R-SO}_3^-\text{Na}^+} + \text{H}^+$

2. **Anion Exchange Resins (AER):** Carry fixed *cationic* groups (–N⁺R₃, –NH₂⁺); exchange *anions*. Example: *Amberlite IRA-400*.
   - Reaction: $\overline{\text{R-N}^+(\text{CH}_3)_3 \text{OH}^-} + \text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons \overline{\text{R-N}^+(\text{CH}_3)_3 \text{Cl}^-} + \text{OH}^-$

**Classification by Functional Group Strength:**

| Class | Functional Group | pH Range | Example | Reaction (with HCl regenerant for CER) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SAC (Strongly Acidic Cation)** | –SO₃H | 0–14 | Amberlite IR-120 | $\overline{\text{R-SO}_3\text{H}} + \text{Na}^+ \rightarrow \overline{\text{R-SO}_3\text{Na}} + \text{H}^+$ |
| **WAC (Weakly Acidic Cation)** | –COOH | ≥ 7 | Amberlite IRC-50 | $\overline{\text{R-COOH}} + \text{Na}^+ \rightarrow \overline{\text{R-COONa}} + \text{H}^+$ |
| **SBA (Strongly Basic Anion)** | –N⁺(CH₃)₃ | 0–14 | Amberlite IRA-400 | $\overline{\text{R-NR}_3\text{OH}} + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \overline{\text{R-NR}_3\text{Cl}} + \text{OH}^-$ |
| **WBA (Weakly Basic Anion)** | –NHR₂ | ≤ 7 | Amberlite IRA-93 | $\overline{\text{R-NHR}_2} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \overline{\text{R-NH}_2^+\text{R}_2 \text{Cl}^-}$ |

**Comparative Notes:**

- **Strong resins** work across the full pH range and need excess regenerant.
- **Weak resins** have higher capacity and higher regeneration efficiency but operate only within a limited pH window.
- **Strong resins** split salts (e.g., NaCl → NaOH + HCl during demineralisation), while **weak resins** do not.

**[7 marks: 2 for CER/AER, 2 for strong/weak, 2 for examples and reactions, 1 for comparative table]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition of Breakthrough Curve:**

> A *breakthrough curve* is a plot of the *effluent concentration* of the solute being removed (or its dimensionless ratio C/C₀) as a function of *time* (or *throughput volume* or *bed volumes*) in a fixed-bed ion exchange column.

**Sketch (Block Diagram Representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Time t equals 0] --> G2[Effluent C equals 0 - clean water]
    G2 --> G3[Breakpoint b - C equals C_threshold]
    G3 --> G4[Stoichiometric point s - midpoint of MTZ at exit]
    G4 --> G5[Exhaustion point e - C equals C0]
    G5 --> G6[Steady state - bed fully spent]
    G1 --- G2 --- G3 --- G4 --- G5 --- G6
```

**Points on the Curve:**

- **Breakpoint (b):** First appearance of the solute in effluent at the *threshold* concentration. Service cycle is terminated here to ensure product quality.
- **Stoichiometric point (s):** The midpoint of the mass-transfer zone (MTZ) reaches the column exit. Resin is in equilibrium with the inlet.
- **Exhaustion point (e):** C = C₀; the bed is fully spent; no further exchange occurs.

**Derivation of LUB — Breakthrough Capacity Relationship:**

Let L = total bed height, H_MTZ = height of mass-transfer zone.

Step 1 — The unused bed (LUB) lies *above* the MTZ; the height of unused bed is:

$$LUB \;=\; L - H_{MTZ}$$

Step 2 — The breakthrough capacity (Q_b) is the amount of solute removed up to the breakpoint:

$$Q_b \;=\; Q_{st} \left(1 - \frac{H_{MTZ}}{L}\right) \;=\; Q_{st} \left(1 - \frac{LUB}{L}\right)$$

Step 3 — Rearranging:

$$\frac{LUB}{L} \;=\; 1 - \frac{Q_b}{Q_{st}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,LUB \;=\; L \left(1 - \frac{Q_b}{Q_{st}}\right)\,}$$

Where:
- Q_st = stoichiometric capacity (full equilibrium capacity)
- Q_b = breakthrough capacity (useful capacity)
- L = total bed height
- LUB = length of unused bed

**Engineering Implication:**

A smaller LUB indicates a sharper MTZ and better bed utilisation. LUB is reduced by:
- Using **smaller resin beads** (shorter diffusion path).
- **Lower flow rates** (more residence time).
- **Higher temperature** (faster diffusion).
- **Higher DVB cross-linking** trade-off: increases strength but slows ion diffusion.

**[7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for sketch, 2 for derivation, 2 for engineering implications]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in PECHT756 Ion Exchange questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing total vs operating capacity** — The total capacity is *never* used for design; always convert to *operating* capacity with the correct utilisation factor.
> 2. **Forgetting units of Q_op** — Many students write Q_op in meq/g and then use it with V_B in mL, producing a 1000× error.
> 3. **Wrong equivalent weight of CaCO₃** — It is **50 g/eq** (= 100 g/mol ÷ 2), not 100 g/eq. This error alone costs 1 full mark.
> 4. **Treating selectivity coefficient as dimensionless without justification** — State explicitly that *activities* are approximated by *concentrations* in dilute solutions.
> 5. **Regenerant stoichiometry** — For a divalent ion exchange, **1 eq of Ca²⁺ requires 1 eq of Na⁺** (not 2 — counter-intuitive but correct, because equivalents already account for charge).
> 6. **Skipping the breakthrough sketch** — A 7-mark question on breakthrough curves *always* loses at least 2 marks if the sketch is missing.
> 7. **Not labelling the axes** — Always label C/C₀ (or mg/L) on the y-axis and t (or volume) on the x-axis.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ion Exchange Resins (Module 3, PECHT756)**

- **Definition:** Insoluble cross-linked polymer with fixed ionic groups exchanging mobile counter-ions stoichiometrically and reversibly.
- **Matrix:** Polystyrene cross-linked with **divinylbenzene (DVB)**. Higher DVB ⇒ stronger, less swelling, slower kinetics.
- **Four resin classes:** SAC (–SO₃H), WAC (–COOH), SBA (–N⁺R₃), WBA (–NHR₂).
- **Strong resins** work at all pH; **weak resins** work only in their effective pH window.
- **Cation resins** carry fixed anions; **anion resins** carry fixed cations.
- **Selectivity coefficient K_B^A** is the dimensionless equilibrium constant of the inter-phase exchange. K > 1 ⇒ resin prefers A; K < 1 ⇒ prefers B.
- **Selectivity hierarchy (SAC):** Al³⁺ > Ba²⁺ > Ca²⁺ > Mg²⁺ > K⁺ > Na⁺ > H⁺ > Li⁺ (for dilute solutions).
- **Regeneration:** Uses concentrated regenerant to reverse the equilibrium. **Concentration-valency effect** helps despite unfavourable selectivity (e.g., NaCl regenerates Ca²⁺-loaded resin).
- **Capacity:** Q_T (total) > Q_op (operating). Typical Q_T for SAC = 4.5–5.0 meq/g dry.
- **Equivalents:** Always use eq or meq — never mol — for stoichiometric balance.
- **CaCO₃ equivalent weight = 50 g/eq** (memorise this).
- **Regenerant dose (theoretical):** D_th = Q_op × V_B × M_reg / z.
- **Breakthrough curve** has three key points: **breakpoint, stoichiometric point, exhaustion point**.
- **LUB = L (1 − Q_b / Q_st)** — shorter MTZ ⇒ smaller LUB ⇒ better bed utilisation.
- **Two-bed demineraliser:** SAC (H⁺ form) + degasser + SBA (OH⁻ form).
- **Mixed-bed polisher:** SAC + SBA in the *same* vessel; produces water with resistivity > 18 MΩ·cm.
- **Service cycle time:** t_s = (Q_op × V_B) / (C₀ × Q).
- **KTU-favourite numerical:** Hardness softening + regenerant mass calculation — practice the CaCO₃-equivalent conversion until fluent.
- **Strong resins split salts; weak resins do not** — high-yield KTU fact.
- **Mixed bed regeneration** uses **backwash separation → acid to SBA from top → caustic to SAC from bottom → air mix**.
- **Engineering utilities:** water softening, demineralisation, hydrometallurgy (U, Au), pharmaceuticals, nuclear waste treatment, solid-acid catalysis (e.g., Amberlyst-15).
- **Always carry units** in the final numerical answer; KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT756 – Module 3):**
> **Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium** is the state of thermodynamic equilibrium established when a solid ion exchange resin (with counter-ions A⁺) is contacted with an aqueous solution containing two competing counter-ions (A⁺ and B⁺), resulting in a reversible stoichiometric exchange of equivalent ionic quantities between the two phases at constant temperature, pressure, and pH.

The general stoichiometric reaction for binary ion exchange is:

$$z_B A^{z_A+} + z_A \overline{B^{z_B+}} \rightleftharpoons z_B \overline{A^{z_A+}} + z_A B^{z_B+}$$

where the **overbar ( ¯ )** denotes the ion residing in the **resin phase**, and the non-overbarred ions are in the **liquid/solution phase**. For simplicity, in many KTU problems we consider **univalent–univalent exchange** (z_A = z_B = 1), where the reaction collapses to:

$$A^+ + \overline{B^+} \rightleftharpoons \overline{A^+} + B^+$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Dance Floor" Model:**
> Imagine a **dance floor (the ion exchange resin)** with a fixed number of seats (the fixed ionic groups, e.g., –SO₃⁻ on a cation resin). Two types of dancers — **Alex (A⁺)** and **Beth (B⁺)** — want to occupy these seats. The **selectivity** of the dance floor is its *preference* for one dancer over the other. The equilibrium is reached when the rate of Alex taking Beth's seat exactly equals the rate of Beth taking Alex's seat. The **temperature, music tempo, and crowd mood** (pH, ionic strength, concentration) determine who stays longer on the floor.
>
> If the floor (resin) **prefers Beth**, then even when there are far fewer Beths in the crowd (solution), they will dominate the floor at equilibrium. This "preference" is quantified by the **selectivity coefficient** and the **separation factor (α)**.

### Standard Metrics Used

- **Equivalent fraction in solution (xᵢ):** Ratio of equivalents of ion *i* in solution to total equivalents of counter-ions in solution. **Dimensionless.**
- **Equivalent fraction in resin (yᵢ):** Ratio of equivalents of ion *i* on the resin to total ion exchange capacity of the resin. **Dimensionless.**
- **Total ion exchange capacity (Q):** Total equivalents of exchangeable ions per unit mass (or volume) of dry (or wet) resin. Units: **eq/kg** or **eq/L**.
- **Selectivity Coefficient (Kₐ or K_AB):** Dimensionless thermodynamic measure of relative preference.
- **Separation Factor (α):** Dimensionless ratio indicating ease of separation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always specify whether concentrations are in **molality, molarity, or mole fraction**, and whether resin loadings are **per unit dry mass or per unit wet volume**. Marks are often lost when units are ambiguous.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mass Action Law for Ion Exchange

Binary ion exchange is treated as a **reversible chemical reaction**. At equilibrium, the **Law of Mass Action** applies. For the univalent exchange:

$$A^+ + \overline{B^+} \rightleftharpoons \overline{A^+} + B^+$$

The thermodynamic equilibrium constant (Kₐ) is:

$$K_a = \frac{\{\overline{A^+}\} \cdot \{B^+\}}{\{A^+\} \cdot \{\overline{B^+}\}}$$

where { } denotes **activity** in the respective phase. Since direct measurement of activities on the resin is impractical, engineers use the **rational selectivity coefficient (K_AB)**, which uses **concentrations or equivalent fractions** instead of activities:

$$K_{AB} = \frac{y_A \cdot x_B}{x_A \cdot y_B}$$

For **asymmetric (heterovalent)** exchange where z_A ≠ z_B, the exponent emerges:

$$K_{AB} = \frac{y_A^{z_B} \cdot x_B^{z_A}}{x_A^{z_B} \cdot y_B^{z_A}}$$

### 2.2 Separation Factor (α) — The Engineer's Workhorse

The **separation factor** is the most widely used parameter in design:

$$\alpha_{BA} = \frac{y_B / x_B}{y_A / x_A} = \frac{y_B \cdot x_A}{y_A \cdot x_B}$$

For univalent exchange, **α = K_AB** numerically (when using equivalent fractions). For multivalent exchange:

$$\alpha_{BA} = \left( K_{AB} \right)^{1/z_A z_B} \cdot \text{concentration correction terms}$$

**Interpretation of α:**

| Value of α | Meaning | KTU Exam Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **α = 1** | No preferential uptake | Resin is *non-selective*; A and B distribute proportionally |
| **α > 1** | Resin prefers B | B is concentrated on resin; A is enriched in solution |
| **α < 1** | Resin prefers A | A is concentrated on resin; B is enriched in solution |
| **α = ∞** | Total preference | Irreversible exchange (only theoretical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Symmetry Property:** α_BA = 1 / α_AB. This is frequently tested in KTU short-answer questions.

### 2.3 Material Balance and Stoichiometric Constraint

For any binary system, the **equivalent fractions must sum to unity** in each phase:

$$x_A + x_B = 1 \quad \text{(in solution)}$$

$$y_A + y_B = 1 \quad \text{(on resin)}$$

The **mass balance over the resin** for a closed-system (batch) contact:

$$Q \cdot (y_i - y_i^0) = -C_0 \cdot (x_i - x_i^0)$$

where Q = resin capacity (eq/L of resin), C₀ = total solution normality, and superscript 0 denotes initial values.

### 2.4 The Equilibrium Operating Line (Batch Contact)

For a **closed batch system** with initial solution concentration C₀ and initial resin loading yᵢ⁰:

$$y_i = y_i^0 + \frac{C_0}{Q} (x_i^0 - x_i)$$

This straight line on an **x–y diagram** connects the initial point (x₀, y₀) to the equilibrium point (x*, y*) and is the **batch operating line**.

### 2.5 Langmuir-Type Ion Exchange Isotherm

Analogous to adsorption, ion exchange equilibrium often follows a **Langmuir-type expression**:

$$y_A = \frac{K_{AB} \cdot x_A}{1 + (K_{AB} - 1) \cdot x_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **univalent binary ion exchange isotherm** that KTU frequently asks in Part B derivations. It directly comes from the selectivity coefficient and the constraint y_A + y_B = 1.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Bookmark this table for ESE preparation.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_{AB} = \dfrac{y_A \cdot x_B}{x_A \cdot y_B}$ | Rational selectivity coefficient (univalent) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha_{BA} = \dfrac{y_B \cdot x_A}{y_A \cdot x_B}$ | Separation factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_a = \dfrac{\bar{a}_A \cdot a_B}{a_A \cdot \bar{a}_B}$ | True thermodynamic constant (activities) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $y_A = \dfrac{K_{AB} \cdot x_A}{1 + (K_{AB} - 1) x_A}$ | Equilibrium isotherm (univalent binary) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $y_i^0 - y_i = \dfrac{C_0}{Q} (x_i - x_i^0)$ | Batch operating line | dimensionless both sides |
| 6 | $Q \, dy_i = -C_0 \, dx_i$ | Differential form of mass balance | eq per unit contact |
| 7 | $q_A = Q \cdot y_A$ | Loading of A on resin | eq/kg or eq/L |
| 8 | $C_A = C_0 \cdot x_A$ | Concentration of A in solution | eq/L or mol/L |
| 9 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$ | Standard free energy of exchange | J/mol |
| 10 | $\ln K_a = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$ | van't Hoff temperature dependence | dimensionless |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Softening:** Removal of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ (hardness) using Na⁺-form cation resin.
- **Demineralization:** Two-stage cation + anion exchange for ultrapure water in **semiconductor, pharmaceutical, and power-plant** industries.
- **Nuclear Waste Treatment:** Selective removal of radioactive Cs⁺, Sr²⁺ from coolant water.
- **Hydrometallurgy:** Recovery of valuable metals (Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺, Co²⁺) from leach liquors.
- **Sugar Refining:** Decolorization and de-ashing using anion/cation exchange.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Purification of antibiotics, amino acids, and APIs.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Binary Ion Exchange Isotherm (Langmuir Form)

We start with the univalent stoichiometric reaction:

$$A^+ + \overline{B^+} \rightleftharpoons \overline{A^+} + B^+$$

**Step 1 — Write the selectivity coefficient using equivalent fractions:**

$$K_{AB} = \frac{y_A \cdot x_B}{x_A \cdot y_B}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stoichiometric constraints:**

$$x_A + x_B = 1 \implies x_B = 1 - x_A$$

$$y_A + y_B = 1 \implies y_B = 1 - y_A$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the selectivity expression:**

$$K_{AB} = \frac{y_A \cdot (1 - x_A)}{x_A \cdot (1 - y_A)}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply and expand:**

$$K_{AB} \cdot x_A \cdot (1 - y_A) = y_A \cdot (1 - x_A)$$

$$K_{AB} \cdot x_A - K_{AB} \cdot x_A \cdot y_A = y_A - y_A \cdot x_A$$

**Step 5 — Collect all y_A terms on one side:**

$$K_{AB} \cdot x_A = y_A - y_A \cdot x_A + K_{AB} \cdot x_A \cdot y_A$$

$$K_{AB} \cdot x_A = y_A \left[ 1 - x_A + K_{AB} \cdot x_A \right]$$

$$K_{AB} \cdot x_A = y_A \left[ 1 + x_A (K_{AB} - 1) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for y_A:**

$$y_A = \frac{K_{AB} \cdot x_A}{1 + (K_{AB} - 1) \cdot x_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental binary ion exchange isotherm** asked repeatedly in KTU ESE. Notice it has exactly the same mathematical structure as the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm**, with K_AB playing the role of the Langmuir constant b.

**Step 7 — Verification of limiting cases:**

- If K_AB = 1: y_A = x_A · (1) / (1 + 0) = x_A → **no preference (linear, 45° line).**
- If K_AB → ∞: y_A = x_A / x_A = 1 for any x_A > 0 → **total preference for A.**
- If K_AB = 0: y_A = 0 → **resin completely rejects A.**

### 3.2 Derivation of the Batch Operating Line

Consider a closed vessel containing:
- Volume of solution V_L (L), total normality C₀ (eq/L)
- Mass of resin m_R (kg), total capacity Q (eq/kg)

**Step 1 — Material balance on ion A (no reaction except exchange):**

The total equivalents of A in the system is constant:

$$C_0 \cdot V_L \cdot x_A^0 + Q \cdot m_R \cdot y_A^0 = C_0 \cdot V_L \cdot x_A + Q \cdot m_R \cdot y_A$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$Q \cdot m_R \cdot (y_A - y_A^0) = C_0 \cdot V_L \cdot (x_A^0 - x_A)$$

**Step 3 — Define the *capacity ratio* (operating parameter):**

$$\Re = \frac{C_0 \cdot V_L}{Q \cdot m_R}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve for y_A:**

$$y_A = y_A^0 + \Re \cdot (x_A^0 - x_A)$$

This is a **straight line** in the (x_A, y_A) plane with slope **–ℜ** passing through the initial point (x_A⁰, y_A⁰).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Number of Stages (Kremser Equation Analogue)

For a **counter-current cascade** of N ideal stages with feed solution normality C_F, raffinate normality C_R, and resin loading y_F (inlet) to y_N (outlet):

$$\frac{y_F - y_N}{y_F - y_N^*} = \frac{1 - (\alpha S)^N}{1 - (\alpha S)}$$

where S = ratio of solution-to-resin equivalent flow rates, and y_N* is the resin loading in equilibrium with raffinate x_R. KTU problems frequently ask the student to **solve for N** given α, S, and the four terminal compositions.

> [!TIP]
> The exact Kremser form for ion exchange is mathematically identical to the Kremser equation for absorption/stripping — the analogy is intentional in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Binary Ion Exchange Process Architecture

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Solution A + B] --> B[Feed Tank]
    B --> C{Pump}
    C --> D[Ion Exchange Column]
    D --> E[Regeneration Tank NaCl or HCl]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Raffinate Enriched in A]
    D --> G[Spent Regenerant Enriched in B]
    H[Fresh Cation Resin H form] --> D
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **raw solution** containing counter-ions A and B enters the feed tank.
- It is pumped through the ion exchange column packed with **cation/anion resin** in the H⁺ or Na⁺ form.
- The **raffinate** (treated solution) leaves the column enriched in the **less preferred ion A**.
- Periodically, the resin is **regenerated** using a concentrated regenerant (e.g., NaCl for softening, HCl for H-form resins).
- The **spent regenerant** carries away the **preferred ion B**.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Binary Equilibrium Stages in a Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1] --> S2[Stage 2]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3]
    S3 --> S4[Stage N]
    S4 --> S5[Raffinate Out]
    R1[Resin In yF] --> S1
    S1 --> R2[Resin Out y1]
    R2 --> S2
    S2 --> R3[Resin Out y2]
    R3 --> S3
    S3 --> R4[Resin Out yN minus 1]
    R4 --> S4
    F1[Fresh Solution xF] --> S1
    S2 --> F2[Interstage x1]
    F2 --> S3
    S3 --> F3[Interstage x2]
    F3 --> S4
```

### 4.3 Schematic — The Equilibrium Curve and Operating Line (x–y Diagram Description)

A typical **x–y diagram** for binary ion exchange plots **y_B (resin)** vs. **x_B (solution)**:

- The **45° line** represents α = 1 (no selectivity).
- The **equilibrium curve** lies **above** the 45° line when α_BA > 1 (resin prefers B).
- The **operating line** (straight) is drawn from (x_F, y_N) to (x_R, y_F) for counter-current operation.
- The **number of stages** is obtained by **stepping** between the operating line and the equilibrium curve, exactly like a **McCabe–Thiele construction**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define the separation factor (α) for binary ion exchange. What is its significance in resin selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **separation factor α_BA** is defined as the ratio of the ratio of the concentration of B to A in the resin phase to that in the solution phase at equilibrium:

$$\alpha_{BA} = \frac{y_B / x_B}{y_A / x_A} = \frac{y_B \cdot x_A}{y_A \cdot x_B}$$

**Significance:**
- If **α_BA > 1**, the resin preferentially sorbs B; if **α_BA < 1**, the resin preferentially sorbs A.
- A value of **α = 1** indicates no selectivity.
- The **magnitude of deviation from unity** indicates the **ease of separation** — larger deviations lead to fewer stages and lower regenerant consumption.
- Used to determine the **number of equilibrium stages** in cascade design via the Kremser equation.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance. Diagram optional but adds value.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S1 Model]**
> **State the Law of Mass Action as applied to binary ion exchange and write the rational selectivity coefficient expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For the reaction A⁺ + B̄⁺ ⇌ Ā⁺ + B⁺ at equilibrium, the **Law of Mass Action** states that the ratio of the product of activities (or concentrations) of products to reactants, each raised to its stoichiometric coefficient, is constant at a given temperature:

$$K_a = \frac{\{\bar{A}^+\} \cdot \{B^+\}}{\{A^+\} \cdot \{\bar{B}^+\}}$$

In practice, using **equivalent fractions** instead of activities, the **rational selectivity coefficient** is:

$$K_{AB} = \frac{y_A \cdot x_B}{x_A \cdot y_B}$$

For univalent exchange, **α = K_AB**. For heterovalent exchange (z_A ≠ z_B), an exponent z_B / (z_A · z_B) is applied.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Question A — 14 Marks**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the binary ion exchange isotherm in the form
> $$y_A = \frac{K_{AB} \cdot x_A}{1 + (K_{AB} - 1) x_A}$$
> starting from the rational selectivity coefficient definition. State clearly the assumptions used.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A cation exchange resin in the Na⁺ form is used to remove Ca²⁺ from water. The univalent-equivalent selectivity coefficient K_Ca,Na = 1.5 (for the 2Na⁺ ⇌ Ca²⁺ exchange). The total solution normality is 0.01 N, the resin capacity is 2.0 eq/kg, and 1 kg of resin is contacted with 100 L of water containing initially 0.005 N Ca²⁺. Calculate the equilibrium concentration of Ca²⁺ in the treated water.

**Model Solution to (a):** *(See derivation in Section 3.1 above.)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Univalent (1:1) stoichiometry — here generalized for illustration.
2. Constant temperature, pressure, and ionic strength.
3. Activity coefficients ≈ 1 (ideal dilute solution).
4. Resin is fully swollen and homogeneous.
5. No co-ion uptake (Donnan exclusion holds).

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert to equivalent fractions.**
Initial calcium equivalent fraction in solution:

$$x_{Ca}^0 = \frac{0.005}{0.01} = 0.50$$

**Step 2 — Initial resin equivalent fraction in Ca form (resin starts as pure Na⁺ form):**

$$y_{Ca}^0 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the capacity ratio ℜ:**

$$\Re = \frac{C_0 \cdot V_L}{Q \cdot m_R} = \frac{0.01 \text{ eq/L} \cdot 100 \text{ L}}{2.0 \text{ eq/kg} \cdot 1 \text{ kg}} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Operating line equation:**

$$y_{Ca} = 0 + 0.5 \cdot (0.50 - x_{Ca}) = 0.25 - 0.5 x_{Ca}$$

**Step 5 — For divalent-univalent exchange, the selectivity relation is:**

$$K_{Ca,Na} = \frac{y_{Ca} \cdot x_{Na}^2}{x_{Ca} \cdot y_{Na}^2} = 1.5$$

But for simplicity, the **equivalent-fraction form** of the isotherm (using total equivalents) for this 2:1 exchange gives:

$$y_{Ca} = \frac{K_{Ca,Na}^{1/2} \cdot x_{Ca}}{1 + (K_{Ca,Na}^{1/2} - 1) x_{Ca}}$$

$$\sqrt{1.5} = 1.2247$$

$$y_{Ca} = \frac{1.2247 \cdot x_{Ca}}{1 + 0.2247 \cdot x_{Ca}}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the operating line and the isotherm:**

$$0.25 - 0.5 x_{Ca} = \frac{1.2247 \cdot x_{Ca}}{1 + 0.2247 \cdot x_{Ca}}$$

Let u = x_Ca. Cross-multiplying:

$$(0.25 - 0.5 u)(1 + 0.2247 u) = 1.2247 u$$

$$0.25 + 0.0562 u - 0.5 u - 0.1124 u^2 = 1.2247 u$$

$$0.25 - 0.4438 u - 0.1124 u^2 = 1.2247 u$$

$$0.1124 u^2 + 1.6685 u - 0.25 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve the quadratic (using positive root):**

$$u = \frac{-1.6685 + \sqrt{1.6685^2 + 4 \cdot 0.1124 \cdot 0.25}}{2 \cdot 0.1124}$$

$$u = \frac{-1.6685 + \sqrt{2.7839 + 0.1124}}{0.2248}$$

$$u = \frac{-1.6685 + \sqrt{2.8963}}{0.2248}$$

$$u = \frac{-1.6685 + 1.7020}{0.2248} = \frac{0.0335}{0.2248} = 0.149$$

**Step 8 — Convert back to normality:**

$$C_{Ca,eq} = x_{Ca} \cdot C_0 = 0.149 \cdot 0.01 = 0.00149 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answer:** The equilibrium Ca²⁺ concentration in treated water is approximately **0.00149 N** (or 1.49 meq/L), which is a **70.2% reduction** in hardness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When the exchange is **heterovalent** (e.g., 2Na⁺ ⇌ Ca²⁺), students often **forget to take the 1/(z_A · z_B) = 1/2 power** of the selectivity coefficient. This single error gives a wrong answer. Also, do **not** use the univalent isotherm directly for divalent exchange — it will not satisfy electroneutrality on the resin.

---

> **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of equivalent fraction in ion exchange. With the help of a neat x–y diagram, show how the operating line and equilibrium curve are used to determine the number of theoretical stages for a counter-current binary ion exchange cascade.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** In a counter-current ion exchange cascade, the feed solution has x_B,F = 0.40, the raffinate has x_B,R = 0.05, the entering resin is in pure A-form (y_B,F = 0), and the leaving resin has y_B,N = 0.30. The separation factor α_BA = 2.0 and the solution-to-resin equivalent flow ratio S = 1.2. Estimate the number of theoretical stages using the Kremser-type equation.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Equivalent fraction concept:**
- In the **solution phase**: x_i = C_i / C_0 (where C_0 is the total counter-ion concentration).
- In the **resin phase**: y_i = q_i / Q (where Q is the total ion exchange capacity).
- Both x_i and y_i are dimensionless and satisfy ∑x_i = ∑y_i = 1.
- They are the natural "concentration" variables for ion exchange, analogous to mole fractions in VLE.

**x–y diagram and stage construction:**

1. Plot the **equilibrium curve** y_B vs. x_B using the isotherm y_B = K_AB · x_B / [1 + (K_AB − 1) x_B] for α > 1, the curve lies above the 45° line.
2. Plot the **operating line** connecting (x_F, y_N) and (x_R, y_F). For counter-current operation:

$$y = y_F + \frac{S}{1} (x_F - x)$$

   (sloped line with slope −S).

3. **Stepping off stages**: Starting at (x_F, y_N), draw a **vertical line** up to the equilibrium curve, then a **horizontal line** to the operating line, then vertical, alternating until the raffinate composition x_R is reached or crossed.
4. The number of "steps" gives the **number of theoretical stages N**.

**Model Solution to (b):**

The Kremser-type equation for ion exchange:

$$N = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\frac{y_F - y_N^*}{y_N - y_N^*}\right) + \left(1 - \frac{1}{\alpha S}\right) \right]}{\ln(\alpha S)}$$

**Step 1 — Compute y_N*, the resin composition in equilibrium with raffinate x_R:**

$$y_N^* = \frac{\alpha \cdot x_R}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_R} = \frac{2.0 \cdot 0.05}{1 + 1.0 \cdot 0.05} = \frac{0.10}{1.05} = 0.0952$$

**Step 2 — Compute α · S:**

$$\alpha S = 2.0 \cdot 1.2 = 2.4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator term:**

$$y_F - y_N^* = 0 - 0.0952 = -0.0952$$

$$y_N - y_N^* = 0.30 - 0.0952 = 0.2048$$

$$\frac{y_F - y_N^*}{y_N - y_N^*} = \frac{-0.0952}{0.2048} = -0.4648$$

**Step 4 — Plug into the Kremser equation:**

$$N = \frac{\ln \left[ -0.4648 + \left(1 - \frac{1}{2.4}\right) \right]}{\ln(2.4)}$$

$$N = \frac{\ln \left[ -0.4648 + 0.5833 \right]}{\ln(2.4)} = \frac{\ln(0.1185)}{0.8755}$$

$$N = \frac{-2.132}{0.8755} = -2.435$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative number of stages** is physically impossible. This indicates the operating line and equilibrium data are **inconsistent** — the specified terminal compositions cannot be achieved with α = 2.0 and S = 1.2. Either α is too low or the regeneration level y_N is unattainable. In a real KTU answer, the student should comment on this inconsistency and propose a corrective action (increase α by choosing a different resin, decrease S, or modify terminal compositions).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** The Kremser equation for ion exchange has the **same form** as for absorption, but the **operating line slope and the y-axis definition differ** (resin equivalent fraction, not mole fraction in gas). Many students lose 2–3 marks by mixing up the Kremser forms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium**

- ✅ **Binary ion exchange** = stoichiometric exchange of two counter-ions A⁺ and B⁺ between resin and solution.
- ✅ **Selectivity coefficient K_AB** = (y_A · x_B) / (x_A · y_B); dimensionless.
- ✅ **Separation factor α_BA** = (y_B · x_A) / (y_A · x_B); α = 1 → no preference; α > 1 → resin prefers B; α < 1 → resin prefers A.
- ✅ **Symmetry rule:** α_BA = 1 / α_AB — memorize this for short-answer questions.
- ✅ **Equivalent fractions** x_i and y_i are the natural working variables; both sum to unity.
- ✅ **Batch operating line** is a straight line: y = y⁰ + ℜ (x⁰ − x) with slope −ℜ.
- ✅ **Counter-current operating line:** straight line connecting (x_F, y_N) to (x_R, y_F) with slope −S (solution-to-resin flow ratio).
- ✅ **Isotherm (univalent)** has Langmuir form: y_A = K·x_A / [1 + (K − 1) x_A].
- ✅ **Heterovalent exchange** requires the (1/z_A z_B)-power correction in K.
- ✅ **Number of stages** found by McCabe–Thiele stepping or Kremser equation.
- ✅ **Regeneration** of resin is essential; cycle time depends on breakthrough capacity and feed flow.
- ✅ **Equilibrium assumptions:** isothermal, ideal dilute, fully swollen resin, Donnan exclusion of co-ions.
- ✅ **Selectivity trends** (general): Al³⁺ > Ca²⁺ > Na⁺ > H⁺ (for sulfonic acid cation exchangers).
- ✅ **Industrial applications:** water softening, demineralization, hydrometallurgy, nuclear waste, sugar refining, pharma purification.

> **End of Module 3 — Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium Notes (PECHT756, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Movement Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion Movement Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Ion Movement Theory** is the foundational framework describing the transport of charged species (ions) across ion-exchange membranes, ion-exchange resins, and electrolyte solutions under the combined influence of **concentration gradients**, **electrical potential gradients**, and **convective bulk flow**. It is governed primarily by the **Nernst-Planck equation**, supplemented by **Donnan equilibrium theory** and the principles of **electroneutrality** within the membrane phase.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Novel Separation Processes), Ion Movement Theory is the mathematical backbone for analyzing:
- **Electrodialysis (ED)**
- **Electrodeionization (EDI)**
- **Membrane-based capacitive deionization (CDI)**
- **Ion-exchange column dynamics**
- **Fuel cell membrane transport**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PECHT756 focuses on the movement of ions through ion-exchange membranes, the electrochemical potential driving force, the selectivity of ion-exchange resins, Donnan exclusion, and the practical design equations for electrodialysis stacks.

## 1.2 The Three Driving Forces for Ion Movement

According to the Nernst-Planck framework, an ion $i$ moves due to **three simultaneous mechanisms**:

| Mechanism | Driving Force | Effect on Ion |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Diffusion** | Concentration gradient $(\partial C_i / \partial x)$ | Moves from high to low concentration |
| **Migration (Electromigration)** | Electrical potential gradient $(\partial \phi / \partial x)$ | Cations move toward cathode, anions toward anode |
| **Convection** | Bulk fluid velocity $v$ | Carries ions along with fluid flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Electroneutrality Principle:** At every point in the solution and within the membrane (except in the very thin Electrical Double Layer, EDL), the total positive charge equals the total negative charge: $\sum_i z_i C_i = 0$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bumper-Cars in a Toll-Plaza" Picture

Imagine a **toll plaza on a highway** where vehicles (ions) can pass through. The toll booth is the **ion-exchange membrane**, and it is staffed by picky gatekeepers who only let certain types of cars through (selectivity).

- **Diffusion** is like cars naturally rolling downhill from a crowded city (concentrate) to an empty one (diluate) — purely based on density imbalance.
- **Migration** is the **traffic police** standing in the middle of the road, blowing a whistle that physically pushes cars in one direction — based on charge.
- **Convection** is the **wind** that blows all cars in one direction regardless of their individual intentions.

The membrane (toll booth) charges a "toll" via the **Donnan potential**, which prevents co-ions (wrong-type ions) from passing through while letting counter-ions (the right type) through freely. The gatekeeper is **picky** — and the extent of pickiness is the **selectivity coefficient**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants Used Throughout

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Constants (always bolded in KTU solutions):**
> - **Faraday's constant:** $F = \mathbf{96{,}485\ C \cdot mol^{-1}}$
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = \mathbf{8.314\ J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$
> - **Absolute temperature:** $T$ in **Kelvin (K)**
> - **Valence of ion $i$:** $z_i$ (dimensionless, signed)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nernst-Planck Equation — The Master Equation

The total flux of an ionic species $i$ in a one-dimensional system is given by the **Nernst-Planck Equation**:

$$
J_i = -D_i \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} \;-\; \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \;+\; C_i v
$$

where:
- $J_i$ = flux of ion $i$ (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $D_i$ = diffusion coefficient of ion $i$ in the medium (m²·s⁻¹)
- $C_i$ = concentration of ion $i$ (mol·m⁻³)
- $z_i$ = charge number of ion $i$
- $F$ = Faraday's constant (C·mol⁻¹)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\phi$ = electric potential (V)
- $v$ = bulk fluid velocity (m·s⁻¹) — the convective term

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in KTU:** The Nernst-Planck equation is **the** central equation for Module 3. It is the only equation that simultaneously handles diffusion, migration, and convection. Memorize it fully — examiners reward students who write the equation correctly with proper sign conventions.

## 2.2 Donnan Equilibrium Theory

When an **ion-exchange membrane** (containing fixed charged groups) is placed in contact with an electrolyte solution, an equilibrium is established where ions of opposite sign to the fixed charge (**counter-ions**) are preferentially absorbed, while ions of the same sign (**co-ions**) are partially excluded.

For a membrane with fixed anionic groups (cation exchanger) in contact with a NaCl solution:

$$
\left( \frac{a_{\text{Na}^+}}{a_{\text{Cl}^-}} \right)_{\text{membrane}} = \left( \frac{a_{\text{Na}^+}}{a_{\text{Cl}^-}} \right)_{\text{solution}}
$$

This gives the **Donnan equilibrium condition**:

$$
C_{\text{Na}^+}^{(\text{memb})} \cdot C_{\text{Cl}^-}^{(\text{memb})} = \left( C_{\text{Na}^+}^{(\text{soln})} \right)^2 = \left( C_{\text{Cl}^-}^{(\text{soln})} \right)^2
$$

(assuming equal activity coefficients in both phases for simplicity).

The **Donnan potential** $\phi_{\text{Don}}$ across the membrane-solution interface is:

$$
\phi_{\text{Don}} = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{a_i^{(\text{soln})}}{a_i^{(\text{memb})}}
$$

## 2.3 Selectivity Coefficient

For a cation exchange resin exchanging ion $A^{z_A^+}$ with ion $B^{z_B^+}$:

$$
z_B \cdot R \bar{A} + z_A \cdot B^{z_B^+} \rightleftharpoons z_B \cdot A^{z_A^+} + z_A \cdot R \bar{B}
$$

The **selectivity coefficient** $K_{A/B}$ is:

$$
K_{A/B} = \frac{(\bar{C}_A)^{z_B} (C_B)^{z_A}}{(\bar{C}_B)^{z_A} (C_A)^{z_B}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If $K_{A/B} > 1$, the resin prefers ion $A$. If $K_{A/B} < 1$, it prefers ion $B$. This controls the order of elution in chromatography and the separation efficiency in ion exchange columns.

## 2.4 Transport Number (Transference Number)

The **transport number** $t_i$ of ion $i$ is the fraction of the total current carried by that ion:

$$
t_i = \frac{z_i F J_i}{i} = \frac{\text{current carried by ion } i}{\text{total current}}
$$

and $\sum_i t_i = 1$ (electroneutrality of current).

For a **perfectly selective cation-exchange membrane**, $t_+ \to 1$ and $t_- \to 0$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains **every** high-yield equation tested in KTU Module 3. Critical: $\vert$ is replaced with `\vert` to keep Markdown table syntax safe.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $J_i = -D_i \dfrac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \dfrac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + C_i v$ | Nernst-Planck total flux | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\phi_{\text{Don}} = \dfrac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \dfrac{a_i^{(\text{soln})}}{a_i^{(\text{memb})}}$ | Donnan potential across interface | V |
| 3 | $\bar{C}_+ \cdot \bar{C}_- = C_s^2$ | Donnan product inside the membrane | (mol·m⁻³)² |
| 4 | $K_{A/B} = \dfrac{(\bar{C}_A)^{z_B}(C_B)^{z_A}}{(\bar{C}_B)^{z_A}(C_A)^{z_B}}$ | Selectivity coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $t_i = \dfrac{z_i F J_i}{i}$ | Transport number of ion $i$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sum_i t_i = 1$ | Summation rule for transport numbers | dimensionless |
| 7 | $D_i = u_i \, RT / ( \vert z_i \vert F)$ | Nernst-Einstein relation | m²·s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\Delta \phi_{\text{memb}} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{a_1}{a_2}$ | Membrane potential (bi-ionic) | V |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{curr}} = \dfrac{z F N_i}{I \, \Delta t}$ | Current efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $R_{\text{ohm}} = \dfrac{d}{\kappa A}$ | Ohmic resistance of membrane | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $\text{Energy} = I^2 R_{\text{ohm}} \Delta t$ | Electrical energy consumption | J |
| 12 | $\dfrac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial J_i}{\partial x}$ | Continuity (mass balance) for ion $i$ | mol·m⁻³·s⁻¹ |
| 13 | $\Lambda_m^\circ = z F u$ | Molar conductivity at infinite dilution | S·m²·mol⁻¹ |
| 14 | $C_{\text{fixed}} = \bar{C}_+ - \bar{C}_-$ | Fixed-ion concentration in membrane | mol·m⁻³ |

## 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Utility Mapping (KTU-mandated):**
> - The **Nernst-Planck equation** is used by **electrodialysis stack designers** (e.g., Asahi Kasei, Suez) to predict current–voltage curves and concentration polarization in desalination plants.
> - **Donnan equilibrium** governs the operation of **Nafion™ proton-exchange membranes** in PEM fuel cells (used in Toyota Mirai, Honda Clarity).
> - **Selectivity coefficients** are tabulated for **Dowex 50, Amberlite IR-120** resins to design **water-softening** and **rare-earth recovery** columns.
> - **Transport numbers** determine the **permselectivity** of membranes in **chlor-alkali cells** (producing Cl₂ and NaOH).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst-Planck Equation from First Principles

**Starting point:** The chemical potential of ion $i$ in an electrochemical system is given by:

$$
\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i + z_i F \phi
$$

where $a_i$ is the activity, $\mu_i^\circ$ is the standard chemical potential, and $\phi$ is the local electric potential.

**Step 1 — Driving force on the ion:**
The thermodynamic force per mole acting on ion $i$ along the $x$-direction is the negative gradient of the electrochemical potential:

$$
F_{\text{drive}, i} = -\frac{\partial \mu_i}{\partial x} = -RT \frac{\partial \ln a_i}{\partial x} - z_i F \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate to flux via mobility:**
The flux is the product of concentration, mobility, and driving force (linear non-equilibrium thermodynamics):

$$
J_i = C_i u_i F_{\text{drive}, i}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the driving force:**

$$
J_i = -C_i u_i RT \frac{\partial \ln a_i}{\partial x} - C_i u_i z_i F \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Nernst-Einstein relation:**
The Nernst-Einstein equation connects mobility and diffusivity:

$$
D_i = u_i \frac{RT}{\vert z_i \vert F} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u_i = \frac{D_i \vert z_i \vert F}{RT}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the flux expression:**

$$
J_i = -\frac{D_i \vert z_i \vert F}{RT} \cdot C_i \cdot RT \frac{\partial \ln a_i}{\partial x} - \frac{D_i \vert z_i \vert F}{RT} \cdot C_i \cdot z_i F \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify using $\frac{\partial \ln a_i}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{C_i} \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x}$ (ideal dilute solution):**

$$
J_i = -D_i \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 7 — Add convective term $C_i v$ for completeness:**

$$
\boxed{\,J_i = -D_i \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + C_i v\,}
$$

This is the **complete Nernst-Planck equation** for ion $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of each term:**
> - Term 1 ($D_i$ term): **Fickian diffusion** — the response to a concentration gradient.
> - Term 2 ($z_i$ term): **Electromigration** — ions respond to the local electric field.
> - Term 3 ($v$ term): **Convection** — bulk flow drag (zero in solid ion-exchange membranes).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Donnan Equilibrium for a Strongly Acidic Cation Exchange Membrane

**Setup:** A cation exchange membrane with fixed sulfonate groups ($-SO_3^-$) carrying a fixed charge density $C_R$ (mol·m⁻³) is in equilibrium with a NaCl solution of bulk concentration $C_s$ (mol·m⁻³).

**Step 1 — Electroneutrality inside the membrane:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} = \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} + C_R
$$

**Step 2 — Donnan equilibrium for Na⁺ and Cl⁻ across the interface:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} \cdot \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = C_s^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$
\left( \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} + C_R \right) \cdot \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = C_s^2
$$

**Step 4 — Quadratic in $\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-}$:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-}^2 + C_R \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} - C_s^2 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve using the quadratic formula:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = \frac{-C_R + \sqrt{C_R^2 + 4 C_s^2}}{2}
$$

(We discard the negative root since concentration must be positive.)

**Step 6 — Solve for $\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+}$ using Step 1:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} = \frac{C_R + \sqrt{C_R^2 + 4 C_s^2}}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Limiting cases:**

- **Case A: $C_s \ll C_R$ (dilute solution, strong exclusion):**

  Using $\sqrt{C_R^2 + 4 C_s^2} \approx C_R \left(1 + \frac{2 C_s^2}{C_R^2}\right)$:

  $$
  \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} \approx \frac{C_s^2}{C_R} \to 0 \quad \text{(strong co-ion exclusion)}
  $$

  $$
  \bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} \approx C_R
  $$

  This is the **Donnan exclusion principle** — co-ions are essentially rejected.

- **Case B: $C_s \gg C_R$ (concentrated solution, exclusion breaks down):**

  $\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} \to C_s$ and $\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} \to C_s$ — the membrane loses selectivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The $C_s^2 / C_R$ scaling is the **most-tested result**. If a question mentions a membrane with $C_R = 2000\ \text{mol/m}^3$ and asks you to find the co-ion leakage in a 10 mM solution, **immediately** apply the dilute limit: $\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = C_s^2 / C_R = 0.01^2 / 2000 = 5 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{M}$. This shows extraordinary selectivity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Membrane Potential (Bi-ionic System)

Consider a membrane separating two solutions of the same electrolyte AB at different concentrations $C_1$ and $C_2$.

**Step 1 — At steady state, no net current flows, so $J_+ + J_- = 0$.**

Using the Nernst-Planck equation (no convection, $v = 0$):

$$
-D_+ \frac{dC_+}{dx} - \frac{z_+ F D_+ C_+}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx} = D_- \frac{dC_-}{dx} + \frac{z_- F D_- C_-}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

**Step 2 — Electroneutrality: $C_+ = C_- = C$, $z_+ = +1$, $z_- = -1$:**

$$
-D_+ \frac{dC}{dx} - \frac{F D_+ C}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx} = D_- \frac{dC}{dx} - \frac{F D_- C}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

**Step 3 — Collect terms:**

$$
-\left(D_+ + D_-\right) \frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{F C}{RT} \left(D_+ - D_-\right) \frac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for $d\phi$:**

$$
d\phi = -\frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{D_+ + D_-}{D_+ - D_-} \cdot \frac{dC}{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from side 1 to side 2:**

$$
\Delta \phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = -\frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{D_+ + D_-}{D_+ - D_-} \cdot \ln \frac{C_2}{C_1}
$$

**Step 6 — Define transport numbers $t_+ = D_+ / (D_+ + D_-)$ and $t_- = D_- / (D_+ + D_-)$:**

$$
\frac{D_+ + D_-}{D_+ - D_-} = \frac{1}{t_+ - t_-} = \frac{1}{2 t_+ - 1}
$$

**Step 7 — Final membrane potential equation (Teorell-Meyer-Siever / TMS equation):**

$$
\boxed{\, \Delta \phi = \frac{RT}{F} \left(2 t_+ - 1 \right) \ln \frac{C_1}{C_2} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** For an **ideal cation-selective membrane**, $t_+ = 1$, so $\Delta \phi = \frac{RT}{F} \ln(C_1/C_2)$ — identical to the Nernst equation. This is the theoretical upper limit on the membrane potential.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 7 marks)

**Problem:** A cation exchange membrane separates a 0.1 M NaCl solution from a 0.01 M NaCl solution at 25 °C. The transport number of Na⁺ in the membrane is $t_+ = 0.95$. Calculate:
(a) the membrane potential, and
(b) the Donnan potential at the high-concentration side, given fixed ion concentration $C_R = 1500\ \text{mol/m}^3$.

**Given:**
- $T = 298\ \text{K}$, $C_1 = 0.1\ \text{M} = 100\ \text{mol/m}^3$, $C_2 = 0.01\ \text{M} = 10\ \text{mol/m}^3$
- $t_+ = 0.95$, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$

**Solution (a):**

$$
\Delta \phi = \frac{RT}{F}(2 t_+ - 1) \ln \frac{C_1}{C_2}
$$

$$
\Delta \phi = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} \times (2 \times 0.95 - 1) \times \ln \frac{100}{10}
$$

$$
\Delta \phi = 0.02569 \times 0.9 \times \ln(10)
$$

$$
\Delta \phi = 0.02569 \times 0.9 \times 2.3026
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta \phi = 0.0532\ \text{V} = 53.2\ \text{mV}}
$$

**Solution (b):**

Using $\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = \dfrac{-C_R + \sqrt{C_R^2 + 4 C_s^2}}{2}$ with $C_s = 100$ mol/m³:

$$
\sqrt{C_R^2 + 4 C_s^2} = \sqrt{1500^2 + 4 \times 100^2} = \sqrt{2{,}250{,}000 + 40{,}000} = \sqrt{2{,}290{,}000} = 1513.3
$$

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = \frac{-1500 + 1513.3}{2} = 6.65\ \text{mol/m}^3
$$

Now apply the Donnan potential (using Cl⁻ as the reference ion, $z = -1$):

$$
\phi_{\text{Don}} = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{a_i^{(\text{soln})}}{a_i^{(\text{memb})}} = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{(-1) \times 96485} \ln \frac{100}{6.65}
$$

$$
\phi_{\text{Don}} = -0.02569 \times \ln(15.04) = -0.02569 \times 2.710 = -0.0697\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi_{\text{Don}} \approx -69.7\ \text{mV}}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates the membrane interior is at a lower electric potential than the solution, consistent with a cation-exchange membrane (negatively charged fixed groups).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marks Distribution Insight (7-mark problems):**
> - 1 mark: Correct formula selection
> - 1 mark: Correct substitution of values
> - 1 mark: Correct log/algebra
> - 1 mark: Correct sign convention
> - 1 mark: Unit conversion (M ↔ mol/m³)
> - 1 mark: Final numerical answer
> - 1 mark: Physical interpretation (what does the sign mean?)

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Continuity Equation & Kohlrausch Condition

For transient (time-dependent) problems, ion fluxes are governed by the **continuity equation**:

$$
\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_i}{\partial x}
$$

Substituting the Nernst-Planck flux (with $v = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} = D_i \frac{\partial^2 C_i}{\partial x^2} + \frac{z_i F D_i}{RT} \left( \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + C_i \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kohlrausch Boundary Condition (very important in KTU papers):** In a binary electrolyte AB, the relationship $\frac{C_+}{z_+} = \frac{C_-}{\vert z_- \vert}$ (electroneutrality) means that the **ratio of diffusivities** governs the local electric field. Specifically:

$$
\frac{C_+}{C_-} = \text{const} \quad \text{along } x \text{ at all times}
$$

This means **the two ions diffuse together** in a coupled manner, which dramatically simplifies analytical solutions of the diffusion equations in electrodialysis.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Equation for KTU Problems

| If the question asks about... | Use this equation | Key concept tested |
|-------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Flux with all 3 driving forces | Full Nernst-Planck (Eq. 1) | All 3 mechanisms |
| Co-ion concentration inside membrane | $\bar{C}_- = C_s^2 / C_R$ (dilute limit) | Donnan exclusion |
| Potential across ion-exchange membrane | TMS equation (Eq. 3.7) | Transport number |
| Total current carried by one ion | $t_i = z_i F J_i / i$ | Electroneutrality |
| Current efficiency of electrodialysis | $\eta_{\text{curr}} = z F N_i / (I \Delta t)$ | Faraday's law |
| Proton conduction in Nafion | Nernst-Einstein $D_i = u_i RT / (\vert z_i \vert F)$ | Mobility-diffusivity link |
| Energy consumption of ED | $E = I^2 R \Delta t$ | Ohmic losses |
| Separation factor in chromatography | $K_{A/B}$ selectivity coefficient | Ion-exchange equilibrium |

## 4.3 Electrodialysis Stack — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC["DC Power Supply"] --> Anode["Anode (+ve)"]
    DC --> Cathode["Cathode (-ve)"]

    Anode --> C1["Concentrate Compartment 1"]
    C1 --> AEM1["Anion Exchange Membrane AEM1"]:::aem
    AEM1 --> D1["Diluate Compartment 1"]
    D1 --> CEM1["Cation Exchange Membrane CEM1"]:::cem
    CEM1 --> C2["Concentrate Compartment 2"]
    C2 --> AEM2["AEM2"]:::aem
    AEM2 --> D2["Diluate Compartment 2"]
    D2 --> CEM2["CEM2"]:::cem
    CEM2 --> C3["Concentrate Compartment 3"]
    C3 --> Cathode

    C1 -.->|"Cl- migrates to Anode"| AEM1
    D1 -.->|"Na+ leaves to right"| CEM1
    C2 -.->|"Cl- from D2"| AEM2
    D2 -.->|"Na+ leaves to right"| CEM2

    classDef cem fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#c60,stroke-width:2px
    classDef aem fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#039,stroke-width:2px
```

**Process logic:**
1. DC current drives anions to the **anode** (left) and cations to the **cathode** (right).
2. The **CEM** allows only cations to pass; the **AEM** allows only anions to pass.
3. **Diluate compartments** lose ions → desalted product.
4. **Concentrate compartments** gain ions → brine stream.

```mermaid

    B --> C["Co-ion (Cl-): uphill barrier = phi_Don"]:::barrier
    B --> D["Counter-ion (Na+): downhill path"]:::pass
    C --> E["Mostly rejected (low C_Cl inside)"]:::reject
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading this diagram for KTU:** The Donnan potential is the **electrostatic hill** that the co-ion must climb to enter the membrane. The taller the hill (larger $C_R$ or smaller $C_s$), the more selective the membrane.
