# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Donnan equilibrium and explain the concept of Donnan exclusion with respect to ion-exchange membranes. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Donnan equilibrium is the electrochemical equilibrium established at the interface between an **ion-exchange membrane** (containing fixed ionic groups) and an external electrolyte solution. At equilibrium, the **electrochemical potentials** of the permeant ions are equal in both phases.

**Donnan exclusion** is the phenomenon by which **co-ions** (ions having the same sign as the fixed groups in the membrane) are prevented from entering the membrane phase, while **counter-ions** (opposite sign) are preferentially absorbed. The fixed ion concentration $C_R$ inside the membrane creates an electric potential (Donnan potential) at the interface that repels co-ions.

Mathematically, for a binary electrolyte AB: $\bar{C}_+ \cdot \bar{C}_- = C_s^2$, and in the dilute limit ($\bar{C}_- \ll C_R$), $\bar{C}_- \approx C_s^2 / C_R \to 0$, demonstrating near-complete exclusion. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: State the Nernst-Planck equation. What are the three mechanisms it represents? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Nernst-Planck equation describes the total flux of an ionic species $i$ under the combined action of three driving forces:

$$
J_i = -D_i \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + C_i v
$$

The three mechanisms are:
1. **Diffusion** — driven by concentration gradient ($-D_i \partial C_i / \partial x$).
2. **Electromigration (Migration)** — driven by electric potential gradient ($-z_i F D_i C_i / (RT) \cdot \partial \phi / \partial x$).
3. **Convection** — bulk fluid flow carrying the ion along ($C_i v$).

This equation is fundamental to the analysis of electrodialysis, ion-exchange processes, and membrane transport. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]

#### (a) Derive the Nernst-Planck equation starting from the electrochemical potential. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Electrochemical potential:**
The electrochemical potential of ion $i$ is:

$$
\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i + z_i F \phi
$$

**Step 2 — Thermodynamic driving force:**
The force per mole along $x$ is:

$$
F_{\text{drive}, i} = -\frac{d\mu_i}{dx} = -\frac{RT}{a_i}\frac{da_i}{dx} - z_i F \frac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear constitutive law (Nernst-Planck assumption):**
The flux is proportional to the driving force:

$$
J_i = C_i u_i F_{\text{drive}, i}
$$

**Step 4 — Nernst-Einstein relation:**

$$
u_i = \frac{D_i \vert z_i \vert F}{RT}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitution (assume ideal dilute: $a_i \approx C_i$):**

$$
J_i = -C_i \cdot \frac{D_i \vert z_i \vert F}{RT} \cdot \frac{RT}{C_i} \frac{dC_i}{dx} - C_i \cdot \frac{D_i \vert z_i \vert F}{RT} \cdot z_i F \frac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

$$
J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx}
$$

**Step 6 — Add convection $C_i v$:**

$$
\boxed{\,J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx} + C_i v\,}
$$

**Assumptions:**
- Isothermal, isobaric system.
- Ideal dilute solution ($a_i \approx C_i$).
- Nernst-Einstein relation is valid.
- No chemical reactions involving ion $i$.
- One-dimensional transport.

(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1-6 + 1 for assumptions)

#### (b) A cation-exchange membrane with $C_R = 1200\ \text{mol/m}^3$ is in equilibrium with a 50 mM NaCl solution. Calculate the co-ion (Cl⁻) and counter-ion (Na⁺) concentrations inside the membrane. Comment on the selectivity. (7 marks)

**Given:** $C_R = 1200\ \text{mol/m}^3$, $C_s = 50\ \text{mM} = 50\ \text{mol/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Donnan product inside membrane:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} \cdot \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = C_s^2 = 2500
$$

**Step 2 — Electroneutrality inside membrane:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} = \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} + C_R
$$

**Step 3 — Quadratic in $\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-}$:**

$$
\left( \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} + 1200 \right) \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = 2500
$$

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-}^2 + 1200 \bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} - 2500 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = \frac{-1200 + \sqrt{1200^2 + 4 \times 2500}}{2} = \frac{-1200 + \sqrt{1{,}450{,}000}}{2}
$$

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Cl}^-} = \frac{-1200 + 1204.16}{2} = \frac{4.16}{2} = 2.08\ \text{mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Find counter-ion concentration:**

$$
\bar{C}_{\text{Na}^+} = 2.08 + 1200 = 1202.08\ \text{mol/m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on selectivity:**
The co-ion concentration inside the membrane is **~24× lower** than in the bulk solution (50 → 2.08 mol/m³), demonstrating strong Donnan exclusion. The membrane exhibits **high selectivity** for Na⁺ over Cl⁻ — the transport number of Na⁺ will be close to unity.

(7 marks: 2 marks quadratic setup, 2 marks arithmetic, 1 mark counter-ion calculation, 2 marks physical interpretation)

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3] (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Explain the Teorell-Meyer-Siever (TMS) model for membrane potential. Derive the membrane potential equation for a binary electrolyte system. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Concept of TMS model:**
The TMS (or TMS) model treats the ion-exchange membrane as a **uniform phase** of fixed charge density $C_R$, sandwiched between two solutions of concentrations $C_1$ and $C_2$. The total membrane potential has **three contributions**:

$$
\Delta \phi_{\text{total}} = \Delta \phi_{\text{Donnan,1}} + \Delta \phi_{\text{diff,int}} + \Delta \phi_{\text{Donnan,2}}
$$

where:
- $\Delta \phi_{\text{Donnan}}$ = potential jumps at the two membrane-solution interfaces.
- $\Delta \phi_{\text{diff,int}}$ = diffusion potential inside the membrane phase.

**Step 2 — Donnan potential at interface 1:**

$$
\Delta \phi_{\text{Donnan,1}} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{C_1}{\bar{C}_{+}^{(1)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Donnan potential at interface 2 (similar).**

**Step 4 — Internal diffusion potential (derived from $J_+ = -J_-$, no net current):**
By setting $J_+ + J_- = 0$ (zero current condition) and using the Nernst-Planck equation with electroneutrality, we derived:

$$
\Delta \phi_{\text{diff,int}} = \frac{RT}{F} (2 t_+ - 1) \ln \frac{\bar{C}_{+}^{(1)}}{\bar{C}_{+}^{(2)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine all three (and use $\bar{C}_{+}^{(1)} \approx C_1$ and $\bar{C}_{+}^{(2)} \approx C_2$ in the limit of a perfect membrane):**

$$
\boxed{\, \Delta \phi = \frac{RT}{F} (2 t_+ - 1) \ln \frac{C_1}{C_2} \,}
$$

**Physical meaning:** The membrane potential is a direct measure of the **permselectivity** of the membrane. For a perfect cation-selective membrane ($t_+ = 1$), it equals the Nernst potential $\frac{RT}{F} \ln(C_1/C_2)$.

(7 marks: 1 mark concept, 2 marks Donnan potentials, 2 marks internal derivation, 2 marks final equation + physical meaning)

#### (b) An electrodialysis stack is used to desalt a 0.05 M NaCl feed at 25 °C using 100 cell pairs. Each membrane area is 0.5 m². The applied current is 30 A. Given $t_+ = 0.95$ for CEM, $t_- = 0.95$ for AEM, and current efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, calculate the salt removal rate. (7 marks)

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.05\ \text{M}$, $T = 298\ \text{K}$, $N_{\text{pairs}} = 100$, $A = 0.5\ \text{m}^2$, $I = 30\ \text{A}$, $t_{\text{CEM}} = 0.95$, $t_{\text{AEM}} = 0.95$, $\eta = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Faraday's law for moles of electrons:**

$$
\dot{n}_{e^-} = \frac{I}{F} = \frac{30}{96485} = 3.11 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/s}
$$

**Step 2 — NaCl transported per electron pair (CEM carries Na⁺ out, AEM carries Cl⁻ out):**

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{NaCl, ideal}} = \dot{n}_{e^-} \times \text{(one mole NaCl per equivalent of charge)} = 3.11 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective transport number for NaCl in cell pair:**
The current utilization for salt transport in a cell pair:

$$
\bar{t} = t_{\text{CEM}} + t_{\text{AEM}} - 1 = 0.95 + 0.95 - 1 = 0.90
$$

**Step 4 — Actual salt flux per cell pair (with current efficiency):**

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{NaCl, actual}} = \bar{t} \cdot \eta \cdot \dot{n}_{e^-} = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 3.11 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{NaCl, actual}} = 2.38 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/s per cell pair}
$$

**Step 5 — Total stack salt removal rate:**

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{total}} = 2.38 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 = 2.38 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Express in convenient units:**

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{total}} = 2.38 \times 10^{-2} \times 58.44\ \text{g/mol} = 1.39\ \text{g/s} \approx 5.0\ \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Salt removal rate} = 2.38 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol/s} \approx 5.0\ \text{kg/h}\,}
$$

(7 marks: 1 mark Faraday's law, 1 mark transport number combination, 1 mark current efficiency application, 2 marks arithmetic, 2 marks final numerical answer with units)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks in Ion Movement Theory questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors in the Nernst-Planck equation:** The migration term is $-z_i F D_i C_i / (RT) \cdot d\phi/dx$. Many students drop the negative sign. **Memorize it as a single block.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $C_R$ (fixed ion concentration) with $C_s$ (bulk solution concentration):** In Donnan problems, $C_R$ is a property of the **membrane**, while $C_s$ is a property of the **solution**. They are not interchangeable. Watch for "mol/m³" vs "M" — convert!
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $2 t_+ - 1$ factor in the TMS equation:** The membrane potential is **not** simply $\frac{RT}{F} \ln(C_1/C_2)$ — it is multiplied by $(2t_+ - 1)$. Writing this incorrectly will cost you 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Activity vs concentration:** The Donnan equation strictly uses **activities** $a_i$. If $C_s$ is high (>0.1 M), use activity coefficients. For dilute solutions, $a_i \approx C_i$ is acceptable.
>
> 5. **Units of concentration:** KTU problems often give concentrations in **mol/L (M)** but the equations expect **mol/m³**. Always multiply M by 1000 to get mol/m³.
>
> 6. **Membrane potential sign:** When asked to find the membrane potential, **state the sign explicitly** and **interpret the sign physically** (e.g., "negative sign indicates the membrane interior is at lower potential, consistent with a cation-exchange membrane"). Examiners award 1–2 marks for interpretation alone.
>
> 7. **Current efficiency definition:** Some students forget the $\eta$ factor when computing salt removal in electrodialysis. The Faraday law gives the **maximum** salt transport; real transport is always less.
>
> 8. **Nernst-Einstein relation:** Don't write $D_i = u_i RT$ — the **$F$** and $|z_i|$ belong in the denominator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Ion Movement Theory**

- The **Nernst-Planck equation** is the master flux equation: $J_i = -D_i (\partial C_i / \partial x) - (z_i F D_i C_i / RT)(\partial \phi / \partial x) + C_i v$.
- Three driving forces: **diffusion, migration, convection**. Memorize the equation in one block.
- **Donnan equilibrium** at the membrane-solution interface gives $\bar{C}_+ \cdot \bar{C}_- = C_s^2$.
- **Donnan exclusion** in the dilute limit: $\bar{C}_{\text{co-ion}} \approx C_s^2 / C_R$.
- **Membrane potential (TMS equation):** $\Delta \phi = (RT / F)(2 t_+ - 1) \ln(C_1 / C_2)$.
- **Selectivity coefficient** $K_{A/B} > 1$ means the resin prefers ion A.
- **Transport number** $t_i$ is the fraction of current carried by ion $i$; $\sum t_i = 1$.
- **Nernst-Einstein relation:** $D_i = u_i RT / (|z_i| F)$.
- **Nafion/PEM** in fuel cells: only H⁺ passes; this is **Donnan exclusion in action**.
- **Electrodialysis salt removal rate:** $\dot{n}_{\text{NaCl}} = (t_{\text{CEM}} + t_{\text{AEM}} - 1) \cdot \eta \cdot (I/F) \cdot N_{\text{pairs}}$.
- **Concentration polarization** occurs when $C_{\text{surface}} \to 0$ (limiting current density).
- **Fixed ion concentration** $C_R$ determines **selectivity** — higher $C_R$ means stronger co-ion exclusion.
- **Limiting cases to remember:**
  - $C_s \ll C_R$: $\bar{C}_+ \approx C_R$, $\bar{C}_- \to 0$ (strong exclusion).
  - $C_s \gg C_R$: $\bar{C}_+ \approx \bar{C}_- \approx C_s$ (no exclusion; membrane "fails").
- **Membrane potential sign convention:** Always state and interpret the sign.
- **Activity vs concentration:** For $C_s < 0.1$ M, $a_i \approx C_i$ is acceptable.
- **Current efficiency** $\eta < 1$ accounts for water splitting, back-diffusion, and parasitic losses.
- **Ohm's law in ED:** Voltage drop = $IR$, energy = $I^2 R \Delta t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Memorization Trigger:** If you can write the **Nernst-Planck equation**, the **Donnan exclusion condition** ($\bar{C}_+ \bar{C}_- = C_s^2$), and the **TMS membrane potential** formula on a blank sheet within 60 seconds, you are **exam-ready** for this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications Without Exchange: Ion Exclusion
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications Without Exchange: Ion Exclusion

# 📘 Module 3 — Applications Without Exchange: Ion Exclusion

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping**
> This topic belongs to **Module 3** of PECHT756 and directly addresses **Course Outcome CO3** — *Apply novel separation techniques for non-reactive and reactive mass transfer operations.* Ion Exclusion is classified under **"Applications Without Exchange"**, meaning the ion-exchange resin is used as a *physical medium*, not as a *chemical reactant*.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ION EXCLUSION (Definition — KTU Board Standard)**
> **Ion Exclusion** is a *non-ionic chromatographic separation process* in which an **ion-exchange resin** is used to separate an **electrolyte** (e.g., NaCl) from a **non-electrolyte** (e.g., glycerol, sucrose, ethylene glycol) based on the **electrostatic (Donnan) repulsion** between the **co-ions** in the feed and the **fixed ionic groups** chemically bound to the resin matrix. The resin does *not* undergo any actual ion exchange; instead, the ionic species are *excluded* from the resin pores and elute first, while the non-ionic species freely partition into the resin and elute later.

In this technique, the **resin is used in a passive physical role** — it acts as a *molecular sieve with an electrostatic gate*, separating ions from neutral molecules by exploiting differences in their ability to enter the resin's internal pore solution.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning in Ion Exclusion Context |
|---|---|
| **Resin** | A cation-exchange (or anion-exchange) resin, normally in a salt-form such as Na⁺ or Cl⁻ |
| **Co-ions** | Ions in solution having the *same charge* as the fixed groups on the resin (e.g., Cl⁻ on a cation resin) |
| **Counter-ions** | Ions in solution having the *opposite* charge to the fixed groups (e.g., Na⁺ on a cation resin) |
| **Non-electrolyte** | A neutral, undissociated molecule (e.g., sugar, glycerol) |
| **Donnan Exclusion** | The phenomenon by which co-ions are electrostatically repelled from the resin phase |
| **Eluate** | The liquid leaving the chromatographic column |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Club" 🏛️

Imagine a **nightclub** with a strict **bouncer at the entrance (the ion-exchange resin)**.

- The bouncer has a **tattoo of a particular gang symbol** (the fixed ionic charge, e.g., negative SO₃⁻ groups on a cation resin).
- **People wearing the same gang colors (co-ions, e.g., Cl⁻)** are *refused entry* — they must wait outside and pass through the column first.
- **People wearing rival colors (counter-ions, e.g., Na⁺)** are *admitted* — but they are limited in number to maintain electrical neutrality (Donnan balance).
- **People with no gang colors at all (neutral molecules, e.g., glycerol)** stroll in *unhindered* and stay inside the club for a long time, eventually exiting much later.

> **Result:** The "gang members" (ions) leave the column **first**, while the "neutral guests" (non-electrolytes) leave **last**. The bouncer (resin) didn't *swap* anyone — he just *sorted* them.

This is precisely how **Ion Exclusion** achieves separation — through **selective electrostatic exclusion**, not chemical exchange.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Resin Particle Size:** typically **0.3 – 1.2 mm** (16–50 mesh)
- **Resin Cross-linkage:** usually **4% – 8% DVB** (divinylbenzene)
- **Column L/D Ratio:** commonly **> 20** for good resolution
- **Operating Temperature:** **20 °C – 60 °C**
- **Linear Flow Rate:** **0.5 – 2.0 cm/min**
- **Resin Capacity (theoretical):** **~5 meq/g** (dry resin), **~2 meq/mL** (wet resin)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Metrics Matter (KTU Examiner Insight)**
> The **cross-linkage (X% DVB)** is the *most critical* parameter: higher DVB ⇒ tighter pores ⇒ better ion exclusion but slower diffusion of non-electrolytes. This trade-off is a **favourite KTU question area**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Donnan Membrane Equilibrium — Theoretical Foundation

The principle of ion exclusion rests on the **Donnan Equilibrium (1911)**, which describes the distribution of ions across a *permselective membrane* — in this case, the **ion-exchange resin bead** acts as such a membrane because the fixed ionic groups cannot leave the resin phase.

### 2.1.1 The Donnan Equilibrium Condition

For a cation-exchange resin in Na⁺ form immersed in a NaCl solution, the **electrochemical potential** of Na⁺ and Cl⁻ must be equal in both the resin and external phases at equilibrium:

$$\mu_{Na^+}^{resin} = \mu_{Na^+}^{solution}$$

$$\mu_{Cl^-}^{resin} = \mu_{Cl^-}^{solution}$$

This leads to the **Donnan product equality**:

$$\left[ Na^+ \right]_{resin} \cdot \left[ Cl^- \right]_{resin} = \left[ Na^+ \right]_{solution}^2$$

where the external solution is electroneutral ($[Na^+] = [Cl^-]$).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Ion Exclusion — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept Chain**
> 1. **Step 1 — Fixed Charges:** The resin has *covalently bonded* anionic groups (e.g., -SO₃⁻) → the resin is *impermeable* to free anions (co-ions).
> 2. **Step 2 — Electroneutrality:** The solution inside the resin pores must remain electroneutral.
> 3. **Step 3 — Concentration Inequality:** This forces $[Cl^-]_{resin} \ll [Cl^-]_{solution}$.
> 4. **Step 4 — Exclusion:** The co-ions (Cl⁻) are *excluded* from the resin; they travel through the column only via the **void (interstitial) volume** between resin beads.
> 5. **Step 5 — Partitioning of Non-electrolytes:** Neutral molecules (glycerol, sucrose) face *no electrostatic barrier* and freely partition into the resin pores governed by simple **sorption isotherms**.
> 6. **Step 6 — Differential Elution:** Since non-electrolytes are *retarded* by their sorption into the resin, they elute *after* the excluded ions.

### 2.3 The Distribution Coefficient — Master Equation

The most important KTU-level equation for ion exclusion is the **Distribution Coefficient** $K_d$ for the non-electrolyte:

$$K_d = \frac{C_{resin}}{C_{solution}} = \frac{\bar{C}}{C}$$

where:
- $C_{resin} = \bar{C}$ = concentration of solute *inside* the resin phase
- $C_{solution} = C$ = concentration of solute in the external liquid

- For **electrolytes (NaCl):** $K_d \approx 0$ (fully excluded)
- For **non-electrolytes (glycerol):** $K_d > 0$ (often 0.3 – 0.8)

### 2.4 Breakthrough Curve Analysis

In a packed chromatographic column, the **breakthrough curve** describes how the effluent concentration of each species changes with time (or volume of eluent).

**Definition of Key Terms:**
- $V_b$ = breakthrough volume (volume of eluent when effluent concentration reaches, say, **5%** of inlet)
- $V_s$ = saturation volume (when effluent concentration = inlet concentration, **100%**)
- $V_d$ = dead volume (interstitial volume between beads)

**Schematic Elution Order (Cation Resin, Na⁺ form):**

| Order | Species | Where It Spends Time | Kd |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1st | **NaCl (electrolyte)** | Interstitial voids only | ≈ 0 |
| 2nd | **Glycerol / Sucrose (non-electrolyte)** | Resin pores + voids | 0.3 – 0.8 |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Description | Units | Use in KTU Problems |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | **Donnan Product** | $[Na^+]_r [Cl^-]_r = [Na^+]_s^2$ | (mol/m³)² | Calculate resin-phase co-ion conc. |
| 2 | **Distribution Coefficient** | $K_d = \bar{C}/C$ | dimensionless | Compare relative retention |
| 3 | **Selectivity Factor** | $\alpha = K_d^{non-elec} / K_d^{elec}$ | dimensionless | Measure separation efficiency |
| 4 | **Breakthrough Volume** | $V_b = V_d + V_r \cdot K_d$ | m³ | Design column cycle time |
| 5 | **Interstitial Volume** | $V_d = \varepsilon \cdot V_{col}$ | m³ | Liquid outside the resin |
| 6 | **Resin Pore Volume** | $V_r = (1-\varepsilon) \cdot \epsilon_p \cdot V_{col}$ | m³ | Liquid inside the resin |
| 7 | **Plate Height (HETP)** | $H = L/N$ | m | Measure column efficiency |
| 8 | **Resolution** | $R_s = \dfrac{\Delta V}{0.5(w_1 + w_2)}$ | dimensionless | Quantify peak separation |
| 9 | **Number of Theoretical Plates** | $N = 16(t_R/w_b)^2$ | dimensionless | KTU-favourite calculation |
| 10 | **External Phase Conc. of Co-ion** | $[Cl^-]_r = [Na^+]_s^2 / Q$ | mol/m³ | Explicit Donnan calculation |

In the last row, **Q** is the **fixed ion concentration** of the resin (mol/m³ of wet resin), typically $Q \approx 1500 - 2500 \text{ mol/m}^3$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ion exclusion is a **workhorse unit operation** in the following industries:

1. **Glycerol Purification** — removes NaCl from crude glycerol (a classical application).
2. **Sugar Refining** — desalting of sucrose or fructose syrups.
3. **Ethylene Glycol Production** — removing NaCl catalyst residue.
4. **Formic Acid / Acetic Acid Recovery** — separating acids from inorganic salts.
5. **Desalination of Fermentation Broths** — in bio-ethanol and citric acid plants.
6. **Water Softening Without Salt** — partial desalination in pharmaceutical water systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Viva**
> Examiners often ask: *"Why use ion exclusion rather than conventional ion exchange?"*
> **Answer:** Ion exclusion **does not consume the resin** — the resin is regenerated *automatically by water wash*, no acid/alkali is required, no chemical waste is generated, and the process is **continuous and isothermal**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Donnan Equilibrium for NaCl with a Cation Resin (Na⁺ form)

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**. We derive the resin-phase co-ion concentration explicitly.

**Given:**
- A cation-exchange resin in **Na⁺ form** (fixed anion = -SO₃⁻)
- External solution: **NaCl** at concentration $C_s$ (mol/m³)
- Resin fixed-ion concentration: $Q$ (mol/m³ of wet resin)
- All activities approximated by concentrations (dilute solution)

**Required:** Find the concentration of Cl⁻ *inside* the resin, $[Cl^-]_r$.

### Step 1 — Define the variables

Let:
- $[Na^+]_r$ = sodium ion concentration *inside* the resin
- $[Cl^-]_r$ = chloride ion concentration *inside* the resin
- $[Na^+]_s = [Cl^-]_s = C_s$ = external concentrations (electroneutrality in solution)
- $Q$ = fixed negative charge of the resin (constant)

### Step 2 — Apply Electroneutrality in the Resin Phase

Inside the resin, the sum of all positive charges must equal the sum of all negative charges:

$$[Na^+]_r = [Cl^-]_r + Q$$

> **Logic:** The resin has $Q$ mol/m³ of *fixed* negative charges (e.g., -SO₃⁻), which must be balanced by the *mobile* cations. Any extra anions (Cl⁻) would disturb this balance.

### Step 3 — Apply the Donnan Product Equality

For NaCl in equilibrium, the product of cation and anion activities must be equal in both phases:

$$[Na^+]_r \cdot [Cl^-]_r = [Na^+]_s \cdot [Cl^-]_s = C_s^2$$

> **Logic:** This arises from equating the *electrochemical potentials* of each ion across the resin-solution interface; the Donnan potential cancels.

### Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2

From Step 2: $[Na^+]_r = [Cl^-]_r + Q$.

Substitute into Step 3:

$$\left( [Cl^-]_r + Q \right) \cdot [Cl^-]_r = C_s^2$$

### Step 5 — Form the Quadratic Equation

Expanding:

$$[Cl^-]_r^2 + Q \cdot [Cl^-]_r - C_s^2 = 0$$

This is a standard quadratic in $[Cl^-]_r$.

### Step 6 — Solve the Quadratic

Using the quadratic formula with $a = 1$, $b = Q$, $c = -C_s^2$:

$$[Cl^-]_r = \frac{-Q \pm \sqrt{Q^2 + 4C_s^2}}{2}$$

Since concentration must be positive:

$$[Cl^-]_r = \frac{-Q + \sqrt{Q^2 + 4C_s^2}}{2}$$

### Step 7 — Analyze the Limiting Case (Highly Cross-linked / Dilute Solution)

When $Q \gg C_s$ (typical for ion exclusion operation):

$$\sqrt{Q^2 + 4C_s^2} \approx Q \cdot \sqrt{1 + 4C_s^2/Q^2} \approx Q + \frac{2C_s^2}{Q}$$

Substituting:

$$[Cl^-]_r \approx \frac{-Q + Q + \dfrac{2C_s^2}{Q}}{2} = \frac{C_s^2}{Q}$$

**Final Approximate Result:**

$$\boxed{\, [Cl^-]_r \approx \dfrac{C_s^2}{Q} \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Interpretation**
> Since $Q \gg C_s$, we have $[Cl^-]_r \ll C_s$, i.e., **the co-ion (Cl⁻) is largely excluded from the resin.** This is the *quantitative proof* of ion exclusion.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:**
A cation-exchange resin in Na⁺ form has a fixed ion concentration $Q = 2000 \text{ mol/m}^3$ (wet). It is contacted with an NaCl solution at $C_s = 100 \text{ mol/m}^3$. Calculate:
1. The Cl⁻ concentration inside the resin (exact).
2. The Cl⁻ concentration inside the resin (using the approximation).
3. The Donnan exclusion factor $\beta = [Cl^-]_r / C_s$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Exact calculation:**

$$[Cl^-]_r = \frac{-2000 + \sqrt{2000^2 + 4 \times 100^2}}{2}$$

Calculate the discriminant:

$$\sqrt{4{,}000{,}000 + 40{,}000} = \sqrt{4{,}040{,}000} \approx 2010.0$$

$$[Cl^-]_r = \frac{-2000 + 2010.0}{2} = \frac{10.0}{2} = 5.0 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step (b) — Approximate calculation:**

$$[Cl^-]_r \approx \frac{C_s^2}{Q} = \frac{100^2}{2000} = \frac{10{,}000}{2000} = 5.0 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

> **Note:** The approximation is *excellent* when $Q \gg C_s$ — this is the regime ion exclusion operates in.

**Step (c) — Exclusion factor:**

$$\beta = \frac{[Cl^-]_r}{C_s} = \frac{5.0}{100} = 0.05 \quad \text{or} \quad 5\%$$

This means **95% of the Cl⁻ is excluded** from the resin — confirming the strong exclusion effect.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Species Behavior

| Property | Electrolyte (NaCl) | Non-Electrolyte (Glycerol) |
|---|---|---|
| **Charge** | Ionic (Na⁺ + Cl⁻) | Neutral |
| **Behavior in Resin** | Co-ion excluded by Donnan effect | Partitions freely into pores |
| **Distribution Coefficient $K_d$** | ≈ 0 | 0.3 – 0.8 |
| **Elution Order** | **First** (early) | **Last** (late) |
| **Residence Time** | Through interstitial voids only | Voids + resin pore volume |
| **Governing Equation** | Donnan product equality | Linear / Freundlich isotherm |
| **Effect of Cross-linkage ↑** | $K_d$ remains ≈ 0 | $K_d$ **decreases** (smaller pores) |
| **Effect of Temperature ↑** | Exclusion improves slightly | $K_d$ **decreases** (exothermic sorption) |

### 3.4 Resin Mass Balance — Deriving the Breakthrough Volume

The breakthrough volume $V_b$ for any species eluting from a column is given by:

$$V_b = V_{void} + K_d \cdot V_{resin}$$

where $V_{void}$ is the **inter-particle (interstitial) volume** and $V_{resin}$ is the **intra-particle (pore) volume**.

**Detailed definitions:**

$$V_{void} = \varepsilon \cdot V_{col}$$

$$V_{resin} = (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \epsilon_p \cdot V_{col}$$

Combining:

$$V_b = \varepsilon \cdot V_{col} + K_d \cdot (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \epsilon_p \cdot V_{col}$$

$$\boxed{\, V_b = V_{col} \left[ \varepsilon + K_d (1 - \varepsilon)\epsilon_p \right] \,}$$

**Symbols:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{col}$ | Total column volume | design variable |
| $\varepsilon$ | External (inter-particle) porosity | 0.35 – 0.45 |
| $\epsilon_p$ | Internal (intra-particle) porosity | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| $K_d$ | Distribution coefficient | 0 (electrolyte) to ~1 (organic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — Why $K_d$ is Key**
> Setting $K_d = 0$ for the electrolyte gives $V_b^{electrolyte} = \varepsilon \cdot V_{col}$, which is the *minimum* possible elution volume (just the interstitial voids). This is the **physical lower bound** of when an electrolyte can elute from an ion-exclusion column.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Ion Exclusion Process (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Mixture: Electrolyte + Non-Electrolyte in Water] --> B[Resin Column: Cation Resin in Na+ Form]
    B --> C{Charged State Check at Pore Entrance}
    C -- Co-ions (e.g., Cl-) --> D[Electrostatic Repulsion: EXCLUDED from Pores]
    C -- Non-electrolytes (e.g., glycerol) --> E[Freely Diffuse INTO Resin Pores]
    C -- Counter-ions (e.g., Na+) --> F[Limited Entry to Maintain Electroneutrality]
    D --> G[Elute FIRST in Interstitial Volume]
    E --> H[Retained in Pore Volume; Elute LATER]
    F --> I[Partitioned to Balance Charges; Co-elute with Cl-]
    G --> J[Collected: Electrolyte-rich Fraction]
    H --> K[Collected: Non-electrolyte-rich Fraction]
    I --> J
    J --> L[Pure Non-electrolyte Product: Glycerol or Sugar]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Water Wash: Resin Regenerated Automatically]
    M --> B
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **central decision node** is the "Charged State Check" — this is the conceptual core of ion exclusion.
> - Co-ions are *excluded* and elute first; non-electrolytes are *retained* and elute later — this gives the separation.
> - The **water-wash regeneration loop** (bottom arrow) is a key advantage over conventional ion exchange.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Column Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ResinColumn[Chromatographic Column]
        direction LR
        F1[Bead 1: -SO3- fixed]:::resin
        F2[Bead 2: -SO3- fixed]:::resin
        F3[Bead 3: -SO3- fixed]:::resin
        F4[Bead 4: -SO3- fixed]:::resin
    end
    classDef resin fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    FeedStream[Feed: NaCl + Glycerol] --> ResinColumn
    ResinColumn --> Out1[Early Eluate: NaCl-rich]
    ResinColumn --> Out2[Late Eluate: Glycerol-rich]
    Out1 --> TankA[Tank A: Electrolyte]
    Out2 --> TankB[Tank B: Product]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — Elution Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time 0: Feed Pulse]:::t0 --> T1[Time 1: Interstitial Elution Begins]:::t1
    T1 --> T2[Time 2: NaCl Peak Elutes]:::t2
    T2 --> T3[Time 3: Column Washed with Water]:::t3
    T3 --> T4[Time 4: Glycerol Peak Elutes]:::t4
    T4 --> T5[Time 5: Cycle Restart]:::t5
    classDef t0 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    classDef t1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    classDef t2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    classDef t3 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    classDef t4 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    classDef t5 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
```

> **Interpretation:** The **green node (T2)** is the electrolyte peak; the **purple node (T4)** is the non-electrolyte peak. The time gap (Δt = T4 − T2) is the **separation window** — a direct measure of the effectiveness of ion exclusion.

## 4.4 Schematic Concentration Profile (Mermaid XY-Plot Approximation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Profile[Elution Profile: C vs. V]
        direction LR
        P1[Region I: Pure NaCl Peak]:::peak1
        P2[Region II: Valley - Low Concentration]:::valley
        P3[Region III: Pure Glycerol Peak]:::peak2
        P1 --- P2 --- P3
    end
    classDef peak1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    classDef peak2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    classDef valley fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

> The **valley** between the two peaks is the *key* to good separation — a deep valley means *baseline resolution*; a shallow valley means *overlap* and *poor purity*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Define ion exclusion. Mention any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Ion exclusion is a non-ionic chromatographic separation process in which an ion-exchange resin is used to separate an electrolyte from a non-electrolyte. The separation is based on the Donnan exclusion effect, where co-ions are electrostatically repelled from the resin pores and elute first, while the non-ionic species are retained in the resin and elute later.
>
> **Applications (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. Desalination of glycerol (removing NaCl from crude glycerol).
> 2. Desalting of sugar syrups (sucrose/fructose).
> 3. Purification of ethylene glycol.
> 4. Recovery of organic acids from inorganic salt mixtures.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is the role of Donnan equilibrium in ion exclusion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Donnan equilibrium describes the distribution of ions across a permselective boundary (the ion-exchange resin bead). It states that the **product of co-ion and counter-ion concentrations inside the resin must equal the square of the external salt concentration** (for a 1:1 electrolyte). Because the resin carries fixed ionic charges (e.g., -SO₃⁻), electroneutrality forces the **co-ion concentration inside the resin to be much lower than in the external solution** — this is the *Donnan exclusion* of co-ions. In ion exclusion, this effect prevents ionic species from entering the resin, while neutral molecules are not subject to the Donnan barrier, hence the separation is achieved.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, each 14-mark question has *two sub-parts* (typically 7 + 7 marks). Below we provide **Question A** and **Question B** as the two internal-choice options.

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) Derive an expression for the concentration of co-ion (Cl⁻) inside a cation-exchange resin in Na⁺ form, in equilibrium with an external NaCl solution. State clearly the assumptions made.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A cation-exchange resin in Na⁺ form with $Q = 2200 \text{ mol/m}^3$ (wet) is used in an ion-exclusion column to separate NaCl from glycerol. The external NaCl concentration is $C_s = 50 \text{ mol/m}^3$. Calculate:**
  - **(i) The Cl⁻ concentration inside the resin (exact).** **(2 marks)**
  - **(ii) The Cl⁻ concentration inside the resin (using the approximation for $Q \gg C_s$).** **(2 marks)**
  - **(iii) The Donnan exclusion factor, defined as the percentage of Cl⁻ excluded.** **(1 mark)**
  - **(iv) Comment on whether ion exclusion is effective in this case.** **(2 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The solution is dilute → concentrations approximate activities.
2. The resin is monofunctional with fixed anionic groups (-SO₃⁻) → fixed charge = $Q$.
3. Only NaCl is the external electrolyte (1:1 type).
4. The system is at equilibrium and electroneutrality holds in both phases.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Electroneutrality in the resin phase:**

$$[Na^+]_r = [Cl^-]_r + Q$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Donnan product equality:**

$$[Na^+]_r \cdot [Cl^-]_r = [Na^+]_s \cdot [Cl^-]_s = C_s^2$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Substitution forming the quadratic:**

$$([Cl^-]_r + Q) \cdot [Cl^-]_r = C_s^2$$

$$[Cl^-]_r^2 + Q[Cl^-]_r - C_s^2 = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Solving the quadratic:**

$$[Cl^-]_r = \frac{-Q + \sqrt{Q^2 + 4C_s^2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Approximate result when $Q \gg C_s$:**

$$[Cl^-]_r \approx \frac{C_s^2}{Q}$$

**Final statement (1 mark):** Since $Q \gg C_s$, $[Cl^-]_r \ll C_s$, confirming that the co-ion is largely excluded from the resin phase — the quantitative basis of ion exclusion.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Exact calculation (2 marks):**

$$[Cl^-]_r = \frac{-2200 + \sqrt{2200^2 + 4 \times 50^2}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{-2200 + \sqrt{4{,}840{,}000 + 10{,}000}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{-2200 + \sqrt{4{,}850{,}000}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{-2200 + 2202.27}{2}$$

$$= \frac{2.27}{2} = 1.135 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**(ii) Approximate calculation (2 marks):**

$$[Cl^-]_r \approx \frac{C_s^2}{Q} = \frac{50^2}{2200} = \frac{2500}{2200} \approx 1.136 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

> **Excellent agreement** with the exact result.

**(iii) Exclusion factor (1 mark):**

$$\beta = \frac{[Cl^-]_r}{C_s} = \frac{1.135}{50} = 0.0227 = 2.27\%$$

**Exclusion percentage:**

$$100\% - 2.27\% = 97.73\% \text{ excluded}$$

**(iv) Comment on effectiveness (2 marks):**

> Since **97.73%** of the Cl⁻ is excluded from the resin, ion exclusion is **highly effective** in this case. The condition $Q \gg C_s$ is well satisfied ($2200 \gg 50$, ratio = 44), so the Donnan exclusion is strong. The non-electrolyte (glycerol) will have a $K_d$ of 0.3–0.8, leading to a *significant* retention time difference from the electrolyte. **Conclusion:** This is an *ideal* operating condition for ion exclusion.

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) With the help of a neat elution profile, explain the separation of NaCl–glycerol mixture using a cation-exchange resin in Na⁺ form. Discuss the role of cross-linkage (DVB%) and particle size on the separation efficiency.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A 1.5 m tall chromatographic column of internal diameter 0.10 m is packed with a cation resin ($Q = 1800 \text{ mol/m}^3$, external porosity $\varepsilon = 0.40$, internal porosity $\epsilon_p = 0.55$). The distribution coefficient of glycerol is $K_d = 0.65$ and that of NaCl is approximately 0.**
  - **(i) Calculate the breakthrough volume of NaCl.** **(2 marks)**
  - **(ii) Calculate the breakthrough volume of glycerol.** **(2 marks)**
  - **(iii) Calculate the volume of eluent (water) needed to fully separate the two components, defined as the difference between the two breakthrough volumes.** **(2 marks)**
  - **(iv) If the resin cross-linkage is increased from 4% to 12% DVB, qualitatively predict the effect on $K_d^{glycerol}$ and explain the trade-off involved.** **(1 mark)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B

**Part (a) — Elution Profile Explanation (7 marks):**

**Description of the elution profile (3 marks):**
- A pulse of feed (NaCl + glycerol in water) is injected at the top of the column.
- The column is then eluted with pure water (isocratic elution).
- Two distinct peaks are observed in the effluent:
  - **First peak (NaCl):** Elutes *first* at the breakthrough volume corresponding only to the interstitial void volume (since $K_d \approx 0$).
  - **Second peak (Glycerol):** Elutes *later* at a higher breakthrough volume, because it partitions into the resin pores ($K_d > 0$).
- The **gap between the peaks** is the *separation window* — sufficient to collect each component in pure form.

**Role of cross-linkage (DVB%) (2 marks):**
- **Higher DVB%** → tighter resin matrix → smaller pores → *better* Donnan exclusion (co-ions excluded more strongly).
- **However**, tighter pores *reduce* the diffusion rate of larger non-electrolyte molecules → **lower $K_d$** for glycerol → glycerol elutes *earlier* → *reduced* separation window.
- **Trade-off:** There is an **optimum DVB% (typically 6–8%)** that balances good exclusion and adequate non-electrolyte retention.

**Role of particle size (2 marks):**
- **Smaller particles** → shorter diffusion path → less band-broadening → sharper peaks → *better* resolution.
- **Larger particles** → broader peaks → poorer resolution, but *lower* pressure drop.
- **Optimum particle size:** **0.3–0.6 mm** in industrial ion-exclusion columns.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Column height: $L = 1.5$ m
- Column internal diameter: $D = 0.10$ m
- Total column volume: $V_{col} = \pi (D/2)^2 \cdot L = \pi \times (0.05)^2 \times 1.5 = 0.01178 \text{ m}^3$ (or 11.78 L)
- External porosity: $\varepsilon = 0.40$
- Internal porosity: $\epsilon_p = 0.55$
- $K_d^{NaCl} \approx 0$
- $K_d^{glycerol} = 0.65$

**Pre-calculation of sub-volumes (1 mark shared across parts):**

$$V_{void} = \varepsilon \cdot V_{col} = 0.40 \times 0.01178 = 4.712 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{resin} = (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \epsilon_p \cdot V_{col} = 0.60 \times 0.55 \times 0.01178 = 3.887 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**(i) Breakthrough volume of NaCl (2 marks):**

$$V_b^{NaCl} = V_{void} + K_d^{NaCl} \cdot V_{resin}$$

$$= 4.712 \times 10^{-3} + 0 \times 3.887 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 4.712 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 4.712 \text{ L}$$

**(ii) Breakthrough volume of glycerol (2 marks):**

$$V_b^{gly} = V_{void} + K_d^{gly} \cdot V_{resin}$$

$$= 4.712 \times 10^{-3} + 0.65 \times 3.887 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 4.712 \times 10^{-3} + 2.527 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 7.239 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 7.239 \text{ L}$$

**(iii) Eluent volume for complete separation (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V = V_b^{gly} - V_b^{NaCl} = 7.239 - 4.712 = 2.527 \text{ L}$$

> **Interpretation:** After collecting the NaCl peak, **2.527 L** of pure water must be flushed through the column to elute the glycerol peak *separately* from the electrolyte.

**(iv) Effect of increasing DVB% from 4% to 12% (1 mark):**

> **Qualitative prediction:** Increasing DVB% from 4% → 12% will **decrease $K_d^{glycerol}$** because the resin pores become *smaller* and the bulky glycerol molecule faces greater *steric hindrance* in diffusing into the matrix. Glycerol will therefore elute *closer* to the NaCl peak, *reducing* the separation window. **Trade-off:** Although higher DVB% *improves* the Donnan exclusion of NaCl, it *worsens* the resolution between the two peaks, so an **optimum DVB%** must be chosen for each separation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in Donnan derivations. Always list: dilute solution, monofunctional resin, 1:1 electrolyte, equilibrium. **(−1 to −2 marks lost)**
> 2. **Confusing** $K_d$ (distribution coefficient, dimensionless) with $K_D$ (mass transfer coefficient) — they are *totally different* quantities.
> 3. **Sign error in quadratic** — using the negative root, which gives a *negative* concentration. Always pick the **positive** root.
> 4. **Not converting units** — KTU problems often mix mol/L and mol/m³. **1 mol/L = 1000 mol/m³**. This is the *single most common unit error*.
> 5. **Misapplying the approximation** $[Cl^-]_r \approx C_s^2 / Q$ — it is *only* valid when $Q \gg C_s$ (typically $Q \geq 10 \cdot C_s$).
> 6. **Forgetting the role of cross-linkage** in long answers — examiners specifically look for a discussion of the **DVB% trade-off**.
> 7. **Writing "resin undergoes ion exchange"** in the definition of ion exclusion — this is *the opposite* of the truth. The resin does **NOT** exchange ions during the separation step. This is a *sure* 1-mark loss if written.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ion Exclusion**

- ✅ **Definition:** Chromatographic separation of electrolyte from non-electrolyte *without* actual ion exchange, using a resin as a passive medium.
- ✅ **Principle:** **Donnan exclusion** — co-ions electrostatically repelled from resin pores.
- ✅ **Key equation (Donnan):** $[Na^+]_r [Cl^-]_r = C_s^2$ (for 1:1 electrolyte with cation resin).
- ✅ **Key approximation:** $[Cl^-]_r \approx C_s^2 / Q$ (valid when $Q \gg C_s$).
- ✅ **Distribution coefficient:** $K_d \approx 0$ for electrolytes; $0.3 - 0.8$ for typical non-electrolytes.
- ✅ **Elution order:** Electrolyte elutes **first**, non-electrolyte elutes **second**.
- ✅ **Breakthrough volume:** $V_b = V_{void} + K_d \cdot V_{resin}$.
- ✅ **Effect of ↑ DVB%:** Better ion exclusion but *lower* $K_d$ for non-electrolytes.
- ✅ **Optimum DVB%:** 6 – 8% (balances exclusion and retention).
- ✅ **Regeneration:** Pure **water wash** — no chemicals needed. ♻️
- ✅ **Applications:** Glycerol purification, sugar desalting, ethylene glycol refining, fermentation broth desalting.
- ✅ **Advantage over ion exchange:** No chemical regeneration; no waste; continuous operation; preserves resin life.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Only works for **non-ionic vs ionic** mixtures; cannot separate two electrolytes.

> **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Applications Without Exchange: Ion Exclusion**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Transfer in Ion Exchange Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mass Transfer in Ion Exchange Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mass Transfer in Ion Exchange Systems** refers to the combined transport phenomena by which counter-ions migrate from the bulk liquid phase, through the external liquid film surrounding the resin bead, and through the internal porous matrix of the ion-exchange resin, ultimately exchanging with ions originally fixed on the resin functional groups. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, this is treated as a **diffusion-controlled sorption process** where the overall rate is governed by the slowest of three sequential resistances: (i) **external film diffusion**, (ii) **internal particle (intraparticle) diffusion**, and (iii) the **chemical reaction at the exchange site** (which is usually instantaneous).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Mass transfer in ion exchange involves the migration of ions across a hydrodynamic boundary layer (film) followed by diffusion through the resin matrix. The driving force is the **electrochemical potential gradient** (concentration + electrical potential), not just concentration alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy railway station with two platforms: **Platform A (Solution Side)** and **Platform B (Resin Bead Side)**. Passengers (ions) from Platform A want to reach Platform B to meet their family (fixed counter-ions). To board the train, they must:

1. First **walk through a crowded ticket-checking zone** (the external liquid film) — this is **film diffusion**.
2. Then **travel inside the train carriage to find their seat** (the internal pore network of the resin) — this is **particle diffusion**.
3. Finally, **swap the seat with the person already sitting** (the actual ion exchange reaction at the functional group).

The slowest of these three steps dictates how fast the whole journey happens. The film is like a "narrow ticket gate" — if it's congested, even fast trains inside cannot compensate. This is the essence of the **rate-controlling step** concept.

### 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,485 C/mol** (charge per mole of electrons)
- **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Resin capacity (Q)** is typically **1 – 5 eq/kg dry resin** for strong acid/strong base resins
- **Particle diameter (d_p)** ranges from **0.3 – 1.2 mm** for standard gel-type resins
- **Diffusion coefficients in resins (D_p)**: **10⁻¹¹ to 10⁻¹³ m²/s** (much lower than in free water: ~10⁻⁹ m²/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> In ion exchange, unlike ordinary gas absorption, we **cannot** use Fick's law alone. We must use the **Nernst–Planck equation** because ion migration is affected by both concentration AND electrical potential gradients (Donnan effect). This is the most important theoretical point in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sequential Mass Transfer Steps

For a counter-ion A⁺ (in solution) exchanging with a counter-ion B⁺ (initially on the resin R⁻):

$$A^+_{(bulk\ solution)} \xrightarrow{Step\ 1} A^+_{(film\ interface)} \xrightarrow{Step\ 2} A^+_{(inside\ resin)} \xrightarrow{Step\ 3} B^+_{(released)}$$

#### **Step 1 — External Film Diffusion**
- Occurs in the **stagnant Nernst liquid film** surrounding the resin bead.
- Driven by the concentration difference between bulk solution (C_b) and the particle surface (C_s).
- The flux is governed by the **liquid-phase diffusion coefficient D_L** and the **film mass transfer coefficient k_f**.
- Mass transfer coefficient depends on the **Reynolds number (Re)** and **Schmidt number (Sc)** via correlations like:

$$Sh = 2 + 0.6\,Re^{1/2}\,Sc^{1/3}$$

#### **Step 2 — Internal Particle Diffusion**
- Occurs inside the **gelular matrix** of the resin bead.
- The ion must navigate through tortuous pores and exchange with fixed functional groups.
- Characterized by the **intraparticle diffusion coefficient D_p**, which is much smaller than D_L.
- Follows **Fick's second law in spherical coordinates**:

$$\frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = D_p\left(\frac{\partial^2 q}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right)$$

#### **Step 3 — Chemical Exchange Reaction**
- The actual stoichiometric exchange at the fixed functional group:

$$R^-B^+ + A^+_{(inside\ resin)} \rightleftharpoons R^-A^+ + B^+$$

- **Generally treated as instantaneous** in strong-acid/strong-base resins.
- The **Donnan equilibrium** governs the final distribution of ions.

### 2.2 Why the Nernst–Planck Equation?

In ion exchange, when ion A⁺ moves in, ion B⁺ (of the same charge sign) must move out to maintain **electroneutrality**. This counter-flow creates an **electric field** inside the resin. The total flux therefore has three components:

1. **Concentration diffusion** (Fick's law)
2. **Electrical migration** (electrophoretic effect)
3. **Convection** (often neglected inside stationary resin)

The combined effect is the **Nernst–Planck equation**:

$$J_i = -D_i\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT}\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x}$$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mass transfer analysis in ion exchange is critical for:
- **Water demineralization** in power plants (removal of Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺)
- **Nuclear waste treatment** (separation of Cs⁺, Sr²⁺ from radioactive streams)
- **Pharmaceutical purification** (antibiotic recovery using ion exchange resins)
- **Hydrometallurgy** (separation of rare earth elements)
- **Sugar refining** (decolorization using anion exchange resins)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (Fickian diffusion) | $J = -D\frac{dC}{dx}$ | Flux proportional to concentration gradient | mol/(m²·s) |
| 2 | Nernst–Planck Equation (1-D) | $J_i = -D_i\frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT}\frac{d\Phi}{dx}$ | Flux including electrical migration | mol/(m²·s) |
| 3 | Fick's Second Law (spherical) | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\left(\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial C}{\partial r}\right)$ | Transient diffusion inside a sphere | — |
| 4 | Film Mass Transfer Coefficient (Wilson-Geankoplis) | $k_f = \frac{D_L}{d_p}\left(0.0025\,Re^{1.4}\,Sc^{0.5}\right)$ | External film coefficient | m/s |
| 5 | Sherwood Number Correlation | $Sh = 2 + 0.6\,Re^{1/2}\,Sc^{1/3}$ | Dimensionless mass transfer | dimensionless |
| 6 | Bi (Biot) Number for Ion Exchange | $Bi = \frac{k_f \, d_p \, C_b}{2 \, D_p \, \rho_s \, q_0}$ | Ratio of film to particle resistance | dimensionless |
| 7 | Film Diffusion Rate | $\frac{dq_A}{dt} = k_f \, a \, (C_A - C_A^*)$ | Rate of external transport | mol/(s·m³) |
| 8 | Particle Diffusion Rate | $\frac{\partial q_A}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 D_p \frac{\partial q_A}{\partial r}\right)$ | Rate inside bead | mol/(m³·s) |
| 9 | Overall Resistance (Additivity) | $\frac{1}{K_L a} = \frac{1}{k_f a} + \frac{1}{k_p a \, m}$ | Resistances in series | s/m |
| 10 | Donnan Equilibrium Relation | $\left(\frac{C_A}{C_B}\right)_{resin} = K_{AB}\left(\frac{C_A}{C_B}\right)_{solution}$ | Ion selectivity coefficient | dimensionless |
| 11 | Separation Factor (α) | $\alpha_{AB} = \frac{y_A/y_B}{x_A/x_B}$ | Selectivity of resin for A over B | dimensionless |
| 12 | Half-Time for Particle Diffusion | $t_{1/2} = 0.030 \frac{r_0^2}{D_p}$ | Time for 50% approach to equilibrium | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**
> The two equations examiners love to test are: (a) the **Nernst–Planck equation** (must write the full 3-component form with proper signs) and (b) the **overall resistance model** showing $1/K = 1/k_f + 1/k_p$. Students who forget the electrical migration term lose 4–5 marks immediately.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst–Planck Equation from Thermodynamic Consistency

The chemical potential of an ion $i$ in solution (including the electrical contribution) is:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT\ln(C_i) + z_i F \Phi$$

**Step 1:** The driving force per mole of ion is the negative gradient of chemical potential:

$$F_i = -\frac{d\mu_i}{dx} = -RT\frac{1}{C_i}\frac{dC_i}{dx} - z_i F \frac{d\Phi}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** The flux is proportional to the driving force and the concentration (Einstein's relation: mobility $u_i = D_i/RT$):

$$J_i = -u_i \, C_i \, \frac{d\mu_i}{dx}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the driving force from Step 1:

$$J_i = -\frac{D_i}{RT} \cdot C_i \cdot \left(-RT\frac{1}{C_i}\frac{dC_i}{dx} - z_i F \frac{d\Phi}{dx}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the expression — the $C_i$ cancels in the first term:

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{d\Phi}{dx}$$

> This is the **Nernst–Planck equation**, the cornerstone of ion exchange mass transfer.

**Why does this matter?**
For neutral molecules, the second term (electrical migration) is zero. But in ion exchange, both ions are charged and move in opposite directions, so an **internal electric field** is set up that **slows down** the faster ion and **speeds up** the slower ion — a phenomenon called **electroneutrality coupling**. This is why pure Fick's law fails for ion exchange.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fick's Second Law in Spherical Coordinates (for Particle Diffusion)

For a spherical resin bead of radius $R$, the total mass of counter-ion in a thin shell of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ is:

$$M = 4\pi r^2 \Delta r \, q(r,t)$$

**Step 1: Mass balance on the shell**
The net rate of accumulation = net inward flux at $r$ − net outward flux at $r + dr$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(4\pi r^2 \Delta r \, q) = 4\pi r^2 J_r - 4\pi (r+dr)^2 J_{r+dr}$$

**Step 2: Expand using Taylor series for the flux at the outer face:**

$$J_{r+dr} = J_r + \frac{\partial J_r}{\partial r} dr$$

**Step 3: Substitute back:**

$$4\pi r^2 \Delta r \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = 4\pi r^2 J_r - 4\pi \left(r^2 + 2r\,dr\right)\left(J_r + \frac{\partial J_r}{\partial r} dr\right)$$

**Step 4: Neglect higher-order $(dr)^2$ terms:**

$$r^2 \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = -2r J_r - r^2 \frac{\partial J_r}{\partial r}$$

**Step 5: Apply Fick's first law inside the particle**, $J_r = -D_p \frac{\partial q}{\partial r}$:

$$r^2 \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = -2r\left(-D_p \frac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right) - r^2 \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(-D_p \frac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Step 6: Simplify to obtain the spherical diffusion equation:**

$$\frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = D_p \left(\frac{\partial^2 q}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right)$$

**Initial & Boundary Conditions:**

$$q(r,0) = q_0 \quad ; \quad \left.\frac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right|_{r=0} = 0 \quad ; \quad D_p\left.\frac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right|_{r=R} = k_f(C_b - C_s^*)$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Resistances-in-Series Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient

In a packed bed, the ion must overcome two resistances: the **external film** and the **internal particle**.

**Step 1:** The flux through the film is:

$$J = k_f (C_b - C_s)$$

**Step 2:** The flux through the particle (using the linear driving force approximation for spherical beads):

$$J = k_p (q_s - \bar{q})$$

**Step 3:** Equating the fluxes at steady state and combining:

$$\frac{C_b - C_s}{J/k_f} = \frac{C_b - \bar{q}/m}{J/K_L}$$

**Step 4:** Result after algebraic manipulation:**

$$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_f} + \frac{1}{m \, k_p}$$

This is the **resistances-in-series** model, directly analogous to heat transfer conduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**
> - If $k_f \ll k_p$ → **Film diffusion controlled** (typical for **low concentration** solutions and **large beads**).
> - If $k_p \ll k_f$ → **Particle diffusion controlled** (typical for **concentrated solutions** and **small beads with tight matrix**).
> - The **Biot number** $Bi = k_f R / (D_p)$ decides the regime: $Bi \gg 1$ → film control; $Bi \ll 1$ → particle control.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Model Problem)

**Problem:** A cation exchange resin (diameter 0.6 mm) is used to remove Cu²⁺ from a dilute wastewater stream. Given:
- $D_p = 2.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s
- $k_f = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
- $R = 0.3 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $C_b = 0.1$ mol/m³, $q_0 = 0.05$ mol/kg (initial)

**Find the Biot number and identify the controlling mechanism.**

**Step 1:** Compute the Biot number:

$$Bi = \frac{k_f \, R}{D_p} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$Bi = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{2.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 6000$$

**Step 2:** Interpretation:

Since $Bi \gg 1$, the **film resistance dominates** → **film diffusion is the rate-controlling step**.

**Step 3:** Verify using the half-time formula for pure film control:

$$t_{1/2} \approx \frac{0.167 \cdot R \cdot C_b}{k_f \cdot C^*}$$

> [!TIP]
> This type of numerical identification question is **highly probable** in KTU ESE. Practice computing Bi numbers from given data.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Mass Transfer Pathway in a Single Resin Bead

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Bulk Liquid Solution with Ion A+"]
    B["Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer Film"]

    B --> C
    C --> D

    E --> F

```

    q1{"Biot Number Bi"}

    low["Bi less than 0 05 Particle Diffusion Control"]
    mid["Bi between 0 05 and 20 Mixed Control"]
    end1["Use kf Dominant Equation"]
    end2["Use Dp Dominant Equation"]
    end3["Use Full Coupled Nernst Planck"]

    start --> q1
    q1 --> high
    q1 --> low
    q1 --> mid
    high --> end1

    F1["Film: Linear Drop Cb to Cs"]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams**
> - The **film region** shows a **linear** concentration drop (Fickian assumption in stagnant film).
> - The **bead interior** shows a **parabolic/curved** drop governed by the spherical diffusion equation.
> - The **center** of the bead is the last point to equilibrate, and this is why small beads reach equilibrium faster than large beads.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of mass transfer in ion exchange and identify the three sequential steps involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mass transfer in ion exchange is the combined transport of counter-ions from the bulk liquid to the interior of the resin bead where they exchange with ions held by the fixed functional groups. The process is **diffusion-controlled** and involves **three sequential steps**:

1. **External film diffusion** — counter-ion travels through the stagnant liquid film surrounding the resin bead; driving force is the concentration difference $C_b - C_s$.
2. **Internal particle (intraparticle) diffusion** — counter-ion diffuses through the porous resin matrix; governed by $D_p$ and the spherical Fickian equation.
3. **Chemical exchange reaction** — actual ion exchange at the functional group; assumed instantaneous for strong-acid/strong-base resins.

The overall rate is governed by the **slowest of these three steps** (rate-controlling step concept).

> **Marking distribution:** Step identification 2 marks, definition 1 mark.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between film diffusion control and particle diffusion control in ion exchange. Mention the conditions under which each dominates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Film Diffusion Control | Particle Diffusion Control |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Rate-limiting step | External liquid film | Internal resin matrix |
| Typical concentration | Low / dilute solutions | High / concentrated solutions |
| Particle size effect | More prominent (large $d_p$) | Less sensitive |
| Typical Biot number | $Bi \gg 1$ | $Bi \ll 1$ |
| Governing coefficient | $k_f$ (mass transfer coeff.) | $D_p$ (intraparticle diffusivity) |
| Mixing effect | Strongly affected by agitation | Less affected by mixing |

**Conditions favoring film control:** dilute solutions, large particles, high temperature in the liquid phase, low resin capacity.
**Conditions favoring particle control:** concentrated solutions, small gel-type beads, highly crosslinked resin, low intraparticle diffusivity.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst–Planck equation for mass transfer in ion exchange systems. Explain why this equation is preferred over simple Fick's law for ion exchange applications.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Chemical potential of an ion**

The chemical potential of an ionic species $i$ in an electrochemical system is:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln(C_i) + z_i F \Phi$$

where $C_i$ is the concentration, $z_i$ the charge, $F$ Faraday's constant, and $\Phi$ the local electric potential.

**Step 2: Driving force**

The driving force per mole on the ion is the negative gradient:

$$F_i = -\frac{d\mu_i}{dx} = -\frac{RT}{C_i}\frac{dC_i}{dx} - z_i F \frac{d\Phi}{dx}$$

**Step 3: Flux (linear response to driving force)**

Using the Nernst–Einstein relation between mobility $u_i$ and diffusivity $D_i = u_i RT$:

$$J_i = -u_i \, C_i \, \frac{d\mu_i}{dx} = -\frac{D_i \, C_i}{RT} \cdot \frac{d\mu_i}{dx}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify**

$$J_i = -\frac{D_i C_i}{RT}\left(-\frac{RT}{C_i}\frac{dC_i}{dx} - z_i F \frac{d\Phi}{dx}\right)$$

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{d\Phi}{dx}$$

**Why preferred over Fick's law:**
- Fick's law works only for **neutral species**.
- In ion exchange, both ions are charged and move in opposite directions → an internal **electric field** is generated.
- The electrical term **$-$($z_i F D_i C_i / RT$)(dΦ/dx)** captures this coupling.
- For uncharged systems, $z_i = 0$ and the Nernst–Planck equation reduces to Fick's law, so it is the more general form.

> **Marking distribution:** Setup of chemical potential 2 marks; driving force 2 marks; final derivation 2 marks; explanation 1 mark.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cation exchange column is loaded with H⁺-form resin. The breakthrough data for a solution containing 0.02 mol/L Na⁺ is fitted to a model. The film mass transfer coefficient is estimated to be $k_f = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s and the particle diffusivity is $D_p = 3.0 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s. The resin has an average radius of $4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m. Determine: (i) the Biot number, (ii) the controlling mechanism, (iii) the effective time constant for particle diffusion.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Biot number**

$$Bi = \frac{k_f \cdot R}{D_p} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-5})(4.0 \times 10^{-4})}{3.0 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$Bi = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-9}}{3.0 \times 10^{-12}} = 1600$$

**Step 2: Identify the controlling mechanism**

Since $Bi = 1600 \gg 1$, the **film resistance dominates** and **film diffusion is the rate-controlling step**.

**Step 3: Particle diffusion time constant**

$$\tau_p = \frac{R^2}{D_p} = \frac{(4.0 \times 10^{-4})^2}{3.0 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{3.0 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\tau_p = 5.33 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} \approx 14.8 \text{ hours}$$

Since the film time constant is much smaller, the slow step is the external film resistance, confirming the conclusion in Step 2.

> **Marking distribution:** Bi calculation 3 marks; mechanism identification 2 marks; time constant 2 marks.

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **resistances-in-series model** for mass transfer in ion exchange. Derive the expression for the overall mass transfer coefficient $K_L$.

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual setup:**
When a counter-ion $A^+$ transfers from bulk solution into a resin bead, it must overcome two resistances in series:
- $R_f$ = resistance of the **external liquid film**
- $R_p$ = resistance of the **internal particle diffusion**

These act like electrical resistors in series (analogy with heat transfer conduction through a composite wall).

**Step 1: Define individual fluxes**

The flux through the film:

$$J = k_f(C_b - C_s)$$

The flux through the particle (using linear driving force):

$$J = k_p(q_s - \bar{q})$$

The relationship at the interface is given by equilibrium: $q_s = m C_s$, so $C_s = q_s / m$.

**Step 2: Total driving force**

The overall driving force across both resistances:

$$\Delta C_{overall} = C_b - \bar{q}/m$$

**Step 3: Using flux additivity at steady state**

The total resistance is the sum of individual resistances:

$$\frac{C_b - \bar{q}/m}{J} = \frac{C_b - C_s}{J} + \frac{C_s - \bar{q}/m}{J}$$

$$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_f} + \frac{1}{m \, k_p}$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation**

- If $k_f \ll m k_p$ → $K_L \approx k_f$ (film control).
- If $m k_p \ll k_f$ → $K_L \approx m k_p$ (particle control).
- The **Biot number** is essentially the ratio $k_f / (D_p / R)$, indicating which resistance dominates.

> **Marking distribution:** Concept 2 marks; Sketch/Figure (not required due to ASCII limitation) 1 mark; Derivation 3 marks; Interpretation 1 mark.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the following ion exchange mass transfer models with their key assumptions, governing equations, and applications:
1. Linear Driving Force (LDF) model
2. Nernst–Planck model
3. Shrinking Core model

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | LDF Model | Nernst–Planck Model | Shrinking Core Model |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Assumption** | Linear concentration profile inside particle | Coupling of concentration + electric field | Sharp reaction front; unreacted core shrinks |
| **Governing equation** | $dq/dt = k_p(q^* - q)$ | Full Nernst–Planck PDE | Surface reaction or diffusion controlled |
| **Geometry** | Lump-sum inside bead | Radial (spherical) | Sharp unreacted core |
| **Use case** | Engineering design, quick estimates | Rigorous modeling of coupled transport | Exchange with slow chemical reaction |
| **Complexity** | Low (analytical) | High (numerical PDE) | Medium |
| **When applicable** | Well-mixed, dilute systems | High concentration or high selectivity | Resins with very slow chemical kinetics |

**Step-by-step LDF derivation:**

The full intraparticle diffusion equation is:

$$\frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = D_p\left(\frac{\partial^2 q}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right)$$

If we assume the radial profile is approximately **parabolic**, the LDF approximation gives:

$$\frac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = k_p(q_s - \bar{q})$$

where $k_p = 15 D_p / R^2$ for a spherical bead.

**Nernst–Planck (recap):**

$$J_i = -D_i\frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT}\frac{d\Phi}{dx}$$

**Shrinking Core (form of control):**

- **Film diffusion control:** $t \propto X$ (linear in conversion)
- **Particle diffusion control:** $t \propto [1 - 3(1-X)^{2/3} + 2(1-X)]$
- **Chemical reaction control:** $t \propto [1 - (1-X)^{1/3}]$

where $X$ is fractional conversion.

> **Marking distribution:** Comparison table 4 marks; LDF derivation 2 marks; Shrinking Core formulas 1 mark.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the electrical term in Nernst–Planck** — examiners specifically test whether students write the full equation with $-z_i F D_i C_i / (RT) \cdot d\Phi/dx$. Omitting this = -4 marks.
> 2. **Using Fick's law directly for ion exchange** — invalid because ions are charged. Always justify with electroneutrality.
> 3. **Confusing $k_f$ and $k_p$** — $k_f$ is the film coefficient (m/s), $k_p$ is the particle-side coefficient (1/s). They have different units.
> 4. **Sign errors in driving forces** — $C_b - C_s$ (not $C_s - C_b$) for film diffusion; $q^* - q$ for the direction of approach to equilibrium.
> 5. **Using "molar" units in Sherwood correlations** — always use **consistent SI units** ($D$ in m²/s, $d_p$ in m, $k_f$ in m/s).
> 6. **Ignoring the Biot number** — a single Bi calculation decides which model applies; skipping it is a major deduction.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**
>
> ✅ **Three mass transfer steps** — Film → Particle → Reaction. The slowest is rate-controlling.
>
> ✅ **Nernst–Planck equation** — includes Fickian + electrical migration. Essential for ion exchange.
>
> ✅ **Fick's second law in spherical coordinates** — $∂q/∂t = D_p (∂²q/∂r² + (2/r) ∂q/∂r)$.
>
> ✅ **Biot number** — $Bi = k_f R / D_p$. Tells you film vs. particle control.
>
> ✅ **Resistance-in-series** — $1/K_L = 1/k_f + 1/(m k_p)$. Direct analogy with heat conduction.
>
> ✅ **Sherwood number correlation** — $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$ for single spheres; use Wilson-Geankoplis for packed beds.
>
> ✅ **Linear Driving Force (LDF) approximation** — $d\bar{q}/dt = k_p(q^* - \bar{q})$; $k_p = 15 D_p / R^2$ for spheres.
>
> ✅ **Half-time formula** — $t_{1/2} \approx 0.030 R^2 / D_p$ for pure particle diffusion control.
>
> ✅ **Equilibrium relation** — $q^* = f(C)$ from Langmuir/Freundlich or constant separation factor $\alpha$.
>
> ✅ **Donnan equilibrium** — governs ion distribution at the resin-solution interface; selectivity coefficient $K_{AB}$ defines preference.
>
> ✅ **Real-world applications** — water softening, nuclear waste, hydrometallurgy, pharmaceutical purification.
>
> ✅ **Units convention** — $D$ in m²/s, $C$ in mol/m³, $q$ in mol/kg dry resin, $J$ in mol/(m²·s).
>
> ✅ **In the exam** — always show all 3 steps, write the full Nernst–Planck, compute Bi to identify the regime, and finish with a clear engineering interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
