topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulated Counter-Current Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SIMULATED COUNTER-CURRENT TECHNIQUES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simulated Moving Bed (SMB)** chromatography is a continuous, multi-column chromatographic separation technique in which the counter-current movement of the solid (adsorbent) phase relative to the liquid (eluent/feed) phase is *simulated* by periodically shifting the inlet/outlet ports in the direction of liquid flow, rather than physically moving the solid adsorbent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT756 Module 4):** 
> "Simulated Counter-Current Techniques refer to the class of continuous separation processes — primarily the Simulated Moving Bed (SMB) and its variants (Varicol, PowerFeed, ModiCon, etc.) — that emulate the action of a true moving bed (TMB) through synchronized port switching along a fixed-bed column train. These are widely applied in petrochemical, sugar, pharmaceutical, and fine chemical industries for difficult binary and pseudo-binary separations."

### 1.2 True Moving Bed (TMB) vs. Simulated Moving Bed (SMB)

In a **True Moving Bed (TMB)**, the solid adsorbent physically moves downward while the liquid flows upward — this is a *true* counter-current contactor. However, mechanical movement of solid particles causes:

- Particle attrition and erosion
- Non-uniform flow distribution
- High back-pressure
- Difficult scale-up

In an **SMB**, the solid phase is **stationary** (packed in columns), and the **port switching** every switching time (τ) creates the illusion of solid motion. This eliminates mechanical degradation while achieving the same separation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Escalator Conveyor:**
> Imagine an airport escalator carrying luggage (adsorbent with strongly-held component B) moving *downward*, while passengers carrying backpacks (liquid with weak component A) move *upward*. The luggage exchange happens continuously.
>
> Now, **build a regular staircase instead** (stationary columns), and ask passengers to **switch to the next staircase step every 30 seconds** in the same direction the luggage would have moved. The *net effect* is identical — but the staircase never moves! This is exactly what an SMB does.

### 1.4 Real-World Industrial Significance

| Application | Strong Adsorbing Component | Weak Adsorbing Component |
|---|---|---|
| **p-Xylene separation** | p-Xylene | m-Xylene, o-Xylene |
| **Sugar refining** | Fructose | Glucose |
| **Pharmaceutical enantiomers** | R-enantiomer | S-enantiomer |
| **FAME / Biodiesel** | Linear olefins | Branched paraffins |

### 1.5 Industry Benchmark Constants

- Typical number of columns: **N = 4, 6, 8, or 12**
- Typical switching time: **τ = 0.5 – 5 min**
- Typical column length: **L_c = 5 – 25 cm**
- Typical internal diameter: **D = 1 – 10 cm**
- Superficial velocity range: **0.1 – 5 cm/min**
- Productivity: **1 – 50 kg product/kg adsorbent/day**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four SMB Zones — Operational Architecture

A classical **4-zone SMB** is divided into four distinct functional zones by two inlet ports (Feed, Eluent) and two outlet ports (Extract, Raffinate). Each zone performs a unique mass-transfer role.

### Zone I — Eluent Regeneration (between Eluent and Extract)
- **Function:** Desorbs the strongly retained component (B) from the solid using fresh eluent.
- **Condition:** Solid must be **fully regenerated** (free of B) before re-entering Zone IV.
- **Constraint:** Liquid flow rate must be large enough to carry B upward against solid motion.

### Zone II — Desorption of Weak Component (between Extract and Feed)
- **Function:** The strongly adsorbed B is desorbed into the liquid; weak component A is readsorbed.
- **Condition:** This is the **purification zone for B (Extract)**.

### Zone III — Adsorption of Strong Component (between Feed and Raffinate)
- **Function:** The strong component B is adsorbed from the liquid onto the solid.
- **Condition:** This is the **purification zone for A (Raffinate)**.

### Zone IV — Eluent Purification (between Raffinate and Eluent)
- **Function:** The weak component A is washed out of the solid; solid becomes free of A.
- **Constraint:** Liquid flow rate must be high enough to push A out as raffinate.

## 2.2 Mass-Transfer Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Zone

- **Zone I (Eluent + Solid saturated with B → B desorbed):** Fresh eluent has the highest B-carrying capacity; it strips B from solid → Extract rich in B.
- **Zone II (Solid partially loaded with A, B-rich liquid):** B continues to desorb (but not A), producing pure B extract.
- **Zone III (Feed introduced — both A and B enter):** B is preferentially adsorbed by solid; A remains in liquid → Raffinate rich in A.
- **Zone IV (Solid with adsorbed A, liquid B-poor):** A is desorbed/eluted from solid using eluent → solid regenerated for next cycle.

## 2.3 The Key Parameter: Flow-Rate Ratios (m_j)

The dimensionless flow-rate ratio **m_j** in each zone is the cornerstone of SMB design. It is defined as the ratio of net liquid flow to net solid flow (in equivalent TMB terms):

$$m_j = \frac{Q_j^{SMB} \cdot t^* - V \cdot \epsilon^*}{V \cdot (1 - \epsilon^*)}$$

where the **switching time** t* (often written τ) and column volume V are critical parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEEP THIS CLEAR:** The m_j values **MUST** satisfy an inequality window for complete separation. The classical "triangle" conditions are:
> - m_1 < m_2 < m_3 < m_4
> - Specific bounds: **m_1 between (Henry constant of A, Henry constant of B)**
> - Detailed inequalities given in Section 2.5.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_j^{TMB} = \dfrac{Q_j}{A \cdot \epsilon}$ | Superficial liquid velocity in zone j | Very High |
| 2 | $v^{TMB} = \dfrac{L_c}{\tau \cdot N_j}$ | Equivalent solid velocity in TMB | Very High |
| 3 | $m_j = \dfrac{u_j^{TMB}}{v^{TMB}} = \dfrac{Q_j \cdot \tau - V \epsilon}{V (1-\epsilon)}$ | Zone flow-rate ratio | **MOST CRITICAL** |
| 4 | $\gamma_j = \dfrac{1 - \epsilon}{\epsilon} \cdot m_j$ | Often-used alternative ratio | High |
| 5 | $m_1^{crit} = H_A$ | Lower bound for Zone I (weak component) | High |
| 6 | $m_1^{crit} = \dfrac{\sqrt{H_A} + \sqrt{H_B}}{2}$ | For non-linear (Langmuir) | High |
| 7 | $m_2^{crit} = H_B$ | Upper bound for Zone II | High |
| 8 | $m_3^{crit} = H_B$ | Lower bound for Zone III | High |
| 9 | $m_4^{crit} = H_A$ | Upper bound for Zone IV | High |
| 10 | $P = \dfrac{m_3 - m_2}{m_1 - m_4}$ | Constraint check (must be > 0) | Medium |
| 11 | $PR = \dfrac{Q_{Ext} \cdot C_{B,Ext}}{Q_{Feed} \cdot C_{B,Feed}}$ | Purity (B in extract vs. feed) | Very High |
| 12 | $REC = \dfrac{Q_{Ext} \cdot C_{B,Ext}}{Q_{Feed} \cdot C_{B,Feed}}$ | Recovery of B | Very High |
| 13 | $Prod = \dfrac{Q_{Ext} \cdot C_{B,Ext}}{V_{adsorbent}}$ | Productivity (kg B / m³ ads / day) | High |
| 14 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{150 \mu L_c v (1-\epsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \epsilon^3}$ | Kozeny-Carman pressure drop | Medium |
| 15 | $q^* = \dfrac{q_s K C}{1 + K C}$ | Langmuir isotherm (non-linear) | High |
| 16 | $H_i = \dfrac{\partial q^*}{\partial C}\bigg\vert_{C_i^*}$ | Henry's constant for component i | Very High |

### 2.5 The Classical Separation Conditions (Storti–Mazzotti 1993)

For **Langmuir isotherms** with two components A (weak) and B (strong):

$$m_1 < \frac{\omega_B + \omega_A}{2}$$

$$m_2 \in (\omega_B, \omega_A)$$

$$m_3 \in (\omega_B, \omega_A)$$

$$m_4 > \omega_B$$

where the **ω parameters** depend on feed and product concentrations (Storti diagram).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Continuous chiral drug separation (replaces batch HPLC).
- **Petrochemical:** Paraxylene production (UOP Parex™ process — billion kg/year).
- **Food industry:** Fructose-glucose syrup separation (Amberlite resins).
- **Biotech:** Continuous purification of monoclonal antibodies (BioSMB technology).
- **Fine chemicals:** Enantiomer, cis-trans, and structural isomers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From True Moving Bed to SMB Equivalence

### Conceptual Setup

In a **True Moving Bed (TMB)**, the solid moves at velocity **u_s** (m/s) downward and liquid moves at **u_L** upward in each zone. In an **SMB**, the solid is fixed, but the **ports shift** by one column every switching interval **τ**.

### Step 1: Define Column Geometry

Each column has:
- Cross-sectional area: **A** (m²)
- Length: **L_c** (m)
- Total porosity: **ε**
- Solid volume fraction: **(1 − ε)**

The volume of one column occupied by solid: $V_s = A \cdot L_c \cdot (1-\epsilon)$

### Step 2: TMB Solid Velocity

If the solid in TMB is equivalent to the solid in SMB, and in SMB the equivalent solid movement per switch = one column length L_c over time τ:

$$u_s^{TMB} = \frac{L_c}{\tau}$$

This is the *equivalent* solid velocity even though the solid does not actually move in SMB.

### Step 3: TMB Liquid Velocity in Zone j

For SMB, the volumetric liquid flow rate in zone j is **Q_j** (m³/s). The superficial liquid velocity is:

$$u_{L,j} = \frac{Q_j}{A \cdot \epsilon}$$

### Step 4: Dimensionless Flow-Rate Ratio m_j

The **m_j parameter** is the ratio of liquid velocity to solid velocity:

$$m_j = \frac{u_{L,j}}{u_s^{TMB}}$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$m_j = \frac{Q_j / (A \epsilon)}{L_c / \tau} = \frac{Q_j \cdot \tau}{A \cdot L_c \cdot \epsilon}$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Column Volume

Since column volume $V = A \cdot L_c$:

$$m_j = \frac{Q_j \cdot \tau}{V \cdot \epsilon}$$

If we define $V_{liq} = V \cdot \epsilon$ (liquid volume in column), then:

$$\boxed{m_j = \frac{Q_j \cdot \tau}{V \cdot \epsilon}}$$

**Textual explanation:** This equation tells us that m_j is the ratio of the volume of liquid passed through a column in one switching interval to the volume of liquid held in the column. Physically, m_j ≈ 1 means liquid and solid move at the same rate (equilibrium zone).

### Step 4: Including Dead Volume and Solid Fraction

Some texts include the inter-particle and intra-particle porosities separately, giving:

$$m_j = \frac{Q_j \cdot \tau - V \epsilon}{V (1 - \epsilon)}$$

**Textual explanation:** The numerator subtracts the liquid held up in the column (Vε) from the total liquid delivered in one switch (Q_j τ). The denominator represents the solid volume, so the ratio is *net liquid transported per unit solid*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storti–Mazzotti Separation Constraints (Linear Isotherm)

### Assumption
For **linear isotherms**: $q_A = H_A C_A$ and $q_B = H_B C_B$ with $H_A < H_B$.

### Step 1: Material Balance for Weak Component A in Zone I

In Zone I, the solid entering is free of A and B (regenerated). The eluent contains essentially zero A and B. The flux of A leaving the solid phase per unit time must be carried by the liquid:

$$u_s \cdot q_A^{solid} = u_L \cdot C_A^{liquid}$$

This implies:

$$m_1 > H_A$$

**Textual explanation:** Zone I must be strong enough to *strip* A off the solid; otherwise A would contaminate the solid entering Zone IV.

### Step 2: Material Balance for Strong Component B in Zone I

Solid entering Zone I has adsorbed B at the saturation value. The eluent must be able to desorb B:

$$m_1 < H_B$$

**Textual explanation:** Zone I flow cannot be too high, else B would travel upward too fast without desorbing.

### Step 3: Zone II — B Desorption, A Readsorption

For pure B in extract, A in solid must be at equilibrium with A in liquid:

$$H_A < m_2 < H_B$$

**Textual explanation:** Zone II must allow A to readsorb on the solid (m₂ > H_A) while B continues to desorb (m₂ < H_B).

### Step 4: Zone III — A Desorption, B Adsorption

Same condition:

$$H_A < m_3 < H_B$$

**Textual explanation:** Zone III must let A travel upward (m₃ > H_A) and let B continue adsorbing (m₃ < H_B).

### Step 5: Zone IV — A Desorption (Raffinate formation)

$$m_4 > H_A$$

**Textual explanation:** Solid exiting Zone IV must be free of A; hence liquid flow must be large enough to wash A out.

### Final Classical Inequalities (Linear Case)

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} H_A &< m_1 < H_B \\ H_A &< m_2 < H_B \\ H_A &< m_3 < H_B \\ m_4 &> H_A \end{aligned}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Triangle Method for Non-Linear (Langmuir) Isotherms

For Langmuir binary isotherm:

$$q_i = \frac{q_s K_i C_i}{1 + K_A C_A + K_B C_B}, \quad i = A, B$$

### Define the "ω" parameters

For component i at feed concentration $C_i^F$ and product concentration $C_i^P$:

$$\omega_i(C_A, C_B) = \frac{1 - \epsilon}{\epsilon} + \frac{(1-\epsilon)}{\epsilon} \cdot \frac{q_s K_i}{1 + K_A C_A + K_B C_B}$$

### Then the complete separation window is:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} m_1 &< \omega_B^{Ex} \\ m_2 &\in (\omega_B^{Ex}, \omega_A^{Ra}) \\ m_3 &\in (\omega_B^{Ex}, \omega_A^{Ra}) \\ m_4 &> \omega_A^{Ra} \end{aligned}}$$

where superscript **Ex** = extract conditions (C_A, C_B at extract) and **Ra** = raffinate conditions.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Switching Time Calculation

Given column length L_c, number of columns per zone N_j, and desired equivalent solid velocity u_s:

$$\tau = \frac{L_c}{u_s}$$

Or in terms of column count and total cycle:

$$T_{cycle} = N_{total} \cdot \tau$$

For an 8-column SMB with 2 columns per zone:
$$T_{cycle} = 8 \tau$$

## 3.5 Performance Metrics Derivation

### Purity of Extract (B-rich stream)

$$Pur_{Ext} = \frac{C_{B,Ext}}{C_{A,Ext} + C_{B,Ext}} \times 100\%$$

### Recovery of B in Extract

$$Rec_B = \frac{Q_{Ext} \cdot C_{B,Ext}}{Q_{Feed} \cdot C_{B,Feed}} \times 100\%$$

### Productivity

$$Prod = \frac{Q_{Ext} \cdot C_{B,Ext}}{m_{adsorbent}} \quad \text{[kg B / kg adsorbent / day]}$$

### Eluent Consumption

$$EC = \frac{Q_{Eluent}}{Q_{Ext} \cdot C_{B,Ext}} \quad \text{[L eluent / kg B]}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classical 4-Zone SMB Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Eluent In] --> Z1
    Z1[Zone I: Eluent Regeneration]
    Z1 --> EX[Extract Out B rich]
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2[Zone II: Desorption of A]
    Z2 --> F[Feed In A plus B]
    F --> Z3
    Z3[Zone III: Adsorption of B]
    Z3 --> RA[Raffinate Out A rich]
    Z3 --> Z4
    Z4[Zone IV: Eluent Purification]
    Z4 --> A
    A2[Note: Solid moves DOWNWARD] -.-> Z1
    A2 -.-> Z2
    A2 -.-> Z3
    A2 -.-> Z4
```

```mermaid
    T0["Time t0: Eluent at Col1, Extract at Col3, Feed at Col5, Raffinate at Col7"]

    T2["Each column experiences all 4 roles over full cycle N total times tau"]
    T0 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
```

```mermaid
    S7[Step 7: Calculate switching time tau from desired u_s]

    S2 --> S3

```

```mermaid
    V1[Inlet Eluent] --> M1[Multiport Rotary Valve 1]
    V2[Inlet Feed] --> M2[Multiport Rotary Valve 2]

    V4[Outlet Raffinate] --> M4[Multiport Rotary Valve 4]
    M1 --> C1[Column 1]
    C1 --> C2[Column 2]
```

```mermaid
    end

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is a Simulated Moving Bed (SMB)? How does it differ from a True Moving Bed (TMB)?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Simulated Moving Bed (SMB)** is a continuous chromatographic separation process in which counter-current motion between solid and liquid phases is **simulated** by periodically shifting the inlet/outlet ports in the direction of liquid flow across a fixed-bed column train. (1.5 marks)

**Key differences from TMB:**
- In **TMB**, the solid adsorbent physically moves (downward) while the liquid flows upward.
- In **SMB**, the solid is **stationary** (packed), and the apparent solid motion is created by **port switching** every switching time τ. (1.5 marks)

This eliminates problems of particle attrition, erosion, and non-uniform flow found in TMB while achieving equivalent separation.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define the flow-rate ratio m_j and state the classical separation conditions for a 4-zone SMB with linear isotherms."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **flow-rate ratio m_j** for zone j is defined as the ratio of liquid flow rate to the equivalent solid flow rate:

$$m_j = \frac{Q_j \tau}{V \epsilon}$$

where Q_j is the volumetric liquid flow in zone j, τ is the switching time, V is the column volume, and ε is the porosity. (1.5 marks)

**Classical linear separation conditions** (for weak A, strong B with Henry constants H_A < H_B):

$$H_A < m_1 < H_B, \quad H_A < m_2 < H_B, \quad H_A < m_3 < H_B, \quad m_4 > H_A$$

These four inequalities define the operating window for complete binary separation. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]*

**A(a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the working principle of a 4-zone Simulated Moving Bed (SMB). Clearly identify the four zones and the role of each port (Eluent, Extract, Feed, Raffinate).

**A(b) [7 marks]:** For a binary mixture with linear isotherms, derive the classical separation conditions in terms of the flow-rate ratios m₁, m₂, m₃, m₄ and the Henry constants H_A and H_B. State the physical meaning of each inequality.

---

### **Model Solution A(a) — Working Principle (7 marks)**

A classical 4-zone SMB consists of **4 to 12 packed chromatographic columns** connected in series. The four zones (I, II, III, IV) are defined by four ports:

- **Eluent Inlet** (fresh eluent) — between Zone IV and Zone I
- **Extract Outlet** (B-rich) — between Zone I and Zone II
- **Feed Inlet** (binary mixture) — between Zone II and Zone III
- **Raffinate Outlet** (A-rich) — between Zone III and Zone IV

**Zone Functions:**

| Zone | Function | Solid State | Liquid State |
|---|---|---|---|
| I (Eluent → Extract) | Desorption of B; solid regeneration | Saturated with B → B desorbed | Eluent becomes B-rich extract |
| II (Extract → Feed) | Readsorption of A; B desorption continues | A is readsorbed | Pure B extract flows out |
| III (Feed → Raffinate) | Adsorption of B; A passes through | B is adsorbed | Liquid becomes A-rich raffinate |
| IV (Raffinate → Eluent) | Desorption of A; solid purification | A is removed | A leaves as raffinate |

**Working Cycle (7 marks distributed as):**
- Diagram: 2 marks
- Explanation of each zone: 3 marks
- Port switching mechanism: 1 mark
- Liquid & solid direction note: 1 mark

---

### **Model Solution A(b) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Linear isotherms** $q_A = H_A C_A$, $q_B = H_B C_B$, with $H_A < H_B$. (1 mark)

**Step 2: Zone I analysis** — Solid entering from Zone IV is free of A and B. For A: $m_1 > H_A$ ensures A is not adsorbed in Zone I. For B: $m_1 < H_B$ ensures B is desorbed from solid in Zone I. Hence: $H_A < m_1 < H_B$. (2 marks)

**Step 3: Zone II** — Solid entering has some A. For pure B extract: A must readsorb (m₂ > H_A) and B must continue desorbing (m₂ < H_B). Hence: $H_A < m_2 < H_B$. (1.5 marks)

**Step 4: Zone III** — Feed enters; B must adsorb (m₃ < H_B) and A must pass through to raffinate (m₃ > H_A). Hence: $H_A < m_3 < H_B$. (1.5 marks)

**Step 5: Zone IV** — Solid exiting must be free of A to avoid contamination in next cycle. Hence $m_4 > H_A$. (1 mark)

**Final inequality set:**

$$\boxed{H_A < m_1 < m_2 < m_3 < H_B, \quad m_4 > H_A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting ε and V in m_j formula** — Many students write $m_j = Q_j \tau / V$ without the porosity ε. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Zone I and Zone IV constraints** — Zone I has BOTH upper and lower bounds; Zone IV has ONLY a lower bound.
> 3. **Mixing m_j with γ_j** — γ_j = m_j × (1−ε)/ε. These are different definitions; examiners penalize incorrect use.
> 4. **No physical meaning** — A common reason for 1–2 mark deduction is stating inequalities without explaining "why."
> 5. **Linear vs. non-linear confusion** — The triangle method (Storti) is for Langmuir, NOT linear isotherms.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice Alternative]*

**B(a) [7 marks]:** Discuss in detail the operating parameters that affect SMB performance. How do the flow-rate ratios m_j, switching time τ, and number of columns per zone (N_j) influence purity, recovery, productivity, and eluent consumption?

**B(b) [7 marks]:** With a flow diagram, describe the **Varicol** process (variable-column port switching) as an advanced SMB variant. Compare its advantages and disadvantages relative to classical SMB.

---

### **Model Solution B(a) — Operating Parameters (7 marks)**

**1. Flow-rate ratios m_j (3 marks):**
- Increasing m₁ → better B purity in extract (more eluent to strip A from solid).
- Decreasing m₃ → better A purity in raffinate (more retention of B on solid).
- The operating point must lie inside the Storti triangle.
- m_j values outside the triangle → contamination of either extract or raffinate.

**2. Switching time τ (2 marks):**
- Smaller τ → closer to TMB behaviour, higher purity.
- Larger τ → columns behave more like batch elution, less efficient.
- Typical range: **0.5 – 5 min**.

**3. Number of columns per zone N_j (2 marks):**
- More columns per zone → sharper separation, higher purity.
- Industry standard: 2 columns per zone (8-column SMB).
- 3 columns/zone (12-column) gives better performance for difficult separations.

---

### **Model Solution B(b) — Varicol Process (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Varicol (Variable-Column) is an SMB variant where the number of columns in each zone is **not fixed** but **varies asynchronously** from one switch to the next.

**Key feature:** While classical SMB has a strict periodic switching every τ, Varicol uses **irregular switching intervals** τ_i, allowing the port positions to "skip" columns in some switches.

**Flow diagram (text):**
```
Classical SMB:  P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 (uniform τ, fixed column count)
Varicol:        P1 → P2 → P3' → P4' (variable τ_i, variable column count)
```

**Advantages (3 marks):**
- 10–30% lower eluent consumption.
- Higher productivity at same purity.
- More flexibility for non-linear isotherms.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- More complex control system.
- Higher capital cost for automation.

**Comparison table (1 mark):**
| Parameter | Classical SMB | Varicol |
|---|---|---|
| Switching | Uniform | Variable |
| Columns/zone | Fixed | Variable |
| Eluent use | Higher | Lower |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Simulated Counter-Current Techniques (SMB):**

- ✅ **SMB simulates TMB** by periodic port switching, not by physical solid movement.
- ✅ **4 zones, 4 ports:** Eluent (E), Extract (X), Feed (F), Raffinate (R) — remember order E → X → F → R as you traverse the liquid flow.
- ✅ **Solid flows DOWN** (or appears to, in equivalent TMB); **liquid flows UP/RIGHT** in the equivalent sense.
- ✅ **m_j = (Q_j × τ) / (V × ε)** is the most important equation in the entire module.
- ✅ **Classical linear separation conditions:**
  - Zone I: $H_A < m_1 < H_B$
  - Zone II: $H_A < m_2 < H_B$
  - Zone III: $H_A < m_3 < H_B$
  - Zone IV: $m_4 > H_A$
- ✅ **Storti Triangle Method** is used for **Langmuir (non-linear)** isotherms, NOT linear.
- ✅ **Switching time τ** typically 0.5 – 5 min; total cycle = N_total × τ.
- ✅ **Typical industrial SMBs:** 6, 8, or 12 columns; UOP Parex uses 24 columns.
- ✅ **Purity, Recovery, Productivity, Eluent Consumption** are the four KPIs.
- ✅ **Variants:** Varicol, PowerFeed, ModiCon, Gradient SMB, MCSGP — know at least 2.
- ✅ **Industrial applications:** p-xylene, fructose, chiral drugs, FAME, mAb purification.
- ✅ **TMB vs. SMB:** TMB has attrition; SMB has no attrition, easier scale-up.
- ✅ **Henry's constant** $H_i = \partial q^* / \partial C \big|_{C_i^*}$ — partial derivative of adsorption isotherm.
- ✅ **Equivalence:** m_j = 1 means liquid and solid velocities are equal; m_j > 1 means liquid dominates.
- ✅ **Operating point** must lie INSIDE the Storti triangle; outside = product contamination.
- ✅ **Common mistake:** confusing γ_j = ((1−ε)/ε) × m_j with m_j itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Numerical Anchors (typical values to memorize for KTU 2024):**
> - Number of columns: **4, 6, 8, 12** (most common: 8)
> - Switching time: **τ ≈ 1 – 3 min** for lab scale
> - Porosity: **ε ≈ 0.4** (typical for silica/chiral stationary phases)
> - Henry constant range: **H = 0.5 – 5**
> - Industrial throughput: **> 99% purity achievable** with proper design

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison with Other Separation Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT756 - NOVEL SEPARATION PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison with Other Separation Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Novel Separation Processes (NSPs)** are non-conventional, energy-efficient mass transfer operations that exploit specific physical, chemical, or electrochemical gradients (rather than phase equilibrium differences) to achieve separation. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus (PECHT756), these are systematically compared against **conventional separation processes** such as distillation, absorption, conventional liquid–liquid extraction, leaching, drying, and crystallization.

A **comparison study** evaluates both families of separation techniques against a unified set of engineering and economic criteria, including:

- **Selectivity ($\alpha$)** — purity attainable per unit driving force.
- **Energy Intensity (MJ/kg product)** — minimum thermodynamic work vs. actual work input.
- **Capital Intensity ($/kg·yr capacity)** — fixed and operating cost ratios.
- **Operating Envelope** — temperature, pressure, and phase compatibility.
- **Environmental Quotient (EQ)** — waste generation, solvent recovery burden, and carbon footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates a quantitative comparison of at least **three novel processes** (commonly Membranes, Adsorption/PSA, and Supercritical Extraction) against their **conventional counterparts** (Distillation, Stripping, Solvent Extraction) using the criteria above.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Coffee Filter Analogy"

Think of a conventional separation method like **boiling a salty seawater pan until all the water evaporates** — this is essentially *distillation*: brute-force, energy-hungry, and indiscriminate.

A **novel separation process**, on the other hand, behaves like a **precision sieve or a magnet** that pulls only the unwanted component (salt ions) through a smart barrier (membrane), leaving the rest untouched. It does not require boiling; it requires only a gentle pressure or concentration difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:**
> - **Distillation = Cook out the water** (High energy, slow, simple equipment).
> - **Membrane/Novel Process = Pick the salt out with tweezers** (Low energy, fast, sophisticated equipment).
> The "right tool" depends on the **mixture's sensitivity to heat**, the **desired purity**, and the **scale of operation**.

## 1.3 Why This Comparison Matters (Engineering Rationale)

In chemical process industries, **separation alone can consume 40–70 % of the total plant energy**. Selecting the right separation route — conventional *vs.* novel — directly determines:

| Decision Driver | Impact on Plant Economics |
|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Energy cost** | Operating expense (OPEX) reduction of 30–80 % |
| **Heat-sensitive products** | Avoids thermal degradation (pharma, food) |
| **Azeotrope formation** | Breaks equilibrium limitations of distillation |
| **Solvent regulations** | Reduces VOC emissions (REACH compliance) |
| **Modular scalability** | Enables distributed/skid-mounted plants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Dimensional Comparison Framework (KTU Standard)

The KTU board examiner expects students to compare separation processes along **six standardized axes**. Each axis uses a quantitative metric so the comparison is not merely qualitative.

### Axis 1 — Selectivity ($\alpha$)
The ability to discriminate between two species $i$ and $j$:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{y_i / x_i}{y_j / x_j}$$

where $x$ is the mole fraction in the feed (raffinate) and $y$ is the mole fraction in the permeate (extract).

### Axis 2 — Thermodynamic Minimum Work of Separation
Reversible (minimum) work for an isothermal, isobaric binary separation:

$$W_{\min} = -RT \sum_{k} n_k \ln(\gamma_k x_k)$$

For an ideal binary mixture separating $n_1$ moles of component 1 from $n_2$ moles of component 2 at composition $x_1$:

$$W_{\min} = -RT \left[ n_1 \ln\left(\frac{x_{1,\text{prod}}}{x_{1,\text{feed}}}\right) + n_2 \ln\left(\frac{x_{2,\text{prod}}}{x_{2,\text{feed}}}\right) \right]$$

### Axis 3 — Actual Energy Consumption
Compare real work input $W_{\text{actual}}$ with $W_{\min}$ via the **Second-Law Efficiency**:

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{W_{\min}}{W_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%$$

A process with $\eta_{\text{II}} > 30\%$ is considered thermodynamically efficient.

### Axis 4 — Mass Transfer Flux (for membrane-based novel processes)
For permeation through a membrane:

$$J_i = P_i \left( p_{i,\text{feed}} - p_{i,\text{permeate}} \right)$$

where $P_i$ is the **permeability** (mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹) and $p$ are partial pressures.

### Axis 5 — Stage Requirement (for equilibrium-based conventional processes)
The Kremser equation for an equilibrium stage:

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - y_0} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

where $A = L / (mV)$ is the absorption factor.

### Axis 6 — Capital Cost per Unit Separation
The **$6/10$ th rule of thumb** for cost scaling (valid for capacity changes between 0.5x and 2x):

$$C_2 = C_1 \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^{0.6}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. Every formula below is examinable.**

| # | Parameter | Equation | Typical Units | Application |
|---|-----------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Selectivity | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{y_i/x_i}{y_j/x_j}$ | dimensionless | Distillation, Extraction, Membrane |
| 2 | Min. Work of Separation | $W_{\min} = -RT \sum n_k \ln(\gamma_k x_k)$ | J | Energy audit |
| 3 | 2nd Law Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{II}} = W_{\min}/W_{\text{actual}}$ | % | Process comparison |
| 4 | Membrane Flux | $J_i = P_i \Delta p_i$ | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | RO, NF, PV |
| 5 | Sieving Coefficient | $S_i = C_{\text{p}} / C_{\text{f}}$ | dimensionless | UF, dialysis |
| 6 | HETP | $\text{HETP} = z / N$ | m | Distillation columns |
| 7 | Cost Scaling | $C_2 = C_1 (S_2/S_1)^{0.6}$ | \$ | CAPEX estimation |
| 8 | Energy per kg | $E = W_{\text{actual}} / m_{\text{product}}$ | MJ/kg | OPEX estimation |
| 9 | Recovery | $R_i = (C_{\text{f}} - C_{\text{r}})/C_{\text{f}}$ | dimensionless | Mass balance check |
| 10 | Permeance | $Q_i = J_i / \Delta p_i$ | GPU or mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ | Membrane spec |

**Note on notation:** Vertical bars in absolute values are written as $\vert x \vert$ to maintain markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Comparison Framework

- **Petroleum Refining:** Selection of extractive distillation *vs.* membrane pervaporation for benzene/cyclohexane separation (an azeotrope). Membranes win on energy but lose on capacity.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Cold separation (chromatography, membrane) of heat-labile APIs that would degrade in distillation.
- **Water Treatment:** Reverse Osmosis vs. Multi-Effect Distillation (MED) — RO wins for salinities < 50 000 ppm; MED wins for higher concentrations.
- **CO₂ Capture:** Amine absorption *vs.* Membrane separation *vs.* PSA — selection depends on flue gas pressure and CO₂ partial pressure.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermodynamic Minimum Work for Binary Separation

We start with the **fundamental Gibbs free-energy change** of mixing at constant T and P for an ideal solution:

$$ \Delta G_{\text{mix}} = nRT \sum_{k=1}^{N} x_k \ln x_k $$

For a binary mixture of $n_1$ moles of component 1 and $n_2$ moles of component 2, with mole fractions $x_1$ and $x_2 = 1 - x_1$:

$$ \Delta G_{\text{mix}} = (n_1 + n_2) RT \left( x_1 \ln x_1 + x_2 \ln x_2 \right) $$

To separate the mixture into two pure streams (each with $n_1$ and $n_2$ moles), we conceptually *un-mix*. The **reverse process** requires the minimum reversible work $W_{\min}$:

$$ W_{\min} = - \Delta G_{\text{mix}} = -(n_1 + n_2) RT \left( x_1 \ln x_1 + (1 - x_1) \ln(1 - x_1) \right) $$

**Logical transition to the per-component form (more useful in real process design):**
Starting from the general Gibbs free energy for a multi-component system:

$$ G = \sum_{k} n_k \mu_k $$

For ideal solutions, $\mu_k = \mu_k^0 + RT \ln x_k$. After separation, the same $n_k$ moles of each component are at pure reference state. So the minimum work per stream becomes:

$$ W_{\min} = -RT \sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k \ln(x_k) $$

We now apply this to a specific engineering case study to make it concrete.

---

### Worked Case Study — Separating 100 kmol of a 50/50 Benzene/Toluene Mixture

**Given:**
- $n_1$ (Benzene) = 50 kmol
- $n_2$ (Toluene) = 50 kmol
- $x_1 = x_2 = 0.5$
- $T = 298$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹

**Goal:** Recover 49 kmol benzene at 99 % purity and 49 kmol toluene at 99 % purity. Remaining 1 kmol each in a mixed residue.

**Step 1 — Compute feed Gibbs penalty:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
W_{\min}^{\text{feed}} &= -RT \sum n_k \ln x_k \\
&= -(8.314)(298) \left[ 50{,}000 \ln(0.5) + 50{,}000 \ln(0.5) \right] \\
&= -(2477.6) \left[ -34{,}657.4 - 34{,}657.4 \right] \\
&= (2477.6)(69{,}314.8) \\
&= 1.717 \times 10^{8} \text{ J} \\
&= 171.7 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 2 — Compute product stream Gibbs (each product at 99 % purity, 1 % impurity):**

For 49 kmol benzene at $x_B = 0.99$:

$$ n_B \ln x_B = 49{,}000 \times \ln(0.99) = 49{,}000 \times (-0.01005) = -492.5 $$

For 1 kmol benzene in residue at $x_B = 0.01$:

$$ n_B \ln x_B = 1{,}000 \times \ln(0.01) = 1{,}000 \times (-4.605) = -4{,}605 $$

Sum for benzene across all streams:

$$ \sum n_B \ln x_B = -492.5 - 4{,}605 = -5{,}097.5 $$

**Step 3 — Sum across both components and apply the formula:**

Total $\sum n_k \ln x_k$ for both products and residue ≈ $-23{,}000$ (summed similarly for toluene).

$$ W_{\min}^{\text{products}} = (8.314)(298)(23{,}000) = 56.9 \text{ MJ} $$

**Step 4 — Net minimum work:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
W_{\min}^{\text{net}} &= W_{\min}^{\text{feed}} - W_{\min}^{\text{products}} \\
&= 171.7 - 56.9 = 114.8 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 5 — Interpret against actual distillation energy:**
A real distillation column for this 100 kmol mixture consumes ≈ **2 800 MJ** of steam (≈ 25× more than $W_{\min}$). Hence:

$$ \eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{114.8}{2800} \times 100\% = 4.1\% $$

A novel process (e.g., extractive distillation with membrane recovery, or Pervaporation) typically achieves $\eta_{\text{II}}$ of **15–35 %**, justifying its selection despite higher capital cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Comparing Membrane vs Distillation using Capital + Energy Cost

Let $C_{\text{cap}}$ be the annualized capital cost ($/yr) and $C_{\text{op}}$ the operating cost ($/yr). The **Total Annualized Cost (TAC)** is:

$$ \text{TAC} = C_{\text{cap}} + C_{\text{op}} $$

For a **membrane system** (capital-dominated, low operating cost):

$$ \text{TAC}_{\text{mem}} = \left( \frac{C_{\text{install}} \cdot f_{\text{capital recovery}}}{\text{Plant life (yr)}} \right) + \left( W_{\text{actual}} \cdot C_{\text{electricity}} \right) $$

For a **distillation column** (operating-dominated, moderate capital):

$$ \text{TAC}_{\text{dist}} = \left( \frac{C_{\text{column + internals + reboiler/condenser}}}{\text{Plant life}} \right) + \left( Q_{\text{reboiler}} \cdot C_{\text{steam}} \right) $$

**Breakeven Condition:** Set $\text{TAC}_{\text{mem}} = \text{TAC}_{\text{dist}}$ and solve for the **critical plant capacity** beyond which distillation becomes cheaper. This is a standard KTU numerical problem.

---

The matrix below is the **single most important KTU-table to memorize** for this topic. It directly answers 14-mark comparison questions.

| Criterion | Distillation (Conv.) | Solvent Extraction (Conv.) | Membrane (Novel) | Adsorption/PSA (Novel) | SCFE (Novel) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Driving force** | Vapor–liquid equilibrium | Distribution coefficient ($\beta$) | Partial pressure / conc. diff. | Adsorption isotherm | Solubility in SCF |
| **Selectivity range** | 1.2 – 50 | 5 – 1000 | 5 – 200 | 10 – 10 000 | 50 – 5000 |
| **Energy (MJ/kg)** | 1.0 – 10.0 | 0.5 – 3.0 | 0.1 – 0.5 | 0.2 – 0.8 | 0.3 – 1.0 |
| **Operating T (K)** | 350 – 500 | 300 – 400 | 280 – 320 | 280 – 350 | 308 – 350 |
| **Operating P (bar)** | 1 – 10 | 1 – 5 | 5 – 80 | 1 – 30 | 80 – 350 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Decision Logic for Selecting Novel vs Conventional

This diagram is the standard KTU "selection flowchart" answer for a 7-mark question on process selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mixture to be separated] --> B{Is mixture heat-sensitive?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Can it form an azeotrope?}
    B -- No --> D{Is scale > 10 000 kg per day?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Choose NOVEL process: Membrane / PSA / SCFE]
    C -- No --> F[Choose NOVEL: Pervaporation / UF]
    D -- Yes --> G{Is selectivity sufficient with distn?}
    D -- No --> H[Choose NOVEL: Modular membrane skid]
    G -- Yes --> I[Choose CONVENTIONAL: Distillation]
    G -- No --> J{Are reboiler temps acceptable?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Choose CONVENTIONAL: Extractive Distn]
    J -- No --> E
    E --> L[End: Novel Process Selected]
    F --> L
    H --> L
    I --> M[End: Conventional Process Selected]
    K --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Comparison Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Boundary Conditions]
        A1[Feed Composition] --> C1
        A2[Required Purity] --> C1
        A3[Capacity] --> C1
        A4[Temperature Limits] --> C1
    end
    subgraph METRICS[Six-Dimensional Comparison Engine]
        C1[Selectivity Alpha] --> D1[Score 0 to 10]
        C2[W min kJ per mol] --> D1
        C3[CAPEX kUSD per year] --> D1
        C4[OPEX kUSD per year] --> D1
        C5[Env. EQ] --> D1
        C6[Scale-up Ease] --> D1
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output: Process Selection]
        D1 --> E1{Sum of weighted scores}
        E1 --> F1[Conventional Recommended]
        E1 --> F2[Novel Recommended]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Energy Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Drying Evaporation] --> P2[Distillation]
    P2 --> P3[Conventional Extraction]
    P3 --> P4[Adsorption PSA]
    P4 --> P5[Membrane Separation]
    P5 --> P6[Supercritical Fluid Extraction]
    P1 -.- NOTE[Energy per kg product DECREASES ascending the pyramid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Each ascending step on the pyramid typically reduces energy per kg product by a factor of **2 to 5×**, but with capital cost rising by a similar factor. The optimal economic choice is the **breakeven** layer for the given capacity.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**Compare the energy efficiency of distillation and reverse osmosis (RO) for desalination of seawater (35 000 ppm TDS) at a capacity of 10 000 m³/day.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Distillation (MED/MSF) requires **phase change** of water, needing ≈ **2257 kJ/kg** as latent heat plus sensible heat losses. Total specific energy ≈ **250–300 kJ/kg** water. **[1 mark]**
- Reverse osmosis uses a **mechanical pressure gradient** of 50–80 bar across a semipermeable membrane. The minimum thermodynamic work of separation is ≈ **1.06 kJ/kg** water. Real RO consumes **3–6 kJ/kg** water (electrical), which is ≈ **40–80× less thermal energy** than distillation. **[1 mark]**
- However, RO has a **concentration polarization** limit and a **maximum recovery** of ≈ 50 % per pass (multi-stage needed for higher recovery), whereas distillation handles any salinity directly. Therefore, **RO is preferred** for salinities up to ≈ 50 000 ppm at the given capacity, but **MED becomes competitive** at very high salinities (e.g., brine concentrators). **[1 mark]**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**
**List any three criteria used to compare novel and conventional separation processes, with one quantitative metric for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per criterion + metric):**

1. **Selectivity ($\alpha_{ij}$)** — dimensionless; e.g., RO membrane $\alpha_{\text{salt/water}} \approx 100$–$200$, while simple distillation $\alpha \approx 1.2$–$5$. **[1 mark]**
2. **Second-Law Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{II}}$)** — %; e.g., novel pervaporation $\eta_{\text{II}} \approx 25$–$35$ %, distillation $\eta_{\text{II}} \approx 2$–$10$ %. **[1 mark]**
3. **Specific Energy Consumption ($E$ in MJ/kg product)** — e.g., RO ≈ 0.004–0.010 MJ/kg; distillation ≈ 1.0–3.0 MJ/kg. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the thermodynamic minimum work of separation for an ideal binary liquid mixture of 60 kmol A (x_A = 0.4) and 40 kmol B (x_B = 0.6) at 300 K. Comment on how the actual energy consumption of distillation compares with this minimum.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat comparison table, evaluate distillation, liquid–liquid extraction, and membrane separation on the basis of: driving force, selectivity range, specific energy, heat-sensitivity compatibility, and environmental impact. Recommend the best process for separating a 50/50 water–ethanol mixture (which forms an azeotrope at x_ethanol = 0.894).

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Given:** $n_A = 60$ kmol, $n_B = 40$ kmol, $x_A = 0.4$, $x_B = 0.6$, $T = 300$ K, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, ideal solution.

**Step 1 — Apply the minimum work formula:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
W_{\min} &= -RT \left[ n_A \ln x_A + n_B \ln x_B \right] \\
&= -(8.314)(300) \left[ 60{,}000 \ln(0.4) + 40{,}000 \ln(0.6) \right] \\
&= -(2494.2) \left[ 60{,}000(-0.9163) + 40{,}000(-0.5108) \right] \\
&= -(2494.2) \left[ -54{,}978 - 20{,}432 \right] \\
&= -(2494.2)(-75{,}410) \\
&= 1.881 \times 10^{8} \text{ J} \\
&= 188.1 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 2 — Convert to per-kmol basis for easy comparison:**

$$ w_{\min} = \frac{188.1 \text{ MJ}}{100 \text{ kmol}} = 1.881 \text{ MJ/kmol} $$

**Step 3 — Compare with actual distillation energy:**
For a 50/50 mixture with relative volatility $\alpha_{AB} \approx 2.5$, a real distillation column (≈ 30 theoretical stages, reflux ratio ≈ 5) consumes about **7–10 MJ/kmol** of steam equivalent. Second-Law efficiency:

$$ \eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{1.881}{8.5} \times 100\% \approx 22\% $$

This is higher than typical because the mixture is concentrated (not dilute). Distillation is reasonably efficient for such well-behaved systems. **[7 marks — 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for numerical substitution, 2 marks for commentary]**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Distillation | LLE | Membrane |
|-----------|--------------|-----|----------|
| Driving force | Vapor–liquid $\alpha$ | Distribution ratio $\beta$ | Pressure/Conc. gradient |
| Selectivity | 1.2 – 50 | 5 – 1000 | 5 – 200 |
| Specific energy (MJ/kg) | 1 – 3 | 0.5 – 1.5 | 0.05 – 0.3 |
| Heat-sensitive compatibility | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Environmental impact | High (heat) | High (solvent) | Low |

**Recommendation for Water–Ethanol Azeotrope:** Conventional distillation **cannot cross** the azeotrope (x_ethanol = 0.894) under atmospheric pressure. Options: **[2 marks]**

1. **Pressure-Swing Distillation** — Distillation at two pressures (1 bar & 10 bar) shifts the azeotrope composition, but requires two columns → high CAPEX and OPEX.
2. **Azeotropic Distillation** — Adding benzene forms a ternary heteroazeotrope; works but introduces toxic third component.
3. **Pervaporation (Membrane)** — A hydrophilic membrane (e.g., NaA zeolite) preferentially permeates water, breaking the azeotrope in a single stage. Energy ≈ 0.1–0.3 MJ/kg ethanol — **3–10× less** than distillation. **Recommended for heat-sensitive pharmaceutical-grade ethanol.** **[2 marks]**

**Verdict:** **Pervaporation is the best choice** — it breaks the azeotrope, saves energy, avoids toxic entrainers, and works at mild temperatures (≤ 350 K). **[7 marks total — 3 for table, 2 for azeotrope analysis, 2 for recommendation]**

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** What is "Second-Law Efficiency" of a separation process? For a benzene-toluene distillation column consuming 12 000 MJ of steam per 100 kmol of feed (50/50), with $W_{\min} = 1.72$ MJ, calculate the second-law efficiency and comment on its significance in selecting a novel separation process.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA) with conventional absorption (amine-based CO₂ capture) on the basis of: principle, selectivity, energy per kg CO₂, equipment footprint, and environmental impact. Which one would you recommend for capturing CO₂ from a flue gas stream at 1 bar and 15 vol % CO₂?

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** Second-Law Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{II}}$) is the ratio of the **thermodynamically minimum reversible work** required for a separation to the **actual work** consumed by the real process, expressed as a percentage:

$$ \eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{W_{\min}}{W_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\% $$

It quantifies how close the real process is to reversible (ideal) performance.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

$$ \eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{1.72 \text{ MJ}}{12{,}000 \text{ MJ}} \times 100\% = 0.0143\% $$

**Commentary (2 marks):** A value of 0.0143 % indicates that **less than 0.02 %** of the input energy is doing "useful" reversible separation work — the remaining 99.99 % is dissipated as irreversibilities (heat losses, pressure drops, mixing). This is a strong justification for adopting a novel process (e.g., membrane + PSA hybrid) which can push $\eta_{\text{II}}$ to 5–20 %.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Amine Absorption (Conv.) | PSA (Novel) |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Principle | Chemical reaction of CO₂ with amines (MEA, MDEA) | Physical adsorption on zeolite/activated carbon; cyclic pressure swing |
| Selectivity (CO₂/N₂) | 5 – 50 (chemistry) | 20 – 200 (adsorbent choice) |
| Energy (MJ/kg CO₂) | 3.0 – 4.5 (mainly reboiler heat) | 0.5 – 1.2 (electrical) |
| Footprint | Large columns, regenerator | Compact modular vessels |
| Env. impact | Amine degradation → toxic waste | Solid adsorbent, regenerable; less waste |

**Recommendation (3 marks):**
For **flue gas at 1 bar and 15 vol % CO₂**:
- **Partial pressure of CO₂** = $0.15 \times 1 = 0.15$ bar.
- Amine absorption works well at low CO₂ partial pressures (high relative driving force) and is the **mature, well-established** technology.
- PSA is more effective when CO₂ partial pressure is **higher (> 0.5 bar)** because the adsorption capacity scales with partial pressure (linear Henry's regime for physical adsorbents).

For the **given conditions (0.15 bar)**, **amine absorption is the more energy-efficient and cost-effective choice**, especially at large scales (> 500 t CO₂/day). **PSA is recommended** only for smaller, modular installations or as a polishing step downstream of absorption in a hybrid scheme.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not write "distillation is always bad"** — the question asks for a *comparison*. State explicitly that distillation is preferred for **large scale**, **high volatility ratios**, and **non-heat-sensitive** mixtures. Board examiners deduct 1–2 marks for one-sided answers.
> 2. **Always include the azeotrope caveat** for ethanol/water, benzene/cyclohexane, and similar systems — the inability of distillation to break azeotropes is the #1 justification for novel processes.
> 3. **Convert units correctly**: $W_{\min}$ in J or kJ, never leave a unitless logarithm. Logarithm of mole fraction is dimensionless, but the *prefactor* carries units.
> 4. **Sign convention matters**: $W_{\min}$ is *positive* (work input required). Writing it as negative loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Do not confuse selectivity ($\alpha$) with separation factor ($\beta$)** — they are different: $\alpha$ is for equilibrium stages, $\beta$ is for extraction ratios.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Novel Separation Processes** exploit **non-equilibrium driving forces** (pressure, concentration, electrical potential) rather than phase changes.
- [x] **Conventional vs Novel** is **NOT** "good vs bad" — it is a **scale-dependent, energy-vs-capital trade-off**.
- [x] **Six comparison criteria** (memorize in this order): Selectivity, Min. Work, 2nd-Law Efficiency, Flux, Stages, Cost.
- [x] **Minimum Work Formula**: $W_{\min} = -RT \sum n_k \ln x_k$ — derive on the spot if asked.
- [x] **Membrane processes** (RO, NF, UF, PV) win on **energy, modularity, and heat-sensitive products**; lose on **selectivity for close-boiling mixtures** and **membrane replacement cost**.
- [x] **PSA** wins on **selectivity** and **no solvent**; loses on **cyclic (batch) operation** and **adsorbent attrition**.
- [x] **SCFE** wins on **environmental friendliness (CO₂)** and **food/pharma compatibility**; loses on **very high pressure (80–350 bar)** equipment cost.
- [x] **Azeotrope-breaking** is the classic justification for novel processes: distillation cannot cross an azeotrope, but pervaporation, extractive membranes, and pressure-swing distillation can.
- [x] **Second-Law Efficiency** of distillation ≈ **2–10 %**; of novel processes ≈ **15–50 %** — this is the strongest KTU-justification for switching.
- [x] **Cost scaling exponent is 0.6** (the famous $6/10$ rule) for both conventional and novel processes.
- [x] **Mermaid diagrams** are not required in answer scripts, but drawing a **selection flowchart** with boxes and arrows earns 1–2 extra marks in long-answer questions.
- [x] **Units checklist**: $W_{\min}$ in J or MJ, $J$ in mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹, $P$ in mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹, $\alpha$ dimensionless, $\eta$ in %.
